topics.title: Materials for MEMS: Silicon - Structure and Orientations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Materials for MEMS: Silicon - Structure and Orientations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Silicon (Si)** is a group IV element with atomic number **14** and a covalent diamond cubic crystal structure. It is the foundational substrate material for the vast majority of Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) due to its excellent mechanical properties, mature fabrication infrastructure (inherited from the semiconductor industry), and predictable anisotropic etching behavior.

In the context of KTU's MEMS syllabus, silicon's relevance extends far beyond its electrical (semiconducting) properties. We treat silicon as a **structural mechanical material** whose elastic modulus, yield strength, and crystallographic orientation directly govern the geometry, residual stress, and functional behavior of micro-sensors and micro-actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diamond Cubic Structure:** Each silicon atom is covalently bonded to **4 neighboring atoms** arranged at the corners of a perfect **tetrahedron**. The lattice constant of silicon is **a = 5.431 Å (0.5431 nm)** at 300 K. The unit cell contains **8 atoms**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine constructing a **giant 3D pyramid made of Lego bricks**, where each brick represents one silicon atom. Now imagine duplicating this pyramid at every corner and at every face-center of an invisible box. The resulting framework forms a **face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice with a two-atom basis** — this is the diamond cubic structure.

- The **FCC scaffold** = the underlying geometry of the crystal lattice.
- The **tetrahedral bonding** = the "glue" that makes silicon mechanically strong, brittle, and orientation-dependent.

When a MEMS engineer "cuts" this crystal along different planes (such as (100), (110), or (111)), the **arrangement of atoms on the exposed surface differs dramatically**. This is analogous to slicing a loaf of bread at different angles — the surface pattern and packing density of raisins (atoms) change depending on how you slice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why silicon for MEMS?** Single-crystal silicon exhibits:
> - **High Young's Modulus:** E ≈ 130–190 GPa (orientation dependent)
> - **Near-zero mechanical hysteresis (no plastic deformation at room temperature)**
> - **Excellent fatigue resistance**
> - **Density ≈ 2.33 g/cm³**
> - **Predictable anisotropic wet etching using KOH, TMAH, EDP**

### 1.3 The Concept of Crystal Orientation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystal Orientation** refers to the specific crystallographic plane that lies parallel to the wafer surface. It is denoted using **Miller Indices (hkl)** — a set of three integers that uniquely identify a plane in a cubic crystal.

A silicon wafer labeled **"(100)"** means the (100) crystallographic plane is parallel to the wafer's top surface. The choice of orientation fundamentally controls:

- Anisotropic etching rates (e.g., KOH etches (100) ≈ 400× faster than (111))
- Mechanical stress anisotropy
- Piezoelectric coupling (in doped variants)
- Surface roughness and film adhesion

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Diamond Cubic Structure — Step-by-Step Logic

The silicon crystal can be described as **two interpenetrating FCC lattices offset by (a/4, a/4, a/4)** along the body diagonal.

**Structural Logic:**

1. **Base Lattice:** An FCC arrangement of silicon atoms at the corners and face centers of the cubic unit cell.
2. **Basis Atoms:** Four additional silicon atoms occupy tetrahedral interstitial sites at positions (¼,¼,¼), (¾,¾,¼), (¾,¼,¾), (¼,¾,¾).
3. **Coordination Number:** Each atom has 4 nearest neighbors at a distance of **√3 a / 4 ≈ 2.35 Å**.
4. **Atomic Packing Factor (APF):** For diamond cubic, **APF = π√3 / 16 ≈ 0.34** (only 34% of the volume is occupied — silicon is *not* close-packed, unlike FCC metals).
5. **Bonding:** Purely **covalent (sp³ hybridization)** with a bond energy of **~226 kJ/mol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because the APF is low, silicon has open channels along the ⟨100⟩ direction. These channels facilitate the diffusion of dopants and the penetration of etchants — a critical fact exploited in MEMS bulk micromachining.

### 2.2 Miller Indices (hkl) — The Language of Planes

**Miller Index Procedure (Mandatory in KTU Board Exams):**

1. Identify the intercepts of the plane on the x, y, z axes in units of the lattice constant `a`.
2. Take the **reciprocals** of these intercepts.
3. Clear fractions to obtain the smallest set of integers.
4. Enclose in parentheses: **(hkl)**.

**Negative intercepts** are denoted with an overbar: e.g., $(\bar{1}00)$.

**Family of equivalent planes** is denoted with curly braces: **{hkl}**.

### 2.3 Important Silicon Planes in MEMS

| Plane | Direction Family | Density of Atoms on Plane | Surface Bond Density | MEMS Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (100) | ⟨100⟩ | 2 atoms / a² | 2 dangling bonds / atom | Standard wafer; isotropic-in-plane stress; preferred for KOH etching |
| (110) | ⟨110⟩ | 4 atoms / a² | 1 dangling bond / atom | Used for vertical sidewalls; deepest channels |
| (111) | ⟨111⟩ | 2 atoms / (a/√2)² = highest packing | 1 dangling bond / atom | Etch-stop plane; very slow etch rate; triangular pyramids |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is high-yield for KTU University Examinations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lattice constant of Si | $a = 5.431 \times 10^{-10}$ | meters (m) |
| 2 | Number of atoms per unit cell (diamond) | $N = 8$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Nearest-neighbor distance | $d_{nn} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,a}{4}$ | meters |
| 4 | Atomic density (volume) | $n_{vol} = \dfrac{8}{a^3} = 5.0 \times 10^{28}$ | atoms/m³ |
| 5 | Interplanar spacing (cubic) | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$ | meters |
| 6 | Surface atomic density (hkl) | $\sigma_{hkl} = \dfrac{\text{atoms in plane}}{A_{plane}}$ | atoms/m² |
| 7 | Atomic Packing Factor (diamond) | $APF = \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{3}}{16} \approx 0.34$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Young's Modulus (Si, ⟨100⟩) | $E_{\langle 100 \rangle} \approx 130$ | GPa |
| 9 | Young's Modulus (Si, ⟨110⟩) | $E_{\langle 110 \rangle} \approx 169$ | GPa |
| 10 | Young's Modulus (Si, ⟨111⟩) | $E_{\langle 111 \rangle} \approx 188$ | GPa |
| 11 | Poisson's Ratio (Si) | $\nu \approx 0.28$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Density of Si | $\rho = 2330$ | kg/m³ |
| 13 | KOH Etch Rate Ratio | $R_{(100)} / R_{(111)} \approx 400 : 1$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | KOH Etch Rate Ratio (110 vs 111) | $R_{(110)} / R_{(111)} \approx 600 : 1$ | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Restriction:** In the KTU Formula Sheet above, note that I used `\vert` / `\mid` syntax is not required because all values are dimensionally unambiguous. However, for absolute value representations like `|h|`, use $\lvert h \rvert$ in your answer scripts to avoid LaTeX table-parsing errors.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The orientation-dependent etch selectivity of silicon is the **workhorse principle behind all bulk micromachining**:

- **(100) wafers + KOH** → Produce **V-grooves, pyramidal pits, diaphragms, cantilevers** with 54.74° sidewalls.
- **(110) wafers + KOH** → Produce **perfectly vertical sidewalls**, ideal for through-wafer vias and comb-drive structures.
- **(111) wafers** → Rarely etched; used as **etch-stops** because of their exceptionally low etch rate.

This predictable geometric control is what made silicon the undisputed king of MEMS materials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Interplanar Spacing for a Cubic Crystal

**Goal:** Derive the spacing $d_{hkl}$ between adjacent (hkl) planes in a cubic lattice.

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system.**

Consider a cubic unit cell with axes $x, y, z$ along the cube edges of length $a$. Let a (hkl) plane intersect the axes at $(a/h, a/k, a/l)$ — by definition of Miller indices.

**Step 2 — Write the equation of the plane.**

The plane equation through three intercepts $(x_0, y_0, z_0) = (a/h,\, a/k,\, a/l)$ is:

$$\frac{x}{a/h} + \frac{y}{a/k} + \frac{z}{a/l} = 1$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{hx}{a} + \frac{ky}{a} + \frac{lz}{a} = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad hx + ky + lz = a$$

**Step 3 — Express distance from origin to plane.**

The perpendicular distance from the origin $(0,0,0)$ to the plane $hx + ky + lz = a$ is given by:

$$d = \frac{\lvert h(0) + k(0) + l(0) - a \rvert}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with a known case.**

For the (100) plane: $h=1, k=0, l=0$

$$d_{100} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{1^2 + 0 + 0}} = a \quad \checkmark$$

For the (111) plane:

$$d_{111} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2}} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $d_{111}$ is the **smallest** spacing among low-index planes (≈ 3.14 Å). Closely spaced, densely packed planes are mechanically stable and chemically inert — explaining why (111) is the slowest-etching plane.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Surface Atomic Density on the (100) Plane

**Step 1 — Identify the atoms on the (100) face of the unit cell.**

Looking down the z-axis onto the top face:
- **4 corner atoms**, each shared with **4 adjacent cells** in-plane → contribution $= 4 \times \tfrac{1}{4} = 1$ atom.
- **1 face-center atom**, fully inside the face → contribution $= 1$ atom.

Total atoms on (100) face $= 2$ atoms.

**Step 2 — Compute the area of the (100) face.**

$$A_{100} = a \times a = a^2$$

**Step 3 — Surface atomic density.**

$$\sigma_{100} = \frac{2}{a^2}$$

Numerically:

$$\sigma_{100} = \frac{2}{(5.431 \times 10^{-10})^2} = 6.78 \times 10^{18} \text{ atoms/m}^2$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Surface Atomic Density on the (111) Plane

**Step 1 — Identify the (111) plane geometry.**

The (111) plane intersects x, y, z axes at $a, a, a$ and forms an equilateral triangle in the unit cell. The triangle has vertices at $(a,0,0), (0,a,0), (0,0,a)$.

**Step 2 — Identify atoms on the plane.**

The (111) plane contains:
- 3 corner atoms (each shared by 6 triangles within the crystal) → contribution $= 3 \times \tfrac{1}{6} = \tfrac{1}{2}$ atom.
- 3 edge atoms at midpoints of the triangle edges (each shared by 2 triangles) → contribution $= 3 \times \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{3}{2}$ atoms.

Total $= 2$ atoms on the triangular plane.

**Step 3 — Compute the area of the equilateral triangle.**

Side length of triangle $= a\sqrt{2}$.

$$A_{111} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} (a\sqrt{2})^2 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot 2a^2 = \frac{a^2 \sqrt{3}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Surface atomic density.**

$$\sigma_{111} = \frac{2}{a^2 \sqrt{3}/2} = \frac{4}{a^2 \sqrt{3}} = \frac{4\sqrt{3}}{3 a^2}$$

Numerically:

$$\sigma_{111} \approx 11.74 \times 10^{18} \text{ atoms/m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** $\sigma_{111} / \sigma_{100} \approx 1.73$. The (111) plane is **~73% denser** in surface atoms than the (100) plane, which directly explains its higher chemical stability and resistance to etchants.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Young's Modulus Anisotropy in Silicon

Silicon's stiffness is **direction-dependent** because of its anisotropic crystal symmetry. The general compliance tensor for cubic crystals yields, after tensor rotation:

$$E_{\langle hkl \rangle}^{-1} = s_{11} - 2 \left( s_{11} - s_{12} - \tfrac{1}{2}s_{44} \right) \cdot \left( \ell_1^2 \ell_2^2 + \ell_2^2 \ell_3^2 + \ell_3^2 \ell_1^2 \right)$$

where $\ell_1, \ell_2, \ell_3$ are direction cosines of the loading axis with respect to cube edges.

For ⟨100⟩ ($\ell_1=1, \ell_2=\ell_3=0$):

$$E_{\langle 100 \rangle} = \frac{1}{s_{11}} \approx 130 \text{ GPa}$$

For ⟨111⟩ ($\ell_1=\ell_2=\ell_3=1/\sqrt{3}$):

$$E_{\langle 111 \rangle} = \frac{1}{s_{11} - \tfrac{1}{3}(s_{11}-s_{12}-\tfrac{1}{2}s_{44})} \approx 188 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** When designing MEMS resonators or stress-sensitive structures, the designer MUST specify the wafer orientation because the stiffness — and therefore the resonant frequency — can vary by **~45%** between ⟨100⟩ and ⟨111⟩ directions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Silicon Crystal Structure Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Silicon Single Crystal"] --> B["FCC Sublattice A"]
    A --> C["FCC Sublattice B"]
    B --> B1["Corner atoms 8"]
    B --> B2["Face center atoms 6"]
    C --> C1["Tetrahedral sites 4"]
    C --> C2["Offset by a by 4 a by 4 a by 4"]
    A --> D["Lattice Constant a equals 5.431 Angstrom"]
    A --> E["Coordination Number 4"]
    A --> F["Bond Type sp3 Covalent"]
    A --> G["APF equals 0.34"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Wafer Orientation Selection Logic for MEMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Choose Wafer Orientation"] --> Q1{"Primary Fabrication Need"}
    Q1 -->|"Bulk etching diaphragms"| W100["Use 100 Wafer"]
    Q1 -->|"Vertical sidewalls"| W110["Use 110 Wafer"]
    Q1 -->|"Etch stop or smooth surface"| W111["Use 111 Wafer"]
    W100 --> E100["KOH produces 54.74 deg sidewalls"]
    W110 --> E110["KOH produces 90 deg vertical walls"]
    W111 --> E111["KOH etch rate negligible acts as stop"]
    E100 --> APP1["Pressure sensors accelerometers"]
    E110 --> APP2["Comb drives through wafer vias"]
    E111 --> APP3["Micro mirrors smooth membranes"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Functional Role of Orientation in MEMS Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Crystal Plane Selection"] --> P2["Wafer Dicing and Cleaning"]
    P2 --> P3["Photolithography"]
    P3 --> P4["Anisotropic Wet Etching KOH TMAH"]
    P4 --> P5{"Orientation Dependent Geometry"}
    P5 --> R1["100 V groove 54.74 deg"]
    P5 --> R2["110 Vertical 90 deg"]
    P5 --> R3["111 Triangular pyramid"]
    R1 --> OUT1["Cantilever Diaphragm"]
    R2 --> OUT2["Comb Drive Trench"]
    R3 --> OUT3["Pyramid Pit Etch Stop"]
```

### 4.4 Miller Indices Construction Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1 Find intercepts on x y z in units of a"]
    S2["Step 2 Take reciprocals of intercepts"]
    S3["Step 3 Multiply by LCM to clear fractions"]
    S4["Step 4 Enclose in parentheses hkl"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S4 --> EX["Example Plane cuts x at 2a y at infinity z at a"]
    EX --> EX2["Reciprocals 1 by 2 0 1"]
    EX2 --> EX3["Multiply by 2 gives 102"]
```

### 4.5 Schematic: Anisotropic Etch Behavior on (100) Wafer

> *(Mermaid Limitation Note: For precise crystallographic 3D drawings of the silicon lattice, the following block-level architecture illustrates the etch-rate hierarchy instead of attempting geometric crystal renderings.)*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W["100 Silicon Wafer in KOH Bath"] --> E1["111 Plane Vertical Sidewalls Etch rate 0.01 micron per min"]
    W --> E2["100 Plane Horizontal Surface Etch rate 1.0 micron per min"]
    W --> E3["110 Plane Edge Surfaces Etch rate 1.5 micron per min"]
    E1 --> G1["Forms 54.74 deg angle with surface"]
    E2 --> G2["Recedes uniformly downward"]
    E3 --> G3["Fastest lateral etching"]
    G1 --> F["V Groove or Pyramid forms"]
    G2 --> F2["Flat membrane forms"]
    G3 --> F3["Undercut release occurs"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define the diamond cubic structure of silicon. Why is it the preferred crystal structure for MEMS applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The diamond cubic structure consists of **two interpenetrating FCC sublattices displaced by (a/4, a/4, a/4)** along the body diagonal. Each unit cell contains **8 atoms**, with each atom covalently bonded to **4 nearest neighbors** in a tetrahedral geometry (lattice constant **a = 5.431 Å**).

It is preferred for MEMS because:
1. **High mechanical stiffness** with low hysteresis and no plastic deformation at room temperature.
2. **Predictable anisotropic etching** — etch rates vary by up to 400:1 between planes, enabling precise 3D microstructures.
3. **Mature fabrication infrastructure** inherited from the semiconductor industry.
4. **Excellent fatigue resistance** for high-cycle MEMS devices like oscillators and gyroscopes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three low-index crystal planes in silicon and state one MEMS-relevant property of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **(100) Plane** — Most commonly used wafer orientation; offers moderate atomic density and yields well-defined 54.74° etch sidewalls in KOH.
2. **(110) Plane** — Highest etch rate in KOH; used for vertical sidewalls in comb-drive actuators.
3. **(111) Plane** — Highest atomic packing density; serves as a natural etch-stop in anisotropic wet etching due to its very low etch rate.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the diamond cubic crystal structure of silicon. List its key crystallographic and mechanical parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the interplanar spacing formula for a cubic crystal and compute the spacing for (100), (110), and (111) planes of silicon. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diamond Cubic Structure Description (7 marks):**

The silicon crystal adopts the **diamond cubic** structure, which can be visualized as two interpenetrating face-centered cubic (FCC) lattices. The first FCC sublattice occupies the conventional cube corners and face centers. The second FCC sublattice is displaced along the body diagonal by the vector $(a/4,\, a/4,\, a/4)$.

Key parameters to list:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Lattice constant $a$ | 5.431 Å |
| Atoms per unit cell | 8 |
| Coordination number | 4 |
| Nearest neighbor distance | $\sqrt{3}\,a/4$ = 2.35 Å |
| Atomic packing factor | 0.34 |
| Density | 2.33 g/cm³ |
| Young's modulus (⟨100⟩) | 130 GPa |
| Poisson's ratio | 0.28 |

Each silicon atom forms 4 covalent $sp^3$ hybridized bonds directed toward the corners of a regular tetrahedron.

**(b) Interplanar Spacing Derivation (7 marks):**

As derived in Section 3.1, for a cubic system:

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

Substituting $a = 5.431 \text{ Å}$:

$$d_{100} = \frac{5.431}{\sqrt{1}} = 5.431 \text{ Å}$$

$$d_{110} = \frac{5.431}{\sqrt{2}} = 3.840 \text{ Å}$$

$$d_{111} = \frac{5.431}{\sqrt{3}} = 3.136 \text{ Å}$$

**Conclusion:** $d_{111} < d_{110} < d_{100}$. The (111) plane has the smallest spacing among low-index planes, justifying its high atomic density and chemical inertness.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Miller indices. For a plane that intercepts the x, y, z axes at $2a, 3a, 4a$, determine the Miller indices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the (100), (110), and (111) silicon planes in terms of surface atomic density and anisotropic etch behavior in KOH. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Miller Indices (7 marks):**

**Miller indices** are a notation system to uniquely identify crystallographic planes using a triplet of integers (hkl). Procedure:

1. Find intercepts on x, y, z in units of $a$.
2. Take reciprocals.
3. Reduce to smallest integers.

For intercepts $2a, 3a, 4a$:

Reciprocals: $\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{4}$

LCM of denominators = 12. Multiply each by 12:

$$\left(\frac{12}{2}, \frac{12}{3}, \frac{12}{4}\right) = (6, 4, 3)$$

**Miller indices: (643)**

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Property | (100) | (110) | (111) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface atom density | $2/a^2$ | $4/a^2$ (highest in-plane) | $4/(a^2\sqrt{3})$ (densest) |
| Surface bonds per atom | 2 dangling | 1 dangling | 1 dangling |
| KOH etch rate | Moderate (~1 µm/min) | Highest (~1.5 µm/min) | Very slow (~0.01 µm/min) |
| Typical angle w.r.t. surface | 54.74° sidewall | 90° vertical | Etch stop |
| MEMS application | Diaphragms, V-grooves | Comb drives, deep trenches | Pyramidal pits, etch stops |

**Conclusion:** The (111) plane is the slowest etching due to its high atomic density and stable surface bonding. This anisotropic selectivity is the foundation of silicon bulk micromachining.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner's Eye-View):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "basis atoms":** Many students describe silicon as "FCC with 4 atoms." This is WRONG. The correct description is "**two interpenetrating FCC lattices** with 8 atoms per unit cell." (-1 mark)
> 2. **Miller indices procedure skipped:** Examiners expect the *reciprocal* step explicitly. Writing only the final (hkl) without showing reciprocals loses method marks. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 3. **Units missing in numerical answers:** Always write **Å** or **nm** with interplanar spacings. Bare numbers = -0.5 mark.
> 4. **Confusing planes and directions:** (100) is a *plane*; [100] is a *direction*; ⟨100⟩ is a *family*. Students often interchange these. (-0.5 mark)
> 5. **No mention of lattice constant** $a = 5.431$ Å in derivations: lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the "why":** Examiners reward the explanation *behind* the etch selectivity. Just stating "(111) is slow" without linking to atomic density loses marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Silicon = diamond cubic = 8 atoms/unit cell = $a = 5.431$ Å = tetrahedral sp³ bonding.**
- ✅ **APF = 0.34** (silicon is *not* close-packed).
- ✅ **Miller indices procedure:** intercepts → reciprocals → smallest integers → (hkl).
- ✅ **Interplanar spacing:** $d_{hkl} = a / \sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$.
- ✅ **Atomic density order:** $\sigma_{111} > \sigma_{100}$ (when computed correctly for the surface plane area).
- ✅ **KOH etch rate order:** (110) > (100) ≫ (111) → enables anisotropic 3D micromachining.
- ✅ **Young's Modulus:** 130 GPa ⟨100⟩, 169 GPa ⟨110⟩, 188 GPa ⟨111⟩.
- ✅ **Sidewall angle on (100) wafer in KOH = 54.74°** (the angle between (100) and (111) planes).
- ✅ **Wafer flat/notch conventions:** Primary flat on (100) wafer is along ⟨110⟩; on (111) wafer along ⟨110⟩ as well.
- ✅ **For KTU exams:** Always show the reciprocals step in Miller index problems and *never* skip the physical explanation of etch anisotropy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties. Other Materials: Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties. Other Materials: Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)

A **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** is a class of smart metallic materials that possess the unique ability to "remember" and recover a pre-defined geometric shape after being subjected to significant mechanical deformation, upon the application of an external stimulus — typically **heat** (thermal activation) or **stress** (mechanical loading).

In the context of **Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, SMAs are employed primarily as active microactuation materials, where the solid-state phase transformation between two crystallographic phases — **Martensite** (low-temperature, low-symmetry monoclinic phase) and **Austenite** (high-temperature, high-symmetry cubic phase) — drives the micro-motion of the device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two governing phenomena for SMA in MEMS are:
> 1. **Shape Memory Effect (SME)** — thermally induced shape recovery.
> 2. **Pseudo-Elasticity / Super-Elasticity (PE/SE)** — mechanically induced shape recovery at constant temperature above $A_f$.

> [!NOTE]
> The most commercially dominant and MEMS-relevant SMA is **Nitinol** (Nickel-Titanium alloy, ~50:50 at%), owing to its high recoverable strain (up to **8%**), excellent biocompatibility, and high power density (~10 J/cm³).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **crumpled paper origami crane** that magically unfolds back into a perfect crane when you blow warm air on it. The crane (Austenite phase) was "programmed" into the paper's fibre memory. When crushed, it deforms (becomes Martensite), but the original creases are still encoded in the material. Heat provides the energy to "snap back" to the original shape.

For MEMS engineers, this is like having a **micro-muscle** — apply a small voltage (which heats the SMA via Joule heating) and it contracts by up to 8% of its length, lifting, gripping, or pumping at the microscale.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants for SMAs

| Metric | Typical Value (Nitinol) |
|---|---|
| Recoverable Strain (ε_T) | **6% – 8%** |
| Phase Transformation Latent Heat (ΔH) | **~ 25 kJ/kg** |
| Density (ρ) | **6450 kg/m³** |
| Young's Modulus (Austenite) | **~ 75 GPa** |
| Young's Modulus (Martensite) | **~ 30 GPa** |
| Electrical Resistivity | **~ 80 – 100 μΩ·cm** |
| Power Density | **~ 10⁷ J/m³** |

> [!NOTE]
> The transformation temperatures **M_s** (Martensite start), **M_f** (Martensite finish), **A_s** (Austenite start), and **A_f** (Austenite finish) are the **four critical temperatures** that fully characterise the thermo-mechanical response of any SMA. KTU examiners frequently test the ordering of these temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Phase Transformation Modes

### Mode 1 — Shape Memory Effect (One-Way Memory)
The material is deformed in the Martensitic phase (at low temperature, $T < M_f$). Upon heating above $A_f$, the lattice reverts to Austenite and the macroscopic shape is recovered. Cooling alone will not return it to the deformed state — it must be re-stressed.

### Mode 2 — Pseudo-Elasticity (Super-Elasticity)
When stressed at a temperature $T > A_f$ (the Austenite is stable), the material undergoes a stress-induced Martensitic transformation. Upon release of the load, it reverts to Austenite and recovers its shape. **No thermal input is required.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked to *compare* SME and Pseudo-Elasticity, always state the **transformation pathway**, the **temperature of operation**, and the **reversibility** explicitly.

---

## 2.2 The Clausius–Clapeyron Relationship (The Core SMA Equation)

The phase transformation in SMAs is a **first-order solid-state phase transition**. The applied stress shifts the transformation temperature according to the Clausius–Clapeyron equation:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta S}{\varepsilon_T} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \varepsilon_T}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Applied uniaxial stress [Pa]
- $T$ = Absolute temperature [K]
- $\Delta H$ = Latent heat of transformation [J/kg]
- $\Delta S$ = Entropy change of transformation [J/(kg·K)]
- $\varepsilon_T$ = Transformation strain (austenite → oriented martensite)

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** in the Clausius–Clapeyron equation indicates that increasing the applied stress **lowers** the transformation temperature for the Austenite → Martensite transformation, while it **raises** the temperature for the reverse (Martensite → Austenite) transformation. This produces the characteristic **phase-transformation hysteresis loop** in SMAs.

---

## 2.3 Constitutive Equations (Tanaka–Liang–Rogers–Brinson Models)

For exam purposes, the **simplest 1D Tanaka form** is sufficient:

$$
\dot{\sigma} = E(\xi) \cdot \dot{\varepsilon} + \Omega(\xi) \cdot \dot{\xi} + \Theta \cdot \dot{T}
$$

Where:
- $E(\xi)$ = Effective Young's Modulus as a function of martensite fraction
- $\xi$ = Martensite volume fraction (0 = full Austenite, 1 = full Martensite)
- $\Omega(\xi)$ = Phase-transformation tensor
- $\Theta$ = Thermoelastic tensor (thermal expansion coefficient)

The martensite fraction evolves with temperature (Brinson sine model):

$$
\xi = \frac{1 - \xi_a}{2} \cos\left[\frac{\pi}{A_f - A_s}\left(T - A_s - \frac{\sigma}{\beta}\right)\right] + \frac{1 + \xi_a}{2}
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T \varepsilon_T}$ | Clausius–Clapeyron phase line slope | Pa/K |
| 2 | $\sigma_{MS} = \sigma_{0} + \beta (T - M_s^{0})$ | Critical stress to induce Martensite | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma = \sigma_{0} + \beta (T - T_{0})$ | Linear phase-line equation (T-σ plane) | Pa |
| 4 | $\xi(T,\sigma)$ | Martensite volume fraction | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $W_{mech} = \int \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Mechanical work per cycle | J/m³ |
| 6 | $\rho_{e} = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon_T$ | Energy density (output) | J/m³ |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ | Joule heating power in SMA wire | W |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{\rho c V}{h A}$ | Thermal time constant | s |
| 9 | $E(\xi) = E_A + \xi(E_M - E_A)$ | Effective Young's modulus (rule of mixtures) | Pa |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{hys} = A_f - M_s$ | Transformation hysteresis width | K or °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, $\beta$ is the **stress-influence coefficient** ($\beta = d\sigma/dT$), with a typical Nitinol value of **6–8 MPa/°C**.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in MEMS Fields

1. **Microgrippers** for surgical robotics — SMAs provide smooth, vibration-free motion.
2. **Micropumps and microvalves** for drug-delivery systems.
3. **Micro-positioning stages** for scanning probe microscopy.
4. **Active morphing skins** for micro air vehicles.
5. **Self-healing structures** in harsh-environment MEMS.
6. **SMA thin-film actuators** deposited by sputtering for on-chip integration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Clausius–Clapeyron Phase Line

### Statement
Derive the slope of the stress-induced Martensitic transformation line in the $\sigma$–$T$ phase diagram.

### Step 1: Define the Gibbs Free Energies
At phase equilibrium, the Gibbs free energy of the Austenite (G_A) and Martensite (G_M) phases must be equal:

$$
G_A(T, \sigma) = G_M(T, \sigma)
$$

### Step 2: Take the Total Differential
Differentiating both sides with respect to $T$ at constant $\sigma$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial G_A}{\partial T}\right)_\sigma = \left(\frac{\partial G_M}{\partial T}\right)_\sigma
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Thermodynamic Identity
From fundamental thermodynamics: $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_\sigma = -S$, where S is the entropy. Substituting:

$$
-S_A = -S_M \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_M - S_A = -\Delta S_{A \to M}
$$

### Step 4: Now Differentiate the Equilibrium Condition with respect to σ
$$
\left(\frac{\partial G_A}{\partial \sigma}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial G_M}{\partial \sigma}\right)_T
$$

### Step 5: Use Strain Identity
$\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial \sigma}\right)_T = \varepsilon$ (mechanical strain). Hence:

$$
\varepsilon_A = \varepsilon_M \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_T = \varepsilon_M - \varepsilon_A
$$

### Step 6: Apply Cauchy Reciprocity (Mixed Partial Equality)
Since $G$ is a state function, $\dfrac{\partial^2 G}{\partial \sigma \, \partial T} = \dfrac{\partial^2 G}{\partial T \, \partial \sigma}$, which gives:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial T}\right)_\sigma = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial \sigma}\right)_T
$$

### Step 7: Substitute into the Phase Equilibrium Expression
The condition for the phase boundary $\sigma(T)$ requires:

$$
dG_A - dG_M = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -S_A \, dT + \varepsilon_A \, d\sigma = -S_M \, dT + \varepsilon_M \, d\sigma
$$

### Step 8: Rearrange to Isolate the Slope
$$
(S_M - S_A) \, dT = (\varepsilon_M - \varepsilon_A) \, d\sigma
$$

### Step 9: Identify Transformation Quantities
Define:
- $\Delta S = S_M - S_A$ (entropy change of Austenite → Martensite transformation)
- $\varepsilon_T = \varepsilon_M - \varepsilon_A$ (transformation strain)

### Step 10: Final Form
$$
\boxed{\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\varepsilon_T}}
$$

For the **reverse** transformation (Martensite → Austenite), $\Delta S$ becomes negative, hence the sign convention used in the KTU formula sheet (the negative sign indicates Austenite stabilisation under stress).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Joule Heating Time Constant

### Statement
Find the thermal time constant $\tau$ of an SMA microactuator wire of length L, cross-section A, resistivity $\rho_e$, density $\rho$, and specific heat c.

### Step 1: Energy Balance
The electrical power dissipated as Joule heat must raise the wire's temperature:

$$
P \cdot dt = m \cdot c \cdot dT + h \cdot A_s \cdot (T - T_\infty) \cdot dt
$$

Where $h$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient and $A_s$ is the lateral surface area of the wire.

### Step 2: Steady-State Temperature Rise
At equilibrium ($dT/dt = 0$):

$$
P_{ss} = h A_s (T_{ss} - T_\infty)
$$

### Step 3: Mass of the Wire
$$
m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot L
$$

### Step 4: Substituting the Power $P = V^2/R_e$ and Resistance $R_e = \rho_e L / A$:

$$
P = \frac{V^2 A}{\rho_e L}
$$

### Step 5: Combine into a First-Order ODE
$$
\rho A L c \frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{V^2 A}{\rho_e L} - h A_s (T - T_\infty)
$$

### Step 6: Define the Time Constant
$$
\tau = \frac{\rho c V}{h A_s} = \frac{\rho c A L}{h (\pi d L)} = \frac{\rho c d}{4h}
$$

### Step 7: Final Form
$$
\boxed{\tau = \frac{\rho c d}{4h}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The thermal time constant scales **linearly with wire diameter d**. This is a critical design parameter for **MEMS-scale SMA actuators**, where the diameter is in the micrometre range, giving $\tau$ in **milliseconds** — enabling kHz-frequency actuation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SMA vs. Other MEMS Actuation Materials

| Property | SMA (Nitinol) | Piezoelectric (PZT) | Electrostatic | Magnetic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Strain | **6 – 8%** | 0.1 – 0.2% | < 1% | < 0.5% |
| Stress Output | **200 – 500 MPa** | 30 – 80 MPa | < 0.1 MPa | 50 – 100 MPa |
| Energy Density | **~ 10⁷ J/m³** | ~ 10⁵ J/m³ | ~ 10⁴ J/m³ | ~ 10⁵ J/m³ |
| Bandwidth | **< 10 Hz** | > 100 kHz | kHz range | Hz–kHz |
| Drive Signal | **Current (Heat)** | Voltage | Voltage | Current |
| Hysteresis | **Large (15–40 °C)** | Moderate | Negligible | Small |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board loves asking: *"Why is SMA preferred over PZT for biomedical microgrippers?"* — Answer: **biocompatibility + large strain + low driving voltage**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SMA Microactuator Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Electrical Power Supply V volts] --> nodeB[Driver Circuit PWM Control]
    nodeB --> nodeC[SMA Thin Film Element NiTi]
    nodeC -- Joule Heating --> nodeD[Temperature Rise above As]
    nodeD -- Austenite Formation --> nodeE[Shape Recovery Contraction]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Mechanical Work Output]
    nodeF --> nodeG[MEMS Load Valve Gripper Cantilever]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Displacement / Force Sensor]
    nodeH -- Feedback --> nodeB
    nodeC -- Heat Loss Convection --> nodeI[Environment T infinity]
    nodeC -- Stress --> nodeJ[Phase Transformation Clausius Clapeyron]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Phase Diagram — Stress vs. Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Low T High sigma Martensite] -->|Heating| nodeP2[High T Low sigma Austenite]
    nodeP2 -->|Cooling| nodeP3[Low T Low sigma Twinned Martensite]
    nodeP3 -->|Loading| nodeP4[High T High sigma Detwinned Martensite]
    nodeP4 -->|Unloading| nodeP1
```

## 4.3 Schematic Process Flow — SMA Thin Film Deposition for MEMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Si Wafer Substrate] --> nodeS2[Thermal Oxidation SiO2 Layer]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Photolithography Patterning]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Sputter Deposition of NiTi Target]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Annealing at 500 to 700 C Crystallisation]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Etch and Release MEMS Structure]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[SMA MEMS Microactuator Device]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Advisory for Mermaid Compilation:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeS7`) and labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering on Mermaid v10+ engines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**
**Q: Differentiate between the Shape Memory Effect (SME) and Pseudo-Elasticity in Shape Memory Alloys. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **Shape Memory Effect (SME)** is a thermally activated phenomenon in which an SMA, after being deformed in its Martensitic phase ($T < M_f$), recovers its original (Austenitic) shape upon heating above the Austenite finish temperature $A_f$. The recovery is permanent and is achieved through the **Martensite → Austenite** phase transformation.

**Pseudo-Elasticity (PE)**, also called **super-elasticity**, is a mechanically activated phenomenon occurring at a constant temperature $T > A_f$. Here, applied stress induces a stress-induced Martensitic transformation, and upon unloading, the material spontaneously reverts to Austenite, recovering its shape with **no thermal input**.

| Aspect | SME | Pseudo-Elasticity |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Heat | Mechanical stress |
| Operating T | $T < M_f$ initially | $T > A_f$ |
| Recovery | One-way (needs re-deformation) | Spontaneous on unloading |

**(3 marks)** — Awarded for: correct definition of SME (1 mark), correct definition of PE (1 mark), comparison table or key distinction (1 mark).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
**Q: List any three important properties of Shape Memory Alloys that make them suitable for MEMS applications. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Large recoverable strain (6–8%)** — an order of magnitude higher than piezoelectrics, enabling macroscopic motion from small structures.
2. **High power/energy density (~ 10⁷ J/m³)** — producing large forces from micro-scale devices, ideal for microgrippers and microvalves.
3. **Biocompatibility and corrosion resistance** — particularly for Nitinol, making it suitable for biomedical MEMS such as stents, drug-delivery pumps, and surgical microtools.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Pattern 2024]**
**Q: (a)** Define the Shape Memory Effect and explain the Martensite-to-Austenite phase transformation in Nitinol with a suitable phase diagram. Discuss the four characteristic transformation temperatures $M_s, M_f, A_s, A_f$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Nitinol SMA wire of diameter $d = 200\,\mu m$ and length $L = 50\,mm$ is used as a microactuator. The transformation strain is $\varepsilon_T = 6\%$, the latent heat is $\Delta H = 25\,\text{kJ/kg}$, the density is $\rho = 6450\,\text{kg/m}^3$, the specific heat is $c = 500\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$, and the convective heat-transfer coefficient is $h = 200\,\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$. Calculate the **thermal time constant** and the **transformation temperature shift per MPa** of applied stress using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation. Take the entropy change $|\Delta S| = 0.24\,\text{J/(g·K)}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition (2 marks):** The Shape Memory Effect is the ability of certain alloys to recover a pre-programmed macroscopic shape upon heating, by virtue of a reversible thermo-elastic Martensitic transformation.

2. **Phase Transformation Explanation (3 marks):**
   - Below $M_f$: Austenite has fully transformed to **twinned Martensite** (B19' monoclinic in Nitinol).
   - On loading below $M_f$: detwinning occurs — the variant reorients to a single Martensite variant, producing macroscopic strain.
   - On heating above $A_f$: lattice reverts to the **Austenite (B2 cubic) phase**, recovering the original shape.

3. **Four Transformation Temperatures (2 marks):**
   - $M_s$ = Martensite start (cooling)
   - $M_f$ = Martensite finish (cooling)
   - $A_s$ = Austenite start (heating)
   - $A_f$ = Austenite finish (heating)
   - Typically $A_f > M_s$ giving a **hysteresis width** $\Delta T_{hys}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Thermal Time Constant (4 marks):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:

$$
\tau = \frac{\rho \, c \, d}{4 \, h}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\tau = \frac{6450 \times 500 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 200}
$$

Computing the numerator:

$$
6450 \times 500 = 3{,}225{,}000
$$

$$
3{,}225{,}000 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 645
$$

Computing the denominator:

$$
4 \times 200 = 800
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tau = \frac{645}{800} = 0.80625 \,\text{s} \approx \mathbf{0.81 \, s}
$$

**Step 2 — Clausius–Clapeyron Slope (3 marks):**

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\varepsilon_T}
$$

Convert $\Delta S$ to SI: $\Delta S = 0.24 \,\text{J/(g·K)} = 240 \,\text{J/(kg·K)}$.

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dT} = \frac{240}{0.06} = 4000 \,\text{Pa/K} = 0.004 \,\text{MPa/K}
$$

Inverting to find the **temperature shift per MPa**:

$$
\frac{dT}{d\sigma} = \frac{1}{0.004} = 250 \,\text{K/MPa}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Thermal time constant: **$\tau \approx 0.81$ s**
- Temperature shift: **250 K/MPa** (or equivalently $d\sigma/dT = 0.004$ MPa/K)

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Pattern 2024]**
**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an SMA-based microactuator for MEMS applications. Discuss the role of Joule heating and the Clausius–Clapeyron equation in its operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the actuation characteristics of SMAs with piezoelectric (PZT) and electrostatic actuators. State two MEMS applications where SMAs are preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The system consists of:
   - Power supply (constant voltage/current source) → Driver circuit (PWM control) → SMA element (Nitinol wire/thin film) → Output mechanical displacement → MEMS load (valve/gripper/cantilever) → Feedback sensor.

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):** When current flows through the SMA element, **Joule heating** raises its temperature above $A_f$. The Martensite → Austenite transformation produces a **shape recovery strain** (up to 8%), which performs mechanical work on the MEMS load. When the current is switched off, the actuator cools below $M_f$ and is reset by an external bias force/spring.

3. **Role of Clausius–Clapeyron (2 marks):** The Clausius–Clapeyron equation governs the **stress–temperature coupling** of the transformation. The applied bias stress shifts the effective $A_f$ upward, allowing the designer to tune the actuation temperature. It is also used to size the actuation voltage through the Joule-heating model $P = V^2/R$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Comparison (5 marks):**

| Property | SMA | PZT | Electrostatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Strain | 6–8% | 0.1–0.2% | < 1% |
| Force Output | High (200–500 MPa) | Moderate (30–80 MPa) | Low (< 0.1 MPa) |
| Drive Voltage | Low (1–5 V) | High (50–200 V) | High (50–200 V) |
| Bandwidth | Low (< 10 Hz) | High (> 100 kHz) | High (kHz) |
| Hysteresis | Large | Moderate | Negligible |
| Biocompatibility | Excellent (Nitinol) | Poor | Poor |

2. **Two MEMS Applications where SMAs are preferred (2 marks):**
   - **Biomedical microgrippers** for minimally invasive surgery — large strain + biocompatibility.
   - **Micropumps / microvalves** for drug delivery — high force density, low drive voltage suitable for implantable devices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Clausius–Clapeyron:** Forgetting the negative sign in $d\sigma/dT = -\Delta H/(T\varepsilon_T)$ will cost **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Mixing up $A_s$ and $A_f$:** Students frequently interchange Austenite start and finish. Memorise the order: **A**ustenite **s**tart < **A**ustenite **f**inish.
> 3. **Confusing SME with Super-Elasticity:** KTU questions that ask "compare" require **both** temperature and reversibility distinctions; writing only one loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical problems:** Every numerical answer MUST carry units (Pa, K, s). Marks are explicitly deducted for missing units.
> 5. **Ignoring the bias-spring in SMA actuators:** The reset mechanism in SME actuators is **mechanical**, not thermal — a common 1-mark trap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SMA** = "smart metal" that returns to a pre-set shape upon heating or unloading.
- **Two core phenomena:** **Shape Memory Effect (SME)** — heat-driven; **Pseudo-Elasticity (PE)** — stress-driven, $T > A_f$.
- **Two key phases:** **Austenite** (high-T, cubic, parent phase) ↔ **Martensite** (low-T, monoclinic, product phase).
- **Four transformation temps:** $M_s < M_f$ (on cooling); $A_s < A_f$ (on heating); **hysteresis** $\Delta T = A_f - M_s$.
- **Master equation:** Clausius–Clapeyron: $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T\varepsilon_T}$ (negative because A → M is exothermic in stress; **magnitude is what matters for problem-solving**).
- **Nitinol** is the **workhorse SMA** for MEMS: 6–8% recoverable strain, ~10⁷ J/m³ energy density, biocompatible.
- **Joule heating** (electrical) is the **standard actuation method** in MEMS-scale SMA devices.
- **Time constant** scales linearly with wire diameter: $\tau = \rho c d / 4h$ — small diameter → fast response (ms scale at micrometre dimensions).
- **Constitutive model (Tanaka form):** $\dot{\sigma} = E(\xi)\dot{\varepsilon} + \Omega(\xi)\dot{\xi} + \Theta \dot{T}$ — relates stress, strain, temperature, and Martensite fraction.
- **MEMS applications:** microgrippers, microvalves, micropumps, micro-positioners, micro air-vehicle skins, drug-delivery implants.
- **Hysteresis width** in Nitinol is typically **15–40 °C** — a key limitation for precise open-loop control.
- **SMA's advantage over PZT** in MEMS: **larger strain, lower drive voltage, biocompatibility, higher force density**.
- **SMA's disadvantage vs. PZT:** **slow bandwidth, large thermal hysteresis, requires bias spring for reset**.
- **For numericals:** Always retain **units**; convert $\Delta S$ from J/(g·K) to J/(kg·K) by multiplying by 1000; strain percentages → decimals before substituting.
- **The martensite volume fraction** $\xi$ lies between 0 (full Austenite) and 1 (full Martensite) and follows a **cosine (Brinson) evolution** with temperature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material Properties. Commercial MEMS Structures and Systems: Overview of Commonly Used Mechanical Structures in MEMS - Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Material Properties in the MEMS Context

In Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS), the success of any fabricated device fundamentally depends on the **mechanical, electrical, thermal, and chemical properties** of the structural materials chosen at the microscale. While a design engineer may select a material based on macroscopic familiarity (e.g., Silicon for electronics, Aluminum for interconnects), MEMS demands a deeper, multi-physics-aware characterization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Material Properties for MEMS):**
> Material properties in MEMS refer to the set of mechanical (Young's modulus $E$, Poisson's ratio $\nu$, density $\rho$, residual stress $\sigma_r$, yield strength $\sigma_y$), thermal (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion $\alpha$, thermal conductivity $k$), and electrical (resistivity $\rho_e$, piezoresistive coefficients $\pi_{ij}$) parameters that govern the static, dynamic, and reliability behavior of microfabricated structures at the micron and sub-micron scale.

At the macroscale, a bridge made of steel and a bridge made of silicon might both "look" rigid, but their **mass-to-stiffness ratios**, **thermal expansion behaviors**, and **failure modes** differ by orders of magnitude. MEMS engineers exploit these differences deliberately — for example, the large **piezoresistive coefficient of silicon** is exploited in pressure sensors, while the **low residual stress of polysilicon** is exploited in inertial sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MEMS material properties are unique:**
> 1. **Surface-to-volume ratio is extremely high** → Surface effects (e.g., surface tension, surface stress, friction) dominate over bulk effects.
> 2. **Grain structure matters** → Polycrystalline thin films show different $E$ compared to single-crystal bulk.
> 3. **Fabrication-induced stresses** → Thin-film deposition introduces residual stress that can buckle or curl beams.

---

## 1.2 Commonly Used Materials in MEMS — A Quick Overview

| Material | Primary Use in MEMS | Key Property Exploited |
|----------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Single-Crystal Silicon** | Structural beams, diaphragms | High $E$, low $\rho$, excellent piezoresistivity |
| **Polysilicon** | Surface micromachined beams | Tunable residual stress |
| **Silicon Dioxide ($SiO_2$)** | Sacrificial layer, insulation | Selective etch chemistry (HF) |
| **Silicon Nitride ($Si_3N_4$)** | Membranes, pressure sensors | High tensile strength, low residual stress |
| **SU-8** | Thick structural layers, bio-MEMS | Photopatternability, biocompatibility |
| **PDMS** | Microfluidics, soft MEMS | Elasticity, optical transparency |
| **Gold / Aluminum** | Electrical interconnects, electrodes | High conductivity |
| **PZT / Lead Zirconate Titanate** | Piezoelectric actuators | Piezoelectric coupling |

---

## 1.3 Commercial MEMS Structures and Systems — Overview

Commercial MEMS refers to **mass-produced, market-deployed microsystems** that integrate mechanical and electrical functionalities on a single chip. The mechanical structures inside these devices are the "moving parts" that translate physical signals (force, pressure, acceleration, light) into electrical signals and vice versa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Commercial MEMS Structures and Systems):**
> Commercial MEMS structures are industry-standard, mass-manufactured microsystem building blocks such as pressure sensors, accelerometers, gyroscopes, micromirrors, inkjet printheads, microphones, and RF MEMS switches, all of which rely on a small library of canonical mechanical structures — **beams, plates/diaphragms, comb drives, and tethered proof masses**.

### 1.3.1 Canonical Commercial MEMS Examples

| Commercial Product | Underlying MEMS Structure | Working Principle |
|--------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| **Analog Devices ADXL Accelerometer** | Suspended proof mass + flexure beams | Inertial displacement → capacitive change |
| **Bosch BMP Pressure Sensor** | Thin diaphragm + piezoresistors | Pressure → strain → resistance change |
| **TI DLP Micromirror** | Torsional beam + mirror plate | Electrostatic tilting for light steering |
| **HP Inkjet Printhead** | Cantilever beam + heater | Thermal bubble → droplet ejection |
| **STMicroelectronics Gyroscope** | Driven + sense comb drives | Coriolis force on vibrating mass |
| **Knowles MEMS Microphone** | Diaphragm + backplate | Acoustic pressure → capacitance change |
| **Qualcomm RF MEMS Switch** | Cantilever beam | Electrostatic actuation → ohmic contact |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Almost **every** commercial MEMS device can be decomposed into a small set of mechanical primitives — primarily **beams**, **plates**, **comb drives**, and **proof masses**. Mastering the beam is therefore the most critical first step in MEMS design.

---

## 1.4 Mechanical Structures in MEMS — Why Beams?

Among all mechanical primitives, the **beam** is the most fundamental and ubiquitous. Every diaphragm is built from a network of beams; every comb drive terminates in beams; every proof mass is suspended by beams.

### 1.4.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine a **diving board** at a swimming pool. It is fixed at one end (the concrete base) and free at the other end where the diver stands. When the diver jumps, the board bends downward — this is a **cantilever beam**. Now imagine a **seesaw** held between two concrete supports — both ends are fixed, and the middle can deflect — this is a **fixed-fixed (clamped-clamped) beam**. MEMS uses the exact same geometries, just shrunk by a factor of **10,000×** (a 100 µm long beam replaces the 1 m diving board). The physics — governed by Euler-Bernoulli beam theory — is identical.

### 1.4.2 Formal Definition of a MEMS Beam

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (MEMS Beam):**
> A MEMS beam is a slender, high-aspect-ratio structural element (typical length $L$ = 10–1000 µm, width $w$ = 1–50 µm, thickness $t$ = 0.5–50 µm) that exhibits bending-dominated elastic behavior under transverse, axial, or residual loading, and which serves as the fundamental mechanical primitive for flexures, suspensions, and sensor/actuator elements in microfabricated systems.

### 1.4.3 The Three Canonical Beam Boundary Conditions

The classification of a beam in MEMS is dictated entirely by how its two ends are constrained:

1. **Cantilever Beam** — One end fixed (clamped/anchored to the substrate), other end free.
2. **Fixed-Fixed Beam (Clamped-Clamped)** — Both ends rigidly anchored.
3. **Simply-Supported Beam** — Both ends pinned (allows rotation, prevents translation).

Each boundary condition yields a **different stiffness, different natural frequency, and different deflection profile**, which is why KTU examiners love testing this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Material Properties — The Engineering Triad

For a MEMS beam to perform predictably, three categories of material properties must be characterized:

### 2.1.1 Mechanical (Elastic) Properties

1. **Young's Modulus ($E$)** — Stiffness against axial or bending deformation. Unit: **Pa** or **N/m²**.
2. **Shear Modulus ($G$)** — Stiffness against torsional/shear deformation. Unit: **Pa**.
3. **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)** — Lateral contraction under axial stretch. Dimensionless, typically 0.06–0.5.
4. **Density ($\rho$)** — Mass per unit volume. Unit: **kg/m³**. Critical for dynamic (resonance) design.
5. **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$ or $\sigma_{ult}$)** — Maximum stress before plastic deformation or fracture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Fact for KTU:** Silicon's Young's modulus is anisotropic. For a beam oriented along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction on a (100) wafer, the relevant $E \approx 169$ GPa; along $\langle 100 \rangle$, $E \approx 130$ GPa. For first-order calculations, **$E_{Si} \approx 160$ GPa** is the textbook value used.

### 2.1.2 Residual Stress ($\sigma_r$) and Stress Gradient

Residual stress arises due to mismatch in thermal expansion, lattice mismatch, or deposition conditions.

$$\sigma_r = \sigma_{thermal} + \sigma_{intrinsic}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Residual stress gradient** (variation of $\sigma_r$ through the beam thickness) is the primary cause of **beam curling** — a critical failure mode in surface micromachined cantilever beams.

### 2.1.3 Electrical & Piezoresistive Properties

For piezoresistive sensing:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

where $\pi_l$ and $\pi_t$ are longitudinal and transverse piezoresistive coefficients. Silicon has $\pi_l \approx 50 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ (for p-type, $\langle 111 \rangle$ orientation), which is **orders of magnitude larger** than metallic strain gauges.

---

## 2.2 MEMS Beam Theory — Euler-Bernoulli Foundation

The static and dynamic behavior of MEMS beams is governed by the **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation**, assuming:

- Slender geometry ($L \gg t$, $L \gg w$).
- Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
- Shear deformation and rotary inertia are negligible (valid when $t/L < 0.1$).

For a beam undergoing transverse deflection $w(x)$ under distributed load $q(x)$:

$$EI \frac{d^4 w}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

where $I$ is the **area moment of inertia** of the cross-section. For a rectangular beam:

$$I = \frac{w t^3}{12}$$

### 2.2.1 Bending Stress Distribution

The maximum bending stress occurs at the surface (top and bottom of the beam):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M c}{I} = \frac{6 M}{w t^2}$$

where $M$ is the bending moment and $c = t/2$ is the distance from the neutral axis.

### 2.2.2 Beam Deflection Under Tip Load (Cantilever)

For a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, with a transverse point load $F$ at the free tip:

$$w_{tip} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E w t^3}$$

The equivalent **spring constant** is therefore:

$$k = \frac{F}{w_{tip}} = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

### 2.2.3 Beam Deflection Under Distributed Load (Cantilever)

For a uniformly distributed load $q$ (force/length):

$$w_{tip} = \frac{q L^4}{8 E I} = \frac{3 q L^4}{2 E w t^3}$$

---

## 2.3 Resonant Frequency of MEMS Beams

The **fundamental natural frequency** of a beam determines its dynamic response, sensor bandwidth, and switching speed.

For a cantilever beam:

$$f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^4}} = \frac{0.5615}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A}}$$

For a fixed-fixed beam:

$$f_1 = \frac{4.730^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^4}} = \frac{1.028}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A}}$$

where $A = w t$ is the cross-sectional area and $\rho$ is the material density.

---

## 2.4 Spring Constants for Common Beam Configurations

The following table is the **single most high-yield reference** for KTU problems on beams:

| Beam Type | Tip Deflection $w_{tip}$ under load $F$ at tip | Spring Constant $k = F/w_{tip}$ | Fundamental Frequency $f_1$ |
|-----------|--------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Cantilever** | $\frac{4 F L^3}{E w t^3}$ | $\frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | $\frac{0.5615}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A}}$ |
| **Fixed-Fixed (Center load)** | $\frac{F L^3}{192 E I}$ | $\frac{192 E I}{L^3} = \frac{16 E w t^3}{L^3}$ | $\frac{1.028}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A}}$ |
| **Simply-Supported (Center load)** | $\frac{F L^3}{48 E I}$ | $\frac{48 E I}{L^3} = \frac{4 E w t^3}{L^3}$ | (lower than fixed-fixed) |
| **Cantilever under UDL $q$** | $\frac{q L^4}{8 E I}$ | $k_{eq} = \frac{8 E I}{L^3}$ for gravity | Same as above |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Stiffness Ratio:** A fixed-fixed beam is **64× stiffer** than a cantilever of identical dimensions, because $\frac{192}{4} \times \frac{1}{1} \cdot ... \to$ actually, comparing tip-load cases: $\frac{k_{ff}}{k_{cant}} = \frac{16 E w t^3 / L^3}{E w t^3 / 4 L^3} = 64$. This **factor of 64** is a favorite KTU numerical question.

---

## 2.5 Step-by-Step Logic: Why the Fixed-Fixed Beam is Stiffer

1. **Both ends anchored** → No free rotation, no free translation.
2. **Deflection shape** → The beam adopts a sinusoidal bending mode with zero slope at both ends.
3. **Effective "lever arm"** → Shorter than the cantilever's $L$ because both halves of the beam resist the load.
4. **Energy stored** → Distributed along the entire length, not just near the anchor.
5. **Result** → Deflection is 1/48 of cantilever's, and stiffness is 48× higher (for a center load). For a tip load on the cantilever vs. a center load on a fixed-fixed, the ratio is **64×**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Beam Type Used | Reason |
|-------------|---------------|--------|
| **AFM Cantilever (Bruker, Veeco)** | Cantilever with tip | Low $k$ for soft sample imaging; high $f_1$ for fast scanning |
| **Comb-drive Accelerometer** | Fixed-fixed flexure beams | High stiffness in sense direction, low out-of-plane stiffness |
| **RF MEMS Switch (Radant, Omron)** | Cantilever or fixed-fixed | Tunable spring constant sets pull-in voltage |
| **Inkjet Printhead (HP, Canon)** | Cantilever with heater | Thermal expansion → deflection → droplet ejection |
| **Pressure Sensor Diaphragm** | Plate supported by 4 fixed-fixed beams | Square diaphragm behaves like an edge-clamped plate |
| **Gyroscope Drive Mode** | Fixed-fixed folded beam | Precise lateral stiffness for comb-drive resonance |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spring Constant of a Cantilever Beam Under Tip Load

**Problem Setup:**
A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, made of a material with Young's modulus $E$, is subjected to a transverse point load $F$ at its free end (tip). Derive the tip deflection and equivalent spring constant.

### Step 1: Write the Governing Equation

For a slender beam under transverse load, the Euler-Bernoulli equation in the absence of a distributed load is:

$$EI \frac{d^4 w(x)}{dx^4} = 0$$

### Step 2: Integrate Four Times

First integration — slope:

$$EI \frac{d^3 w}{dx^3} = V(x) = C_1$$

Second integration — bending moment:

$$EI \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = M(x) = C_1 x + C_2$$

Third integration — slope:

$$EI \frac{dw}{dx} = \frac{C_1 x^2}{2} + C_2 x + C_3$$

Fourth integration — deflection:

$$EI w(x) = \frac{C_1 x^3}{6} + \frac{C_2 x^2}{2} + C_3 x + C_4$$

### Step 3: Apply Boundary Conditions for Cantilever with Tip Load

The cantilever has its fixed end at $x = 0$ and free end at $x = L$:

| BC # | Location | Condition | Reason |
|------|----------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | $x = 0$ | $w(0) = 0$ | No deflection at anchor |
| 2 | $x = 0$ | $\frac{dw}{dx}\big\vert_{x=0} = 0$ | No slope at anchor |
| 3 | $x = L$ | $M(L) = EI \frac{d^2 w}{dx^2}\big\vert_{x=L} = 0$ | No moment at free end |
| 4 | $x = L$ | $V(L) = EI \frac{d^3 w}{dx^3}\big\vert_{x=L} = F$ | Shear equals applied tip load |

### Step 4: Solve for Constants

From BC 1: $C_4 = 0$
From BC 2: $C_3 = 0$
From BC 3: $C_1 L + C_2 = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = -C_1 L$
From BC 4: $C_1 = F$

Therefore:

$$C_2 = -FL, \quad C_3 = C_4 = 0$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back to Get Deflection Field

$$w(x) = \frac{F}{6 EI} \left( x^3 - 3 L x^2 \right) = \frac{F x^2}{6 EI} (x - 3L)$$

### Step 6: Evaluate Tip Deflection at $x = L$

$$w_{tip} = w(L) = \frac{F}{6 EI} \left( L^3 - 3L^3 \right) = \frac{F}{6 EI} \left( -2 L^3 \right)$$

Taking the magnitude (downward deflection):

$$w_{tip} = \frac{F L^3}{3 EI}$$

### Step 7: Substitute $I = w t^3 / 12$

$$w_{tip} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E \left( \frac{w t^3}{12} \right)} = \frac{12 F L^3}{3 E w t^3} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E w t^3}$$

### Step 8: Spring Constant

$$k = \frac{F}{w_{tip}} = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the single most common KTU problem. Students must memorize the result **AND** be able to reproduce the integration steps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spring Constant of a Fixed-Fixed Beam Under Center Load

**Problem Setup:**
A beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, with **both ends clamped**, is loaded by a transverse force $F$ at its midpoint. Find the center deflection and spring constant.

### Step 1: Use Symmetry to Reduce to Half-Beam

By symmetry, the center of the beam has zero slope. We analyze the left half (length $L/2$) with effective BCs:
- At $x = 0$ (center): slope = 0.
- At $x = L/2$ (clamped end): deflection = 0 and slope = 0.
- Effective tip load on the half-beam is $F/2$.

### Step 2: Treat as Cantilever with Tip Load $F/2$ and Effective Length $L/2$

Using the cantilever formula derived above:

$$w_{center} = \frac{4 (F/2) (L/2)^3}{E w t^3} = \frac{4 F L^3}{16 E w t^3} = \frac{F L^3}{4 E w t^3}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $I = w t^3/12$ to Get the Standard Form

$$w_{center} = \frac{F L^3}{4 E \cdot \frac{w t^3}{12}} = \frac{12 F L^3}{4 E w t^3} = \frac{3 F L^3}{E w t^3}$$

Wait — this is incorrect. Let us redo it via the standard full-beam formula. The correct approach:

The fixed-fixed beam with center load has the well-known result from strength-of-materials:

$$w_{center} = \frac{F L^3}{192 E I} = \frac{F L^3}{192 \cdot \frac{w t^3}{12}} = \frac{12 F L^3}{192 E w t^3} = \frac{F L^3}{16 E w t^3}$$

### Step 4: Spring Constant

$$k = \frac{F}{w_{center}} = \frac{16 E w t^3}{L^3}$$

### Step 5: Stiffness Ratio (Cantilever Tip-Load vs Fixed-Fixed Center-Load)

$$\frac{k_{ff}}{k_{cant}} = \frac{16 E w t^3 / L^3}{E w t^3 / 4 L^3} = \frac{16 \times 4}{1} = 64$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Factor of 64 Rule:** A fixed-fixed beam is exactly **64× stiffer** than a cantilever beam of identical dimensions when both are loaded in their respective most-compliant direction. This is a favorite KTU conceptual question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fundamental Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever Beam

**Problem Setup:**
Derive the fundamental natural frequency $f_1$ of a uniform cantilever beam.

### Step 1: Equation of Motion (Euler-Bernoulli)

$$EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

### Step 2: Assume Harmonic Solution

Let $w(x,t) = W(x) e^{j \omega t}$. Substituting:

$$EI \frac{d^4 W}{dx^4} - \rho A \omega^2 W = 0$$

### Step 3: Define the Wave Number

Let $\beta^4 = \frac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$. Then:

$$\frac{d^4 W}{dx^4} - \beta^4 W = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$W(x) = A \cos(\beta x) + B \sin(\beta x) + C \cosh(\beta x) + D \sinh(\beta x)$$

### Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions for Cantilever

At $x = 0$ (clamped end): $W(0) = 0$ and $W'(0) = 0$
At $x = L$ (free end): $W''(L) = 0$ and $W'''(L) = 0$

### Step 5: Solve the Transcendental Equation

The non-trivial solution requires:

$$\cos(\beta L) \cosh(\beta L) = -1$$

The smallest positive root is:

$$\beta_1 L \approx 1.875$$

### Step 6: Extract the Frequency

$$\omega_1 = \beta_1^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} = \frac{(1.875)^2}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

$$\omega_1 \approx \frac{3.516}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{\omega_1}{2\pi} \approx \frac{0.5615}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** A silicon cantilever beam is $L = 200$ µm long, $w = 20$ µm wide, $t = 2$ µm thick. Given $E_{Si} = 160$ GPa, $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³. Calculate:
> (a) Spring constant $k$
> (b) Fundamental resonance frequency $f_1$

**Solution:**

(a) Spring constant:

$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3} = \frac{(160 \times 10^9)(20 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4 (200 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

Numerator: $160 \times 10^9 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 160 \times 20 \times 8 \times 10^{9-6-18} = 25600 \times 10^{-15} = 2.56 \times 10^{-11}$

Denominator: $4 \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 3.2 \times 10^{-14}$

$$k = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{-11}}{3.2 \times 10^{-14}} = 800 \text{ N/m}$$

(b) Fundamental frequency:

$$I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(20 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 8 \times 10^{-24}}{12} = 1.333 \times 10^{-23} \text{ m}^4$$

$$A = w t = 4 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\frac{EI}{\rho A} = \frac{(160 \times 10^9)(1.333 \times 10^{-23})}{(2330)(4 \times 10^{-11})} = \frac{2.133 \times 10^{-12}}{9.32 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.289 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} = 4.785 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{0.5615}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^2} \times 4.785 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{0.5615}{4 \times 10^{-8}} \times 4.785 \times 10^{-3} = 1.404 \times 10^{7} \times 4.785 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$f_1 \approx 67{,}180 \text{ Hz} \approx 67.2 \text{ kHz}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Beam Selection Decision Table

| Design Goal | Recommended Beam | Reason |
|-------------|------------------|--------|
| Maximum deflection sensitivity | Cantilever | Lowest stiffness |
| Maximum resonant frequency | Fixed-fixed | Stiffest, highest $f_1$ |
| In-plane actuation (comb drive) | Folded fixed-fixed | Symmetric stiffness |
| Out-of-plane sensing (AFM) | Cantilever with tip | Out-of-plane compliance |
| Pressure sensing (diaphragm) | Edge-clamped plate (4 fixed-fixed edges) | Pressure → strain |
| Thermal isolation | Long, narrow cantilever | Low thermal conduction |
| Low pull-in voltage | Soft cantilever | Low $k$ → low actuation force |
| High shock survival | Short, thick fixed-fixed | Highest yield margin |
