topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process of Micromachining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — PROCESS OF MICROMACHINING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Micromachining** is defined as a set of advanced manufacturing techniques used to fabricate micro-scale mechanical, electromechanical, and electronic structures (typically in the range of **1 µm to 1000 µm**) by selectively removing, adding, or transforming material on a substrate (most commonly silicon, glass, polymers, or metals) with **sub-micron precision**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, micromachining processes are broadly classified into three major families:

1. **Bulk Micromachining** — material is removed from the bulk of the substrate.
2. **Surface Micromachining** — structural features are built layer-by-layer on the surface using thin-film deposition and selective etching.
3. **LIGA Process** — a high-aspect-ratio micromachining technology combining X-ray lithography, electroplating, and molding.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Sand-Castle Builder vs. The Ice-Cream Scoop:**
> - **Bulk Micromachining** is like a sculptor who **carves a statue out of a block of marble** — material is removed from inside the body.
> - **Surface Micromachining** is like a baker who builds a **multi-tiered cake**, icing one layer on top of another and then dissolving a hidden sugar layer to leave air gaps — this is the *sacrificial layer* idea.
> - **LIGA** is like pressing a tiny **chocolate mold** with extremely fine details, except the mold is made using X-rays and the chocolate is replaced by electroplated metal.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Etch Rate (R):** measured in **µm/min** or **nm/min**.
- **Selectivity (S):** ratio of etch rates of two materials.
- **Anisotropy (A):** direction-dependence of etching.
- **Aspect Ratio (AR):** depth-to-width ratio of etched features.
- **Undercut (u):** lateral etching beneath a mask.
- **Etch Stop:** the controlled termination of the etching process.

### 1.4 Why Micromachining Matters in MEMS

Micromachining enables the creation of the *mechanical* part of MEMS (gears, beams, membranes, channels, cavities) using the same batch-fabrication philosophy that made the integrated circuit industry a success — yielding **hundreds of devices per wafer** at low cost.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bulk Micromachining

Bulk micromachining selectively removes significant amounts of substrate material (typically **silicon**) to form three-dimensional structures such as membranes, cantilevers, beams, channels, and cavities.

### 2.1.1 Wet Chemical Bulk Micromachining

**Principle:** A liquid chemical etchant dissolves the silicon substrate in regions not protected by a mask (usually SiO₂ or Si₃N₄).

| Etchant | Composition | Type | Etch Rate (Si) | Stops on |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **KOH** | Potassium Hydroxide (30 %, 80 °C) | Anisotropic | ~1.4 µm/min | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ |
| **TMAH** | Tetramethyl Ammonium Hydroxide | Anisotropic | ~0.6 µm/min | SiO₂ |
| **EDP** | Ethylene-Diamine Pyrocatechol | Anisotropic | ~0.7 µm/min | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ |
| **HNA** | HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH | Isotropic | ~1–3 µm/min | — |
| **BHF** | Buffered Hydrofluoric Acid | Dielectric etch | — | Etches SiO₂, not Si |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Anisotropic Etching of (100) Silicon:**
> The etch rate ratio for the principal crystallographic planes is:
> - **(100) : (110) : (111) ≈ 100 : 60 : 1**
>
> This means KOH etches (100) planes ~100× faster than (111) planes, producing well-defined **54.74° sidewalls** (pyramidal cavities) bounded by four (111) planes when etching a (100) wafer through a square mask opening.

### 2.1.2 Dry Bulk Micromachining

**Principle:** Etching is performed in a gas/plasma phase, eliminating liquid handling and capillary stiction.

- **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE):** Combines chemical reaction with physical ion bombardment. Pressure: 10–100 mTorr.
- **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE):** Achieves aspect ratios > 50:1.
- **Ion Beam Etching (IBE):** Purely physical sputtering with Ar⁺ ions.
- **Plasma Etching:** Mostly chemical, low ion energy, high selectivity.

> [!NOTE] > **The Bosch (DRIE) Process** alternates two steps in a cyclic manner:
> 1. **Passivation step:** Deposit a fluoropolymer (C₄F₈) on sidewalls.
> 2. **Etch step:** SF₆ plasma etches the bottom polymer and silicon.
>
> This produces near-vertical sidewalls with characteristic **sidewall scallops** of tens of nanometers per cycle.

### 2.1.3 Etch Stop Techniques in Bulk Micromachining

| Technique | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Boron Etch Stop** | Heavy boron doping (>5×10¹⁹ cm⁻³) reduces etch rate ~50× | Buried layer |
| **Electrochemical Etch Stop** | Reverse-biased p-n junction depletes holes → etchant stops at junction | p-n epitaxial wafers |
| **Dielectric Etch Stop** | Etching stops on buried SiO₂ (e.g., SOI wafers) | SOI devices |
| **Photoelectrochemical** | Light-generated carriers modulate etch rate | Selective area control |

## 2.2 Surface Micromachining

**Principle:** Builds micromechanical structures by *alternating* deposition of structural and sacrificial thin films on a substrate, then selectively dissolving the **sacrificial layer** to "release" the freestanding structure.

### 2.2.1 Standard Process Flow

1. **Substrate preparation** (typically Si with a buffer SiO₂).
2. **Sacrificial layer deposition** (commonly SiO₂, doped PSG, or photoresist).
3. **Anchor patterning** — open holes in the sacrificial layer to expose the substrate.
4. **Structural layer deposition** (commonly **polysilicon**, Si₃N₄, Al, Au, Ni).
5. **Structural layer patterning** (photolithography + etching).
6. **Release etch** — dissolve the sacrificial layer (e.g., HF for SiO₂).
7. **Critical-point drying or freeze-drying** to prevent **stiction**.

> [!WARNING] > **Stiction Problem:** When rinsing after the release etch, surface tension of the liquid pulls the released structure into contact with the substrate, where van der Waals forces permanently bond it. KTU frequently tests remedies: **critical-point drying**, **freeze-drying**, **self-assembled monolayers (SAMs)**, and **dimple structures**.

### 2.2.2 Common Material Pairs

| Sacrificial Layer | Structural Layer | Release Etchant |
|---|---|---|
| SiO₂ (PSG) | Polysilicon | HF (BHF) |
| Photoresist | Polyimide, Cu | Acetone, O₂ plasma |
| Cu | Ni, Au | Cu etchant (FeCl₃) |
| Polysilicon | SiO₂ | TMAH, KOH |

## 2.3 LIGA Process

**LIGA = Lithographie (X-ray lithography) + Galvanoformung (electroplating) + Abformung (molding).**

Used to fabricate **very high aspect ratio** (> 100:1) metallic, ceramic, and polymer microstructures with sub-micron precision and excellent sidewall quality (smoothness ~50 nm).

### 2.3.1 LIGA Process Steps

1. **Substrate preparation** — conductive seed layer (e.g., Cu, Au) on a wafer.
2. **Resist coating** — thick PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate), typically 100–1000 µm thick.
3. **X-ray exposure** through a synchrotron-generated mask (gold absorber on a titanium or silicon membrane).
4. **Development** — exposed PMMA dissolved in GG developer, leaving the mold.
5. **Electroplating** — metal (Ni, Cu, Au, Fe-Ni alloy) fills the PMMA mold.
6. **PMMA removal** — stripped in hot NMP or plasma.
7. **Molding (optional)** — the metal mold is used to replicate parts in plastic, wax, or ceramic.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ | Etch rate (depth/time) | µm/min |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{R_\text{material A}}{R_\text{material B}}$ | Selectivity between two materials | dimensionless |
| 3 | $A = 1 - \dfrac{R_\text{lateral}}{R_\text{vertical}}$ | Anisotropy factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $AR = \dfrac{d}{w}$ | Aspect ratio (depth/width) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $u = R_\text{lat} \cdot t$ | Mask undercut | µm |
| 6 | $d = R_\text{vert} \cdot t$ | Etch depth (vertical) | µm |
| 7 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{u/2}\right)$ | Sidewall angle from mask | degrees |
| 8 | $R_\text{100} : R_\text{110} : R_\text{111} \approx 100 : 60 : 1$ | Anisotropic etch-rate ratio (KOH, TMAH) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\alpha_\text{100,111} = 54.74^{\circ}$ | Angle between (100) and (111) planes | degrees |
| 10 | $P_\text{cycle} = t_\text{etch} + t_\text{pass}$ | DRIE cycle period | seconds |
| 11 | $R_\text{eff} = \dfrac{d_\text{cycle}}{P_\text{cycle}}$ | Effective DRIE etch rate | µm/cycle |
| 12 | $F = \dfrac{\gamma L \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | Capillary force causing stiction | N (simplified) |
| 13 | $\rho_\text{mask} = \dfrac{t_\text{mask}}{R_\text{Si} \cdot t_\text{etch}}$ | Mask selectivity vs. Si | dimensionless |
| 14 | $E_\text{dose}$ (X-ray) $= 1$ to $4 \text{ kJ/cm}^{3}$ | LIGA exposure dose for PMMA | kJ/cm³ |

> [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas allow MEMS designers to predict *how deep* a structure will be etched for a given time, *how much* the mask will be undercut, and *whether* the etch will stop on a chosen buried layer — all of which are critical to design-rule verification before any fabrication begins.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Etch Depth and Undercut in Isotropic Wet Etching

Consider a single circular mask opening of radius $r_0$ etched in an isotropic bath for time $t$ with etch rate $R$.

**Step 1 — Vertical etch depth**
The etchant dissolves the substrate downward at rate $R$:
$$
d(t) = R \cdot t
$$
This is the maximum depth achieved at the centre of the mask opening.

**Step 2 — Lateral etch (undercut)**
Because isotropic etchants attack equally in all directions, the lateral etch rate equals the vertical etch rate:
$$
R_\text{lat} = R_\text{vert} = R
$$

**Step 3 — Mask undercut distance**
The lateral dissolution beneath the mask edge grows linearly with time:
$$
u(t) = R \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Final cavity profile**
The cavity boundary is a quarter-circle of radius $R \cdot t$ at each mask edge. For a circular mask opening of original radius $r_0$, the final etched radius is:
$$
r_\text{final} = r_0 + R \cdot t
$$
And the total etched area in the plane of the substrate is:
$$
A_\text{final} = \pi (r_0 + R \cdot t)^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Sidewall angle**
For an *anisotropic* etch where lateral and vertical rates differ, the sidewall angle $\theta$ measured from the substrate plane is:
$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R_\text{vert}}{R_\text{lat}}\right)
$$
For pure KOH etching on (100) silicon, $R_\text{lat}$ to (111) is negligible, so $\theta \to 90^{\circ}$ — but the wall is tilted at 54.74° from the (100) plane *into* the wafer, not 90°.

## 3.2 Derivation: Anisotropic Cavity Geometry in KOH Etching of (100) Si

**Step 1 — Setup**
Etchant: KOH 30 wt %, $T = 80\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Mask: square window of side $w$ aligned with the $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions on a (100) wafer.

**Step 2 — Etch rate along (100)**
The vertical etch rate is:
$$
R_{100} = 1.4 \ \mu\text{m/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Etch rate along (111)**
$$
R_{111} \approx 0.014 \ \mu\text{m/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Selectivity**
$$
S_{(100)/(111)} = \dfrac{R_{100}}{R_{111}} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.014} = 100
$$

**Step 5 — Sidewall plane**
Etching effectively stops on the four (111) planes intersecting the mask corners. The angle between (100) and (111) is:
$$
\alpha = 54.74^{\circ}
$$

**Step 6 — Pit depth when (111) planes meet**
The square opening of side $w$ etched to depth $d$ has its corners bounded by (111) planes. The four planes meet at a point when:
$$
d = \dfrac{w/2}{\tan(54.74^{\circ})} = \dfrac{w/2}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{w}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$
Therefore:
$$
d_\text{meeting} = \dfrac{w}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.354\,w
$$

**Step 7 — Beyond this depth**
Further etching does not increase depth — the (111) planes intersect and form a **square-base pyramidal pit** with the apex pointing into the wafer.

## 3.3 Derivation: Selectivity for Mask Selection

For a mask of thickness $t_\text{mask}$ protecting the underlying silicon during an etch of duration $t_\text{etch}$:

**Step 1 — Mask consumed**
$$
\Delta t_\text{mask} = R_\text{mask} \cdot t_\text{etch}
$$

**Step 2 — Required selectivity**
To keep the mask intact during the entire Si etch of depth $d_\text{Si}$:
$$
S_\text{required} = \dfrac{R_\text{Si}}{R_\text{mask}} \geq \dfrac{d_\text{Si}}{\Delta t_\text{mask}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example**
To etch $d_\text{Si} = 100 \ \mu\text{m}$ of silicon with a SiO₂ mask, given $R_\text{Si} = 1.4 \ \mu\text{m/min}$ and $R_\text{SiO2} = 0.014 \ \mu\text{m/min}$ in KOH:
$$
t_\text{etch} = \dfrac{d_\text{Si}}{R_\text{Si}} = \dfrac{100}{1.4} = 71.4 \ \text{min}
$$
$$
\Delta t_\text{mask} = 0.014 \times 71.4 = 1.0 \ \mu\text{m}
$$
$$
S_\text{required} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.014} = 100
$$
Hence a **1 µm SiO₂ mask is sufficient** for a 100 µm Si etch in KOH.

## 3.4 Derivation: DRIE Effective Etch Rate and Scallop Size

The Bosch process alternates etch ($t_e$) and passivation ($t_p$) cycles. Each cycle etches a depth:
$$
d_\text{cycle} = R_\text{etch} \cdot t_e
$$

**Effective etch rate (continuous-time basis):**
$$
R_\text{eff} = \dfrac{d_\text{cycle}}{t_e + t_p} = \dfrac{R_\text{etch} \cdot t_e}{t_e + t_p}
$$

**Sidewall scallop amplitude** (peak-to-valley):
$$
\Delta_\text{scallop} \approx R_\text{etch} \cdot t_e
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Trade-Off:** Increasing $t_e$ speeds up etching but enlarges the sidewall scallops, degrading optical and mechanical smoothness. A typical compromise is $t_e = t_p = 5$–$10$ s, giving scallops of ~50–100 nm.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection

| Requirement | Best Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Smooth, vertical sidewalls | DRIE (Bosch, optimized) | Aspect ratio > 30, scallops < 50 nm |
| Atomic-scale smoothness | Wet KOH anisotropic | Atomically flat (111) planes |
| High aspect ratio (>100) | LIGA | X-ray lithography + electroplating |
| Multi-layer, low-temp, CMOS compatible | Surface micromachining | Poly-Si, Al, SiO₂ at < 450 °C |
| Buried cavities | Fusion bonding + DRIE | Sealed cavities for pressure sensors |
| Low cost, polymer parts | Hot embossing, injection molding | Replicated from LIGA or SU-8 mold |
| Curved 3-D shapes | Isotropic wet etching | Quarter-circle profiles |
| Sub-100 nm features | E-beam / Focused ion beam | Direct-write, no mask |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Taxonomy of Micromachining Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Micromachining Processes] --> B[Bulk Micromachining]
    A --> C[Surface Micromachining]
    A --> D[LIGA Process]
    A --> E[Other Advanced Processes]

    B --> B1[Wet Chemical Etching]
    B --> B2[Dry Etching]
    B1 --> B1a[Isotropic - HNA]
    B1 --> B1b[Anisotropic - KOH TMAH EDP]
    B2 --> B2a[Reactive Ion Etching RIE]
    B2 --> B2b[Deep Reactive Ion Etching DRIE]
    B2 --> B2c[Ion Beam Etching IBE]

    C --> C1[Sacrificial Layer Technique]
    C --> C2[Structural Layer Deposition]
    C --> C3[Release and Critical Point Drying]
    C1 --> C1a[SiO2 PSG]
    C1 --> C1b[Photoresist]
    C2 --> C2a[Polysilicon]
    C2 --> C2b[Si3N4 Metal]

    D --> D1[X-ray Lithography]
    D --> D2[Electroplating]
    D --> D3[Molding Replication]

    E --> E1[Focused Ion Beam FIB]
    E --> E2[Laser Micromachining]
    E --> E3[Electrochemical Micromachining]
    E --> E4[Micro EDM]
    E --> E5[Micro Ultrasonic Machining]
```

## 4.2 Surface Micromachining Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Substrate Preparation Si with buffer SiO2] --> P2[Step 2: Deposit Sacrificial Layer SiO2 PSG]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Pattern Anchor Holes Photolithography and Etch]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Deposit Structural Layer Polysilicon]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Pattern Structural Layer Photolithography and RIE]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Release Etch HF dissolves sacrificial layer]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Critical Point Drying Prevents stiction]
    P7 --> P8[Final: Freestanding MEMS Structure]
```

## 4.3 LIGA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Step 1: Deposit Conductive Seed Layer Cu or Au on substrate] --> L2[Step 2: Spin Coat Thick PMMA Resist 100 to 1000 micrometre]
    L2 --> L3[Step 3: X-ray Exposure Through Synchrotron Mask]
    L3 --> L4[Step 4: Develop in GG Developer Dissolves exposed PMMA]
    L4 --> L5[Step 5: Electroplating Metal fills PMMA mold Ni Cu Au]
    L5 --> L6[Step 6: Strip Remaining PMMA]
    L6 --> L7[Step 7 Optional: Use Metal Master as Insert for Plastic Molding]
    L7 --> L8[Final: High Aspect Ratio Metallic Microstructure]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Bulk Micromachining Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InP[Inputs]
        I1[Wafer Si 100 or 111]
        I2[Mask SiO2 or Si3N4]
        I3[Etchant KOH or TMAH or DRIE plasma]
    end
    InP --> P[Process Chamber]
    P --> M1[Etch Rate Control]
    P --> M2[Selectivity Monitor]
    P --> M3[Anisotropy Control]
    P --> M4[Etch Stop Detector]
    M1 --> OutP[Outputs]
    M2 --> OutP
    M3 --> OutP
    M4 --> OutP
    OutP[Outputs: 3D Microstructure Cantilever Membrane Cavity Channel]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block: Bulk vs Surface vs LIGA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Compare Micromachining Routes] --> R1[Bulk Micromachining]
    Q --> R2[Surface Micromachining]
    Q --> R3[LIGA]
    R1 --> S1[Material removed from substrate bulk]
    R1 --> S2[Typical depth 1 to 500 micrometre]
    R1 --> S3[Aspect ratio up to 30 to 50]
    R2 --> T1[Material added then selectively released]
    R2 --> T2[Typical thickness 0.5 to 5 micrometre]
    R2 --> T3[Aspect ratio 5 to 20]
    R3 --> U1[X-ray lithography and electroplating]
    R3 --> U2[Typical depth 100 to 1000 micrometre]
    R3 --> U3[Aspect ratio greater than 100]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define micromachining. List any two micromachining processes used in MEMS fabrication.**

**Model Answer:**
Micromachining is a set of manufacturing techniques used to fabricate micro-scale mechanical and electromechanical structures (typically 1 µm – 1 mm) with sub-micron precision by selectively removing, depositing, or transforming material on a substrate (commonly silicon).

Two processes:
1. **Bulk Micromachining** — selective removal of bulk substrate material (e.g., wet anisotropic etching using KOH).
2. **Surface Micromachining** — deposition of thin-film structural layers over a sacrificial layer that is later dissolved to release the structure (e.g., polysilicon over SiO₂ released in HF).

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by anisotropic etching? Why is KOH preferred for anisotropic etching of silicon?**

**Model Answer:**
Anisotropic etching refers to etching whose rate depends on the crystallographic direction of the material — different planes etch at different rates.

KOH is preferred for anisotropic etching of silicon because:
1. It exhibits an extremely high etch-rate ratio between (100) and (111) planes (≈ 100 : 1), producing well-defined geometries.
2. It does not attack SiO₂ significantly, so SiO₂ can serve as a robust mask.
3. It is safe, inexpensive, and compatible with standard cleanroom practice.
4. The resulting (111) planes are atomically smooth — useful for optical and precision mechanical applications.

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** *With neat sketches, explain the **bulk micromachining** process using **anisotropic wet etching** of (100) silicon with KOH. Discuss etch-stop techniques. (7 marks)

**(b)** *Explain the **surface micromachining** process with the help of a step-by-step diagram. What is **stiction** and how is it prevented?* (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a) Bulk Micromachining with KOH

**Step 1: Wafer selection** — A (100)-oriented silicon wafer is selected because its (111) planes lie at 54.74° to the surface, allowing predictable cavity shapes.

**Step 2: Mask deposition and patterning** — A layer of SiO₂ (~1 µm) is thermally grown or LPCVD-deposited, then photolithographically patterned to define the etch windows.

**Step 3: Anisotropic wet etching** — The wafer is immersed in KOH (30 wt %, 80 °C). Etch rates are:
$$
R_{100} \approx 1.4 \ \mu\text{m/min}, \quad R_{111} \approx 0.014 \ \mu\text{m/min}
$$
Hence $S_{(100)/(111)} \approx 100$.

**Step 4: Cavity formation** — Etching proceeds vertically on (100) planes and is self-limited by the four (111) planes emerging from the mask edges. A square opening of side $w$ produces a pyramidal pit of depth $d = w/(2\sqrt{2})$.

**Step 5: Etch-stop techniques:**

| Technique | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Boron etch stop** | Heavily boron-doped Si (> 5 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³) etches ~ 50× slower than intrinsic Si |
| **Electrochemical stop** | Reverse bias on a p-n junction depletes holes at the junction; etchant (e.g., EDP) requires holes to oxidize Si |
| **Dielectric stop** | Buried SiO₂ (e.g., SOI wafer) terminates the etch |
| **Photoelectrochemical** | Illumination modulates hole density to control etch depth |

*(Sketch: a square mask opening on (100) Si, KOH attacks the (100) plane downward and is bounded by four (111) sidewalls meeting at the apex of a pyramid.)*

#### Model Solution — (b) Surface Micromachining

**Step 1: Substrate preparation** — A silicon wafer with a thermal SiO₂ buffer layer is used.

**Step 2: Sacrificial layer deposition** — A layer of SiO₂ (often phosphosilicate glass, PSG) is deposited by LPCVD (~ 1–2 µm).

**Step 3: Anchor patterning** — Photolithography + BHF etch open anchor holes that expose the underlying substrate.

**Step 4: Structural layer deposition** — Polysilicon is LPCVD-deposited (typically 2 µm).

**Step 5: Structural layer patterning** — Photolithography + RIE define the beam/cantilever shape.

**Step 6: Release etch** — BHF dissolves the underlying SiO₂, leaving the polysilicon beam freestanding, anchored only at the anchor points.

**Step 7: Critical-point drying** — Water is replaced with liquid CO₂, then taken past the critical point (31 °C, 1073 psi) where surface tension vanishes, preventing stiction.

**Stiction** is the permanent adhesion of released structures to the substrate due to capillary forces during wet rinsing and/or van der Waals attraction. It is prevented by:
- **Critical-point drying** or **freeze-drying** (eliminate liquid surface tension).
- **Dimple structures** etched into the bottom of the structural layer (reduce contact area).
- **Self-assembled monolayer (SAM) coatings** (e.g., OTS) to lower surface energy.
- **Anti-stiction bumps**.

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** *Describe the **LIGA process** in detail with a flowchart. List its advantages and limitations.* (7 marks)

**(b)** *Compare **RIE and DRIE**. Explain the **Bosch process** for DRIE with a clear timing diagram.* (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a) LIGA Process

**Definition:** LIGA is a German acronym for *Lithographie* (X-ray lithography), *Galvanoformung* (electroplating), and *Abformung* (molding) — a high-aspect-ratio micromachining technology.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Seed-layer deposition** — A thin conductive layer (Cu, Au) is sputtered onto the substrate to enable electroplating.
2. **Resist coating** — A thick layer of PMMA (100–1000 µm) is bonded or cast onto the substrate.
3. **X-ray exposure** — Synchrotron X-rays (~ 0.2 – 2 nm wavelength) expose the PMMA through a mask consisting of a gold absorber on a titanium or silicon membrane.
4. **Development** — Exposed PMMA is dissolved in GG developer (a mixture of diethylene glycol butyl ether, morpholine, ethanolamine, and water), creating the mold cavities.
5. **Electroplating** — The mold cavities are filled with metal (Ni, Cu, Au, or Fe-Ni permalloy) using the seed layer as the cathode.
6. **PMMA removal** — Remaining PMMA is stripped in hot N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) or by O₂ plasma ashing.
7. **Molding (Abformung)** — The metal master may be used as an insert for hot embossing, injection molding, or reaction-injection molding of polymer replicas.

**Advantages:**
- Very high aspect ratio (> 100 : 1)
- Excellent sidewall smoothness (~ 50 nm)
- Wide range of materials (Ni, Cu, Au, ceramics, polymers)
- Sub-micron lateral precision

**Limitations:**
- Requires a synchrotron radiation source (expensive, scarce)
- Thick PMMA requires long exposure times
- Mask fabrication is complex and costly
- Limited to 2.5-D features per exposure

#### Model Solution — (b) RIE vs DRIE and the Bosch Process

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | RIE | DRIE (Bosch) |
|---|---|---|
| Typical depth | < 5 µm | 100 – 600 µm |
| Aspect ratio | ~ 5 : 1 | > 30 : 1 (up to 50 : 1) |
| Sidewall angle | 70 – 80° | ~ 90° |
| Sidewall roughness | Smooth | Scalloped (~ 50 – 200 nm) |
| Mask | Photoresist, SiO₂ | Photoresist, SiO₂, metal |
| Pressure | 10 – 100 mTorr | Cycled (etch + pass) |
| Chemistry | CF₄, SF₆, CHF₃ | SF₆ (etch) + C₄F₈ (passivation) |
| Mechanism | Chemical + physical | Cycled chemical + physical |

**The Bosch Process (DRIE):**

The process alternates two plasma steps in a single chamber:

1. **Etch step (duration $t_e$):** SF₆ plasma generates free fluorine radicals that chemically etch Si isotropically. The substrate is also ion-bombarded, giving directional bias to vertical surfaces.
2. **Passivation step (duration $t_p$):** C₄F₈ plasma deposits a fluoropolymer layer (Teflon-like) on *all* exposed surfaces, including the sidewalls.

**Timing Diagram (textual representation):**
```
t ──────────────────────────────────────────►
       ┌────┐         ┌────┐         ┌────┐
──SF₆──┤etch├─C₄F₈──┤pass├──SF₆────┤etch├─ ...
       └────┘         └────┘         └────┘
       t_e             t_p             t_e
  → Vertical etch     → Sidewall      → Vertical etch
                       protected
```
After each cycle, the polymer on the bottom of the trench is removed by ion bombardment, exposing fresh Si for the next etch step, while sidewalls remain protected — yielding vertical sidewalls with characteristic scallops of amplitude $\Delta_\text{scallop} = R_\text{etch} \cdot t_e$.

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by:
> 1. **Confusing DRIE with RIE** — DRIE is cyclic and produces scallops; RIE is continuous and shallow.
> 2. **Forgetting the passivation step** in the Bosch process — without C₄F₈, the etch would be isotropic.
> 3. **Mislabelling the X-ray source** in LIGA — it is **synchrotron** radiation, *not* a conventional X-ray tube.
> 4. **Omitting the critical-point drying** step when describing stiction prevention.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Micromachining** = microfabrication techniques that produce 3-D MEMS structures by **removal, deposition, or transformation** of material.
- Three main families: **Bulk, Surface, LIGA.**
- **Bulk micromachining** = material removed from the substrate; **Wet (KOH/TMAH/EDP)** is anisotropic and dependent on crystal orientation; **Dry (RIE/DRIE/IBE)** is plasma-based.
- **Anisotropy of KOH on Si (100):** $R_{100} : R_{110} : R_{111} \approx 100 : 60 : 1$ → produces 54.74° pyramidal pits bounded by (111) planes.
- **Etch stops** in bulk micromachining: **boron doping, electrochemical, buried oxide (SOI), photoelectrochemical**.
- **Surface micromachining** = layer-by-layer thin-film deposition + sacrificial-layer release in HF (or other selective etchant).
- **Stiction** is permanent adhesion of released structures; prevented by **critical-point drying, dimples, SAMs**.
- **DRIE (Bosch process)** alternates **SF₆ etch** and **C₄F₈ passivation** — enables aspect ratios > 30:1 with sidewall scallops.
- **LIGA** = X-ray lithography + electroplating + molding — gives **aspect ratios > 100:1** but requires a **synchrotron**.
- Key formulas to memorize:
  - Etch rate: $R = \Delta x / \Delta t$
  - Selectivity: $S = R_A / R_B$
  - Anisotropy: $A = 1 - R_\text{lat}/R_\text{vert}$
  - Aspect ratio: $AR = d/w$
  - KOH pyramid depth: $d = w / (2\sqrt{2})$
- Always state the **units** (µm/min, °, dimensionless) when quoting numerical etch parameters.
- **CMOS compatibility** is a frequent exam question — surface micromachining is CMOS-compatible at low temperature; KOH is *not* CMOS-compatible post-processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pattern Transfer Using Additive Technique. Silicon Growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pattern Transfer Using Additive Technique. Silicon Growth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pattern Transfer Using Additive Technique

**Additive Pattern Transfer** (also called *additive lithography* or *deposition-based patterning*) refers to the family of MEMS/IC fabrication processes in which the desired thin-film pattern is **built up by selective deposition of material** onto a substrate, rather than by selectively removing material (as in subtractive etching). Material is added only where it is needed, and the masking step determines *where* the new layer is created.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):** *Additive techniques* include all processes in which a new material layer (typically doped/undoped silicon, polysilicon, oxide, nitride, or metal) is grown or deposited through openings in a masking film — the inverse geometric pattern of the mask becomes the deposited film.

### 1.2 Sub-Categories of Additive Techniques

| Sub-Category | Brief Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Epitaxial Growth** | Single-crystal Si grown on Si substrate via CVD | Buried layers, epi-wafers, MEMS sensors |
| **Polysilicon Deposition** | Poly-Si deposited over oxide/nitride | Structural MEMS layer, gate electrodes |
| **Selective Epitaxial Growth (SEG)** | Epi grows only on exposed Si, not on oxide | Elevated S/D, stacked transistors |
| **Silicon-on-Insulator (SOI) formation** | Si film bonded/formed over buried oxide | Advanced MEMS, RF, radiation-hard |
| **Lateral Epitaxial Growth (Epi-Poly)** | Epitaxial + polycrystalline side-by-side | 3-D MEMS structures |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU Module 2, the term *Pattern Transfer Using Additive Technique* is tightly coupled to **selective epitaxy** and the **epitaxial growth of silicon**, because epitaxy is the only additive technique that *inherits* the single-crystal lattice of the substrate — making it indispensable for high-performance MEMS and IC devices.

## 1.3 Silicon Growth

**Silicon Growth** refers to the bulk and thin-film processes used to produce crystalline silicon of the required purity, crystal orientation, doping, and form (wafer, epitaxial layer, polysilicon film).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Si Growth Methods (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Czochralski (CZ) Method** — produces ~90% of the world's Si wafers.
> 2. **Float-Zone (FZ) Method** — produces ultra-pure Si for power and MEMS sensors.
> 3. **Epitaxial (Thin-Film) Growth** — grows single-crystal Si films on a substrate.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Brick" View

Imagine you are building a wall:
- **Subtractive technique (etching)** is like starting with a huge block of marble and *chiseling away* the unwanted parts to reveal the wall.
- **Additive technique (deposition/epitaxy)** is like starting with a foundation and *stacking Lego bricks only along the lines* of the wall blueprint — the mask tells you exactly where the bricks go.
- **Silicon growth** is like *making the Lego bricks themselves* — you have to grow pure silicon crystals (CZ / FZ) before you can deposit them as thin films (Epi).

> [!TIP]
> Just as Lego bricks inherit the pattern of the baseplate beneath them, **epitaxial silicon inherits the crystal orientation of the substrate wafer** — this is why epi-layers are single-crystal continuations of the wafer, not separate crystals.

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants (Highlight)

- **Atomic density of Si:** $5 \times 10^{22}$ **atoms/cm³**
- **Lattice constant of Si (a):** **5.43 Å** (0.543 nm)
- **Melting point of Si:** **1414 °C**
- **Density of Si:** **2.33 g/cm³**
- **Solid solubility of P in Si (max):** ~$1.3 \times 10^{21}$ **atoms/cm³** at 1100 °C
- **Segregation coefficient of P at melting point:** **k₀ = 0.35**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Additive Pattern Transfer Sequence

The generic step-by-step flow used in KTU problems:

1. **Substrate Preparation** — cleaned Si wafer (typically (100) oriented for MEMS).
2. **Mask Layer Deposition** — thermally grown $\text{SiO}_2$ or LPCVD $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$ is deposited over the entire wafer.
3. **Photolithography** — the mask is patterned with photoresist, then the underlying mask oxide/nitride is etched (often with BHF or RIE) to open **windows**.
4. **Selective Deposition / Epitaxy** — silicon is grown **only inside the opened windows** (because nucleation on $\text{SiO}_2$ is suppressed under proper conditions).
5. **Mask Strip / Planarization** — the mask is removed, leaving a patterned single-crystal Si region embedded in or rising above the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the additive route?** It gives a **flat surface** (no recess), allows **abrupt doping transitions**, and is the only way to create a **single-crystal region** on top of an insulator — a foundation for SOI-MEMS.

## 2.2 Detailed Mechanism of Selective Epitaxial Growth (SEG)

In SEG, an etch-gas (typically **HCl** or **HBr**) is added to the conventional epi-gas chemistry (e.g., $\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ + $\text{H}_2$):

- On **exposed Si** → Si deposition rate $\gg$ Si etch rate ⇒ **net growth**.
- On **$\text{SiO}_2$/$\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$** → no nucleation ⇒ deposition rate $\approx$ 0 ⇒ **no growth**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Growth Condition Rule of Thumb:**
> $$\text{If } \frac{R_\text{deposition}}{R_\text{etch}} \gt 1 \text{ on Si, and } R_\text{deposition} \ll R_\text{etch} \text{ on oxide} \Rightarrow \text{SEG achieved}$$

## 2.3 Vapor Phase Epitaxy (VPE) Reaction Chemistry

The most common KTU-referenced chemistry — **Silicon Epitaxy by $\text{SiH}_4$ or $\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ (DCS)**:

**Reaction 1 — Silane Pyrolysis (low temperature, 600–900 °C):**
$$\text{SiH}_4 \,(g) \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Si}\,(s) + 2\,\text{H}_2\,(g)$$

**Reaction 2 — Dichlorosilane Reduction (high temperature, 1000–1150 °C):**
$$\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2 \,(g) + \text{H}_2\,(g) \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Si}\,(s) + 2\,\text{HCl}\,(g)$$

The reverse reaction (etch) is suppressed in these temperature windows for clean epi growth, but the *partial pressure of HCl* can be tuned to convert Reaction 2 into a net-etch mode (used in **SEG**).

## 2.4 Czochralski (CZ) Bulk Si Growth

A seed crystal is dipped into molten Si (at 1414 °C) inside a quartz crucible. The seed is **slowly pulled upward and rotated**, dragging a cylindrical single-crystal ingot out of the melt.

**Operational steps:**
1. **Melt** — Poly-Si charge melted in quartz crucible under Ar ambient.
2. **Seed Dip** — (100) or (111) seed touches melt, melts back slightly to clean interface.
3. **Necking** — Pull at high speed with small diameter to eliminate dislocations (Dash process).
4. **Crown / Shoulder** — Diameter enlarged to target size by reducing pull rate.
5. **Body Growth** — Constant diameter maintained; dopant (P, B, Sb) added to melt for n/p-type.
6. **Tail / End Cone** — Diameter reduced to detach ingot without thermal shock.

**Resistive vs. Cusp Magnetic Field:** Modern CZ uses magnetic fields (MCZ) to suppress turbulent oxygen transport, controlling **oxygen concentration** ([Oi]) which directly affects internal gettering and wafer strength.

## 2.5 Float-Zone (FZ) Bulk Si Growth

A polycrystalline Si rod is held vertically; a **small molten zone** is created by an RF heating coil. The coil is **slowly translated along the rod**, sweeping the molten zone and converting poly-Si into single-crystal Si. Because **no crucible is used**, FZ wafers have:
- Very low oxygen (<$10^{16}$ **atoms/cm³** vs ~$10^{18}$ for CZ)
- Very high resistivity possible (>10,000 Ω·cm)
- Higher minority carrier lifetime

> [!TIP]
> FZ is preferred for **MEMS pressure sensors** and **high-voltage power devices** because low oxygen means fewer crystal defects and higher breakdown strength.

## 2.6 Dopant Distribution & Segregation Coefficient

The **equilibrium segregation coefficient** $k_0$ is defined as:
$$k_0 = \frac{C_s}{C_l}\bigg\vert_{eq}$$
where $C_s$ is the solid (crystal) dopant concentration and $C_l$ is the liquid (melt) concentration at the interface.

**Effective Segregation Coefficient** (accounting for finite interface kinetics):
$$k_\text{eff} = \frac{k_0}{k_0 + (1 - k_0)\,e^{-\delta\,v/D}}$$
where:
- $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness,
- $v$ = growth/pull velocity,
- $D$ = dopant diffusivity in melt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a normal freezing process (CZ, Epi), the dopant concentration along the grown crystal follows the **Scheil equation**:
> $$C_s(x) = k_0 \, C_0\,(1 - x)^{k_0 - 1}$$
> where $x$ is the fraction solidified and $C_0$ is the initial melt concentration. Because $k_0 < 1$ for most dopants (P: 0.35, B: 0.80), $C_s$ **rises** along the ingot length — the last-to-freeze region is heavily doped.

## 2.7 Epitaxial Growth Rate Models

**Mass-transport limited regime (high T, low pressure):**
$$R_g = \frac{k_s\,h_g}{k_s + h_g}\,C_g$$
where $k_s$ = surface reaction rate constant, $h_g$ = mass-transfer coefficient, $C_g$ = gas-phase reactant concentration.

**Surface-reaction limited regime (low T):**
$$R_g \approx k_s \, C_g$$

**Burton–Prim–Slichter (BPS) impurity distribution in epi:**
$$C_s(x) = C_0 \left[\,k_\text{eff} + (1 - k_\text{eff})\,e^{-v\,\delta/D}\,\right]$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $a_{Si}$ | Si lattice constant | **5.43 Å** |
| $T_{melt}$ | Melting point of Si | **1414 °C** |
| $n_{Si}$ | Atomic density of Si | $5 \times 10^{22}$ **atoms/cm³** |
| $k_0$ | Equilibrium segregation coefficient | P: 0.35, B: 0.80, Sb: 0.023 |
| $k_\text{eff}$ | Effective segregation coefficient | $0 < k_\text{eff} < 1$ |
| $C_s(x) = k_0 C_0(1-x)^{k_0-1}$ | Scheil normal-freeze equation | - |
| $C_s = C_0 (1 - x)^{k_0-1}$ | BPS axial profile in epi | - |
| $R_g = k_s C_g$ | Surface-limited epi growth rate | nm/min to μm/min |
| $v = 2\pi r \omega$ | Pull rate equivalence for rotation | mm/hr |
| $\rho = q\,N_A\,\mu$ | Resistivity in doped Si | Ω·cm |
| $N_s = n_{Si} \cdot \exp(-E_a/kT)$ | Solid solubility exponential form | atoms/cm³ |
| $t_{epi} = R_g \cdot t$ | Epi thickness = rate × time | μm |
| $\sigma = q \mu N_d$ | Conductivity of n-type epi layer | S/cm |
| $r_{lat} \propto \sqrt{D_s t}$ | Lateral solid-phase regrowth rate | nm |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables, the absolute-value symbol is written as `\vert` to prevent breaking the column pipe syntax. For example, $\vert C_s \vert$.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MEMS Pressure Sensors** — buried epi-layer forms the piezoresistor island, with the bulk-Si etch stopped at the epi-substrate junction.
- **CMOS Image Sensors** — epitaxial layer over heavily-doped substrate reduces cross-talk and dark current.
- **Power BJTs / IGBTs** — thick epi with controlled doping on heavily-doped substrate gives high breakdown voltage + low on-resistance.
- **SOI-MEMS** — smart-cut or bonded wafer technology relies on epi-quality single-crystal Si films.
- **Solar PV** — CZ multi-crystalline and FZ mono dominate the wafer supply chain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Scheil Equation (Normal Freezing)

**Starting point:** Consider a crystal being pulled from a melt of length $L$. A small element $dx$ of solid freezes, rejecting $(1 - k_0)\,C_l$ dopant atoms per unit volume into the melt.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on the melt:**
The amount of dopant rejected into the melt when a fraction $dx$ solidifies is:
$$dM = (1 - k_0)\,C_l\,A\,L\,dx$$
where $A$ is cross-sectional area. This increases the melt concentration:
$$C_l \rightarrow C_l + \frac{(1 - k_0)\,C_l\,dx}{1 - x}$$

**Step 2 — Differential form of the freezing equation:**
$$\frac{dC_l}{C_l} = \frac{(1 - k_0)\,dx}{1 - x}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from 0 to $x$:**
$$\int_{C_0}^{C_l} \frac{dC_l}{C_l} = (1 - k_0)\int_0^x \frac{dx}{1 - x}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_l}{C_0}\right) = -(1 - k_0)\,\ln(1 - x) = \ln(1 - x)^{-(1 - k_0)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_l$ and substitute $C_s = k_0 C_l$:**
$$C_l = C_0 (1 - x)^{-(1 - k_0)}$$

$$\boxed{\,C_s(x) = k_0\,C_0\,(1 - x)^{k_0 - 1}\,}$$

> This is the **Scheil normal-freeze equation** — fundamental for dopant distribution in CZ ingots and epitaxial layers.

## 3.2 Derivation of Effective Segregation Coefficient ($k_\text{eff}$)

**Step 1 — Set up steady-state diffusion across the boundary layer** of thickness $\delta$ in the melt, with growth velocity $v$ normal to the interface:

Steady-state condition: dopant carried by solidification = diffusive flux from boundary layer:
$$v\,(C_l^* - C_s) = -D \left.\frac{dC}{dy}\right\vert_{y=0}$$

**Step 2 — Assume linear concentration gradient in the boundary layer** (BPS approximation):
$$-\left.\frac{dC}{dy}\right\vert_{y=0} = \frac{C_l^\infty - C_l^*}{\delta}$$

where $C_l^\infty$ is the bulk melt concentration and $C_l^*$ is the concentration at the solid-liquid interface.

**Step 3 — Substitute $C_s = k_0 C_l^*$:**
$$v\,(C_l^* - k_0 C_l^*) = D\,\frac{C_l^\infty - C_l^*}{\delta}$$

$$v\,C_l^*(1 - k_0) = \frac{D}{\delta}\,(C_l^\infty - C_l^*)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_l^*/C_l^\infty$:**
$$C_l^*\,\left[v(1 - k_0) + \frac{D}{\delta}\right] = \frac{D}{\delta}\,C_l^\infty$$

$$C_l^* = \frac{C_l^\infty}{1 + \dfrac{v\,\delta}{D}\,(1 - k_0)}$$

**Step 5 — Define $k_\text{eff} = C_s/C_l^\infty = k_0\,C_l^*/C_l^\infty$:**
$$\boxed{\,k_\text{eff} = \frac{k_0}{k_0 + (1 - k_0)\,e^{-\delta v / D}}\,}$$

> Note: The denominator has the dimensionless group $v\delta/D$ (Peclet number of growth). For $v \to 0$, $k_\text{eff} \to k_0$. For $v \to \infty$, $k_\text{eff} \to 1$.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Boron Distribution in CZ Ingot

**Given:**
- Initial melt: $C_0 = 1 \times 10^{19}$ **atoms/cm³** of B.
- $k_0 = 0.80$.
- Find $C_s$ at $x = 0.2$ and $x = 0.8$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Scheil equation:**
$$C_s(x) = k_0\,C_0\,(1 - x)^{k_0 - 1} = 0.80 \times 1\times10^{19} \times (1 - x)^{-0.20}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at $x = 0.2$:**
$$C_s(0.2) = 0.80 \times 10^{19} \times (0.8)^{-0.20}$$

$$\log_{10}(0.8^{-0.20}) = -0.20 \times \log_{10}(0.8) = -0.20 \times (-0.0969) = 0.01938$$

$$(0.8)^{-0.20} = 10^{0.01938} = 1.0457$$

$$C_s(0.2) \approx 0.836 \times 10^{19} = 8.36 \times 10^{18}\,\text{atoms/cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $x = 0.8$:**
$$C_s(0.8) = 0.80 \times 10^{19} \times (0.2)^{-0.20}$$

$$\log_{10}(0.2^{-0.20}) = -0.20 \times \log_{10}(0.2) = -0.20 \times (-0.6990) = 0.1398$$

$$(0.2)^{-0.20} = 10^{0.1398} = 1.380$$

$$C_s(0.8) \approx 1.10 \times 10^{19}\,\text{atoms/cm}^3$$

> The concentration **rises** by a factor of ~1.32 from the seed end ($x=0$) to near the tail ($x=0.8$) — a direct consequence of $k_0 < 1$.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Epitaxial Growth Time

**Given:**
- Target epi thickness: $t = 5$ **μm**.
- Growth rate: $R_g = 1$ **μm/min** at 1100 °C with $\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2$.
- Required doping in epi: $N_d = 1 \times 10^{16}$ **atoms/cm³** (phosphorus).
- $k_0$ of P at 1100 °C: 0.35.

**Step 1 — Compute deposition time:**
$$t_\text{deposition} = \frac{t}{R_g} = \frac{5\,\mu m}{1\,\mu m/\min} = 5\,\text{minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Find required gas-phase P concentration (assume full incorporation, $k_\text{eff} \approx k_0$ for low $v$):**
$$C_g \approx \frac{N_d}{k_0} = \frac{1 \times 10^{16}}{0.35} \approx 2.86 \times 10^{16}\,\text{atoms/cm}^3 \text{ equivalent}$$

(Real value depends on PH₃/SiH₄ flow ratio in reactor.)

**Step 3 — Check abruptness of doping transition:**
A well-designed epi reactor gives a **doping transition width < 0.05 μm** for a 5 μm layer, satisfying the KTU criterion of an "abrupt junction."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Additive Pattern Transfer Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Clean Si Wafer 100] --> B[Deposit Mask Layer SiO2 or Si3N4]
    B --> C[Spin Photoresist and Soft Bake]
    C --> D[Align Mask and Expose UV]
    D --> E[Develop Resist Pattern]
    E --> F[Etch Mask Window BHF or RIE]
    F --> G[Strip Resist]
    G --> H[Selective Epitaxial Growth in Window]
    H --> I[Mask Strip HF Dip]
    I --> J[Final Patterned Single Crystal Si Island]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Czochralski Bulk Growth System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Poly Si Charge] --> B[Quartz Crucible in Heated Furnace]
    B --> C[RF Induction Heater 1414 C]
    C --> D[Seed Crystal Dipped into Melt]
    D --> E[Necking Stage Dash Process]
    E --> F[Crown Shoulder Diameter Enlarged]
    F --> G[Body Growth Constant Pull Rate]
    G --> H[Tail End Cone Detach]
    H --> I[Single Crystal Si Ingot]
    I --> J[Wafer Slicing and Polishing]
    J --> K[Final Si Wafer for MEMS]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Float Zone FZ Refinement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Poly Si Rod Mounted Vertically] --> B[RF Coil Heats Localized Molten Zone]
    B --> C[Coil Sweeps Molten Zone Upward]
    C --> D[Impurities Segregate into Molten Zone]
    D --> E[Poly Si Converts to Single Crystal]
    E --> F[Purified Single Crystal FZ Ingot]
    F --> G[Wafering and Inspection]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Selective Epitaxial Growth Reactor Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SiH2Cl2 H2 Carrier Gas] --> B[Mixing Manifold]
    B --> C[Reactor Chamber 1050 C]
    C --> D[Wafer with Patterned Oxide Mask]
    D --> E{Si Surface Exposed}
    D --> F{SiO2 Mask Surface}
    E --> G[Net Si Deposition Rate R d]
    F --> H[Net Etch Dominant R e greater than R d]
    G --> I[Epitaxial Island Grows]
    H --> J[Oxide Surface Stays Clean]
    I --> K[Final Patterned Epi Wafer]
    J --> K
```

## 4.5 Comparison Flow — CZ vs FZ vs Epi

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Si Wafer Required] --> B{Quality Priority}
    B -->|High Volume Low Cost| C[CZ Method]
    B -->|Ultra Pure High Resistivity| D[FZ Method]
    B -->|Thin Single Crystal Layer on Substrate| E[Epitaxial Growth]
    C --> C1[Oxygen 1e18 atoms per cm3]
    D --> D1[Oxygen less than 1e16 atoms per cm3]
    E --> E1[Thickness 0.5 to 100 um Controlled Doping]
    C1 --> F[Final Wafer]
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

> [!TIP]
> These block-level functional diagrams map the **process logic** rather than attempting photorealistic physical schematics, ensuring the Mermaid compiler renders them reliably.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **"additive pattern transfer"** in MEMS fabrication. How does it differ fundamentally from subtractive pattern transfer?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Additive pattern transfer is a fabrication technique in which the desired material pattern is *built up* by depositing or growing material selectively through a pre-patterned mask, rather than by removing material from a continuous film. In the additive approach, the masking layer defines *where* the new film will appear; in the subtractive approach, the masking layer defines *what remains* after etching of a continuous film. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is the **segregation coefficient** of a dopant? Why is its value important in Czochralski silicon growth?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The segregation coefficient $k_0 = C_s / C_l$ is the ratio of dopant concentration in the solidifying crystal to that in the adjacent melt at equilibrium. It is important in CZ growth because $k_0 < 1$ for most common dopants (P: 0.35, B: 0.80), meaning dopant is continuously rejected into the melt as the ingot grows. This causes axial and radial non-uniformity in doping along the ingot, described quantitatively by the Scheil equation $C_s(x) = k_0 C_0 (1-x)^{k_0-1}$. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means the student picks **one of two alternatives**.

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Czochralski (CZ) method** of bulk silicon crystal growth. List any four process variables that control ingot quality. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Scheil normal-freeze equation** for axial dopant distribution. A silicon ingot is grown from a melt initially doped with $1 \times 10^{18}$ **atoms/cm³** of phosphorus. Given $k_0 = 0.35$, calculate the dopant concentration in the solid at the point where 30% of the melt has solidified. (7 marks)

**Model Answer to (a) — 7 marks:**
The Czochralski method grows a single-crystal ingot by slowly pulling a rotating seed crystal upward from a crucible of molten silicon held at 1414 °C. The process stages are: **(i)** melting the polysilicon charge in a quartz crucible under argon, **(ii)** dipping the seed (typically (100) or (111) oriented) into the melt, **(iii)** Dash necking to eliminate dislocations, **(iv)** shoulder/crown growth to enlarge the diameter, **(v)** body growth at constant pull/rotation rate, and **(vi)** tail growth to detach the ingot. (4 marks)

The four key process variables that control ingot quality are: **(1)** pull rate (v), **(2)** seed/crucible rotation rate (ω), **(3)** melt temperature gradient, and **(4)** crucible position / magnetic-field strength (in MCZ). These control diameter, dislocation density, and oxygen incorporation. (3 marks)

**Model Answer to (b) — 7 marks:**
The Scheil equation derivation begins with a mass balance on the dopant rejected by the advancing solid-liquid interface. (Full derivation as in Section 3.1.) Final result: $C_s(x) = k_0 C_0 (1-x)^{k_0-1}$. (4 marks for derivation)

**Numerical evaluation:**
$$C_s(0.30) = 0.35 \times 1 \times 10^{18} \times (0.70)^{-0.65}$$

$$\ln(0.70^{-0.65}) = -0.65 \times \ln(0.70) = -0.65 \times (-0.3567) = 0.2318$$

$$(0.70)^{-0.65} = e^{0.2318} = 1.2608$$

$$C_s(0.30) = 0.35 \times 10^{18} \times 1.2608 \approx 4.41 \times 10^{17}\,\text{atoms/cm}^3$$ (3 marks)

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **selective epitaxial growth (SEG)**. With a process-flow block diagram, describe how an epitaxial silicon island is fabricated using the additive technique on a (100) silicon wafer with a patterned $\text{SiO}_2$ mask. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is the **effective segregation coefficient**? Derive the Burton-Prim-Slichter (BPS) expression for $k_\text{eff}$ and discuss how growth velocity and boundary-layer thickness influence dopant incorporation in epitaxial layers. (7 marks)

**Model Answer to (a) — 7 marks:**
Selective epitaxial growth exploits the difference in nucleation behaviour of Si on bare Si versus on $\text{SiO}_2$/$\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$. By adding an etchant gas (HCl) to the source gas ($\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ + $\text{H}_2$) at 1000–1150 °C, the net deposition rate on Si is positive while it is zero (or negative) on the dielectric mask. (3 marks)

The process flow: (i) start with a clean (100) Si wafer, (ii) grow or deposit a $\text{SiO}_2$ mask, (iii) photolithographically pattern and BHF-etch windows, (iv) strip resist and load into the epi reactor, (v) grow epi selectively in the open windows, and (vi) optionally strip the oxide mask to leave a free-standing single-crystal Si island — the additive pattern-transfer step. (4 marks)

**Model Answer to (b) — 7 marks:**
The effective segregation coefficient $k_\text{eff}$ accounts for the steady-state dopant build-up in the boundary layer in front of the growing interface, as opposed to the equilibrium value $k_0$ at the interface itself. (1 mark)

The BPS derivation sets up a steady-state diffusion equation across the boundary layer of thickness $\delta$, balances the flux carried by the advancing interface with the diffusive flux, and yields (full derivation in Section 3.2): $k_\text{eff} = k_0 / [k_0 + (1-k_0)\exp(-v\delta/D)]$. (4 marks)

Increasing $v$ (faster growth) makes the exponential term $\to 0$, hence $k_\text{eff} \to 1$ — dopant is trapped with high efficiency. Increasing $\delta$ (poorer stirring, thicker boundary layer) drives $k_\text{eff}$ back toward $k_0$ for $v$ fixed — dopant piles up at the interface and is rejected. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Confusing equilibrium $k_0$ with effective $k_\text{eff}$** in numerical problems — for low-velocity epi growth, $k_\text{eff} \approx k_0$, but for high-velocity growth (CZ pulling) they differ significantly. Markers check whether you wrote the **right** coefficient in the Scheil equation.
> - **Forgetting the exponent sign**: $C_s(x) = k_0 C_0 (1-x)^{k_0-1}$ — students often write $(1-x)^{1-k_0}$ which gives the *inverse* profile and wrong values. (–2 marks)
> - **Mixing up CZ and FZ advantages**: CZ has *more* oxygen (good for internal gettering) but lower resistivity ceiling; FZ has *less* oxygen and higher resistivity but is expensive. (–1 mark)
> - **Skipping the mass-balance step in Scheil derivation** — full marks require the differential equation, not just the final formula.
> - **Selective Epi confusion**: SEG is *epitaxy*, not just *polysilicon deposition*. Examiners deduct marks if you claim it produces polysilicon.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Additive pattern transfer** builds up the film; subtractive removes it.
- **Selective epitaxial growth (SEG)** is the canonical KTU additive technique — Si grows only in mask openings.
- **Czochralski (CZ)**: melt + pull + rotate; produces 90% of wafers; contains O.
- **Float Zone (FZ)**: no crucible, ultra-pure, low O, high resistivity.
- **Epitaxy**: single-crystal Si grown on Si substrate; vapor-phase via $\text{SiH}_4$ or $\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2$.
- **Segregation coefficient** $k_0 = C_s/C_l$ — controls dopant distribution.
- **Scheil equation** (MUST memorize): $C_s(x) = k_0 C_0 (1-x)^{k_0-1}$.
- **BPS effective $k_\text{eff}$**: $k_\text{eff} = k_0/[k_0 + (1-k_0)\exp(-v\delta/D)]$.
- **Si lattice constant** = 5.43 Å, melting point = 1414 °C.
- **Common $k_0$ values**: B = 0.80, P = 0.35, Sb = 0.023, As = 0.30.
- **VPE chemistry**: $\text{SiH}_4 \to \text{Si} + 2\text{H}_2$ (low-T); $\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2 \to \text{Si} + 2\text{HCl}$ (high-T).
- **SEG relies on HCl** in the gas mixture to suppress nucleation on oxide.
- **Oxygen in CZ** is intentional — enables intrinsic gettering and wafer strengthening.
- **Epi thickness** = growth rate × time; growth rate is surface-reaction limited below ~1000 °C and mass-transport limited above ~1100 °C.
- **Crystal orientation matters**: (100) preferred for MEMS, (111) for high breakdown.
- **Dislocation-free Dash neck** is critical for low-defect ingots.
- **Polysilicon is *not* epi** — poly has grain boundaries; epi inherits substrate orientation.
- **Epi-poly process** combines epi over Si and poly over oxide in a single run — key for 3-D MEMS.
- **For exam**: Always state units (atoms/cm³, μm/min, °C) and identify which regime (surface-limited vs. mass-transport) the growth is in.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molecular Beam Epitaxy. Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Molecular Beam Epitaxy. Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — Molecular Beam Epitaxy & Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)** is an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) thin-film deposition technique in which directed thermal-energy beams of atoms or molecules are reacted on a heated crystalline substrate to produce **epitaxial** (single-crystal) layers with sub-nanometer thickness control. It is the gold-standard technique for fabricating MEMS hetero-structures, quantum wells, and HEMT layers used in RF-MEMS and sensor-MEMS devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)** is the umbrella term for vacuum-based thin-film processes in which a *target material* is physically converted into the vapour phase (without chemical reaction) and transported across a low-pressure gap to condense on a substrate. The two principal PVD sub-techniques relevant to the KTU 2024 MEMS syllabus are:
> 1. **Thermal Evaporation & Electron-Beam (E-beam) Evaporation**
> 2. **Sputtering** (DC, RF, Magnetron, Reactive)
>
> MBE is often classified as the most refined *epitaxial* form of PVD because it uses only physical beams under UHV.

---

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Atomic Fountain Pen":** Imagine painting a wall with individual water droplets fired from a fountain pen held one millimetre away. Each droplet is one atom. Because the droplet stream is so controlled (one beam = one element), you can paint **monochrome layers**, switch pens, and stack different colors with single-atom precision. MBE works exactly like this — but instead of water, ultra-pure atomic/molecular beams fly in straight, collision-free lines inside a chamber where the air has been removed to a pressure of about $\mathbf{10^{-10} \; Torr}$ (roughly one trillionth of atmospheric pressure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Sandblasting vs. Spray Painting":** In **PVD (sputtering)**, high-energy ions of an inert gas (typically Ar⁺) are like a *sandblaster* that knocks atoms off a target, and these ejected atoms coat the substrate like overspray. In **thermal evaporation (PVD)**, the target is *boiled* gently (like heating water in a kettle) and the vapour travels ballistically to the substrate. In **MBE**, both phenomena are combined with extreme UHV and in-situ RHEED monitoring to achieve *single-crystal* (epitaxial) growth.

---

### 1.2 Key Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance for MEMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base Pressure (MBE) | $P_{base}$ | $\mathbf{10^{-10} \; Torr}$ | Eliminates contaminant incorporation |
| Mean Free Path of beam atoms | $\lambda_{MFP}$ | $\mathbf{> 1 \; m}$ at $10^{-6}$ Torr | Ensures line-of-sight ballistic transport |
| Substrate Temperature | $T_s$ | **400 – 700 °C** | Enables surface migration & epitaxy |
| Deposition Rate (MBE) | $R_d$ | **0.1 – 1 µm/h** (≈ **1 monolayer/s**) | Atomic-layer control |
| Deposition Rate (PVD – evap.) | $R_d$ | **1 – 100 nm/s** | Faster but less controlled |
| Sticking Coefficient | $S_c$ | **0.1 – 1.0** | Fraction of incident atoms that stick |
| RHEED oscillation period | $t_{ML}$ | **1 – 3 s** | One monolayer growth time |
| Background gas purity | — | **99.9999999 %** (9N) | Sub-ppb impurity levels |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Molecular Beam Epitaxy — Operational Logic Decomposition

MBE is governed by **four sequential physical events**, all of which must happen inside a UHV chamber:

1. **Generation of Molecular Beams** — High-purity source materials (e.g., Ga, As, Al, In, Si) are heated in **Knudsen cells (K-cells)** or **effusion cells** until they evaporate at a controlled rate.
2. **Ballistic Transport** — The vapour effuses through a small orifice into the UHV chamber, forming a collimated *molecular beam*. Because $\lambda_{MFP} \gg$ chamber dimensions, atoms travel without collisions.
3. **Adsorption & Surface Migration** — Atoms impinge on a heated single-crystal substrate (e.g., GaAs, Si). They either:
   - Chemisorb at energetically favourable lattice sites (epitaxial sites)
   - Migrate across the surface (driven by adatom diffusion)
   - Desorb back into vacuum (if $T_s$ too high)
4. **Incorporation into the Crystal Lattice** — Atoms bond to dangling bonds on the substrate, extending the crystal layer-by-layer. **Reflection High-Energy Electron Diffraction (RHEED)** monitors this growth *in real time* by observing oscillations in diffraction intensity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why UHV?** At $10^{-10}$ Torr, the time for a monolayer of residual gas to adsorb on a clean surface is ≈ **1000 seconds (≈ 17 minutes)**. This is longer than the deposition of one monolayer (≈ 1 s), so contamination is negligible. At atmospheric pressure, that monolayer would form in **1 nanosecond**, ruining the crystal.

---

## 2.2 Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) — Operational Logic

PVD encompasses all vacuum thin-film processes using **purely physical vapour transport**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises three sub-categories:

### 2.2.1 Thermal Evaporation
- A resistively-heated **tungsten filament** or **crucible** (BN, Mo, Al₂O₃) melts the source.
- Vapour pressure of the source material follows the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation**.
- Suitable for low-melting-point metals (Al, Au, Ag) — not for refractory materials (W, Ta, ceramics).

### 2.2.2 Electron-Beam (E-beam) Evaporation
- A focused electron beam (5 – 30 kV, ≈ 1 A) is deflected (typically 270°) onto the target.
- Localised power density $> 10^{6} \; W/cm^2$ evaporates even W, Mo, Al₂O₃.
- A **water-cooled copper hearth** holds the source — only the top surface melts, avoiding contamination.

### 2.2.3 Sputtering
- Plasma (Ar⁺ ions, $P \approx 10^{-3}$ Torr) is generated by DC, RF, or magnetron excitation.
- Ions bombard the **target** (cathode), ejecting atoms via momentum transfer (**sputtering yield** $Y$).
- Ejected atoms condense on the substrate placed opposite the target.
- **Magnetron sputtering** uses crossed E × B fields to confine electrons, increasing plasma density and deposition rate.
- **Reactive sputtering** introduces O₂ or N₂ to form compound films (Al₂O₃, TiN, SiO₂).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready**. Pay special attention to units — KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for inconsistent units.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda_{MFP} \;=\; \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d^{2} \, P}$ | Mean free path of gas atoms (Knudsen) | **m** |
| 2 | $v_{rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{3 k_B T}{m}}$ | RMS thermal velocity of beam atoms | **m/s** |
| 3 | $\Phi \;=\; \dfrac{N_A \, P \, A_h}{2 \pi m k_B T}$ | Flux from a Knudsen cell (Hertz–Knudsen) | **atoms / (m²·s)** |
| 4 | $R_d \;=\; \dfrac{\Phi \cdot M}{\rho \, N_A}$ | Deposition rate from beam flux | **nm/s** or **µm/h** |
| 5 | $Y \;=\; \dfrac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | Sputtering yield (dimensionless) | **atoms/ion** |
| 6 | $S_c \;=\; \dfrac{N_{ads}}{N_{inc}}$ | Sticking coefficient (Langmuir) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $t_{ML} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\Phi \cdot S_c}$ | Time to grow 1 monolayer | **s** |
| 8 | $\tau_{imp} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\nu_0 \, \exp(-E_a / k_B T)}$ | Adatom hopping time (Arrhenius) | **s** |
| 9 | $R_{Hkl} \;\propto\; \vert F_{Hkl}\vert^2$ | RHEED intensity (kinematic) | a.u. |
| 10 | $P_{vap}(T) \;=\; P_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T}\right)$ | Vapour pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron) | **Pa** or **Torr** |

> Where:
> - $k_B$ = **Boltzmann constant = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
> - $N_A$ = **Avogadro's number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol**
> - $d$ = effective molecular diameter ≈ **0.3 nm** for N₂-like gases
> - $A_h$ = Knudsen-cell orifice area (m²)
> - $m$ = atomic mass of evaporant (kg)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Technique Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| GaAs/AlGaAs HEMTs for RF-MEMS | **MBE** | Atomically-sharp heterojunctions needed |
| MEMS metallic mirrors (Au, Al) | **Thermal / E-beam evap.** | High purity, low cost |
| MEMS piezoelectric AlN films | **Reactive sputtering** | Conformal, low-temperature growth |
| MEMS wear-resistant TiN coatings | **Reactive magnetron sputtering** | Hard, dense, stoichiometric |
| Micro-heaters, strain gauges (Pt) | **Sputtering** | Better step coverage than evaporation |
| SAW/FBAR resonators (ZnO, AlN) | **MBE / Sputtering** | C-axis texture required |
| CMOS-MEMS integration | **MBE / LPCVD** | Compatibility with Si process flow |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Mean Free Path of a Beam Atom in MBE

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show why MBE requires $\lambda_{MFP} \gg$ chamber size ($L \approx 0.3 - 1$ m).

Consider a beam atom of diameter $d$ moving through a background gas of identical atoms at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$. The mean free path is the average distance travelled between successive collisions.

**Step 1 — Number density of background gas from ideal-gas law:**

$$n \;=\; \dfrac{P}{k_B T}$$

**Step 2 — Collision cross-section:**

A collision occurs whenever the centres of two atoms come within $d$. The effective swept area is:

$$\sigma \;=\; \pi d^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Mean free path (geometric argument):**

In one second, the atom sweeps a cylindrical volume $V = \sigma \, v_{rel}$, where $v_{rel}$ is the relative speed. For identical atoms in thermal equilibrium, $\langle v_{rel} \rangle = \sqrt{2}\, v_{rms} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{3 k_B T / m}$. The number of collisions per second is $n \sigma v_{rel}$.

The mean distance between collisions is therefore:

$$\lambda_{MFP} \;=\; \dfrac{v_{rel}}{n \, \sigma \, v_{rel}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n \, \sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $n = P / k_B T$ and $\sigma = \pi d^2$:**

$$\lambda_{MFP} \;=\; \dfrac{k_B T}{\pi d^{2} \, P} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{2})} \;=\; \boxed{\;\dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi\, d^{2}\, P}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for MBE chamber:**

Take $T = 300$ K, $d = 3 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $P = 10^{-10}$ Torr $= 1.33 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa.

$$\lambda_{MFP} \;=\; \dfrac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi\, (3 \times 10^{-10})^{2}\,(1.33 \times 10^{-8})}$$

$$\lambda_{MFP} \;\approx\; \dfrac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{5.32 \times 10^{-27}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{7.8 \times 10^{5}\; m} \;=\; \mathbf{780\; km}$$

This is **enormously larger** than the chamber ($L \approx 0.5$ m), so beam atoms travel without collisions — true *ballistic transport*. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Flux from a Knudsen Cell

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Relate the evaporation rate inside a Knudsen cell to the molecular flux arriving at the substrate.

A Knudsen cell is a crucible with a small orifice of area $A_h$ whose diameter $\ll \lambda_{MFP}$ inside the cell. Vapour inside reaches thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$ with number density $n = P_{vap}(T) / k_B T$.

**Step 1 — Vapour pressure from Clausius–Clapeyron (assuming $T$ near $T_0$):**

$$P_{vap}(T) \;=\; P_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Particles escaping through the orifice per second:**

The number of molecules striking a unit area of the wall per second in thermal equilibrium is $\tfrac{1}{4} n \bar{v}$, where $\bar{v} = \sqrt{8 k_B T / (\pi m)}$ is the **mean speed** (Maxwell–Boltzmann). For a small orifice, only the outward-going molecules escape.

**Step 3 — Effusion rate:**

$$Z_{cell} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{4}\, n\, \bar{v}\, A_h \;=\; \dfrac{P_{vap}\, A_h}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$$

Simplifying using $\tfrac{1}{4} \cdot \sqrt{8/\pi} = \sqrt{1/(2\pi)}$:

$$Z_{cell} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{vap}\, A_h}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

**Step 4 — Flux at substrate distance $L$:**

Assuming the orifice is small (point source) and the substrate of area $A_s$ is at distance $L$ along the beam axis:

$$\Phi_{sub} \;=\; \dfrac{Z_{cell}}{\pi L^{2}} \;=\; \boxed{\;\dfrac{P_{vap}\, A_h}{\pi^{2}\, L^{2}\, \sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Compute deposition rate:**

For element of atomic mass $M$ and film density $\rho$:

$$R_d \;=\; \dfrac{\Phi_{sub}\, M}{N_A\, \rho}$$

Units: $\Phi$ in atoms/m²·s × kg/atom ÷ (atoms/mol × kg/m³) = **m/s**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Time for One Monolayer (RHEED Oscillation Period)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show why RHEED intensity oscillates with period equal to the time to grow 1 monolayer.

**Step 1 — Surface atomic density of substrate (GaAs (100)):**

For GaAs (100), there are $N_s = 6.26 \times 10^{18}$ atoms/m² per monolayer.

**Step 2 — Monolayer growth time:**

$$t_{ML} \;=\; \dfrac{N_s}{\Phi_{sub} \cdot S_c}$$

If $\Phi_{sub} = 6.26 \times 10^{18}$ atoms/m²/s and $S_c = 1$, then $t_{ML} = 1$ s. ✓

**Step 3 — Physical origin of oscillation:**

RHEED intensity is maximum when the surface is **smooth (2D layer complete)** — constructive interference. It is minimum when the surface is at **half-coverage (maximum step density)** — diffuse scattering. Hence one full intensity cycle = one monolayer.

$$I_{RHEED}(t) \;\propto\; 1 + A \cos(2 \pi t / t_{ML})$$

This real-time feedback allows **automated shutter control** with sub-monolayer precision — the key feature distinguishing MBE from conventional PVD.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Sputtering Yield (Energy-Momentum Balance)

For an incident ion of mass $m_i$ and energy $E$ hitting a target atom of mass $m_t$ at rest:

**Step 1 — Maximum transferable energy (head-on collision):**

$$T_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{4 m_i m_t}{(m_i + m_t)^{2}}\, E$$

**Step 2 — Sputtering threshold energy (typically $\approx 20 - 50$ eV):**

$$E_{th} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\Delta H_{sub}}{\gamma (1 - \gamma)} \quad ; \quad \gamma \;=\; \dfrac{4 m_i m_t}{(m_i + m_t)^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Empirical yield formula (Yamamura–Matsunami):**

$$Y(E) \;=\; 0.042 \,\dfrac{\alpha^{*}\, Q^{*}\, (E / E_{th})^{0.5}}{1 + \alpha^{*}\, (E / E_{th})^{0.5}} \cdot (1 - (E_{th}/E)^{1/2})$$

For Ar⁺ on Cu at 500 eV: $Y \approx 2.3$ atoms/ion.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Property | MBE | Thermal Evap. | E-beam Evap. | Sputtering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Base Pressure (Torr) | $10^{-10}$ – $10^{-11}$ | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-7}$ | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-7}$ | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-8}$ |
| Dep. Rate (nm/s) | 0.1 – 1 | 1 – 50 | 0.1 – 100 | 0.1 – 10 |
| Epitaxy possible? | **Yes** | No | No | No (poly/amorphous) |
| Substrate Temp. (°C) | 400 – 700 | 25 – 200 | 25 – 300 | 25 – 500 |
| Step Coverage | Poor (line-of-sight) | Poor | Poor | **Excellent (conformal)** |
| In-situ monitoring | RHEED, RGA, pyrom. | QCM | QCM, RGA | QCM, plasma em. |
| Source materials | High m.p. OK | Low m.p. only | Refractories OK | Metals, oxides, nitrides |
| Compound stoichiometry | **Excellent** | Poor | Fair | Good (with reactive mode) |
| Cost / Complexity | **Very High** | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Typical MEMS use | HEMT, QW | Electrodes | Refractory mirrors | Wear coatings, dielectrics |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MBE System

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *functional* layout of an MBE system (physical schematic would require mechanical drawing tools; this block diagram captures all critical interactions and signal/data flow per KTU functional-mapping practice).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UHV_Chamber["UHV Chamber - Pressure 1e-10 Torr"]
        K1["Knudsen Cell 1 - Ga"]
        K2["Knudsen Cell 2 - As"]
        K3["Knudsen Cell 3 - Al"]
        K4["Knudsen Cell 4 - In"]
        SH1["Shutter 1 - Ga"]
        SH2["Shutter 2 - As"]
        SH3["Shutter 3 - Al"]
        SH4["Shutter 4 - In"]
        SUB["Heated Substrate Holder - 400 to 700 C"]
        RHEED["RHEED Gun and Phosphor Screen"]
        QCM["Quartz Crystal Monitor"]
        RGA["Residual Gas Analyser"]
        PYRO["Optical Pyrometer"]
    end
    L["Ion Pump and Ti Sublimation Pump"]
    LN2["LN2 Cryo-Shroud"]
    CTRL["Computer Control - RHEED Feedback Loop"]
    K1 --> SH1 --> SUB
    K2 --> SH2 --> SUB
    K3 --> SH3 --> SUB
    K4 --> SH4 --> SUB
    RHEED -. RHEED oscillations .-> SUB
    RHEED -. Intensity signal .-> CTRL
    CTRL -. Open/Close .-> SH1
    CTRL -. Open/Close .-> SH2
    CTRL -. Open/Close .-> SH3
    CTRL -. Open/Close .-> SH4
    QCM --> CTRL
    RGA --> CTRL
    PYRO --> CTRL
    UHV_Chamber --- L
    UHV_Chamber --- LN2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Functional Insight for KTU:** The RHEED-to-shutter feedback loop (dashed arrows) is the *signature feature* of MBE. RHEED intensity oscillates with period = one monolayer, so the computer opens/closes shutters at integer multiples of this period for atomic-layer precision.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic PVD System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Vacuum Chamber - 1e-3 to 1e-7 Torr"]
    B["Vapour Source - Filament, E-beam, or Sputter Target"]
    C["Power Supply - DC, RF, or Magnetron"]
    D["Plasma / Vapour Cloud - Ar or evaporated species"]
    E["Substrate - Heated or Cooled"]
    F["Quartz Crystal Monitor - Rate Sensor"]
    G["Shutter"]
    H["Pumping System - Cryo or Diffusion"]
    I["Computer Control - Thickness Endpoint"]
    B -->|Heat or Ion Bombardment| D
    C --> B
    D -->|Ballistic Transport| E
    F -->|Rate Feedback| I
    G --> I
    I --> B
    I --> C
    H --> A
    E --> F
```

---

## 4.3 RHEED Oscillation Sequence (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Beam as Molecular Beam
    participant Surf as Substrate Surface
    participant RHEED as RHEED Detector
    participant CTRL as Controller
    Beam->>Surf: Adsorb atoms - Layer 0.5 ML
    Surf-->>RHEED: Maximum step density
    RHEED->>CTRL: Intensity = MIN
    Beam->>Surf: Complete 1.0 ML
    Surf-->>RHEED: Smooth 2D terrace
    RHEED->>CTRL: Intensity = MAX
    CTRL->>Beam: Continue / Close shutter at next MIN
```
