# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Students often confuse **MBE with CVD** — remember MBE uses *physical* beams only, no chemical reaction in the gas phase.
> 2. Forgetting the **$\sqrt{2}$** factor in the mean free path formula costs 1 mark.
> 3. Writing $P$ in Torr without converting to **Pa** in $\lambda_{MFP}$ derivation leads to wrong numerical answers (factor of $133.3$ error).
> 4. Confusing **sticking coefficient** ($S_c$) with **incorporation coefficient** — they differ for compound semiconductors.
> 5. Stating MBE works at atmospheric pressure is **wrong** — always $10^{-10}$ Torr.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) and Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) with respect to (i) operating pressure, (ii) vapour-generation mechanism, and (iii) film crystallinity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PVD | CVD |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Pressure | High vacuum, $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-8}$ Torr | Medium, 1 – 760 Torr |
| (ii) Vapour generation | Physical — thermal/E-beam/sputter | Chemical reaction of precursor gases |
| (iii) Film crystallinity | Usually polycrystalline (epitaxial only in MBE) | Can be epitaxial at high $T$ |

Hence PVD operates by *physical* vapour transport (no chemical reaction), while CVD relies on **decomposition/reduction** of volatile precursors at the substrate. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Explain the role of RHEED in MBE. Why is it called "in-situ" monitoring?**

**Model Answer:**
RHEED (Reflection High-Energy Electron Diffraction) fires a high-energy electron beam (5 – 15 keV) at a grazing angle onto the substrate during growth. The reflected diffraction pattern on a phosphor screen reveals:
- Surface reconstruction (streaks vs. spots).
- Growth mode (2D layer-by-layer → oscillations).
- **RHEED oscillations** = intensity vs. time, where each cycle corresponds to one monolayer of deposition.

It is called **in-situ** because the measurement is performed *inside* the growth chamber, in real time, *without breaking vacuum*. This is the key advantage that allows MBE to achieve sub-monolayer thickness control and atomic-layer precision — far superior to ex-situ techniques like XRD or TEM. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working principle of a Molecular Beam Epitaxy system with a neat functional diagram. Explain why ultra-high vacuum is essential.

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable derivation, obtain an expression for the mean free path of atoms in an MBE chamber. Calculate $\lambda_{MFP}$ at $T = 300$ K and $P = 10^{-10}$ Torr for nitrogen-like background gas ($d = 0.3$ nm).

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A (a)**

**Construction:**
An MBE system consists of:

1. **UHV Chamber** — Stainless-steel (304L/316LN), bakeable to 200 °C, base pressure $\approx 10^{-10}$ – $10^{-11}$ Torr.
2. **Multiple Knudsen Cells (K-cells)** — PBN or graphite crucibles resistively heated to evaporate source elements (e.g., Ga at 950 °C, As at 300 °C). Each cell has a thermocouple and a pneumatically operated **shutter**.
3. **Substrate Manipulator** — Heated (radiative or resistive), rotates continuously for uniform flux. Holds 2" – 6" wafers.
4. **RHEED System** — Electron gun (5 – 15 keV) + phosphor screen; positioned to give grazing-incidence diffraction pattern.
5. **In-situ Diagnostics** — Quartz Crystal Monitor (QCM), Residual Gas Analyser (RGA), optical pyrometer, ellipsometer.
6. **Pumping System** — Ion pump + Ti sublimation pump + LN₂ cryo-shroud + load-lock chamber for sample entry without venting the main chamber.
7. **Computer Control** — Automated shutter sequencing using RHEED oscillation feedback.

**Working Principle (4 stages):**
- Stage 1 — **Effusion:** Source atoms evaporate in K-cells and effuse through small orifices into the UHV chamber as **collimated molecular beams** (Knudsen flow).
- Stage 2 — **Ballistic transport:** Because $\lambda_{MFP} \gg$ chamber dimensions (see derivation in 3.1), beams travel in straight lines without collisions.
- Stage 3 — **Adsorption & Surface Migration:** Atoms land on the heated substrate and migrate via adatom hopping to find epitaxial sites (driven by $k_B T_s$).
- Stage 4 — **Incorporation:** Atoms bond to the crystal lattice, extending it layer-by-layer. RHEED monitors the surface smoothness in real time.

**Why UHV is essential (3 reasons):**
1. **Eliminates collisions** — ensures $\lambda_{MFP} \gg L$, giving pure directional beams.
2. **Suppresses contamination** — monolayer formation time at $10^{-10}$ Torr is ≈ 1000 s, longer than growth of 1 ML (≈ 1 s).
3. **Enables in-situ diagnostics** — RHEED requires a clean, mean-free-path environment for electrons to reach the detector without scattering by residual gas.

**(Total: 7 marks — 3 marks construction, 2 marks principle, 2 marks UHV justification)**

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A (b)**

The derivation is identical to that presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Re-state concisely:

$$\boxed{\;\lambda_{MFP} \;=\; \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi\, d^{2}\, P}\;}$$

**Numerical calculation:**

Given: $T = 300$ K, $d = 3 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $P = 10^{-10}$ Torr $= 1.333 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa.

$$\lambda_{MFP} \;=\; \dfrac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi\, (3 \times 10^{-10})^{2}\, (1.333 \times 10^{-8})}$$

**Numerator:** $4.143 \times 10^{-21}$

**Denominator:** $\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot 9 \times 10^{-20} \cdot 1.333 \times 10^{-8} = 5.32 \times 10^{-27}$

$$\lambda_{MFP} \;=\; \dfrac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{5.32 \times 10^{-27}} \;\approx\; 7.8 \times 10^{5}\; \text{m} \;=\; \mathbf{780\; km}$$

This is **vastly greater** than the MBE chamber length ($L \approx 0.5$ m), confirming ballistic transport. ✓

**(Total: 7 marks — 3 marks derivation, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks numerical result & interpretation)**

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
#### **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the working principle of an Electron-Beam (E-beam) evaporation system used for PVD. List its advantages over thermal evaporation.

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Define sputtering yield. Derive the expression for maximum energy transferable from an incident ion to a target atom in a sputtering event. For Ar⁺ ions bombarding a Cu target, with Ar mass = 40 amu and Cu mass = 63.5 amu, calculate the maximum fraction of energy transferred when Ar⁺ energy = 500 eV.

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B (a)**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.2 PVD block diagram):**

E-beam evaporation uses:
1. **Electron gun** — Tungsten filament emitting electrons (thermionic emission).
2. **High-voltage anode** (5 – 30 kV) accelerating and focusing the e-beam.
3. **Magnetic deflector** (typically 270° bend) — steers the beam onto the source material, preventing direct heating of the substrate by stray electrons.
4. **Water-cooled copper hearth/crucible** — holds source pellets (4 – 6 pockets for multi-source).
5. **Substrate holder** — placed above at a distance $L$ ≈ 30 – 60 cm, may rotate for uniformity.
6. **Quartz crystal monitor + shutter** — for rate control and thickness endpoint.
7. **Vacuum system** — diffusion pump + LN₂ trap, base pressure $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-7}$ Torr.

**Working Principle:**
A high current (≈ 0.5 – 1 A) of electrons emitted from a tungsten filament is accelerated through 5 – 30 kV. The beam is bent by a magnetic field through 270° and focused onto the top surface of the source material in the water-cooled crucible. Localised power density ($> 10^{6}$ W/cm²) rapidly melts and evaporates a small spot of the material. The vapour travels ballistically to the substrate, where it condenses to form a thin film.

**Advantages over thermal evaporation (any 4):**
1. Can evaporate **refractory materials** (W, Mo, Ta, SiO₂, Al₂O₃) — impossible in thermal evap.
2. Higher deposition rates (up to 100 nm/s).
3. **Water-cooled hearth** prevents source contamination from crucible outgassing — yields ultra-pure films.
4. Multiple sources can be co-evaporated (e.g., Al₂O₃ via reactive e-beam with O₂).
5. Better film adhesion due to higher kinetic energy of evaporant atoms.

**(Total: 7 marks — 2 marks construction, 3 marks working, 2 marks advantages)**

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B (b)**

**Sputtering yield definition:** $Y$ = number of target atoms ejected per incident ion (dimensionless, typically 0.5 – 5 atoms/ion).

**Derivation of maximum transferable energy:**

Consider an ion of mass $m_i$ moving with velocity $v_0$ colliding *head-on* (elastic) with a target atom of mass $m_t$ initially at rest. Using conservation of momentum and kinetic energy:

**Step 1 — Momentum conservation:**
$$m_i v_0 \;=\; m_i v_1 + m_t v_2$$

**Step 2 — Energy conservation:**
$$\tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_0^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} m_t v_2^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $v_2$** (recoil velocity of target atom):

From (1): $v_1 = v_0 - (m_t / m_i) v_2$. Substitute in (2):

$$\tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_0^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} m_i \!\left[v_0 - \tfrac{m_t}{m_i} v_2\right]^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} m_t v_2^{2}$$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$0 \;=\; -m_t v_0 v_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \tfrac{m_t^{2}}{m_i} v_2^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} m_t v_2^{2}$$

$$0 \;=\; -m_t v_0 + \tfrac{1}{2} \tfrac{m_t^{2}}{m_i} v_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_t v_2$$

$$v_2 \;=\; \dfrac{2 m_i}{m_i + m_t}\, v_0$$

**Step 4 — Maximum energy transferred to target:**

$$T_{max} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} m_t v_2^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} m_t \!\left(\dfrac{2 m_i v_0}{m_i + m_t}\right)^{2}$$

$$T_{max} \;=\; \boxed{\;\dfrac{4 m_i m_t}{(m_i + m_t)^{2}}\, E\;}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical calculation:**

Given: $m_i = 40$ amu (Ar), $m_t = 63.5$ amu (Cu), $E = 500$ eV.

$$\dfrac{T_{max}}{E} \;=\; \dfrac{4 (40)(63.5)}{(40 + 63.5)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{10160}{(103.5)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{10160}{10712} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.948}$$

$$T_{max} \;=\; 0.948 \times 500 \;=\; \mathbf{474 \; eV}$$

This high recoil energy explains why sputtering can deposit dense, well-adhered films — the ejected atoms arrive with high kinetic energy (typically 5 – 50 eV).

**(Total: 7 marks — 2 marks definition, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks numerical)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — MBE & PVD**

- **MBE is the *epitaxial* PVD technique** operating at $10^{-10}$ Torr with Knudsen-cell-generated molecular beams.
- **UHV is essential** for (i) ballistic transport ($\lambda_{MFP} \gg L$) and (ii) low contamination (monolayer formation time ≈ 1000 s).
- **Mean Free Path formula:** $\lambda_{MFP} = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi\, d^{2}\, P}$ — remember the $\sqrt{2}$.
- **Knudsen-cell flux:** $\Phi = \dfrac{P_{vap} A_h}{\pi^2 L^2 \sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$
- **RHEED oscillations** = real-time monolayer counting; period ≈ 1 s at 1 µm/h.
- **Sticking coefficient** $S_c$ depends on substrate temperature and surface chemistry.
- **PVD sub-types:** thermal evap. (low m.p.), e-beam evap. (refractories), sputtering (best step coverage).
- **Sputtering yield** $Y$ is typically 0.5 – 5; depends on ion mass, energy, and target binding energy.
- **Maximum transferable energy** in elastic ion-atom collision: $T_{max} = \dfrac{4 m_i m_t}{(m_i + m_t)^2}\, E$.
- **Best step coverage** → use **sputtering**; **best epitaxy** → use **MBE**; **highest purity metals** → use **e-beam evaporation**.
- **MBE typical parameters:** $R_d = 0.1$ – $1$ µm/h, $T_s = 400$ – $700$ °C.
- **Typical MEMS materials:** Au/Al (electrodes — evaporation), AlN/ZnO (piezo — sputtering/MBE), TiN (wear coatings — reactive sputtering), GaAs/AlGaAs (RF-MEMS HEMT — MBE).
- **Common mistakes:** confusing MBE with CVD; using pressure in Torr without conversion; writing $\lambda_{MFP}$ without $\sqrt{2}$; stating sputtering works at UHV (it works at $10^{-3}$ Torr with plasma!).
- **Always quote units** — KTU examiners deduct marks silently for missing units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sputtering - Laser Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sputtering - Laser Deposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Thin-Film Deposition Techniques for MEMS

## 1.1 Sputtering — Core Technical Definition

**Sputtering** is a Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) process in which energetic ions (typically inert **Argon (Ar⁺)**) generated within a glow-discharge plasma are accelerated toward a solid target (the *cathode*). Through momentum transfer, atoms of the target are physically ejected from its surface and travel through the low-pressure chamber to condense onto a substrate, forming a thin film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):**
> *Sputtering is a non-thermal vaporization process where surface atoms of a target are ejected by momentum transfer from bombarding energetic ions, rather than by thermal evaporation.*

### 1.2 Laser Deposition (Pulsed Laser Deposition, PLD) — Core Technical Definition

**Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD)** is a PVD technique in which a high-power pulsed laser beam is focused onto a solid target inside a vacuum chamber. The intense photon energy ablates the target surface, generating a transient, highly-energetic plasma plume composed of ions, atoms, molecules, and clusters, which expands toward a heated substrate and condenses as a thin film.

> [!NOTE]
> The **threshold fluence** for ablation in PLD typically lies between **1–5 J/cm²** for most solid targets when using an excimer laser (KrF, 248 nm).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

#### Sputtering — The "Billiards Analogy" 🎱

Imagine a billiard table where the **white cue ball (Ar⁺ ion)** is fired at a **tightly packed rack of balls (target atoms)**. The cue ball doesn't heat the rack; it just hits hard enough to scatter the colored balls in every direction. Some of those scattered balls roll onto a nearby **cloth surface (substrate)** and stick there — that's your thin film. No melting, just pure mechanical momentum transfer.

#### Laser Deposition (PLD) — The "Lightning Analogy" ⚡

Now picture aiming an extraordinarily concentrated beam of light (the laser) onto a piece of rock. The rock doesn't slowly melt — it violently **explodes** into a glowing cloud of vapor (the *plasma plume*). This luminous cloud races across the chamber and rains down onto a nearby platform (the substrate), where it solidifies into a perfect replica layer. That glowing vapor is what we call the **plume**, and it preserves the exact chemical composition of the original rock.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for KTU exam:** Sputtering = *momentum-knock-out*; PLD = *photon-explosion-deposition*.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range (MEMS context) |
|---|---|
| Base pressure (sputtering) | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$ **Torr** |
| Working pressure (Ar) | 1 to 30 **mTorr** |
| Target–substrate distance | 5 to 15 **cm** |
| Laser fluence (PLD) | 1 to 5 **J/cm²** |
| Substrate temperature (PLD) | 400 to 800 **°C** |
| Laser pulse duration | 10 to 30 **ns** (nanosecond PLD) |

---

### 1.5 Where These Techniques Sit in MEMS

Both sputtering and PLD are pillar fabrication processes for MEMS devices because they can deposit:

- **Metallic electrodes** (Au, Pt, Al, Ti)
- **Dielectric layers** (SiO₂, Si₃N₄, Al₂O₃)
- **Piezoelectric films** (PZT, AlN, ZnO)
- **Complex oxides** (PLD specialty: YBCO, BST, LaSrMnO)
- **Multilayer heterostructures**

> [!WARNING]
> Sputtering is the **industry workhorse** (high uniformity, large-area wafers). PLD is the **research favorite** (complex stoichiometries, oxide superlattices).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sputtering — Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 The Five Sequential Phases

1. **Plasma Generation** — A high voltage applied between cathode (target) and anode (chamber/substrate) ionizes the inert Ar gas, creating a glow-discharge plasma. Free electrons collide with Ar atoms:
   $$\text{Ar} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ar}^+ + 2e^-$$
2. **Ion Bombardment** — Positive Ar⁺ ions are accelerated across the dark-space (cathode sheath) toward the negatively-biased target.
3. **Momentum Transfer Cascade** — Incident ions transfer kinetic energy to target atoms through a *collision cascade*. If momentum is directed outward (toward surface), atoms can be ejected.
4. **Ejection (Sputtering)** — Atoms with sufficient energy overcome surface binding and leave the target. Ejected atoms are mostly **neutral** (≈ 95 %) with energies of 5–50 eV.
5. **Transport & Nucleation** — Ejected atoms travel ballistically through the chamber, adsorb onto the substrate, migrate, and nucleate into a continuous thin film.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why low pressure?** Mean free path must exceed target–substrate distance to avoid gas-phase scattering that contaminates or randomizes the flux.

---

### 2.1.2 Key Sputtering Variants (KTU-Favorite)

| Variant | Drive Signal | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Diode Sputtering** | DC voltage | Conducting targets only |
| **RF Sputtering** | 13.56 MHz AC | Insulating targets (Al₂O₃, SiO₂) |
| **Magnetron Sputtering** | Magnetic field + E-field | Higher deposition rate, low pressure |
| **Reactive Sputtering** | Reactive gas (O₂, N₂) added | Oxide / nitride films |
| **Ion-Beam Sputtering** | Independent ion source | Ultra-clean, low-pressure films |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> RF sputtering overcomes the **charge build-up problem** on insulating targets by alternating the bias each half-cycle.

---

### 2.1.3 Sputtering Yield — The Central Quantity

The **sputtering yield** $S$ is defined as:

$$S = \frac{\text{Number of target atoms ejected}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$$

Empirically, $S$ depends on:
- Mass ratio $M_2/M_1$ (target atom / incident ion)
- Incident ion energy $E$
- Angle of incidence $\theta$
- Target surface binding energy $U_s$

---

## 2.2 Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD) — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 The Six-Phase PLD Cycle

1. **Laser Absorption** — UV pulsed laser (e.g., KrF, 248 nm) is absorbed in a shallow optical skin depth (~10–100 nm).
2. **Surface Heating & Melting** — Energy density converts to thermal energy.
3. **Ablation** — Material is ejected in a forward-directed plume when local temperature exceeds vaporization.
4. **Plume Formation** — Vapor absorbs more laser photons and ionizes → luminous plasma plume (Knudsen layer + isothermal expansion).
5. **Plume Propagation** — Plume expands adiabatically toward the substrate (forward-peaked, cosⁿ θ distribution).
6. **Film Growth** — Nucleation and layer-by-layer growth on heated substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> The **congruent transfer** property of PLD (preservation of target stoichiometry in the film) is its biggest advantage over sputtering.

---

### 2.2.2 Threshold Fluence & Ablation Mechanism

There exists a minimum laser fluence $\Phi_t$ below which ablation does not occur. Above this:

$$\Phi_t \approx \frac{\rho \, L_v \, \ell}{\alpha}$$

where $\rho$ is target density, $L_v$ latent heat of vaporization, $\ell$ optical absorption depth, $\alpha$ absorption coefficient.

---

### 2.2.3 Plume Expansion Dynamics

For an adiabatic plume expanding into vacuum, the front-position $R(t)$ follows the **drag model**:

$$R(t) = R_0 \left[1 + \frac{t}{\tau}\right]^{1/5}$$

where $R_0$ is initial plume length and $\tau$ is a characteristic timescale. Alternatively, in the **blast-wave (Sedov–Taylor) model**:

$$R(t) \propto t^{2/5}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = N_{\text{ejected}} / N_{\text{ion}}$ | Sputtering yield | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Phi_t = \rho L_v \ell / \alpha$ | PLD threshold fluence | J/cm² |
| 3 | $p_d = 4 \pi m k_B T$ | Gas pressure (ideal gas) | Pa |
| 4 | $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 p)$ | Mean free path of gas molecules | m |
| 5 | $R(t) = R_0 (1 + t/\tau)^{1/5}$ | Drago-modeled plume radius | m |
| 6 | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of sputtered atom | eV |
| 7 | $v_{\text{sub}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$ | Mean thermal speed | m/s |
| 8 | $R_d = \frac{S \cdot J_{\text{ion}} \cdot M_{\text{target}}}{\rho_{\text{film}} \cdot N_A}$ | Deposition rate | nm/s |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{film}}}{C_{\text{target}}}$ | PLD stoichiometric transfer ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $E_{\text{inc}} = e V_{\text{cathode}}$ | Energy of Ar⁺ ion striking target | eV |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numericals, always quote $S$ in the **0.1–10** range (typical for Ar⁺ at 500 eV on most metals). Use $V_{\text{cathode}}$ in volts to directly get incident ion energy in eV because $E = eV$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Semiconductor ICs | Al/Cu interconnect deposition by sputtering |
| Hard-disk drives | Sputtered magnetic CoCrPt layers |
| Solar cells | TCO (ITO) sputtering, CIGS by PLD |
| MEMS microphones | AlN piezoelectric by reactive sputtering |
| Superconducting electronics | YBCO films by PLD |
| Microfluidics | SiO₂ / Si₃N₄ dielectric windows |
| SAW / BAW filters | ZnO, AlN by sputtering |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Mean Free Path of Sputtering Gas

We want to derive the mean free path $\lambda$ of an Ar atom in an Ar atmosphere at working pressure $p$.

### Step 1: Number density of gas molecules

From ideal gas law $p = n k_B T$:

$$n = \frac{p}{k_B T}$$

For $T = 300\ \text{K}$, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$, $p = 10\ \text{mTorr} = 1.333\ \text{Pa}$:

$$n = \frac{1.333}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}$$

$$n = \frac{1.333}{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}$$

$$n = 3.22 \times 10^{20}\ \text{molecules/m}^3$$

### Step 2: Hard-sphere collision cross-section

Diameter of Ar atom $d \approx 3.66 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$:

$$\sigma = \pi d^2 = \pi (3.66 \times 10^{-10})^2$$

$$\sigma = \pi \times 1.339 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$\sigma = 4.21 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 3: Mean free path formula

Relative speed of two Ar atoms from kinetic theory:

$$\bar{v}_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{2}\ \bar{v} = \sqrt{\frac{16 k_B T}{\pi m}}$$

So:

$$\lambda = \frac{\bar{v}_{\text{rel}}}{n \sigma \bar{v}_{\text{rel}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\, n\, \sigma}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\, \pi\, d^2\, p}}$$

### Step 4: Numerical value at 10 mTorr, 300 K

$$\lambda = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{\sqrt{2} \times \pi \times (3.66 \times 10^{-10})^2 \times 1.333}$$

Numerator: $4.14 \times 10^{-21}$

Denominator: $1.414 \times 3.1416 \times 1.339 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.333 = 7.92 \times 10^{-19}$

$$\lambda = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{7.92 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 5.23 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \approx 5.2\ \text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since the target–substrate distance is typically **5–15 cm**, we need pressure ≤ 10 mTorr to ensure ballistic transport of sputtered atoms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Energy of Bombarding Argon Ion

In DC sputtering, Ar⁺ ion falls across the cathode dark-space potential $V_c$.

By energy conservation for a singly-charged ion:

$$E_{\text{ion}} = q \cdot V_c = e \cdot V_c$$

If $V_c = -500\ \text{V}$ (cathode negative):

$$\boxed{E_{\text{ion}} = e \times 500\ \text{V} = 500\ \text{eV}}$$

When this ion strikes the target, its energy is partitioned by a *nuclear stopping cross-section* into:

$$E_{\text{transferred}} = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2}\, E_{\text{ion}}$$

For Ar⁺ (M₁ = 40 amu) on Cu target (M₂ = 63.5 amu):

$$f = \frac{4 \times 40 \times 63.5}{(40 + 63.5)^2} = \frac{10160}{10712.25} \approx 0.949$$

So ~95 % of incident energy is transferred to the Cu atom — explaining why **sputtering yield is high** for heavy targets with heavy ions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Sputtering Yield from Bohdansky–Yamamura Formula

A semi-empirical formula widely used for engineering estimates:

$$S(E) = \frac{0.042\, \alpha\, S_n(E)}{U_s\, [1 + 0.35\, U_s\, s_n(\varepsilon)]} \cdot \left[1 - \sqrt{\frac{E_{th}}{E}}\right]^2$$

where:

- $S_n(E) = \dfrac{4 \pi a_0 Z_1 Z_2 e^2 M_1}{M_2\, E}\, s_n(\varepsilon)$ — nuclear stopping
- $s_n(\varepsilon) = \dfrac{\ln(1 + 1.1383\varepsilon)}{2 [\varepsilon + 0.01321 \varepsilon^{0.21226} + 0.19593 \varepsilon^{0.5}]}$
- $\varepsilon = \dfrac{M_2\, E}{M_1 + M_2} \cdot \dfrac{a_0}{Z_1 Z_2 e^2}$
- $E_{th}$ — threshold energy
- $U_s$ — surface binding energy (≈ sublimation enthalpy)
- $\alpha$ — empirical correction factor

### Worked Numerical Example

**Target:** Cu, $U_s = 3.5\ \text{eV}$, $Z_2 = 29$, $M_2 = 63.5\ \text{amu}$
**Ion:** Ar⁺, $M_1 = 40\ \text{amu}$, $Z_1 = 18$, $E = 500\ \text{eV}$

Compute $\varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{63.5 \times 500}{40 + 63.5} \cdot \frac{0.529 \times 10^{-10}}{18 \times 29 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2 / (1.602 \times 10^{-19})}$$

Simplifying the denominator term $Z_1 Z_2 e^2$:

$$Z_1 Z_2 e^2 = 18 \times 29 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 8.363 \times 10^{-17}\ \text{J·eV}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{63.5 \times 500}{103.5} \cdot \frac{0.529 \times 10^{-10}}{8.363 \times 10^{-17}} \cdot \frac{1.602\times 10^{-19}}{1}$$

Numerator step 1: $63.5 \times 500 / 103.5 = 306.76$

Numerator step 2: $306.76 \times 0.529 \times 10^{-10} = 1.623 \times 10^{-8}$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1.623 \times 10^{-8}}{8.363 \times 10^{-17}} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$\varepsilon = 1.94 \times 10^{8} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \approx 0.0311$$

Plugging $\varepsilon = 0.0311$ into $s_n(\varepsilon)$:

$$s_n = \frac{\ln(1 + 1.1383 \times 0.0311)}{2 [0.0311 + 0.01321 \times 0.0311^{0.212} + 0.19593 \times 0.0311^{0.5}]}$$

$$s_n = \frac{\ln(1.0354)}{2 [0.0311 + 0.00536 + 0.0347]} = \frac{0.0348}{2 \times 0.0712} = 0.244$$

With $E_{th} \approx 23\ \text{eV}$ for Cu, and using a tabulated $\alpha \approx 0.21$:

$$S_n(500) = \frac{4 \pi (0.529 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot 18 \cdot 29 \cdot (1.602\times10^{-19}) \cdot 40}{63.5 \cdot 500 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \cdot 0.244$$

Simplifying: $S_n \approx 9.2 \times 10^{-20} \times 0.244 / (5.08 \times 10^{-17}) \approx 4.42 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{eV}^{-1}$... → continued through lookup tables gives $S \approx 2.3$ atoms/ion, which is consistent with measured Cu sputtering yield by 500 eV Ar⁺.

$$\boxed{S_{\text{Cu}} \approx 2.3\ \text{atoms/ion at 500 eV Ar}^+}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — PLD Plume Drag Model

The plume front position $R(t)$ from a stationary point source expanding into a background gas of density $\rho_g$ obeys:

$$R(t) \frac{d^2 R}{dt^2} = \frac{E_0}{M(t)} - \beta \rho_g \left(\frac{dR}{dt}\right)^2$$

where $M(t)$ is the entrained gas mass. Assuming plume sweeps up background gas proportional to volume, integrating yields:

$$R(t) = R_0 \left[1 + \frac{t}{\tau}\right]^{1/5},\quad \tau = \frac{R_0}{v_0}$$

with $v_0$ initial plume velocity. Therefore velocity decays as:

$$v(t) = \frac{dR}{dt} = \frac{R_0}{5\tau} \left[1 + \frac{t}{\tau}\right]^{-4/5}$$

The plume decelerates rapidly — important because **high kinetic energy at the substrate** controls film crystallinity.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Sputtering | Pulsed Laser Deposition |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Ion kinetic energy | Photon energy |
| Typical energy per particle | 5–50 eV | 10–100 eV |
| Stoichiometry transfer | Often non-congruent | Highly congruent |
| Uniformity on 6″ wafer | Excellent | Limited (narrow plume) |
| Deposition rate | 1–100 nm/min | 0.1–10 nm/min |
| Target compatibility | Metals, insulators (with RF) | Almost any material |
| Best for | MEMS mass production | R&D complex oxides |
| Vacuum requirement | 1–30 mTorr (working) | 10⁻⁵–10⁻⁷ Torr |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sputtering System — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the main functional blocks of a typical DC/RF magnetron sputtering system and their physical/logical interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Vacuum Pump Stack] --> A2[Process Chamber]
    B1[Argon Gas Cylinder] --> B2[Mass Flow Controller]
    B2 --> A2
    A2 --> C1[Cathode Magnetron Assembly]
    C1 --> C2[Target Material - Cathode]
    C2 --> D1[Plasma Generation Region]
    A2 --> E1[Substrate Holder - Anode]
    E1 --> F1[Heater and Bias Supply]
    F1 --> E1
    D1 --> G1[Collision Cascade at Target]
    G1 --> G2[Ejected Neutral Atoms]
    G2 --> H1[Ballistic Transport Region]
    H1 --> E1
    D1 --> I1[RF or DC Power Supply]
    I1 --> C1
    A2 --> J1[Pressure Gauge and Shutter]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Gas (B1) flows through MFC (B2) into the chamber (A2) evacuated by pumps (A1).
- Power supply (I1) energizes the magnetron cathode (C1) → target (C2).
- Plasma (D1) generated → ions bombard target → cascade (G1) → sputtered atoms (G2).
- Ejected atoms traverse ballistic region (H1) and condense on substrate (E1).
- Substrate heater/bias (F1) controls film microstructure.

---

## 4.2 Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD) — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Excimer or NdYAG Laser] --> L2[Beam Steering Optics]
    L2 --> L3[Focussing Lens]
    L3 --> L4[Laser Window into Vacuum]
    L4 --> L5[Target Surface - Rotating]
    L5 --> L6[Ablation Spot]
    L6 --> L7[Knudsen Layer Formation]
    L7 --> L8[Luminous Plasma Plume]
    L8 --> L9[Plume Expansion toward Substrate]
    L9 --> L10[Heated Substrate Holder]
    L10 --> L11[Deposited Thin Film]
    V1[Turbo Molecular Pump] --> V2[Vacuum Chamber]
    V2 --> L5
    V2 --> L10
    G1[Oxygen or Reactive Gas Inlet] --> V2
    H1[Substrate Heater Controller] --> L10
    T1[Target Rotator Motor] --> L5
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Laser (L1) → optics (L2, L3) → window (L4) → target (L5).
- At ablation spot (L6), material is vaporized → Knudsen layer (L7) → plume (L8).
- Plume (L9) deposits onto heated substrate (L10) forming the film (L11).
- Vacuum chamber (V2) is pumped (V1); reactive gas (G1) can be introduced.
- Heater controller (H1) sets substrate T; target rotator (T1) ensures uniform ablation.

---

## 4.3 Process-Comparison Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Choose Thin-Film Deposition Method] --> P1{Is target an insulator?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2{Is large-area uniformity critical?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Use RF Magnetron Sputtering]
    P2 -- No --> P4[Use RF Diode Sputtering]
    P1 -- No --> P5{Is stoichiometry of complex oxide critical?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Use Pulsed Laser Deposition]
    P5 -- No --> P7{Is high deposition rate needed?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Use DC Magnetron Sputtering]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Use Ion-Beam Sputtering]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam tip:** A question may give a target material and film requirement — the above decision tree mirrors how to justify the choice.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define sputtering yield. Mention any two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**

The sputtering yield $S$ is the average number of target atoms ejected per incident ion:

$$S = \frac{N_{\text{target atoms ejected}}}{N_{\text{incident ions}}}$$

Two factors affecting $S$:

1. **Incident ion energy $E$** — Yield rises with $E$ above the threshold $E_{th}$ (~20–40 eV), peaks around a few keV, then decreases due to ion implantation.
2. **Mass ratio $M_2/M_1$** — Maximum momentum transfer occurs when $M_1 \approx M_2$ (Ar⁺ on Cu, Ni is good; Ar⁺ on light Li is poor).
3. *(Additional)* Angle of incidence, target surface binding energy $U_s$, and crystal orientation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the threshold fluence in PLD? Why is a UV laser preferred over an IR laser?**

**Model Answer:**

**Threshold fluence $\Phi_t$** is the minimum laser energy per unit area required at the target surface to initiate ablation. Typical values are **1–5 J/cm²** for UV excimer lasers.

**Why UV preferred over IR:**
- UV photons have **higher photon energy** ($E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$). At 248 nm (KrF), $E \approx 5\ \text{eV}$ vs. 1064 nm (Nd:YAG) $E \approx 1.17\ \text{eV}$.
- Many wide-bandgap materials have a small absorption coefficient at IR → poor coupling → melting rather than ablation.
- UV excimer pulses produce **non-thermal (photochemical) ablation** giving cleaner stoichiometric transfer.
- Shorter wavelength → tighter focus → higher fluence at lower pulse energy.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DC magnetron sputtering system** used for MEMS thin-film deposition. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A copper target is sputtered by 500 eV Ar⁺ ions at a current density of 2 mA/cm². The sputtering yield is 2.3 atoms/ion and the deposited film density is 8.96 g/cm³. Calculate the **deposition rate** in nm/min. (Atomic mass of Cu = 63.5 g/mol, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$). (7 Marks)

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Working of DC Magnetron Sputtering

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):

1. **Chamber evacuation:** The stainless-steel chamber is pumped to a base pressure of $10^{-6}$–$10^{-7}$ Torr.
2. **Gas introduction:** Argon is admitted through an MFC to maintain working pressure of 5–30 mTorr.
3. **Plasma generation:** A DC voltage (–300 to –800 V) is applied between cathode (target) and grounded anode (chamber walls + substrate). Free electrons ionize Ar → Ar⁺ + e⁻. The discharge glows.
4. **Magnetron enhancement:** Permanent magnets behind the target create a magnetic field parallel to the target surface, trapping electrons in a cycloidal path → longer electron residence time → higher ionization efficiency → higher ion current at lower pressure.
5. **Ion bombardment:** Ar⁺ ions accelerate across the cathode dark-space and strike the target.
6. **Collision cascade & ejection:** Momentum is transferred to target atoms; those reaching the surface with energy > $U_s$ are ejected (≈ 5–50 eV).
7. **Film growth:** Ejected neutrals travel ballistically to the heated substrate (200–400 °C) and form a thin film.

**Key advantages for MEMS:**
- Low substrate temperature
- Excellent step coverage
- Uniformity over large wafers
- Compatible with photoresist (no high-T damage)

---

#### Part (b) — Deposition Rate Calculation

**Given:**
- Ion energy $E = 500\ \text{eV}$
- Current density $J = 2\ \text{mA/cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A/cm}^2$
- Sputtering yield $S = 2.3$
- Cu density $\rho = 8.96\ \text{g/cm}^3$
- $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5\ \text{g/mol}$
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$

**Step 1 — Ion flux (ions/cm²/s):**

Each ion carries charge $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$.

$$n_{\text{ion}} = \frac{J}{e} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$n_{\text{ion}} = 1.249 \times 10^{16}\ \text{ions/cm}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 2 — Atoms ejected per second per cm²:**

$$n_{\text{atoms}} = S \times n_{\text{ion}} = 2.3 \times 1.249 \times 10^{16}$$

$$n_{\text{atoms}} = 2.872 \times 10^{16}\ \text{atoms/cm}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass deposited per second per cm²:**

$$m' = \frac{n_{\text{atoms}} \times M_{\text{Cu}}}{N_A} = \frac{2.872 \times 10^{16} \times 63.5}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$m' = 3.027 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{g/cm}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 4 — Thickness per second:**

$$t' = \frac{m'}{\rho} = \frac{3.027 \times 10^{-6}}{8.96} = 3.378 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{cm/s}$$

$$t' = 3.378\ \text{nm/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to nm/min:**

$$R_d = 3.378 \times 60 = 202.7\ \text{nm/min}$$

$$\boxed{R_d \approx 203\ \text{nm/min}}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD)** process with a schematic diagram. Discuss the role of **threshold fluence**, **laser wavelength**, and **substrate temperature** on film quality. (7 Marks)

**(b)** In a PLD system, the plume expands according to $R(t) = R_0 (1 + t/\tau)^{1/5}$ where $R_0 = 1\ \text{cm}$ and $\tau = 5\ \mu\text{s}$. The target–substrate distance is 5 cm. Determine the **time taken by the plume front to reach the substrate** and the **plume velocity at that instant**. (7 Marks)

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — PLD Process

**Schematic** (refer Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram):

A high-power pulsed laser (KrF 248 nm or Nd:YAG 1064 nm, ns pulses) is focused onto a rotating target inside a vacuum chamber back-filled with oxygen (for oxides) or inert gas.

**Stages:**

1. **Photon absorption:** UV photons absorbed in the optical skin depth (~10–100 nm) of the target.
2. **Ablation & plasma formation:** Above $\Phi_t$, target material is vaporized and ionized → luminous plasma plume.
3. **Plume propagation:** Plume expands forward (cosⁿ θ distribution) toward the heated substrate.
4. **Nucleation & growth:** Condensing species nucleate and grow into a thin film.

**Role of key parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on Film Quality |
|---|---|
| **Threshold fluence $\Phi_t$** | Below $\Phi_t$ → no ablation; well above → splashing/microspheres. Optimal range 1–5 J/cm². |
| **Laser wavelength** | UV preferred — high photon energy breaks bonds directly, reducing thermal damage. Shorter λ → better coupling in wide-bandgap oxides. |
| **Substrate temperature $T_s$** | Higher $T_s$ (400–800 °C) increases adatom mobility → better crystallinity, epitaxy. Too low → amorphous film; too high → re-evaporation. |

---

#### Part (b) — Plume Travel Time and Velocity

**Given:** $R_0 = 1\ \text{cm}$, $\tau = 5\ \mu\text{s}$, target–substrate distance $L = 5\ \text{cm}$.

**Step 1 — Solve for arrival time $t_a$:**

$$R(t_a) = L \implies 5 = 1 \times \left(1 + \frac{t_a}{5\ \mu\text{s}}\right)^{1/5}$$

Raise both sides to power 5:

$$5^5 = 1 + \frac{t_a}{\tau}$$

$$3125 = 1 + \frac{t_a}{5\ \mu\text{s}}$$

$$\frac{t_a}{5\ \mu\text{s}} = 3124$$

$$\boxed{t_a = 15{,}620\ \mu\text{s} = 15.62\ \text{ms}}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity at arrival:**

$$v(t) = \frac{dR}{dt} = \frac{R_0}{5\tau} \left(1 + \frac{t}{\tau}\right)^{-4/5}$$

At $t = t_a$, $(1 + t_a/\tau) = 3125$, so:

$$v(t_a) = \frac{1}{5 \times 5} \cdot (3125)^{-4/5}\ \text{cm}/\mu\text{s}$$

$$v(t_a) = 0.04 \times (3125)^{-0.8}\ \text{cm}/\mu\text{s}$$

Compute $(3125)^{0.8}$:

$$\ln(3125^{0.8}) = 0.8 \times \ln(3125) = 0.8 \times 8.047 = 6.438$$

$$(3125)^{0.8} = e^{6.438} \approx 625.4$$

So $(3125)^{-0.8} \approx 1/625.4 \approx 1.599 \times 10^{-3}$

$$v(t_a) = 0.04 \times 1.599 \times 10^{-3} = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{cm}/\mu\text{s}$$

Convert to m/s:

$$v(t_a) = 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-2} / (10^{-6}) = 0.64\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v(t_a) \approx 0.64\ \text{m/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The plume front slows down considerably due to drag — useful for allowing adatoms to migrate on the substrate for high-quality crystalline growth.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing incident-ion energy with sputtered-atom energy.** Sputtered neutrals have 5–50 eV; the Ar⁺ ion has hundreds of eV.
> 2. **Wrong units in deposition-rate numerics.** Forgetting to convert cm/s → nm/min loses 1–2 marks. Always state your conversions.
> 3. **Plume drag model — algebraic slips.** Raising both sides of $R(t) = R_0(1+t/\tau)^{1/5}$ to the power **5** is the most commonly missed step.
> 4. **Confusing RF with DC sputtering applicability.** Insulating targets cannot be DC sputtered — write this explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting congruent transfer** when comparing PLD to sputtering for complex oxides.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- **Sputtering** is a momentum-transfer PVD process; PLD is a photon-energy ablation process.
- **Sputtering yield $S$** typically ranges 0.1–10 atoms/ion; it peaks near the mass-matched condition $M_1 \approx M_2$.
- **Threshold energy $E_{th}$** for sputtering is 20–40 eV for most metals.
- **RF sputtering** is mandatory for **insulating targets** (e.g., SiO₂, Al₂O₃).
- **Magnetron sputtering** increases ionization efficiency via magnetic confinement → lower pressure, higher rate.
- **Mean free path** must exceed target–substrate gap → working pressure 1–30 mTorr.
- **PLD threshold fluence** = 1–5 J/cm²; below it, no ablation occurs.
- **PLD excels** at depositing **complex stoichiometric oxides** (YBCO, PZT, BST) — congruent transfer is its hallmark.
- **PLD weaknesses:** narrow angular plume distribution, splashing of particulates, limited area uniformity.
- **Deposition rate (sputtering)** = $R_d = S \cdot J_{\text{ion}} \cdot M_{\text{target}} / (\rho_{\text{film}} \cdot N_A)$.
- **Plume drag model:** $R(t) = R_0(1 + t/\tau)^{1/5}$ → solves easily by raising to power 5.
- **Reactive sputtering** uses O₂/N₂ to grow oxides/nitrides from metallic targets.
- **Common MEMS films:** Al, Au, Ti, Pt (electrodes); SiO₂, Si₃N₄ (dielectrics); AlN, ZnO (piezoelectric); YBCO, PZT (PLD specialty).
- **Excimer (UV) lasers** dominate PLD over IR due to higher photon energy and non-thermal ablation.
- **Energy of an Ar⁺ ion** at cathode potential $V_c$ is $E = e V_c$ in eV — remember this shortcut for numerics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any KTU 2024 long-answer on sputtering or PLD, **always include a labelled block diagram**, **state the relevant formula**, and **show the derivation step-by-step** — these are the three pillars on which marks are awarded.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Plating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ion Plating # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ion Plating** is a Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) process in which the substrate and the growing film are simultaneously subjected to a flux of energetic ions (typically inert gas ions such as Ar⁺) generated by a glow discharge or plasma, while the coating material is vaporized either by evaporation, sputtering, or other means. The process was first reported by **D. M. Mattox in 1964** at the Sandia National Laboratories (USA), originally developed to solve the adhesion problem of gold films on beryllium substrates used in aerospace components. The technique bridges the gap between **Evaporation** (high deposition rate, poor adhesion) and **Sputtering** (good adhesion, lower deposition rate). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Tag – Module 2 Highlight:** > Ion Plating is grouped under the "Vacuum & Thin Film Deposition Techniques" cluster. Expect conceptual questions (3 marks) on working principles and comparative questions (7–14 marks) on ion plating vs. evaporation vs. sputtering. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Imagine you are spray-painting a wall (this is like **evaporation** — the paint "sticks," but if you scratch it, it peels off easily because there is no mechanical interlocking). Now imagine the same wall is being gently sand-blasted with fine particles *at the same time* you are spray-painting it (this is like **ion plating** — the ion bombardment cleans the surface, knocks atoms into microscopic crevices, and creates a thin "mixed" interface layer). The paint now grips the wall so tightly that even a knife cannot peel it off. In technical language: - The **paint** = vaporized coating atoms (e.g., Ti, Au, Al). - The **sand-blast** = energetic inert gas ions (e.g., Ar⁺ at 1–5 keV). - The **wall** = the MEMS substrate (Si, glass, metal). The energetic ions perform **four critical functions simultaneously**: 1. **Sputter-cleaning** the substrate before deposition (removing native oxides/contaminants). 2. **Surface activation** by creating dangling bonds and defect sites. 3. **Atomic peening** — bombarding already-deposited atoms to drive them deeper into the substrate, forming a graded interface. 4. **Enhanced adatom mobility**, leading to denser, smoother films. > [!NOTE] > The substrate is held at a **negative bias (typically –0.5 kV to –5 kV)** so that positive ions from the plasma are accelerated toward it with controllable kinetic energy. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic The ion plating process can be decomposed into **eight sequential physical events**: | # | Event | Physics Involved | Engineering Significance | |---|-------|------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Vacuum pump-down** | Base pressure ≤ 10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁶ Torr (≈ 10⁻³ – 10⁻⁴ Pa) | Reduces contamination, increases mean free path of vapor atoms | | 2 | **Inert gas (Ar) introduction** | Working pressure ≈ 1–10 mTorr (0.13–1.3 Pa) | Sustains the glow discharge plasma | | 3 | **Plasma ignition** | DC or RF excitation at –0.5 to –5 kV | Ionizes Ar → Ar⁺ + e⁻ | | 4 | **Substrate sputter-cleaning** | Ar⁺ bombardment removes ~5–50 nm of native surface | Improves adhesion by exposing clean surface | | 5 | **Source vaporization** | Evaporation (resistive, e-beam) or sputtering target | Generates coating atom flux | | 6 | **Ion-assisted transport** | Coating atoms pass through plasma region | Some get ionized (10–50 %) → enhanced reactivity | | 7 | **Film growth + ion bombardment** | Bias-accelerated ions strike growing film | Densifies film, promotes (111) texture | | 8 | **Post-deposition cooling** | Substrate cools under vacuum/inert gas | Prevents oxidation of fresh film | ## 2.2 Key Process Parameters (Engineering Levers) > [!IMPORTANT] > A KTU examiner will test your ability to correlate process parameters with film properties. Memorize the four parameters below: 1. **Substrate Bias Voltage (V_b)** — controls ion energy. 2. **Argon Pressure (P_Ar)** — controls plasma density and mean free path. 3. **Substrate Temperature (T_s)** — controls adatom surface mobility. 4. **Deposition Rate (R_d)** — controlled by source power. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are directly examinable. Numerical problems in Part B frequently use these equations. | # | Formula | Description / Definition of Terms | Units | |---|---------|-----------------------------------|-------| | 1 | $E_{ion} = e \cdot V_{bias}$ | Kinetic energy of an accelerated ion (single-charge assumption). $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C | eV (numerically equal to V_b in volts) | | 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 P}$ | Mean free path of vapor atom. $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $d$ = atomic diameter, $P$ = pressure | meters | | 3 | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{N_v}{N_s \cdot \rho}$ | Deposition rate, $N_v$ = atoms arriving per second, $N_s$ = site density, $\rho$ = density | nm/s or Å/s | | 4 | $V_{m\_Teff} = V_{m\_T0} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_d}{k_B T_s}\right)$ | Adatom mobility (Arrhenius), $E_d$ = surface diffusion energy | — | | 5 | $\Phi_{ion} = \dfrac{j_{ion}}{e}$ | Ion flux reaching substrate, $j_{ion}$ = ion current density | ions/m²·s | | 6 | $T_{hom} \approx 0.4 \cdot T_{melt}$ | Homologous temperature for good film densification (K) | K | | 7 | $Y_s = \dfrac{N_{sput}}{N_{ion}}$ | Sputtering yield (atoms ejected per incident ion), typical 0.5–20 for Ar⁺ on metals | dimensionless | > **Critical Tip:** Attenuation of ion energy follows Child-Langmuir law for a collisionless sheath: > $$\boxed{\,j_{ion} = \dfrac{4}{9}\,\varepsilon_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{2e}{m_i}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{bias}^{3/2}}{d_{sheath}^{2}}\,}$$ > where $d_{sheath}$ is the dark-space thickness, $m_i$ = ion mass. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MEMS Tribological Coatings:** TiN, CrN, DLC (diamond-like carbon) coatings on gears, bearings, and micromirror hinges to reduce stiction and wear. - **Adhesion Promoter Layers:** Ti or Cr ion-plated before Au bonding pads in wire-bonded MEMS packages. - **Optical MEMS:** Anti-reflection and protective coatings on IR sensors and microbolometers. - **Biomedical MEMS:** Hydroxyapatite ion plating on Ti implants for osseointegration. - **Microelectronics:** Barrier layers (TiN/TaN) in Cu interconnects to suppress diffusion. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Free Path of Vapor Atoms in Working Gas (Why Vacuum is Required) > **Problem Statement:** Show that to obtain a mean free path of 0.5 m at room temperature (T = 300 K) in Argon, the required pressure is approximately 0.4 mTorr. Use atomic diameter $d = 3.66 \times 10^{-10}$ m for Ar. **Step 1 — Start with the kinetic theory expression for mean free path:** $$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^{2} \, n}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute number density $n$ using ideal gas law:** $$n = \frac{P}{k_B T}$$ $$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^{2} \, P}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for pressure $P$:** $$P = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^{2} \, \lambda}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:** $$\begin{aligned} k_B T &= (1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300) \\ &= 4.143 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} &= 1.4142 \times 3.1416 \times (3.66 \times 10^{-10})^{2} \\ &= 4.4429 \times 1.3396 \times 10^{-19} \\ &= 5.952 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $P$ in Pascals:** $$P = \frac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{5.952 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5} = \frac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{2.976 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.392 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 6 — Convert Pa → mTorr:** $$1 \text{ Torr} = 133.322 \text{ Pa} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 \text{ mTorr} = 0.1333 \text{ Pa}$$ $$P = \frac{1.392 \times 10^{-2}}{0.1333} = 0.1045 \text{ mTorr}$$ > **Conclusion:** To get $\lambda = 0.5$ m in Ar, the working pressure must be ≤ **0.1 mTorr** (more stringent than the 1–10 mTorr used in ion plating — this is why ion plating is a *moderately* vacuum process relying on shorter mean free paths to keep ions energetic). ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Allowable Bias Voltage (Sputtering-Limited Growth) > **Problem Statement:** Find the maximum Ar⁺ ion energy $E_{ion}$ below which Ar⁺ bombardment will **not** resputter a freshly deposited Au film. Given: sputtering threshold energy $E_{th} \approx 30$ eV for Ar⁺ on Au. **Step 1 — Energy of single-charge ion accelerated through bias $V_b$:** $$E_{ion} = e \cdot V_b$$ **Step 2 — Condition for no resputtering (ion energy must stay below threshold):** $$E_{ion} \leq E_{th}$$ $$e \cdot V_b \leq 30 \text{ eV}$$ $$\boxed{\,V_b \leq 30 \text{ V}\,}$$ > **Interpretation:** For Au, bias voltage must remain **below 30 V** to avoid resputtering. Harder materials (Ti, Cr) have higher thresholds (~50–80 eV), allowing bias up to ~80 V. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ion-Plating Adhesion Energy Estimate (Mattox Model) Mattox proposed that the adhesion improvement scales with the energy deposited per atom at the interface: $$\Delta E_{adh} = \Phi_{ion} \cdot E_{ion} \cdot \sigma_{mix}$$ where: - $\Phi_{ion}$ = ion flux (ions/m²·s) - $E_{ion}$ = ion energy (eV) - $\sigma_{mix}$ = effective atomic mixing cross-section ≈ $10^{-20}$ m² **Numerical Example:** For $\Phi_{ion} = 10^{19}$ ions/m²·s, $E_{ion} = 100$ eV, $\sigma_{mix} = 10^{-20}$ m²: $$\Delta E_{adh} = 10^{19} \times 100 \times 10^{-20} = 10 \text{ eV/atom}$$ This 10 eV/atom matches the typical chemical bond energy of strong metallic bonds, explaining the order-of-magnitude improvement in adhesion. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ion Plating vs. Related Processes > [!IMPORTANT] > This comparative table is **the single most-asked table** on ion plating in KTU exams. | Property | Evaporation | Sputtering | **Ion Plating** | |----------|-------------|------------|-----------------| | Adhesion | Poor | Good | **Excellent** | | Film density | Low (porous) | High | **Very High** | | Deposition rate | Very High (nm/s) | Low (Å/s) | **Medium–High** | | Substrate heating | Minimal | Moderate | **High (ion bombardment)** | | Throwing power | Poor (line-of-sight) | Good | **Excellent** | | Coverage of 3-D features | Poor | Fair | **Best (wrap-around)** | | Equipment cost | Low | Medium | **Medium–High** | | Ion energy control | None | Limited | **Full (V_b control)** | | Substrate damage | None | Moderate | **Controllable** | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic of an Ion Plating System (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The diagram below maps the functional architecture of a DC diode ion-plating chamber — the classic configuration used in MEMS foundries. ```mermaid flowchart TB VACC["Vacuum Chamber
Base Pressure 10 to the power minus 6 Torr"] ARIN["Ar Gas Inlet
MFC Controlled Flow"] BIAS["DC Bias Supply
Minus 0.5 to Minus 5 kV"] SUBS["Substrate Holder
Water Cooled Cathode"] PLASMA["Glow Discharge Plasma
Ar Plus Electrons"] SOURCE["Vapor Source
Resistive or E beam Evaporator"] CRUC["Crucible with Coating Material
Ti Au Al Cr"] SHUTTER["Shutter
Prevents Contamination"] IONS["Accelerated Ar Plus Ions
Energy 50 to 500 eV"] FILM["Growing Thin Film
On MEMS Substrate"] PUMP["Cryogenic Diffusion Pump
Plus Rotary Backing"] ARIN --> VACC PUMP --> VACC BIAS --> SUBS VACC --> PLASMA SOURCE --> VACC CRUC --> SOURCE SHUTTER --> SUBS PLASMA --> IONS BIAS --> IONS IONS --> FILM SOURCE --> FILM SUBS --> FILM VACC --> PUMP ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Ar gas flows in via Mass Flow Controller (MFC). - DC bias creates the plasma between substrate (cathode) and chamber wall (anode). - Vapor from the crucible condenses on the substrate while being simultaneously bombarded by Ar⁺ ions. - Cryopump maintains low base pressure. ## 4.2 Process Sequence State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> PumpDown PumpDown --> ArFlush : Base Pressure Reached ArFlush --> PlasmaOn : Working Pressure Set PlasmaOn --> PreClean : Sputter Etch 2 to 5 min PreClean --> Deposition : Shutter Open Deposition --> IonBombard : Continuous Bias IonBombard --> Deposition : Concurrent Vapor Flux Deposition --> CoolDown : Source Shut CoolDown --> Vent : Below 50 Degree Celsius Vent --> [*] ``` **KTU Insight:** The `PlasmaOn → PreClean → Deposition` triplet is what distinguishes ion plating from a mere evaporation process. Skipping pre-clean ruins adhesion. ## 4.3 Energy-Level Schematic of Ion-Assisted Film Growth ```mermaid flowchart LR ATOM1["Vapor Atom
E equals 0.1 eV
Thermal"] ACCEL["Acceleration in Sheath
Energy Gain equals eVbias"] SURF["Substrate Surface
Binding Energy 3 to 10 eV"] MOBIL["Surface Diffusion
Migration Length 10 to 100 nm"] BURIED["Atomic Peening
Knock On 1 to 2 nm Deep"] INTER["Graded Interface
Mixing Zone 5 to 50 nm"] FILM2["Dense Crystalline Film
Adhesion equals 50 to 100 MPa"] ATOM1 --> ACCEL ACCEL --> SURF SURF --> MOBIL MOBIL --> BURIED BURIED --> INTER INTER --> FILM2 ``` > **Take-away for KTU:** Without ion assistance (evaporation), atoms land at ~0.1 eV and barely migrate → porous columnar film. With ion plating at 100 eV, atoms dig in → dense film with mixed interface → adhesion improves by 10×. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q1. Define ion plating. Name the inventor and the year of invention.** **Model Answer:** Ion plating is a Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) technique in which a substrate is simultaneously coated with vaporized material and bombarded with energetic ions (typically inert gas ions such as Ar⁺) to enhance film adhesion, density, and coverage. The technique was invented by **D. M. Mattox in 1964** at Sandia National Laboratories. > **Examiner's Note (3-mark scheme):** Definition = 2 marks, Mattox + 1964 = 1 mark. Any missing element = −1 mark. > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q2. List any four advantages of ion plating over vacuum evaporation.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Superior adhesion** — due to ion bombardment creating a graded interface. 2. **Higher film density** — peening effect eliminates columnar porosity. 3. **Better step coverage / throwing power** — ions can coat non-line-of-sight features. 4. **Lower substrate temperature required** for dense films compared to thermal evaporation. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the working principle of ion plating with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of substrate bias. (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare ion plating with evaporation and sputtering in terms of adhesion, film density, and throwing power. (7 Marks) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Derive an expression for the mean free path of vapor atoms in a working gas. If the working gas is Argon at 300 K and pressure 5 mTorr, calculate the mean free path. Given $d_{Ar} = 3.66 \times 10^{-10}$ m. (7 Marks) **(b)** With suitable examples, explain how ion plating is used in MEMS devices. (7 Marks) --- ### 5.2.1 Model Solutions #### Question A — Model Solution **(a) Working Principle & Substrate Bias (7 marks):** Working principle of ion plating: - The substrate is placed inside a vacuum chamber and held at a **negative DC bias** of typically **–0.5 kV to –5 kV**. - The chamber is back-filled with inert gas (Ar) to a pressure of **1–10 mTorr**, and a glow discharge plasma is ignited. - Ar atoms are ionized → Ar⁺ ions + electrons. The positively charged Ar⁺ ions are accelerated by the negative substrate bias and bombard the substrate continuously. - Simultaneously, the coating material (e.g., Ti, Au) is vaporized from a crucible using resistive heating, e-beam, or a sputtering target. - The vapor atoms travel through the plasma region and condense on the substrate, where they are continually "peened" by incoming Ar⁺ ions, leading to dense, well-adhered films. Role of substrate bias: - **Negative bias** (e.g., −2 kV) accelerates positive Ar⁺ ions toward the substrate with kinetic energy $E_{ion} = eV_b$. - This performs sputter-cleaning (before deposition), creates dangling bonds, drives surface diffusion, and causes atomic mixing at the interface. - **Excessive bias** (> resputtering threshold) can damage the film and re-sputter the deposit. - **Optimum bias** for most metal-on-Si MEMS systems is **−50 V to −500 V**. **(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Property | Evaporation | Sputtering | Ion Plating | |----------|-------------|------------|-------------| | Adhesion | Poor (weak physisorption) | Moderate to Good | Excellent (chemisorption + mixing) | | Film density | ~70–80 % of bulk | ~90–95 % of bulk | ~95–100 % of bulk | | Throwing power | Line-of-sight only | Moderate | Excellent (gas scattering) | | Ion energy control | None | Limited | Full control via V_b | > **Conclusion:** Ion plating combines the high deposition rate of evaporation with the energetic bombardment of sputtering, giving the best of both worlds. --- #### Question B — Model Solution **(a) Mean Free Path Derivation (7 marks):** From kinetic theory of gases, the mean free path $\lambda$ of a gas atom is: $$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\, n}$$ Using the ideal gas relation $P = n k_B T$, i.e., $n = P/(k_B T)$: $$\boxed{\,\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\, P}\,}$$ **Numerical calculation** (P = 5 mTorr): Convert: $P = 5 \times 0.1333 = 0.6665$ Pa. $$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot (3.66 \times 10^{-10})^{2} \cdot 0.6665} \\ &= \frac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{1.414 \times 3.1416 \times 1.3396 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.6665} \\ &= \frac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{3.967 \times 10^{-19}} \\ &\approx 1.044 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \\ &\approx \boxed{1.04 \text{ cm}} \end{aligned}$$ > **Interpretation:** At 5 mTorr, vapor atoms travel ~1 cm before colliding with Ar — short enough to be thermalized but long enough to reach the substrate, enabling good step coverage. **(b) MEMS Applications (7 marks):** 1. **Tribological coatings on MEMS gears & bearings:** TiN and DLC coatings deposited by ion plating reduce friction and wear in silicon micromotors (e.g., Sandia SUMMiT process). 2. **Bonding pads:** Ti/Au ion-plated films provide excellent wire-bond adhesion in MEMS packages. 3. **Microbolometers & IR sensors:** Ion-plated absorbing layers (e.g., black gold or vanadium oxide) enhance IR absorption uniformity. 4. **Biomedical implants:** Hydroxyapatite on titanium MEMS scaffolds improves bone-implant integration. 5. **Hermetic packaging:** Ion-plated Al or SiNₓ sealing layers in wafer-level packaging. 6. **Microfluidic channels:** Hydrophobic ion-plated films control capillary flow in lab-on-chip devices. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Points in Ion-Plating Questions:** > > 1. ✗ Forgetting to mention **Mattox (1964)** — usually a free 1 mark. > 2. ✗ Writing "ion plating uses high-energy ions" without quantifying — examiners expect **eV range (50–500 eV)** or **bias (–0.5 to –5 kV)**. > 3. ✗ Confusing **ion plating** with **ion implantation** — they are entirely different! Ion plating **deposits** a film; ion implantation **dopes** a substrate. > 4. ✗ Drawing the schematic with the substrate connected to **positive** bias — it must be the **cathode (negative)**. > 5. ✗ Omitting the **sputter-cleaning** step — many students miss that pre-cleaning is integral to ion plating. > 6. ✗ Writing mean free path derivation without including the $\sqrt{2}$ factor — loses 2 marks. ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Invented by D. M. Mattox in 1964** at Sandia National Labs, USA. - ✅ **Definition:** PVD process combining vapor deposition + energetic ion bombardment of substrate. - ✅ **Typical parameters:** Ar pressure 1–10 mTorr, substrate bias –0.5 to –5 kV, ion energy 50–500 eV. - ✅ **Four roles of ion bombardment:** sputter-clean, surface activation, atomic peening, densification. - ✅ **Schematic essentials:** vacuum chamber, Ar inlet, plasma, negatively biased substrate, vapor source (crucible + e-beam/resistive heater). - ✅ **Key formulas:** - Mean free path: $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P)$ - Ion energy: $E_{ion} = eV_b$ - Adhesion energy: $\Delta E_{adh} = \Phi_{ion} E_{ion} \sigma_{mix}$ - Child-Langmuir sheath current: $j_{ion} \propto V_{bias}^{3/2}/d_{sheath}^{2}$ - ✅ **Comparison vs Evaporation / Sputtering:** Ion plating gives best adhesion, density, throwing power; medium deposition rate. - ✅ **MEMS uses:** tribological coatings (TiN, DLC), bonding pads (Ti/Au), IR absorbers, biomedical HA coatings, hermetic seals. - ✅ **Pitfalls:** ion plating ≠ ion implantation; bias is **negative**; substrate temp can rise due to ion bombardment. ---

topics.title: Cluster Beam Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cluster Beam Technology # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cluster Beam Technology (CBT)** is an advanced vapor-phase thin-film deposition and surface-engineering technique in which **agglomerates of typically 10 to 10,000 atoms or molecules** (called *clusters*) are generated in the gas phase, optionally ionized, accelerated through controlled electric fields, and directed onto a substrate to form ultra-smooth, low-defect-density, highly adherent films. In the context of **KTU PEICT632 (MEMS)**, cluster beam methods are taught as a *next-generation physical vapor deposition (PVD)* route that overcomes the limitations of conventional thermal evaporation and sputtering — particularly when depositing films on temperature-sensitive MEMS substrates (polymers, biomaterials, released micro-structures). > [!NOTE] > **Cluster (n.)** — A stable, nano-scale aggregate of atoms/molecules bound by weak van der Waals forces, covalent bonds, or metallic bonds, with diameters typically in the **1–10 nm** range. Clusters represent an intermediate state of matter *between isolated atoms and bulk solids*. ## 1.2 Classification of Clusters | Cluster Type | Bonding Nature | Typical Examples | Formation Method | |---|---|---|---| | **Van der Waals clusters** | Weak dispersion forces (≈ 0.01–0.1 eV/bond) | Ar_n, (CO₂)_n, (H₂O)_n | Supersonic expansion | | **Covalent / Molecular clusters** | Strong covalent bonds (≈ 1–5 eV) | C₆₀ (Buckminsterfullerene), Si_n, S_n | Laser ablation, plasma | | **Metallic clusters** | Metallic bonding | Au_n, Ag_n, Cu_n, Al_n | Effusive + condensation | | **Ionic clusters** | Electrostatic binding | (NaCl)_n, (CsI)_n | Electrospray, thermionic emission | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cluster as a Soft-landing Bullet" Imagine you are throwing **individual marbles (atoms)** at a brick wall (substrate) versus throwing a **soft snowball (cluster)** at the same wall: - **Single-atom bombardment (ion implantation, sputtering):** Each marble hits hard. It *craters* the surface, creates defects, and embeds deep — damaging delicate MEMS structures. - **Cluster bombardment:** The snowball arrives with the same total kinetic energy, but because that energy is **shared among 1,000–10,000 atoms**, the energy *per atom* is tiny (≈ 1–100 eV/atom). The cluster **shatters on impact** and the atoms **"soft-land"** with almost no penetration, no damage, but excellent surface mobility → ultra-smooth, adherent film. > [!TIP] > **The KTU "magic number":** For a cluster of *n* atoms accelerated to total energy *E*, the per-atom energy is **E/n**. This ratio governs whether the cluster **sticks and migrates** (desired for deposition) or **sputters the substrate** (desired for surface etching). Typical ICB depositions operate at **E/n ≈ 1–100 eV**. ## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Significance | Year | Milestone | |---|---| | **1956** | Becker & Henkes — first cluster beam from supersonic nozzle | | **1972** | Takagi, Yamada & Kunori — proposed **Ionized Cluster Beam (ICB)** deposition at Kyoto University | | **1980s** | ICB commercialized for Al, Ag, Au, Pb, ZnO thin films | | **1990s** | **Gas Cluster Ion Beam (GCIB)** developed (Yamada, Matsuo) | | **2000s–Present** | GCIB used in **semiconductor doping, surface smoothing, MEMS release-etch** | > [!NOTE] > **Standard cluster size metrics used in KTU syllabus:** > - **Cluster diameter (d): 1–10 nm** > - **Cluster size (N): 10 – 10,000 atoms** > - **Mean free path in source chamber: λ > 10 cm** (to prevent coalescence) > - **Operating vacuum: 10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁷ Torr** (high vacuum regime) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle of a Cluster Beam System A complete cluster beam deposition (CBD) system comprises **four sequential stages**. Understanding each stage is essential for KTU derivations and numerical problems. ### Stage 1 — Cluster Generation (Source) The source material is vaporized and forced through a **converging-diverging (Laval) nozzle** into a high-vacuum chamber. During adiabatic expansion, the vapor **super-cools**, and atoms condense into clusters via **three-body collisions**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Condensation criterion:** A cluster nucleus is stable only when its size exceeds a **critical radius** given by the Gibbs–Thomson relation. Below this radius, thermal fluctuations dissolve the nucleus. ### Stage 2 — Ionization (Optional but critical) Clusters pass through an **electron bombardment ionizer** (typical electron energy = **50–200 eV**). Only a small fraction (~ 1–10%) are ionized — most remain neutral to avoid space-charge repulsion that would disperse the beam. $$\text{e}^- + \text{C}_n \longrightarrow \text{C}_n^+ + 2\text{e}^-$$ ### Stage 3 — Acceleration Ionized clusters are extracted and accelerated by a **high-voltage electrode stack** (typical V = **0.5–10 kV**). The kinetic energy gained by a cluster of mass *m* and charge *q* is: $$E_{\text{kin}} = qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$ ### Stage 4 — Deposition on Substrate Clusters impact the substrate. They **fragment upon impact** (Coulomb explosion for ionized clusters, mechanical shattering for energetic neutral clusters), and individual atoms migrate to energetically favorable lattice sites → **epitaxial-like growth at low substrate temperature**. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Logic - **Why condense into clusters?** Single atoms have *low sticking coefficients* on clean surfaces and cause *radiation damage*. Clusters have **higher sticking probability** and **damage-free landing**. - **Why ionize?** Electrostatic acceleration is much more efficient than thermal acceleration. Also enables **mass filtering** (only clusters of selected size reach the substrate). - **Why use a supersonic nozzle?** It produces a **collimated, mono-energetic beam** with controlled cluster size distribution — essential for reproducibility in MEMS fabrication. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas are the **highest-yield** items for KTU PEICT632 Module-2 numericals and derivations. | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{\text{kin}} = qV$ | Cluster kinetic energy gained in accelerator | eV or J | | 2 | $\dfrac{E_{\text{kin}}}{n} = \dfrac{qV}{n}$ | Energy **per atom** in the cluster | eV/atom | | 3 | $E_{\text{kin}} = \dfrac{1}{2} M v^2$ | Translational kinetic energy (M = total cluster mass) | J | | 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 P}$ | Mean free path (gas kinetic theory) | m | | 5 | $M = \dfrac{v}{a} = \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{\gamma k_B T / m}}$ | Mach number in supersonic nozzle | dimensionless | | 6 | $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | Mass flow rate through nozzle | kg/s | | 7 | $v_{\infty} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\cdot \dfrac{k_B T_0}{m}}$ | Terminal velocity of expanded gas | m/s | | 8 | $P \cdot V^{\gamma} = \text{const}$ | Adiabatic expansion relation | — | | 9 | $r^* = \dfrac{2\gamma V_m}{R T \ln(P/P_\infty)}$ | Critical nucleus radius (Gibbs–Thomson) | m | | 10 | $f(n) = \dfrac{1}{n\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(\ln n - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$ | Log-normal cluster size distribution | probability density | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In Equation 4 (mean free path), students frequently forget the √2 factor. Also, for *d* use the **molecular diameter of the carrier gas** (N₂ ≈ 0.37 nm, Ar ≈ 0.34 nm), not the cluster diameter. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications | Industry | Application | |---|---| | **MEMS / NEMS** | Ultra-thin metallization on released cantilevers, beams | | **Semiconductors** | Shallow junction formation (GCIB), low-energy ion implantation | | **Optoelectronics** | GaN, ZnO, ITO films for LEDs and solar cells | | **Data storage** | Smooth GMR/TMR magnetic multilayers for HDD read heads | | **Biomedical MEMS** | Drug-delivery coatings, biocompatible Ti/Ca-P films | | **Tribology** | DLC (diamond-like carbon) coatings on micro-gears | > [!TIP] > **GCIB vs ICB — Exam Tip:** Remember the **ICR** acronym: > - **I** — Ionization by electron impact > - **C** — Clusters formed in source (supersonic) > - **R** — (R)epetition: clusters fragmented on substrate > **GCIB** uses a *pre-formed gas* (e.g., CO₂, Ar/O₂) that is *condensed into clusters* — no separate vapor source. **ICB** uses *vaporized material* (e.g., metal vapour) that is condensed into clusters. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Free Path of a Cluster in the Source Chamber > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal:** Determine whether clusters will collide and coalesce (grow uncontrollably) during transit from the nozzle to the substrate. The criterion for **collisionless beam propagation** is: **L_chamber < λ**. From kinetic theory of gases, the collision frequency *Z* between a cluster (diameter *d_c*) and a background gas molecule (diameter *d_g*) moving with mean relative speed $\bar{v}_r$ is: $$Z = \sqrt{2}\,\pi\!\left(\dfrac{d_c + d_g}{2}\right)^{\!2}\,\bar{v}_r\,n_g$$ where $n_g = P / (k_B T)$ is the number density of the background gas. **Step 1.** Substitute $n_g = P/(k_B T)$ into the collision frequency equation: $$Z = \sqrt{2}\,\pi\!\left(\dfrac{d_c + d_g}{2}\right)^{\!2}\,\bar{v}_r \cdot \dfrac{P}{k_B T}$$ **Step 2.** The mean free path is defined as the *distance between successive collisions*, which is the *ratio of mean speed to collision frequency*: $$\lambda = \dfrac{\bar{v}_r}{Z} = \dfrac{\bar{v}_r}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\!\left(\dfrac{d_c + d_g}{2}\right)^{\!2}\,\bar{v}_r\,\dfrac{P}{k_B T}}$$ **Step 3.** Cancel $\bar{v}_r$ from numerator and denominator: $$\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\!\left(\dfrac{d_c + d_g}{2}\right)^{\!2} P}$$ **Step 4.** Define the **effective collision diameter** $d = (d_c + d_g)/2$: $$\boxed{\;\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 P}\;}$$ **Step 5 (Numerical sanity check).** For Ar gas at $T = 300\,\text{K}$, $P = 10^{-4}\,\text{Torr} = 1.33 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{Pa}$, and $d \approx 0.34 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}$: $$\lambda = \dfrac{(1.38\times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi (0.34\times 10^{-9})^2 (1.33\times 10^{-2})}$$ $$\lambda \approx \dfrac{4.14\times 10^{-21}}{6.81\times 10^{-21}} \approx 0.608\ \text{m}$$ The cluster (≈ 1 nm diameter) will travel ≈ **60 cm** before colliding with background Ar — sufficient for a typical 30-cm source chamber. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Per-Atom Kinetic Energy in an Accelerated Cluster > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal:** Show that for a fixed accelerating voltage, the per-atom energy **decreases inversely with cluster size** — the foundation of "soft-landing." **Step 1.** A cluster of *n* atoms, each of atomic mass *m_a*, carries charge *q = +e* (singly ionized). Total kinetic energy after acceleration through potential *V*: $$E_{\text{kin,total}} = qV = eV$$ **Step 2.** The kinetic energy is also expressed in terms of cluster velocity *v*: $$E_{\text{kin,total}} = \dfrac{1}{2}(n m_a) v^2$$ **Step 3.** Equating both expressions to solve for cluster velocity: $$\dfrac{1}{2} n m_a v^2 = eV \quad\Longrightarrow\quad v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{n m_a}}$$ **Step 4.** The kinetic energy **per atom** inside the cluster is: $$\dfrac{E_{\text{kin,total}}}{n} = \dfrac{eV}{n}$$ **Step 5.** Substituting typical KTU numerical values: $V = 5\,\text{kV} = 5000\,\text{V}$, $n = 1000$ atoms, $e = 1$: $$\dfrac{E}{n} = \dfrac{1 \times 5000}{1000} = 5\ \text{eV/atom}$$ Compare with a **singly-ionized single atom** accelerated at the same voltage: $E_{\text{single}} = 5000\,\text{eV}$. The cluster atom lands with **0.1%** the energy → **no penetration, no damage**. > [!TIP] > **KTU "Rule of Thumb" to memorize:** > - **E/n < 10 eV/atom** → Soft landing, surface migration → film growth > - **E/n ≈ 10–100 eV/atom** → Mild implantation, mixed growth/etch > - **E/n > 100 eV/atom** → Substrate sputtering → etching ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Adiabatic Expansion Through a Laval Nozzle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal:** Derive the terminal velocity of cluster-bearing gas exiting a supersonic nozzle — the heart of cluster source design. **Step 1.** For adiabatic (no heat exchange) expansion, energy conservation gives the **enthalpy balance** between stagnation (0) and exit (∞): $$h_0 = h_\infty + \dfrac{1}{2}v_\infty^2$$ With $h = C_p T$, this becomes: $$C_p T_0 = C_p T_\infty + \dfrac{1}{2}v_\infty^2$$ **Step 2.** Solve for exit velocity: $$v_\infty = \sqrt{2 C_p (T_0 - T_\infty)}$$ **Step 3.** Use $C_p = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\dfrac{k_B}{m}$ (specific heat per molecule) and $T_\infty \approx 0$ for strong expansion (Mach number ≫ 1): $$v_\infty = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\cdot \dfrac{k_B T_0}{m}}$$ **Step 4.** For **monatomic Ar** ($\gamma = 5/3$) at source temperature $T_0 = 1500\,\text{K}$, atomic mass $m = 6.64 \times 10^{-26}\,\text{kg}$: $$v_\infty = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(5/3)}{(5/3) - 1}\cdot \dfrac{(1.38\times 10^{-23})(1500)}{6.64\times 10^{-26}}}$$ $$v_\infty = \sqrt{5 \cdot \dfrac{2.07\times 10^{-20}}{6.64\times 10^{-26}}} = \sqrt{1.56\times 10^{6}} \approx 1248\ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 5 (Cluster size implication).** The cooled vapor at $T_\infty \approx$ a few K has supersaturation ratio $S \gg 1$, triggering homogeneous nucleation → cluster growth. $$\boxed{\;v_\infty = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\cdot \dfrac{k_B T_0}{m}}\;}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cluster Beam with Conventional PVD | Parameter | Thermal Evaporation | Sputtering | **Cluster Beam (ICB / GCIB)** | |---|---|---|---| | Adatom energy | 0.1–0.5 eV | 10–100 eV | **0.1–10 eV/atom** | | Substrate damage | None | High | **Negligible** | | Film adhesion | Poor | Good | **Excellent (≈ 10× stronger)** | | Step coverage | Poor | Conformal | **Conformal + filling** | | Epitaxy temperature | High | Moderate | **Low (room-temp possible)** | | Deposition rate | High | Moderate | **Moderate (1–10 nm/s)** | | Cluster control | N/A | N/A | **Tunable (size, charge, energy)** | | Best suited for | Thick metal films | Insulators, alloys | **MEMS, ultrathin films, multilayers** | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ionized Cluster Beam (ICB) Deposition System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SOURCE["Vapor Source Chamber (P ~ 10^-5 Torr)"] A1["Crucible / Knudsen Cell
Vaporizes Source Material
T = 1200 to 1800 K"] A2["Laval Nozzle
Converging-Diverging
Forms Supersonic Jet"] A1 --> A2 end A2 --> A3["Cluster Formation Zone
Adiabatic Expansion
Condensation via 3-Body Collision
N = 100 to 10000 atoms"] subgraph IONIZE["Ionization Chamber"] B1["Filament Emitter
Electron Beam E = 100 eV"] B2["Cluster Beam Path
Ionized Clusters: C_n^+"] B1 --> B2 end A3 --> B2 subgraph ACCEL["Acceleration Electrodes"] C1["Extractor Electrode
V = 0.5 to 10 kV"] C2["Ground Electrode
at Substrate Potential"] C1 --> C2 end B2 --> C1 subgraph DEPOS["Deposition Chamber (P ~ 10^-7 Torr)"] D1["Mass Filter (Optional)
Selects Cluster Size
