Equating: $$\frac{m_e v_\perp^2}{r_L} = e v_\perp B$$ Solving for the rotation (cyclotron) angular frequency: $$\omega_c = \frac{v_\perp}{r_L} = \frac{eB}{m_e}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the resonance condition Resonance occurs when the driving frequency matches the natural cyclotron frequency: $$\omega = \omega_c$$ $$2\pi f = \frac{eB}{m_e}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for the resonance magnetic field $$B_{res} = \frac{2\pi f \, m_e}{e}$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute numerical values for $f = 2.45\,\text{GHz}$ $$B_{res} = \frac{2\pi \times (2.45 \times 10^9) \times (9.109 \times 10^{-31})}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$ Breaking into simpler sub-computations: $$2\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^9 = 1.5394 \times 10^{10}\,\text{rad/s}$$ $$(1.5394 \times 10^{10}) \times (9.109 \times 10^{-31}) = 1.4023 \times 10^{-20}$$ $$\frac{1.4023 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 8.753 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{T}$$ $$\boxed{B_{res} \approx 0.0875\,\text{T} = 875\,\text{Gauss}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Free Path Argument for Low-Pressure ECRCVD Operation We want to show that ECRCVD requires **low pressure** so that the mean free path of electrons is comparable to or larger than the chamber dimensions, allowing efficient energy absorption before collisions. ### Step 1 — Define mean free path $$\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{1}{n \sigma}$$ where $n$ is the gas number density and $\sigma$ is the collision cross-section. ### Step 2 — Use ideal gas law $$p = n k_B T \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n = \frac{p}{k_B T}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute $$\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{k_B T}{p \, \sigma}$$ For Ar at $T = 300\,\text{K}$, $\sigma \approx 3 \times 10^{-20}\,\text{m}^2$, and $k_B T \approx 4.14 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{J}$: At $p = 10^{-3}\,\text{Torr} = 1.33 \times 10^{-1}\,\text{Pa}$: $$\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{(1.33 \times 10^{-1})(3 \times 10^{-20})} \approx 1.0\,\text{m}$$ This $\lambda_{mfp} \sim 1\,\text{m}$ is *larger* than typical chamber dimensions (~ 30 cm), so electrons travel many orbits between collisions — ideal for **resonant ECR energy absorption**. At $p = 1\,\text{Torr}$: $$\lambda_{mfp} \approx 1\,\text{mm}$$ Electrons collide too often to complete a cyclotron orbit → no resonance possible. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** ECRCVD requires $p < 10\,\text{mTorr}$ so that electron mean free path exceeds chamber size, enabling resonant energy absorption. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ion Energy Across the Plasma Sheath The ion bombarding energy at the substrate (in eV) equals the potential drop across the sheath times the electron charge: $$E_{ion}\,[\text{eV}] = e \cdot V_{sheath}\,[\text{V}] / e = V_{sheath}$$ For a standard PECVD driven at 13.56 MHz with self-bias: $$V_{sheath} \approx 50 - 200\,\text{V} \;\Rightarrow\; E_{ion} = 50 - 200\,\text{eV}$$ For ECRCVD, the divergent magnetic field creates a gentle potential gradient, and the substrate is typically placed **outside** the resonance zone: $$V_{sheath} \approx 10 - 30\,\text{V} \;\Rightarrow\; E_{ion} = 10 - 30\,\text{eV}$$ This **lower ion energy** is why ECRCVD films exhibit: - Less ion-bombardment damage - Lower surface trap densities - Better stoichiometry in dielectrics - Higher breakdown field --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PECVD vs. ECRCVD | Feature | Standard PECVD | ECRCVD | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Frequency | 13.56 MHz | 2.45 GHz | Microwave enables resonant absorption | | Magnetic field | Absent | ~875 G (resonant) | Confines electrons, enhances ionization | | Pressure | 0.1 – 10 Torr | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻² Torr | Low p → long mean free path needed | | Ionization % | < 1 % | > 10 % | ECR gives denser plasma | | Electron density | 10⁹ – 10¹⁰ cm⁻³ | 10¹¹ – 10¹² cm⁻³ | Higher density = higher rate & quality | | Ion energy | 50 – 200 eV | 10 – 30 eV | ECR = lower damage | | Substrate temp | 200 – 400 °C | RT – 300 °C | ECR enables low-T processing | | Film quality | Good | Excellent (low defect, low H) | ECR used for advanced gate dielectrics | | Cost | Moderate | High (magnets, MW source) | ECR is for premium applications | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Typical PECVD System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Gas Cylinder Bank
SiH4 N2O NH3 N2] --> B[Mass Flow Controllers
MFC1 MFC2 MFC3] B --> C[Gas Mixing Manifold] C --> D[Showerhead
Top Electrode] E[RF Generator
13.56 MHz] --> F[Auto Match Network] F --> D G[Vacuum Pump
Rotary plus Turbo] --> H[Reaction Chamber] D --> H H --> I[Heated Substrate Stage
Bottom Electrode 200 to 400 C] I --> H H --> G I --> J[Temperature Controller] H --> K[Optical Emission Monitor
OES Endpoint Detection] H --> L[Throttle Valve
Pressure Control] L --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > The dual role of the bottom electrode (substrate stage) — it is **electrically connected to ground via the match network** and also serves as the **mechanical heater**. This is the standard parallel-plate PECVD configuration. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram of an ECRCVD Reactor ```mermaid flowchart TB A[2.45 GHz Microwave Source
Magnetron] --> B[Waveguide
WR-340] B --> C[Quartz Vacuum Window
Couples MW into plasma] D[Magnetic Coil Top
Upper Solenoid] --> E[ECR Resonance Zone
B approx 875 G] C --> E E --> F[Plasma Stream
Dense ECR Plasma] F --> G[Divergent Magnetic Field Region] G --> H[Substrate Stage
Outside Resonance Zone] I[Magnetic Coil Bottom
Lower Solenoid] --> G J[Gas Inlet Ring
SiH4 plus O2 plus Ar] --> E K[Vacuum Pump
10 mTorr Regime] --> H H --> K H --> L[Heater plus Bias Supply
Optional RF Bias] ``` > [!NOTE] > The substrate is **deliberately placed outside the resonance zone**, downstream along the diverging magnetic field. This separation gives ECRCVD its signature **low-damage, high-quality** film deposition. --- ## 4.3 Functional Flow — How an ECR Plasma is Generated ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Static B field applied
875 Gauss at resonance plane] --> B[Electrons undergo helical motion
cyclotron frequency equals MW frequency] B --> C[Resonant energy absorption
Electron gains keV equivalent temperature] C --> D[Electron impact ionization
gas density peaks at 10 to the power 12 per cc] D --> E[Ions plus radicals transported
along diverging B field] E --> F[Low energy ions plus radicals
strike substrate] F --> G[High quality film growth
SiO2 Si3N4 DLC etc] ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Direct vs. Remote PECVD Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DIRECT[Direct PECVD] A1[RF Electrode Top] --> A2[Plasma Region] A2 --> A3[Wafer on RF Electrode Bottom] end subgraph REMOTE[Remote PECVD] B1[RF Coil Upstream] --> B2[Plasma Tube] B2 --> B3[Radical Transport Line] B3 --> B4[Wafer outside plasma
No ion bombardment] end ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Plasma Enhanced Chemical Vapor Deposition (PECVD). Why is it preferred over thermal CVD for post-metallization MEMS processing? `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Set A]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** PECVD is a thin-film deposition technique in which a glow-discharge plasma, generated by an RF (typically 13.56 MHz) electric field, supplies the activation energy required to dissociate precursor gases. This allows film deposition at substrate temperatures (**200 – 400 °C**) much lower than thermal CVD (**600 – 900 °C**). **Reason for preference (1.5 marks):** In MEMS, dielectric layers must often be deposited **after metal interconnect formation and even after structural release**. High temperatures would (i) melt or diffuse Al/Cu metallization, (ii) cause thermal-stress-induced deformation of released MEMS structures (e.g., beams curling), and (iii) degrade polymeric or biological coatings. The low substrate temperature of PECVD avoids these problems. --- ### Question 2: What is the resonance condition for Electron Cyclotron Resonance (ECR)? Compute the resonance magnetic field for a 2.45 GHz microwave source. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Resonance condition (1.5 marks):** ECR occurs when the angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ of the applied microwave field equals the electron cyclotron angular frequency $\omega_c = \dfrac{eB}{m_e}$: $$2\pi f = \frac{eB}{m_e}$$ **Numerical computation (1.5 marks):** $$B_{res} = \frac{2\pi f \, m_e}{e} = \frac{2\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^9 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$\boxed{B_{res} \approx 0.0875\,\text{T} = 875\,\text{G}}$$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A: Explain the construction and working of a Plasma Enhanced CVD system. Discuss its advantages over thermal CVD for MEMS applications. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Set B | 14 Marks]` #### Part (a) — Construction and Working of PECVD (7 marks) **Construction (3.5 marks):** A PECVD system comprises the following sub-systems: 1. **Gas delivery system** — Gas cylinders (e.g., SiH₄, N₂O, NH₃, N₂, Ar), mass-flow controllers, and a mixing manifold feeding a **showerhead** inlet at the top of the chamber. 2. **Reaction chamber** — A vacuum-sealed aluminium or stainless-steel chamber with viewports, a **top showerhead electrode** (often the powered electrode) and a **bottom substrate-stage electrode** (grounded and heated). 3. **Plasma generation system** — **13.56 MHz RF generator** connected via an **automatic impedance-matching network** to the top electrode; this minimizes reflected power and sustains a stable plasma. 4. **Substrate heating system** — Resistive heaters (or halogen lamps) integrated into the bottom electrode capable of **200 – 400 °C** with ± 1 °C control. 5. **Vacuum & pressure control** — A combination of rotary + turbo-molecular pump achieving base vacuum of **10⁻⁶ Torr**, and a **throttle valve** controlling process pressure in the **0.1 – 10 Torr** range. 6. **Process monitoring** — Optical emission spectroscopy (OES) for endpoint detection; manometers for pressure; thermocouples for temperature. **Working (3.5 marks):** 1. Substrate is loaded and the chamber pumped down to base vacuum. 2. Substrate is heated to the process temperature (e.g., 300 °C). 3. Reactant gases flow at controlled rates; chamber pressure is stabilized via the throttle valve. 4. RF power is applied; the alternating electric field accelerates free electrons, which undergo ionizing collisions, igniting a **glow discharge**. 5. Energetic electrons dissociate precursors into radicals, e.g.: $$\text{SiH}_4 + e^{-} \rightarrow \text{SiH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet} + e^{-}$$ 6. Radicals diffuse to the substrate, adsorb, and react to form the film: $$\text{SiH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{N}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + \text{H}_2 + \text{N}_2 \quad \text{(for SiO}_2\text{)}$$ 7. Volatile by-products are removed by the vacuum pump. #### Part (b) — Advantages Over Thermal CVD for MEMS (7 marks) 1. **Low substrate temperature (200 – 400 °C)** — Critical for MEMS since metallization (Al, Au) is already present and released mechanical structures cannot tolerate high thermal stress. 2. **Compatibility with post-release processing** — Allows dielectric encapsulation of released comb drives, cantilevers, and membranes. 3. **Good step coverage** — Plasma-generated radicals are highly reactive and conform to high-aspect-ratio MEMS features (e.g., deep trenches, via holes). 4. **Versatile film chemistries** — SiO₂, Si₃N₄, SiON, a-Si:H, amorphous carbon from simple gas mixtures. 5. **Fast deposition rates** — 10 – 100 nm/min, comparable to thermal CVD. 6. **In-situ plasma cleaning** — The same plasma source can be used for pre-dep surface preparation, improving film adhesion. 7. **Lower thermal-budget processing** — Enables 3D integrated MEMS/IC stacks without damaging lower layers. --- ### Question B: With a neat schematic, describe the working principle of Electron Cyclotron Resonance CVD (ECRCVD). Derive the resonance condition and explain why ECRCVD produces higher-quality films than conventional PECVD. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]` #### Part (a) — Schematic and Working Principle (7 marks) **Schematic description (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 above.)* The ECRCVD reactor consists of a **2.45 GHz microwave source** (magnetron) coupled via a **rectangular waveguide** and a **quartz vacuum window** into a cylindrical **ECR source chamber**. Two coaxial magnetic coils produce a divergent axial magnetic field with $B \approx 875\,\text{G}$ at the resonance plane. The substrate stage is located *downstream*, outside the resonance zone. Process gases (e.g., SiH₄ + O₂) are introduced near the resonance zone. A high-vacuum pump maintains pressure at **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻² Torr**. **Working principle (4 marks):** 1. A static magnetic field $B$ is established; at the resonance plane $B = 875\,\text{G}$, electrons gyrate at the cyclotron frequency $\omega_c = eB/m_e$. 2. 2.45 GHz microwave enters the chamber; its angular frequency $\omega_{MW} = 2\pi f$ exactly matches $\omega_c$. 3. The rotating microwave electric field continuously accelerates the gyrating electrons — **resonant energy absorption**. 4. Electrons reach energies of several eV, **efficiently ionizing** the process gas. Plasma densities of **10¹¹ – 10¹² cm⁻³** are achieved (ionization efficiency **> 10 %**). 5. The divergent magnetic field transports the plasma from the resonance zone to the substrate as a columnated beam. 6. The substrate is **electrically floating** (or may have a small RF bias). Ions arrive with **low energy (10 – 30 eV)** because the sheath potential is small. 7. Radicals and low-energy ions react at the substrate surface, depositing high-quality, low-damage films. #### Part (b) — Derivation of Resonance Condition and Film-Quality Justification (7 marks) **Derivation (3.5 marks):** *(See Section 3.1 derivation above for full step-by-step.)* Equating Lorentz force to centripetal force for an electron in magnetic field $B$: $$\frac{m_e v_\perp^2}{r_L} = ev_\perp B \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_c = \frac{eB}{m_e}$$ Resonance requires: $$\omega = \omega_c \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2\pi f = \frac{eB}{m_e} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_{res} = \frac{2\pi f\, m_e}{e}$$ For $f = 2.45\,\text{GHz}$, $B_{res} \approx 875\,\text{G}$. **Why ECRCVD gives higher-quality films (3.5 marks):** 1. **Higher plasma density** — 10¹¹ – 10¹² cm⁻³ ensures a larger flux of reactive radicals per unit time → better surface coverage and stoichiometry. 2. **Lower ion energy (10 – 30 eV)** — Reduces ion-bombardment damage to the growing film. Conventional PECVD has ions at 50 – 200 eV which create point defects, dangling bonds, and traps. 3. **Low operating pressure (10⁻⁴ – 10⁻² Torr)** — Longer mean free path → fewer gas-phase collisions → cleaner radical chemistry and less particle generation. 4. **Decoupling of plasma generation from substrate** — The wafer sits outside the resonance zone, so it is not directly exposed to the harsh microwave field, unlike in some PECVD configurations. 5. **Lower hydrogen content in films** — Important for low-k dielectrics and gate oxides where Si-H and N-H bonds degrade dielectric performance. 6. **Lower substrate temperature capability (RT – 300 °C)** — Enables deposition on temperature-sensitive substrates (polymer MEMS, flexible electronics). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of $2\pi$** in the resonance formula — students often write $B = fm/e$ instead of $B = 2\pi fm/e$, losing 2 marks in derivation questions. > 2. **Confusing plasma frequency with cyclotron frequency** — $\omega_p = \sqrt{n_e e^2/(\varepsilon_0 m_e)}$ is a *different* quantity and is **not** used in the ECR condition. > 3. **Stating "PECVD uses plasma to heat the wafer"** — this is incorrect. The plasma energizes the *gas-phase electrons*, not the wafer directly. The wafer is heated by a separate heater. > 4. **Omitting units** — Always quote $B_{res}$ in both **Tesla** (SI) **and Gauss** (CGS) since KTU examiners use both. > 5. **Writing "ECR plasma operates at 13.56 MHz"** — wrong; ECR uses **2.45 GHz** microwave, while standard PECVD uses **13.56 MHz** RF. > 6. **Confusing sheath potential with plasma potential** — ion energy at the substrate equals sheath drop, not the bulk plasma potential. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize for KTU exams):** - **PECVD = Plasma-Enhanced CVD.** Uses RF (13.56 MHz) plasma to lower deposition temperature to 200 – 400 °C. - **ECR condition:** $B_{res} = 2\pi f m_e / e$. For 2.45 GHz → **875 Gauss (0.0875 T)**. - **ECR uses 2.45 GHz microwave**, NOT 13.56 MHz. - **Pressure regimes:** PECVD 0.1 – 10 Torr; ECRCVD 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻² Torr. - **Ion energies:** PECVD 50 – 200 eV; ECRCVD 10 – 30 eV. - **Plasma densities:** PECVD 10⁹ – 10¹⁰ cm⁻³; ECRCVD 10¹¹ – 10¹² cm⁻³. - **Ionization efficiency:** PECVD < 1 %; ECRCVD > 10 %. - **Substrate is placed *outside* the ECR resonance zone** in ECRCVD. - **Direct PECVD** — plasma touches wafer; **Remote PECVD** — only radicals reach wafer. - **Why MEMS uses PECVD:** low thermal budget preserves metallization and released structures. - **Standard PECVD films for MEMS:** SiO₂, Si₃N₄, a-Si:H, SiON, amorphous carbon. - **Standard ECRCVD films:** high-quality SiO₂, Si₃N₄, diamond-like carbon, high-κ oxides. - **Formulae to memorize:** Arrhenius, ECR resonance, Larmor radius, mean free path, sheath voltage relation. - **Advantages of ECRCVD over PECVD:** higher plasma density, lower ion damage, lower pressure, lower substrate temperature. - **Disadvantages of ECRCVD:** high equipment cost (magnets, microwave source), more complex tuning. - **Direct-plasma damage mechanisms:** ion bombardment, charge buildup, UV photon damage, contamination from chamber walls.

topics.title: Dry Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dry Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dry Etching** is a **vapor-phase, non-wet** material removal process used in MEMS and semiconductor fabrication in which the substrate is exposed to either a **reactive gaseous plasma**, a **beam of energetic inert ions**, or a combination of both, to selectively remove exposed material **without** the use of liquid chemical etchants.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT632 — Micro Electro Mechanical Systems), **Dry Etching** is the third major patterning/removal technique taught under **Module 3: Micromachining Processes**, sitting alongside wet chemical etching, bulk micromachining, and surface micromachining. It is the **workhorse process** for sub-micron, high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures.

The three principal categories recognized by the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Physical Dry Etching** — pure momentum-transfer (e.g., Sputter Etching / Ion Beam Etching)
2. **Plasma Etching (Chemical Dry Etching)** — purely chemical reaction between radicals and the film
3. **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** — synergistic combination of physical bombardment + chemical reaction
4. **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** — high-aspect-ratio variant (e.g., Bosch process)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Pressure Range:** Dry etching is typically performed at **0.1 Pa to 100 Pa** (≈ 1 mTorr – 750 mTorr), orders of magnitude below atmospheric pressure (≈ 10⁵ Pa).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of removing a stone sculpture from a rock face. You have **two extreme options**:

| Method | Real-World Analogy | Etching Equivalent |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Hosing it with a powerful sandblaster** | High-pressure grit physically chips off material — works on *anything* but is messy and rough | **Sputter / Ion Beam Etching** (Physical) |
| **Pouring acid that chemically dissolves the rock** | Highly selective (acid attacks rock, ignores metal pipes) but spreads sideways | **Wet Chemical Etching** |
| **Spraying a reactive gas while firing soft sand at it** | Both reaction *and* momentum work together — fast, straight walls | **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** |
| **Alternating "etch then protect" pulses like a chiseler** | Carves perfectly vertical trenches hundreds of times deeper than wide | **Deep RIE (Bosch Process)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Dry Etching = **Plasma + Vacuum**. The plasma provides the "reactive fingers," and the vacuum ensures the reactive species travel in straight lines (no collisions) so they only attack *where they are aimed*.

---

## 1.3 Why Dry Etching Dominates MEMS Fabrication

- **Anisotropy**: Produces **near-vertical sidewalls** essential for high-aspect-ratio MEMS (comb drives, accelerometers, gyroscopes).
- **Sub-micron resolution**: Pattern fidelity below **100 nm** is routine.
- **CMOS compatibility**: Does not leave ionic residues that destroy integrated electronics — critical for **monolithic MEMS + IC integration**.
- **Process control**: Endpoint detection via optical emission spectroscopy (OES).
- **Automation-friendly**: Plasma tools are cluster-compatible (load-lock systems).

---

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Always Bolded in KTU Answers)

- **Etch Rate (ER)** — nm/min or µm/min
- **Selectivity (S)** — dimensionless ratio
- **Anisotropy (A)** — dimensionless ratio (0 to 1)
- **Uniformity (U)** — % across wafer
- **Aspect Ratio** — depth / width
- **Mean Free Path (λ)** — cm at typical plasma pressures

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Plasma: The Heart of Dry Etching

Dry etching is fundamentally a **plasma-driven process**. Understanding plasma physics is non-negotiable for KTU answers.

### 2.1.1 What is a Plasma?

> A **plasma** is a **quasi-neutral ionized gas** containing roughly equal densities of **positive ions**, **negative electrons**, and **neutral radicals**, exhibiting **collective behavior** under electromagnetic fields.

### 2.1.2 Plasma Generation in RIE Chambers

- **RF Source (13.56 MHz)** is applied between two parallel electrodes inside a vacuum chamber.
- Free electrons (lighter, mobile) accelerate first and collide with neutral gas molecules (e.g., SF₆, CF₄, Cl₂, O₂).
- Collisions produce:
  - **Ions** (e.g., SF₅⁺, Cl⁺) — heavy, accelerated toward substrate by DC bias
  - **Radicals** (e.g., F•, Cl•, O•) — neutral, chemically reactive, diffuse to surface
  - **Secondary electrons** — sustain the discharge (avalanche)

### 2.1.3 Sheath Formation and DC Bias

The mobile electrons strike electrode surfaces faster than the heavy ions → electrodes accumulate a **negative DC self-bias (V_dc)**. A positively-charged **plasma sheath** forms adjacent to the wafer, accelerating ions **perpendicular** to the substrate — this is the **physical origin of anisotropy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sheath voltage ≈ 10 V – 1000 V** depending on RF power, pressure, and electrode geometry. This is the ion-bombardment energy.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Dry Etching (KTU Mandatory Knowledge)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly demands the classification below:

```
                        DRY ETCHING
                              |
        -----------------------------------------------------
        |                       |                           |
   PHYSICAL               CHEMICAL                    PHYSICO-CHEMICAL
   (Sputter / IBE)        (Plasma Etch)               (RIE / DRIE)
        |                       |                           |
   Ar+ ions              F•, Cl• radicals         Ions + Radicals together
   No chemistry          No bombardment           Synergistic effect
   Highly anisotropic    Isotropic (low power)    Highly anisotropic + selective
```

### 2.2.1 Physical Dry Etching (Sputter Etching / Ion Beam Etching — IBE)

- **Mechanism**: Inert gas (usually **Ar⁺**) ions accelerated to **0.5 keV – 2 keV** strike the wafer.
- **Knock-on momentum transfer** ejects surface atoms.
- **Etch rate**: governed by **Sputter Yield Y** (atoms ejected per incident ion).
- **Pros**: extremely anisotropic.
- **Cons**: poor selectivity (etches almost everything), radiation damage, low etch rate (**20–100 nm/min**).

### 2.2.2 Plasma Etching (Chemical Dry Etching)

- **Mechanism**: only **radicals** react with film to form **volatile products**.
- **Example**: CF₄ + O₂ plasma etching of Si → SiF₄ (gas, volatile).
- **Pros**: excellent selectivity (often > 50:1), no damage.
- **Cons**: tends toward **isotropy** at low pressures because radicals diffuse laterally.

### 2.2.3 Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)

- **Mechanism**: combines both — ions provide directional bombardment + radicals provide chemical reaction.
- **Synergy**: ion bombardment *damages* the surface making it more reactive to radicals (chemical enhancement) AND helps **desorb** reaction products (physical enhancement).
- **Typical conditions**: 1–100 Pa, 100–1000 W RF, V_dc ≈ 200–800 V.

### 2.2.4 Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) — Bosch Process

The flagship process for **HAR (High Aspect Ratio)** MEMS — e.g., through-silicon vias (TSV), inertial sensors, micro-fluidic channels.

- **Bosch Process** = rapid alternation of three steps:
  1. **Etch step** (e.g., SF₆, isotropic Si etch by F radicals — a few seconds).
  2. **Passivation step** (e.g., C₄F₈, deposits a fluorocarbon polymer (–CF₂–)ₙ on all surfaces — a few seconds).
  3. **Ion bombardment** during next etch step removes passivation from horizontal surfaces only, exposing Si to F radicals again; sidewalls remain protected.
- **Result**: vertical sidewalls with characteristic **scalloping** (≈ 50–500 nm amplitude).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** — they appear in **at least 70%** of past KTU Module-3 numerical questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Mean Free Path** | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P}$ | m | $k_B$ = Boltzmann const, $d$ = molecular diameter, $P$ = pressure |
| 2 | **Plasma Frequency (electrons)** | $\omega_{pe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{n_e e^{2}}{m_e \varepsilon_0}}$ | rad/s | $n_e$ = electron density, $m_e$ = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg |
| 3 | **Debye Length** | $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^{2}}}$ | m | Measure of sheath thickness |
| 4 | **Etch Rate** | $ER = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | nm/min or µm/min | $\Delta d$ = depth etched |
| 5 | **Selectivity** | $S = \dfrac{ER_{\text{film}}}{ER_{\text{mask}}}$ | dimensionless | Higher is better |
| 6 | **Anisotropy** | $A = 1 - \dfrac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | A = 1 → perfectly vertical |
| 7 | **Sputter Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | dimensionless | Depends on ion energy & mass |
| 8 | **Aspect Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{width}}$ | dimensionless | DRIE can exceed 30:1 |
| 9 | **Knudsen Number** | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | dimensionless | $L$ = chamber dimension |
| 10 | **Uniformity** | $U = \dfrac{ER_{\max} - ER_{\min}}{2 \cdot ER_{\text{avg}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Lower is better |
| 11 | **DC Bias (approx)** | $V_{dc} \propto \sqrt{P_{RF}/P}$ | V | Empirical scaling |
| 12 | **Ion Energy at Cathode** | $E_{ion} \approx e \cdot V_{dc}$ | eV | If no collisions in sheath |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Dry Etching is Used

- **Inertial Sensors** (accelerometers, gyroscopes) — Bosch DRIE on SOI wafers.
- **RF MEMS** — RIE of Al, Au, Si, SiO₂ for switches and FBAR resonators.
- **Microfluidics** — DRIE for through-wafer channels.
- **TSVs (Through-Silicon Vias)** — DRIE for 3D IC stacking.
- **Photonic Crystals** — Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) RIE for vertical holes in Si.
- **Hard-disk drive heads** — IBE for precise milling.
- **CMOS gates** — RIE of polysilicon and metal layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sputter Yield from Linear Cascade Theory

> **Problem (KTU Style):** An Ar⁺ ion of mass $m_i$ strikes a silicon target atom of mass $m_t$ at rest. Using hard-sphere (binary collision) approximation, find the **maximum energy transfer** to the target atom.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Two-Body Collision

Conservation of **momentum** and **kinetic energy** apply. Let $v_i$ = initial ion velocity, $v_i'$ = ion velocity after collision, $v_t'$ = target velocity after collision.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Momentum:}\quad & m_i v_i = m_i v_i' + m_t v_t' \\
\text{Energy:}\quad & \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i'^{\,2} + \tfrac{1}{2} m_t v_t'^{\,2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $v_t'$

Multiply momentum equation by $v_i'$:
$$
m_i v_i v_i' = m_i v_i'^{\,2} + m_t v_t' v_i'
$$

Subtract from the energy equation (×2):
$$
m_i v_i^{2} - 2 m_i v_i v_i' + m_i v_i'^{\,2} = m_t v_t'^{\,2} - 2 m_t v_t' v_i'
$$

$$
(m_i v_i - m_i v_i')^{2} = m_t v_t'(v_t' - 2 v_i')
$$

But from momentum: $v_i - v_i' = \dfrac{m_t}{m_i} v_t'$. Substitute:

$$
\left(m_t v_t'\right)^{2} = m_t v_t'(v_t' - 2 v_i') \;\Rightarrow\; m_t v_t' = v_t' - 2 v_i'
$$

Solving for $v_t'$:

$$
v_t' = \frac{2 m_i v_i}{m_i + m_t}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Maximum Energy Transfer

Kinetic energy of target after collision:
$$
E_t' = \tfrac{1}{2} m_t v_t'^{\,2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_t \left(\frac{2 m_i v_i}{m_i + m_t}\right)^{2}
$$

Original ion kinetic energy:
$$
E_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i^{2}
$$

**Energy transfer fraction:**
$$
\boxed{\;\frac{E_t'}{E_i} = \frac{4 m_i m_t}{(m_i + m_t)^{2}}\;}
$$

This is maximized when $m_i = m_t$ (i.e., **use Ar to sputter Si? No — Ar (40) vs Si (28)** — momentum transfer is highly efficient).

**Numerical check** ($m_i = 40$ u, $m_t = 28$ u):

$$
\frac{E_t'}{E_i} = \frac{4 \times 40 \times 28}{(40 + 28)^{2}} = \frac{4480}{4624} \approx 0.969
$$

So ~97% of kinetic energy can be transferred to a Si atom in a *head-on* collision with Ar⁺ — explaining why Ar is a near-universal sputter gas.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Etch Rate and Selectivity in RIE

**Given:**
- Vertical etch rate in Si = 500 nm/min
- Lateral etch rate = 25 nm/min
- Etch rate of SiO₂ mask = 25 nm/min
- Etch depth target = 5 µm

### Compute Anisotropy $A$:

$$
A = 1 - \frac{ER_{\text{lat}}}{ER_{\text{vert}}} = 1 - \frac{25}{500} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95
$$

### Compute Selectivity $S$ (Si over SiO₂):

$$
S = \frac{ER_{\text{Si}}}{ER_{\text{SiO}_2}} = \frac{500}{25} = 20
$$

### Time to etch 5 µm of Si:

$$
t = \frac{d}{ER_{\text{vert}}} = \frac{5000 \text{ nm}}{500 \text{ nm/min}} = 10 \text{ min}
$$

### Mask thickness consumed:

$$
d_{\text{mask}} = ER_{\text{SiO}_2} \times t = 25 \times 10 = 250 \text{ nm}
$$

So the mask must be **at least 250 nm** thick — KTU examiners love this multi-step calc.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean Free Path in an RIE Chamber

**Given:** Ar gas at $T = 300$ K, $P = 5$ Pa, molecular diameter $d = 3.66 \times 10^{-10}$ m.

$$
\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P}
$$

Plug in: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T = 300$ K, $P = 5$ Pa.

$$
\lambda = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi (3.66 \times 10^{-10})^{2} \times 5}
$$

Numerator: $1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 = 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J.

Denominator:
$$
\sqrt{2}\,\pi (1.34 \times 10^{-19}) \times 5 = 4.443 \times 1.34 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \approx 2.98 \times 10^{-18}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{2.98 \times 10^{-18}} \approx 1.39 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.39 \text{ mm}
$$

So at 5 Pa, an Ar atom travels ~1.4 mm before colliding — far smaller than typical chamber size (~30 cm), so plasma is **collisional**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Etching Techniques

| Parameter | Wet Etching | Plasma Etch | Sputter (IBE) | RIE | DRIE (Bosch) |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|---------------|-----|--------------|
| Anisotropy | Low (crystal-dependent) | Low | Very High | High | Very High |
| Selectivity | Very High | High | Low | Medium-High | High |
| Etch Rate | Fast (µm/min) | Slow–Medium | Slow | Medium | High |
| Aspect Ratio | ~1:1 | ~1:1 | ~3:1 | ~10:1 | >30:1 |
| Damage | None | None | High | Medium | Medium |
| CMOS Compatible | Risky | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Mask Required | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (thick) |
| Scalloping | No | No | No | No | Yes (micro) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Dry Etching Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DRY ETCHING] --> B[PHYSICAL]
    A --> C[CHEMICAL PLASMA]
    A --> D[PHYSICO CHEMICAL]

    B --> B1[Sputter Etching]
    B --> B2[Ion Beam Etching IBE]

    C --> C1[Radical Etching]
    C --> C2[Downstream Plasma]

    D --> D1[Reactive Ion Etching RIE]
    D --> D2[Inductively Coupled Plasma RIE]
    D --> D3[Deep RIE Bosch]

    B1 --> B1A[Ar ions 500 to 2000 eV]
    B1 --> B1B[Knock on momentum transfer]
    B2 --> B2A[Kaufman ion source]
    C1 --> C1A[F radicals from CF4 plus O2]
    D1 --> D1A[Synergy of ions plus radicals]
    D3 --> D3A[Alternating etch and passivation]
    D3 --> D3B[Scalloped sidewalls]
```

## 4.2 RIE Chamber — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    RF[RF Generator 13.56 MHz] --> MATCH[Matching Network]
    MATCH --> UPPER[Upper Electrode Anode]
    LOWER[Lower Electrode Cathode Wafer] --> BIAS[DC Bias Vdc]
    BIAS --> PUMP[Vacuum Pump 0.1 to 100 Pa]

    GAS[Gas Inlet SF6 CF4 Cl2 Ar O2] --> CHAMBER[Plasma Chamber Quartz or Al]
    CHAMBER --> UPPER
    CHAMBER --> LOWER

    CHAMBER --> RAD[Radicals F Cl O]
    CHAMBER --> IONS[Positive Ions SF5 plus Cl plus]
    RAD --> SURFACE[Wafer Surface Chemical Etch]
    IONS --> SURFACE
    IONS --> BOMB[Physical Bombardment]
    SURFACE --> PROD[Volatile Products SiF4 CO2]
    PROD --> EXHAUST[Exhaust to Pump]

    MONITOR[OES Endpoint Detector] --- CHAMBER
    MONITOR --> SIGNAL[Plasma Emission 440 nm SiF etc]
```

## 4.3 Bosch DRIE Process Cycle Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    STEP0[Start of Cycle Si exposed] --> S1[Step 1 SF6 Etch 5 to 10 s]
    S1 --> S1A[Isotropic Si etch by F radicals]
    S1A --> S2[Step 2 C4F8 Passivation 5 to 10 s]
    S2 --> S2A[Fluorocarbon polymer CF2 deposits on all surfaces]
    S2A --> S3[Step 3 Ion Bombardment Next Cycle]
    S3 --> S3A[Vertical ions remove polymer from bottom only]
    S3A --> S4[Sidewalls remain protected Polymer preserved]
    S4 --> S5[Vertical Etch Continues]
    S5 --> CHECK{Desired depth reached}
    CHECK -->|No| S1
    CHECK -->|Yes| END[Stop Process Scalloped HAR sidewalls]
```

## 4.4 Etch Profile Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mask Opening Width w] --> B1[Wet Etch Anisotropic crystal]
    A --> B2[Isotropic Plasma]
    A --> B3[Sputter Etch Highly Directional]
    A --> B4[RIE Anisotropic]
    A --> B5[DRIE Bosch Scalloped Vertical]

    B1 --> R1[54.74 degree sidewalls Si 100]
    B2 --> R2[Semicircular undercut]
    B3 --> R3[Straight vertical no undercut]
    B4 --> R4[Slight taper or vertical]
    B5 --> R5[Scalloped vertical high aspect ratio]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Exam]**
*Define dry etching. List any two advantages of dry etching over wet etching in MEMS fabrication.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Dry etching** is a vapor-phase material removal technique in which a substrate is etched using **reactive gases, ion beams, or plasma** in a low-pressure environment, without using liquid chemicals.

**Any two advantages** (1.5 marks each):
1. **Higher anisotropy** — produces near-vertical sidewalls essential for high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures (comb drives, accelerometers).
2. **Better process control** — small feature sizes (< 1 µm), reproducibility, and compatibility with photoresist masks.
3. **CMOS-compatible** — no ionic contamination that degrades integrated circuits.
4. **Smaller undercut** — critical dimension control.
5. **Endpoint detection** possible via optical emission spectroscopy.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is anisotropy in dry etching? A process gives vertical etch rate = 800 nm/min and lateral etch rate = 40 nm/min. Calculate the anisotropy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Anisotropy $A$ is the degree to which etching proceeds preferentially in the vertical direction relative to the lateral direction.

$$
A = 1 - \frac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}}
$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
A = 1 - \frac{40}{800} = 1 - 0.05 = \mathbf{0.95}
$$

> High anisotropy (closer to 1) indicates nearly vertical sidewalls.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, **Part B questions carry an internal choice**. You must attempt **one** of the two alternatives provided. Each Part-B carries 14 marks, split typically into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** system. Discuss the role of plasma, DC self-bias, and the sheath in achieving anisotropic etching. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon wafer is etched in an RIE system using SF₆ plasma. The vertical etch rate of Si is 600 nm/min and the etch rate of the SiO₂ mask is 30 nm/min. If the required etch depth is 4 µm, calculate: **(i)** Selectivity, **(ii)** Anisotropy (given lateral ER = 30 nm/min), **(iii)** Mask thickness required, **(iv)** Etch time. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) RIE Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**

An RIE system consists of:
- A **vacuum chamber** (typically aluminum or quartz) evacuated to **0.1 – 100 Pa**.
- **Two parallel electrodes** — the upper (anode, grounded) and the lower (cathode) where the wafer sits.
- An **RF power supply** at **13.56 MHz** connected via a **matching network** to the lower electrode.
- **Gas inlets** for etchant gases (SF₆, CF₄, Cl₂, O₂, Ar).
- A **vacuum pump** system (rotary + turbomolecular).
- **Optical emission spectrometer (OES)** for endpoint detection.

**Working (3 marks):**

1. Etchant gas (e.g., SF₆) is introduced; RF field ionizes the gas, creating a **plasma**.
2. Plasma contains **ions** (SF₅⁺), **electrons**, and **radicals** (F•).
3. Because electrons are far more mobile, the cathode accumulates a **negative DC self-bias (V_dc ≈ 200–800 V)**.
4. A positively-charged **sheath** forms near the wafer; ions are accelerated **perpendicularly** across the sheath toward the wafer surface.
5. Vertical ion bombardment **damages the Si surface**, making it more reactive to F radicals — **chemical etch** occurs forming volatile SiF₄.
6. The **synergy** of physical bombardment + chemical reaction yields **high anisotropy + reasonable selectivity**.

**Role of Plasma, DC Bias, Sheath (1 mark):**

> Plasma provides reactive species; DC bias establishes ion energy; sheath ensures directional (anisotropic) ion trajectories perpendicular to the wafer.

---

#### (b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:** $ER_{\text{Si, vert}} = 600$ nm/min, $ER_{\text{SiO}_2} = 30$ nm/min, $ER_{\text{Si, lat}} = 30$ nm/min, target depth $d = 4\ \mu\text{m} = 4000$ nm.

**(i) Selectivity (2 marks):**

$$
S = \frac{ER_{\text{Si}}}{ER_{\text{SiO}_2}} = \frac{600}{30} = \mathbf{20:1}
$$

**(ii) Anisotropy (2 marks):**

$$
A = 1 - \frac{ER_{\text{lat}}}{ER_{\text{vert}}} = 1 - \frac{30}{600} = 1 - 0.05 = \mathbf{0.95}
$$

**(iii) Etch time (1.5 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{d}{ER_{\text{vert}}} = \frac{4000 \text{ nm}}{600 \text{ nm/min}} = \mathbf{6.67 \text{ min}}
$$

**(iv) Mask thickness required (1.5 marks):**

$$
d_{\text{mask}} = ER_{\text{SiO}_2} \times t = 30 \times 6.67 \approx \mathbf{200 \text{ nm}}
$$

Add a safety factor of ~20% → use a **240 nm SiO₂ mask**.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]**

#### **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bosch Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** process with a process-flow diagram. Discuss the formation of **scalloping** and its effect on sidewall roughness. How is HAR (high aspect ratio > 20:1) achieved? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Sputter Etching, Plasma Etching, and RIE** in terms of mechanism, anisotropy, selectivity, etch rate, and applications. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) Bosch DRIE Process (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**

> The Bosch process (patented by Robert Bosch GmbH, 1990s) is a **time-multiplexed** DRIE technique used to etch **high-aspect-ratio** structures (aspect ratio > 30:1) in silicon using **alternating etch and passivation cycles**.

**Process Flow (4 marks):**

Each cycle (~10–20 seconds) consists of two alternating steps:

1. **Etch step (SF₆, ~5–10 s):**
   - SF₆ plasma generates F• radicals.
   - F• isotropically etches exposed Si → SiF₄ (volatile).
   - Unprotected horizontal and vertical Si both etch.

2. **Passivation step (C₄F₈, ~5–10 s):**
   - C₄F₈ plasma generates **CF₂ radicals** that polymerize on *all* surfaces.
   - A **fluorocarbon polymer film (–CF₂–)ₙ** (~10–100 nm) deposits on sidewalls + bottom.

3. **Next etch step's ion bombardment:**
   - Ions accelerated by the DC bias strike **perpendicularly** to the wafer.
   - Polymer on **horizontal (bottom) surfaces** is removed by ion sputtering.
   - Polymer on **vertical (sidewall) surfaces** is preserved (ions don't strike them).
   - Fresh Si at the bottom is exposed to F• radicals again.

**Result:** Net vertical etch progresses while sidewalls remain protected → **HAR vertical trenches**.

**Scalloping (1 mark):**

- The cyclic nature produces characteristic **scallops** (waves of amplitude 50–500 nm) on sidewalls.
- **Smaller cycles** → smoother walls but slower process.
- **Larger cycles** → faster but rougher.
- For ultra-smooth walls: cryogenic DRIE (continuous, no passivation cycle).

**HAR achievement (1 mark):**

> Aspect ratio > 30:1 is achieved by **balancing the passivation layer thickness, ion energy, and step timing**, ensuring sidewalls are continually re-protected as the trench deepens.

---

#### (b) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per parameter + 2 for tabular summary)

| Parameter | Sputter Etching (IBE) | Plasma Etching | RIE |
|-----------|-----------------------|----------------|-----|
| **Mechanism** | Pure physical momentum transfer of Ar⁺ ions | Pure chemical reaction with radicals | Synergistic: physical + chemical |
| **Active Species** | Ar⁺ ions (inert) | F•, Cl•, O• radicals | Ions + Radicals |
| **Anisotropy** | Very High (≈ 1) | Low (isotropic at low V_dc) | High (0.8–0.98) |
| **Selectivity** | Low (~1:1) | Very High (>50:1) | Medium–High (10–50:1) |
| **Etch Rate** | Low (20–100 nm/min) | Low–Medium | Medium–High (500–1000 nm/min) |
| **Sidewall Profile** | Vertical, faceted | Curved, undercut | Near vertical |
| **Damage** | High (energetic ions) | None | Medium |
| **Mask Erosion** | Significant | Minimal | Moderate |
| **Typical Pressure** | 10⁻³ – 10⁻² Pa (low) | 10 – 100 Pa (high) | 0.1 – 10 Pa |
| **Applications** | IBE milling, hard masks, III–V materials | Resist stripping, oxide etch | MEMS, CMOS gate etch, microstructures |

**Concluding remark (1 mark):**
> **RIE combines the best of both** — it offers the anisotropy of physical etching and the selectivity of chemical etching — making it the **industry-standard dry etching technique** for MEMS and VLSI fabrication.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students LOSE marks in dry etching questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Plasma Etching and RIE** — Plasma etching is *chemical only* (low anisotropy); RIE is *physical + chemical* (high anisotropy). Many students write them interchangeably. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
>
> 2. **Anisotropy formula sign error** — Always use $A = 1 - \dfrac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}}$. Some students write $A = \dfrac{ER_{\text{vertical}}}{ER_{\text{lateral}}}$. **Wrong.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in etch rate** — Always write **nm/min** or **µm/min** explicitly. A bare "500" loses 0.5 mark.
>
> 4. **In Bosch process explanation** — Students often *omit the role of ion bombardment in removing passivation from the bottom only*. This is the **key reason** HAR etching works — without it, you get isotropic etching. **Worth 3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 5. **Selecting wrong gas** — Si is etched by F-based chemistry (SF₆, CF₄). Al is etched by Cl-based chemistry. Photoresist is stripped by O₂ plasma. Mixing up = **−2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Confusing Selectivity and Anisotropy** — Selectivity is *material vs mask*; Anisotropy is *vertical vs lateral direction*. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 7. **Not drawing DC bias or sheath in RIE diagrams** — Mandatory in 7-mark answers; missing them costs **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU Module-3 Dry Etching cheat sheet — memorize before exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Dry Etching = vapor-phase etching using plasma, ions, or radicals — no liquids.
- ✅ **Plasma** = quasi-neutral ionized gas with ions + electrons + radicals.
- ✅ **DC self-bias** on cathode = origin of ion energy; **sheath** = origin of directionality.
- ✅ **Three categories:** Physical (Sputter/IBE), Chemical (Plasma), Physico-chemical (RIE/DRIE).
- ✅ **Sputter Etching:** Ar⁺, physical only, very high anisotropy, very low selectivity.
- ✅ **Plasma Etching:** Radicals only, isotropic at low pressure, very high selectivity.
- ✅ **RIE:** Ions + radicals, **synergistic**, anisotropic + selective — industry workhorse.
- ✅ **DRIE / Bosch:** Time-multiplexed SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation; aspect ratio > 30:1; scalloped sidewalls.
- ✅ **Key formulas:**
  - $A = 1 - \dfrac{ER_{\text{lat}}}{ER_{\text{vert}}}$
  - $S = \dfrac{ER_{\text{film}}}{ER_{\text{mask}}}$
  - $ER = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$
  - $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P}$
  - $\omega_{pe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{n_e e^{2}}{m_e \varepsilon_0}}$
  - $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^{2}}}$
- ✅ **Common gases:** SF₆, CF₄, Cl₂, O₂, Ar, C₄F₈.
- ✅ **Anisotropy = 1** means perfectly vertical; **Selectivity = ∞** means no mask erosion.
- ✅ **Bosch = alternating SF₆ (etch) and C₄F₈ (passivate)** — repeat 50–500 times.
- ✅ **Cryogenic DRIE** avoids scalloping by using continuous F• etch at low temperature (−110 °C).
- ✅ **Endpoint detection** uses Optical Emission Spectroscopy (OES) — e.g., Si at 288 nm or SiF at 440 nm.
- ✅ **DRIE applications:** TSVs, inertial sensors, microfluidics, photonic crystals.
- ✅ **Wet etching** → crystal-dependent (e.g., KOH on Si{100} gives 54.74° walls); **Dry etching** → independent of crystal orientation.
- ✅ **Mean Free Path at low pressure** → ions travel in straight lines (anisotropic); **at high pressure** → many collisions (isotropic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Physical Etching**, in the context of Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) fabrication, is a subset of **dry etching** processes in which material is removed from a substrate *exclusively* through **kinetic energy transfer** from accelerated, inert, non-reactive ions (typically Argon, $\text{Ar}^+$) bombarding the wafer surface. Unlike chemical etching, there is **no chemical reaction** between the etchant and the substrate; the process is governed purely by **momentum exchange** and the resulting physical ejection of surface atoms — a phenomenon known as **Sputtering**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Physical Etching refers to material removal by momentum transfer of high-energy ions to surface atoms. It is non-selective, anisotropic, and is characterized by the Sputter Yield ($Y_s$).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Sandblasting at the Atomic Scale"

Imagine you are an artist trying to carve a delicate sculpture out of a stone wall using a high-pressure sandblaster.

- Instead of sand grains, you fire **accelerated Argon ions** ($\text{Ar}^+$).
- Instead of a stone wall, you have a **silicon wafer**.
- The ions are like millions of tiny bullets that physically **knock atoms out** of the wafer surface.
- The pattern you want to carve is protected by a **mask** (like a stencil taped to the wall — wherever the stencil is, the bullets bounce off; where there is no stencil, the wall gets eroded).

**Why does this matter in MEMS?** Because MEMS devices have tiny features (often under 10 µm), chemical etching (which isotropically undercuts the mask) ruins the geometry. Physical etching drives **straight down** (anisotropic), producing sharp, near-vertical sidewalls — essential for high-aspect-ratio microstructures like accelerometers and gyroscopes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Golden Properties of Pure Physical Etching:**
> 1. **Anisotropic** — etches almost exclusively in the vertical direction.
> 2. **Non-selective** — etches most materials (Si, $\text{SiO}_2$, metals, photoresist) at similar rates.
> 3. **No chemical reaction** — purely a momentum-driven process.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The performance of physical etching is quantified by a single dimensionless number:

> **Sputter Yield ($Y_s$):** The average number of substrate atoms ejected per incident ion.
> - **Typical Range:** $Y_s \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 5$ atoms/ion for most materials under standard MEMS conditions (Argon ions, 500–1000 eV).
> - **Typical Etch Rate:** $50 \text{ to } 150 \text{ nm/min}$ for silicon.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Sputtering — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The sputtering process unfolds in four distinct physical stages when an energetic ion strikes a solid surface:

### Step 1 — Ion Acceleration Across the Plasma Sheath
In a vacuum chamber (pressure $\approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ Torr), a plasma is ignited by applying an **RF (Radio Frequency, typically 13.56 MHz)** or DC voltage between two parallel electrodes. The wafer sits on the cathode (negatively biased electrode). Because the plasma is quasi-neutral but electrons are far more mobile, the cathode develops a large negative self-bias voltage ($V_{dc}$), which **accelerates positive ions** perpendicularly toward the wafer.

### Step 2 — Collision Cascade Generation
When a high-energy $\text{Ar}^+$ ion (energy $E_i = 500$–$1000$ eV) strikes the substrate surface, it penetrates the top few nanometers and collides with a surface atom via a **binary elastic collision**. The displaced atom (called a **primary knock-on atom**, or PKA) then collides with other atoms in a cascading manner — this is known as the **collision cascade**.

### Step 3 — Momentum Redistribution Toward the Surface
Some of the energy in the collision cascade is directed back toward the surface. If a surface atom receives sufficient outward momentum **and** overcomes its **surface binding energy** ($U_s$, typically 2–8 eV for most materials), it will be ejected into the vacuum.

### Step 4 — Adsorption & Exhaust
The ejected (sputtered) atoms travel ballistically through the chamber and condense on the chamber walls or are pumped away by the vacuum system.

## 2.2 The Critical "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why accelerate ions with a negative bias?** To give them kinetic energy ($KE = qV$) so they can displace surface atoms upon impact. Without acceleration, ions would simply land softly and bounce off.
- **Why use inert gases (Ar, Xe)?** Because they do not react chemically with the substrate. This isolates the *purely physical* mechanism of etching, eliminating selectivity issues that arise with chemical reactions.
- **Why are there trade-offs in physical etching?** Because the same high-energy ions also **sputter the etch mask**, meaning the mask erodes nearly as fast as the substrate. This limits the achievable etch depth.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for the KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Y_s = \dfrac{N_{\text{ejected}}}{N_{\text{incident}}}$ | Definition of **Sputter Yield** | Dimensionless atoms/ion |
| 2 | $E_i = q \, V_{dc}$ | Kinetic energy of incident ion | eV (where $q = e$ for singly charged ion) |
| 3 | $Y_s(E_i) = \dfrac{A \cdot E_i}{U_s \, \left[1 + (B \, E_i)^{0.5}\right]}$ | **Yamamura Formula** — empirical fit for $Y_s$ vs ion energy | $A, B$ are fitting constants |
| 4 | $E_{th} \approx \dfrac{U_s}{\gamma \, (1-\gamma)}$ | **Threshold Energy** for sputtering ($\gamma = \dfrac{4 M_i M_s}{(M_i + M_s)^2}$ is the mass transfer coefficient) | eV; typically $20$–$50$ eV |
| 5 | $R = Y_s \cdot \dfrac{J_{\text{ion}}}{n_s \cdot q}$ | **Etch Rate** of the substrate | nm/min; $J_{\text{ion}}$ is ion current density, $n_s$ is atomic density |
| 6 | $D_{\text{mask}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{substrate}}}{R_{\text{mask}}}$ | **Selectivity / Etch Ratio** | Dimensionless; physical etching gives $D_{\text{mask}} \approx 1$ |
| 7 | $A_f = \dfrac{\text{Vertical etch rate}}{\text{Lateral etch rate}}$ | **Anisotropy Factor** | Dimensionless; physical etching gives $A_f \to \infty$ ideally |
| 8 | $d_{\text{sidewall}} = d_{\text{mask}} \cdot (1 - 1/D_{\text{mask}})$ | **Lateral Mask Erosion** (faceting) | Determines dimensional loss |
| 9 | $\lambda_{mfp} = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 P}$ | Mean free path of ions in the plasma | cm; must be $> d_{\text{electrode}}$ for directional etching |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Physical etching is the workhorse of:
- **IC Manufacturing** — defining polysilicon gates, metal interconnect layers.
- **MEMS Fabrication** — releasing suspended microstructures (comb drives, membranes), etching high-aspect-ratio trenches in silicon (Deep Reactive Ion Etching — DRIE — Bosch process).
- **Optoelectronics** — fabricating Distributed Bragg Reflectors (DBRs), photonic crystals.
- **Failure Analysis & TEM Sample Prep** — Focused Ion Beam (FIB) milling for nanometer-precision sectioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Threshold for Sputtering

This derivation shows **why** there is a minimum ion energy required before any sputtering can occur. This is a recurring KTU 14-mark question.

### Setting Up the Collision Geometry

Consider an ion of mass $M_i$ traveling with kinetic energy $E_i$ that strikes a surface atom of mass $M_s$ initially at rest. We model this as an **elastic hard-sphere collision** (a valid approximation at ion energies below $\approx 1$ keV).

For maximum energy transfer to the surface atom, we consider a **head-on (1D) collision**, which is the worst-case (most energy transferred) scenario.

**Step 1 — Conservation of Momentum (1D elastic head-on):**

$$M_i v_i = M_i v_i^{\prime} + M_s v_s^{\prime}$$

where $v_i, v_i^{\prime}$ are the ion's initial and final velocities, and $v_s^{\prime}$ is the surface atom's final velocity.

**Step 2 — Coefficient of Restitution for elastic collision (relative velocity reverses):**

$$v_i^{\prime} - v_s^{\prime} = -v_i$$

**Step 3 — Solve the two linear equations for $v_s^{\prime}$:**

Adding the two equations:
$$2 v_i = 2 v_s^{\prime} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{M_i}{M_s}\right)$$

Therefore:
$$v_s^{\prime} = \dfrac{2 M_i}{M_i + M_s} \, v_i$$

**Step 4 — Energy transferred to the surface atom:**

The kinetic energy of the surface atom after the collision is:

$$E_{\text{transferred}} = \dfrac{1}{2} M_s (v_s^{\prime})^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} M_s \cdot \dfrac{4 M_i^2}{(M_i + M_s)^2} \, v_i^2$$

Since $E_i = \frac{1}{2} M_i v_i^2$, we can rewrite:

$$E_{\text{transferred}} = E_i \cdot \dfrac{4 M_i M_s}{(M_i + M_s)^2} = \gamma \cdot E_i$$

where $\gamma = \dfrac{4 M_i M_s}{(M_i + M_s)^2}$ is the dimensionless **energy transfer coefficient**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting cases of $\gamma$:**
> - If $M_i = M_s$ (e.g., $\text{Ar}^+$ on Si), then $\gamma = 1$ (maximum energy transfer).
> - If $M_i \ll M_s$ or $M_i \gg M_s$, then $\gamma \to 0$ (poor energy transfer — the ion simply bounces off).

**Step 5 — Apply the Surface Binding Energy Condition:**

For the surface atom to be ejected, the energy it receives must exceed the **surface binding energy** $U_s$ (the energy required to break atomic bonds and free the atom from the surface):

$$E_{\text{transferred}} \geq U_s$$

$$\gamma \cdot E_i \geq U_s$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the Threshold Energy $E_{th}$:**

Setting $E_i = E_{th}$ (the minimum energy required):

$$\boxed{E_{th} = \dfrac{U_s}{\gamma} = \dfrac{U_s (M_i + M_s)^2}{4 M_i M_s}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical example (KTU favourite):** For Argon ions ($M_i = 40$ amu) bombarding Silicon ($M_s = 28$ amu) with $U_s \approx 4.63$ eV:
>
> $$\gamma = \dfrac{4 \cdot 40 \cdot 28}{(40+28)^2} = \dfrac{4480}{4624} \approx 0.969$$
>
> $$E_{th} = \dfrac{4.63}{0.969} \approx 4.78 \text{ eV}$$
>
> Experimentally measured $E_{th}$ for $\text{Ar}^+$ on Si is $\approx 12$–$15$ eV (the discrepancy arises because the head-on 1D model is an idealization; real sputtering requires higher energies due to angular dependencies and surface crystallography).

## 3.2 Derivation: Sputter Yield as a Function of Ion Energy

The **Yamamura empirical formula** is widely used in KTU papers. We derive the **low-energy limit** behavior from first principles.

**Step 1 — At low ion energies ($E_i < E_{th}$):** No sputtering occurs ($Y_s = 0$).

**Step 2 — Just above threshold ($E_i > E_{th}$):** The yield rises approximately linearly with the *excess* energy above threshold:

$$Y_s \propto (E_i - E_{th})$$

This is because each additional eV of ion energy contributes roughly linearly to the number of atoms receiving enough energy to escape.

**Step 3 — At intermediate energies ($100 \text{ eV} < E_i < 1000 \text{ eV}$):** The yield follows:

$$Y_s(E_i) = \dfrac{A \cdot E_i}{U_s \left[1 + (B \cdot E_i)^{0.5}\right]}$$

The denominator's square-root term represents the increased probability of energy being deposited *deeper* into the bulk (away from the surface) as ion energy increases — fewer atoms near the surface receive enough outward momentum.

**Step 4 — At very high energies ($E_i > 10 \text{ keV}$):** Ion implantation dominates; the yield saturates or even decreases because most energy is deposited deep in the bulk, far from the surface.

**Step 5 — Total atoms ejected per unit time:**

$$N_{\text{ejected}} = Y_s \cdot \dfrac{I_{\text{ion}}}{q} = Y_s \cdot \dfrac{J_{\text{ion}} \cdot A_{\text{etch}}}{q}$$

where $I_{\text{ion}}$ is the total ion current and $A_{\text{etch}}$ is the exposed etch area.

**Step 6 — Volume removal rate:**

$$\text{Volume rate} = \dfrac{N_{\text{ejected}} \cdot \Omega}{N_A / \rho}$$

where $\Omega$ is the atomic volume, $\rho$ is the substrate density, and $N_A$ is Avogadro's number.

**Step 7 — Linear etch rate:**

$$R_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{\text{Volume rate}}{A_{\text{etch}}} = \dfrac{Y_s \cdot J_{\text{ion}} \cdot \Omega \cdot \rho}{q \cdot N_A}$$

Substituting $\Omega = \dfrac{M_s}{\rho \cdot N_A}$:

$$\boxed{R_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{Y_s \cdot J_{\text{ion}} \cdot M_s}{q \cdot N_A^2}}$$

This compact expression is invaluable for KTU numerical problems.

## 3.3 Derivation: Anisotropy from Incident Angle

The sputter yield depends on the angle of incidence $\theta$ of the ion (measured from the surface normal).

**Step 1 — Empirical form (Yamamura, 1981):**

$$\dfrac{Y_s(\theta)}{Y_s(0)} = (\cos \theta)^{-f}$$

where $f$ is a material-dependent exponent ($f \approx 1.5$ for Si, $f \approx 1.7$ for $\text{SiO}_2$).

**Step 2 — At grazing angles ($\theta \to 90°$):** The yield drops to zero because ions merely skim the surface without penetrating.

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:** At normal incidence ($\theta = 0°$), maximum momentum is deposited directly into the substrate, perpendicular to the surface — promoting **vertical (anisotropic) etching**. At oblique incidence, the cascade spreads laterally, and most ejected atoms travel sideways — promoting **isotropic** behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why physical etching is anisotropic:** The plasma sheath accelerates ions perpendicular to the wafer surface (along the electric field lines). Hence ions arrive near-normal to the surface, depositing their energy directly downward — producing **vertical sidewalls**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Physical Etching Variants

| Variant | Ion Source | Pressure | Key Feature | KTU Application |
|---------|-----------|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Sputter Etching (Ion Beam Etching, IBE)** | Broad-beam Kaufman ion source | $\approx 10^{-4}$ Torr | Separate plasma & wafer chambers; very directional | Optical MEMS, fine metal features |
| **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** | RF plasma between parallel plates | $10$–$100$ mTorr | Combines physical + chemical etching | Polysilicon gates, general MEMS |
| **Deep RIE (Bosch Process)** | Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) | Alternating etch/passivation cycles | $20$–$500$ µm deep Si trenches with vertical sidewalls | Through-wafer vias, inertial sensors |
| **Focused Ion Beam (FIB)** | Liquid metal ion source (Ga+) | High vacuum | Sub-10 nm spot size, maskless | TEM sample prep, device repair |
| **Plasma Etching** | Barrel reactor plasma | $\approx 1$ Torr | Mostly chemical, very isotropic | Stripping photoresist |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Sputter Etching System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vacuum Chamber Base Pressure 10-6 Torr] --> B[Introduce Argon Gas to 10-3 Torr]
    B --> C[Apply RF 13.56 MHz Voltage to Cathode]
    C --> D[Argon Plasma Ignites Electrons Strike Ar to form Ar+]
    D --> E[Negative DC Self-Bias Accelerates Ar+ Toward Wafer]
    E --> F[Ar+ Ions Bombard Wafer Surface]
    F --> G[Collision Cascade Displaces Surface Atoms]
    G --> H{Atom Receives Energy Greater Than Surface Binding Energy?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Atom Ejected Into Vacuum - Sputtered]
    H -- No --> J[Atom Remains in Substrate Lattice]
    I --> K[Atom Condenses on Chamber Wall or Pumped Out]
    J --> L[Etch Front Recedes Vertically]
    L --> M[Anisotropic Vertical Sidewall Achieved]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Reactor["Parallel Plate RIE Reactor Chamber"]
        subgraph Top["Top Electrode Showerhead"]
            G1[Ar Gas Inlet]
            G2[Reactive Gas Inlet SF6 or CF4]
        end
        subgraph Bottom["Bottom Electrode Cathode Wafer Stage"]
            W[Wafer with Mask Pattern]
        end
    end
    PS[DC or RF Power Supply 13.56 MHz] -->|Connects to| Top
    PS -->|Connects to| Bottom
    P[Vacuum Pump] -->|Evacuates| Reactor
    Top -->|Generates| Plasma[Quasi-Neutral Plasma Ions Electrons Radicals]
    Plasma -->|Physical Component| A1[Ar+ Ion Bombardment Vertical Momentum Transfer]
    Plasma -->|Chemical Component| A2[Reactive Radical Adsorption Lateral Etching]
    A1 --> W
    A2 --> W
    W --> O[Anisotropic + Selective Etch Profile]
```

## 4.3 Etch Profile Comparison — Physical vs Chemical vs RIE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Phys["A. Pure Physical Etching IBE"]
        P1[Mask Photoresist] --> P2[Substrate Si]
        P2 -.-> P3[Ejected Atoms]
        P1 -.-> P4[Sputtered Mask Atoms]
    end
    subgraph Chem["B. Pure Chemical Wet Etching"]
        C1[Mask Si3N4] --> C2[Substrate Si 100]
        C2 -.-> C3[Undercut Etchant Spreads Laterally]
    end
    subgraph RIE["C. Reactive Ion Etching RIE"]
        R1[Mask SiO2] --> R2[Substrate Si]
        R2 -.-> R3[Vertical Ion Bombardment]
        R2 -.-> R4[Lateral Chemical Reaction]
    end
```

## 4.4 Collision Cascade Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Ar+ Ion Energy 500 eV] -->|Penetrates Surface| B[First Collision with Surface Atom PKA]
    B -->|Recoils| C[Second Collision]
    B -->|Recoils| D[Third Collision]
    C -->|Recoils| E[Fourth Collision]
    D -->|Recoils| F[Surface Atom Receives Outward Momentum]
    E -->|Recoils| G[Atom Has Energy less than Us Remains in Bulk]
    F -->|Energy greater than Us| H[ATOM EJECTED SPUTTERED]
```

## 4.5 Deep RIE — Bosch Process Cyclic Etching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Cycle Start Time 0] --> S1[Step 1 SF6 Plasma Etch Phase 5 to 10 sec]
    S1 --> S2[Silicon Isotropic Etch Forms Bowls Beneath Mask]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2 C4F8 Plasma Passivation Phase 3 to 5 sec]
    S3 --> S4[Teflon like Polymer Deposits on All Surfaces]
    S4 --> S5[Step 3 SF6 Plasma Etch Phase Resumes]
    S5 --> S6[Ions Remove Polymer from Horizontal Surfaces Only Vertical Sidewalls Protected]
    S6 --> S7[Etch Cycle Repeats]
    S7 -->|If depth target not met| S1
    S7 -->|If depth target met| End[Cycle End Deep Vertical Trench Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The characteristic **scallop pattern** on the sidewalls of a Bosch-processed trench is a frequently-asked KTU question. The amplitude of each scallop is $\approx 100$–$500$ nm depending on the cycle time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Physical Etching. Why is it also called Sputter Etching?**

**Model Answer:**
Physical Etching is a dry etching process in which material is removed from a substrate purely through **momentum transfer** from high-energy inert ions (typically $\text{Ar}^+$) accelerated toward the wafer surface. The process is called **Sputter Etching** because the dominant physical mechanism is **sputtering** — the ejection of surface atoms by momentum exchange with incident ions. Unlike chemical etching, no chemical reaction occurs between the etchant and the substrate. *(Word count: ~70, well within the 3-mark answer range.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is Sputter Yield? List the two main parameters on which it depends.**

**Model Answer:**
**Sputter Yield ($Y_s$)** is defined as the average number of substrate atoms ejected per incident ion:

$$Y_s = \dfrac{\text{Number of ejected atoms}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$$

It is dimensionless. The two main parameters on which $Y_s$ depends are:
1. **The kinetic energy of the incident ion ($E_i$)** — yield increases with energy up to a saturation point.
2. **The angle of incidence ($\theta$)** — yield is maximum at an intermediate angle and drops at grazing incidence.

Secondary dependencies include the **mass ratio** of ion to substrate atom and the **surface binding energy** $U_s$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Physical Etching with Reactive Ion Etching (RIE).** RIE has BOTH physical AND chemical components. Pure physical etching (Sputter Etching / IBE) has NO chemical component.
> 2. **Always state the units** when writing etch rate formulas (typically nm/min).
> 3. **Numerical problems** frequently test $E_{th}$ derivation — students lose marks by forgetting the $\gamma$ factor.
> 4. **Mask selection** is critical: physical etching erodes masks fast, so use hard masks ($\text{SiO}_2$, metals) for deep etches.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of Physical Etching (Sputter Etching) in detail, with a labeled diagram. Discuss the role of the plasma sheath and DC self-bias.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **threshold energy** of sputtering and compute the threshold energy for $\text{Ar}^+$ ions on a silicon substrate, given that $M_{\text{Ar}} = 40$ amu, $M_{\text{Si}} = 28$ amu, and the surface binding energy of Si is $U_s = 4.63$ eV.

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**1. Plasma Generation:**
A parallel-plate reactor is filled with inert Argon gas at low pressure ($\approx 10^{-3}$ Torr). An RF voltage (13.56 MHz) applied between the electrodes ionizes the gas, forming a **plasma** — a quasi-neutral mixture of $\text{Ar}^+$ ions, electrons, and neutral Ar atoms.

**2. Sheath Formation:**
Because electrons are far more mobile than ions (mass ratio $\approx 1:1836$), they reach the electrodes faster, leaving a thin positive-ion-rich region near each electrode called the **plasma sheath**. This sheath sustains a potential drop that prevents further electron loss.

**3. DC Self-Bias:**
When RF is applied to the cathode (smaller electrode, where the wafer sits), the sheath acts as a diode rectifier. During the negative half-cycle, electrons are repelled and ions are attracted; during the positive half-cycle, electrons are attracted but their mass is too small to neutralize the surface charge built up during the negative half-cycle. The result is a **large negative DC self-bias voltage** $V_{dc}$ on the cathode (typically $-300$ to $-800$ V).

**4. Ion Acceleration and Bombardment:**
$\text{Ar}^+$ ions are accelerated across the sheath by the potential $V_{dc}$, gaining kinetic energy:

$$E_i = q \cdot V_{dc}$$

These ions travel almost **perpendicular** to the wafer surface (because the electric field in the sheath is perpendicular to the electrode).

**5. Collision Cascade and Sputtering:**
Upon impact, the ion transfers momentum to surface atoms, generating a collision cascade. Surface atoms receiving outward energy exceeding $U_s$ are ejected — this is **sputtering**.

**6. Directional Etching:**
Because ions arrive near-normal to the surface, material is preferentially removed in the **vertical direction**, producing **anisotropic** etch profiles with near-vertical sidewalls.

*(A labeled schematic similar to Section 4.1 should accompany this answer — 2 marks reserved for the diagram.)*

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of momentum and energy for elastic head-on collision:**

For an ion of mass $M_i$ and velocity $v_i$ striking a stationary atom of mass $M_s$:

$$M_i v_i = M_i v_i^{\prime} + M_s v_s^{\prime}$$

$$v_i^{\prime} - v_s^{\prime} = -v_i$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $v_s^{\prime}$:**

$$v_s^{\prime} = \dfrac{2 M_i}{M_i + M_s} v_i$$

**Step 3 — Energy transferred:**

$$E_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{1}{2} M_s (v_s^{\prime})^2 = \dfrac{4 M_i M_s}{(M_i + M_s)^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} M_i v_i^2 = \gamma \cdot E_i$$

**Step 4 — Threshold condition:**

$$\gamma \cdot E_{th} = U_s \implies E_{th} = \dfrac{U_s}{\gamma}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the mass transfer coefficient $\gamma$:**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{4 \times 40 \times 28}{(40 + 28)^2} = \dfrac{4480}{4624} = 0.969$$

**Step 6 — Compute the threshold energy:**

$$E_{th} = \dfrac{4.63 \text{ eV}}{0.969} \approx 4.78 \text{ eV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The calculated value is the *ideal* lower bound. Experimental measurements typically yield $E_{th} \approx 12$–$15$ eV because real collisions are not perfectly head-on and surface crystallography increases the effective binding. **Full marks** are awarded for correctly stating the discrepancy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Sputter Etching (Physical)**, **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)**, and **Wet Chemical Etching** with respect to: (i) mechanism, (ii) anisotropy, (iii) selectivity, (iv) etch rate, and (v) typical application in MEMS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **Deep RIE (Bosch) process** for fabricating high-aspect-ratio silicon structures. Why is cyclic passivation necessary?

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Property | Sputter Etching (Physical) | RIE | Wet Chemical Etching |
|----------|---------------------------|-----|----------------------|
| **(i) Mechanism** | Pure momentum transfer by inert ions | Combined physical + chemical (ion bombardment + reactive radicals) | Pure chemical reaction (e.g., KOH + Si) |
| **(ii) Anisotropy** | Highly anisotropic ($\cos\theta$ dependence) | Anisotropic (physical component dominates) | Isotropic (or crystallographic for anisotropic wet etchants like KOH) |
| **(iii) Selectivity** | Poor (etches all materials similarly) | Good (chemical component provides selectivity) | Excellent (e.g., KOH etches Si $\gg$ $\text{SiO}_2$) |
| **(iv) Etch Rate** | Low ($50$–$150$ nm/min) | Moderate ($200$–$1000$ nm/min) | High ($1$–$2$ µm/min for KOH on Si) |
| **(v) Typical Application** | Optical coatings, metal patterning, MEMS release | Polysilicon gates, MEMS structural layers | Bulk micromachining, V-groove formation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Pure physical etching is reserved for applications requiring **high anisotropy with no chemical compatibility concerns** (e.g., etching inert metals, optical multilayers). For most MEMS structural silicon etching, **RIE or DRIE** is preferred.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**1. The Need for Deep Si Etching:**
Many MEMS devices (inertial sensors, pressure sensor membranes) require **through-wafer** silicon etching — depths of $200$–$500$ µm with aspect ratios exceeding $20:1$. Standard RIE cannot achieve this because:
- High ion energies needed for deep etching damage the substrate.
- The chemical etch component becomes undercut-dominated at long etch times.
- Mask selectivity limits the achievable depth.

**2. The Bosch Process — Cyclic Etching:**
The Bosch process (patented by Robert Bosch GmbH, 1994) overcomes these limits by alternating between two plasma chemistries in a single chamber:

**Step 1 — Etch Phase (5–10 seconds):**
- Gas: $\text{SF}_6$ (sulfur hexafluoride).
- Plasma generates $\text{F}^*$ radicals that chemically etch silicon isotropically, AND $\text{SF}_x^+$ ions that physically bombard the surface.
- Etching is brief — only the exposed horizontal Si is attacked.

**Step 2 — Passivation Phase (3–5 seconds):**
- Gas: $\text{C}_4\text{F}_8$ (octafluorocyclopentene).
- Plasma polymerizes a **Teflon-like fluorocarbon film** ($\text{CF}_2$ polymer) on ALL exposed surfaces (sidewalls AND floor).

**Step 3 — Re-Etch Phase:**
- $\text{SF}_6$ plasma resumes. Ion bombardment **preferentially removes the polymer from horizontal surfaces** (because ions arrive perpendicular), but the **vertical sidewall polymer is preserved**.
- The newly exposed horizontal Si is etched again, while sidewalls remain protected.

**4. Why Cyclic Passivation is Necessary:**
Without passivation, $\text{SF}_6$ etching would undercut the mask and create tapered sidewalls. The passivation step protects the sidewalls, allowing **near-vertical profiles** even at high aspect ratios.

**5. Resulting Profile:**
The trench sidewall has a characteristic **scalloped texture** (amplitude $\approx 100$–$500$ nm per cycle). For smooth sidewalls, shorter cycles or post-Bosch annealing can be used.

*(Schematic similar to Section 4.5 should be drawn — 3 marks reserved for diagram.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [x] **Physical Etching = Sputter Etching = Pure Momentum Transfer** (no chemical reaction).
- [x] **Primary Ion:** Argon ($\text{Ar}^+$, $M = 40$ amu); sometimes Xenon for heavier bombardment.
- [x] **Sputter Yield $Y_s$:** dimensionless, typically $0.5$–$5$ atoms/ion.
- [x] **Threshold Energy Formula:** $E_{th} = U_s / \gamma$ where $\gamma = 4M_i M_s / (M_i + M_s)^2$.
- [x] **Three Properties of Physical Etching:** Anisotropic, Non-selective, No chemical reaction.
- [x] **Plasma Sheath** + **DC Self-Bias** are essential for ion acceleration.
- [x] **Etch Rate Equation:** $R_{\text{etch}} = Y_s \cdot J_{\text{ion}} \cdot M_s / (q \cdot N_A^2)$.
- [x] **Anisotropy Factor:** $A_f = R_{\text{vertical}} / R_{\text{lateral}} \to \infty$ for pure physical etching.
- [x] **Mask Erosion:** Physical etching erodes masks rapidly; prefer hard masks ($\text{SiO}_2$, Al, Cr) for deep etches.
- [x] **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE):** Hybrid (physical + chemical) — NOT the same as pure physical etching.
- [x] **Deep RIE (Bosch Process):** Cyclic $\text{SF}_6$ etch / $\text{C}_4\text{F}_8$ passivation — produces scalloped high-aspect-ratio trenches.
- [x] **Key Differences vs Wet Etching:** Dry = anisotropic & expensive; Wet = isotropic/anisotropic-crystallographic & cheap.
- [x] **Damage:** High-energy ions cause surface damage and implantation — important for sensitive MEMS structures.
- [x] **FIB (Focused Ion Beam):** Uses $\text{Ga}^+$ ions for sub-10 nm precision maskless etching/repair.
- [x] **Remember to state units** in every numerical answer (eV, nm/min, atoms/ion, Å).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Numericals to Practice:**
> 1. Threshold energy for various ion-substrate combinations.
> 2. Etch rate given $Y_s$ and $J_{\text{ion}}$.
> 3. Mass transfer coefficient $\gamma$ for different ion/substrate pairs.
> 4. Time to etch a given depth given etch rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Etching or Sputtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion Etching or Sputtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ion Etching (Sputtering)** is a purely *physical* dry etching technique used in MEMS and VLSI fabrication in which atoms are ejected from the surface of a target (substrate) material by the **momentum transfer** from an incident beam of high-energy inert gas ions — most commonly **Argon (Ar⁺)** ions accelerated through a potential difference of typically **0.5 kV to 5 kV**.

The process is governed by the **kinematic momentum exchange** between the incoming ion and the surface atoms of the target, rather than by any chemical reaction. The ratio of ejected target atoms to incident ions is termed the **Sputtering Yield (S)**, which is the fundamental figure of merit for the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Ion etching is classified as a *Physical Dry Etching* process. It is *anisotropic* in nature, which makes it ideal for transferring fine-line photoresist patterns vertically into the substrate without significant undercutting — a critical requirement for sub-micron MEMS structures.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing beside a **billiards table**. You strike a cue ball (the incoming **Ar⁺ ion**) with great force toward a tight cluster of billiard balls arranged on the table (the **target atoms in the substrate**). 

- If you hit hard enough, the cue ball knocks one or more balls out of the cluster and sends them flying off the table.
- These ejected balls are the **sputtered atoms** that leave the substrate surface.
