2. **Nucleophilic attack** on surface Si–H or Si–Si back-bonds.
3. **Oxidation** of surface Si atoms to form silicate intermediates: Si + 2 OH⁻ → Si(OH)₂²⁻.
4. **Hydration and dissolution** into soluble silicate species: Si(OH)₂²⁻ + 4 H₂O → Si(OH)₆²⁻ + 2 H₂↑.
5. **Hydrogen gas evolution** — visible as bubbles during the etch.

> [!NOTE]
> The **overall stoichiometric reaction** in KOH is:
> **Si + 2 KOH + H₂O → K₂SiO₃ + 2 H₂↑**
>
> Hydrogen bubble evolution is a *visual indicator* of etch progress and must be properly degassed to avoid *micro-masking* artifacts (non-uniform etching due to bubble adherence).

---

## 2.2 Etch-Stop Mechanisms — The Three Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Anisotropic wet etching becomes a precision MEMS tool only because we can **stop the etch at a precisely controlled depth**. Three mechanisms dominate:

### 2.2.1 (111) Crystallographic Etch-Stop
- Valid **only** for (100)-oriented wafers.
- The slow-etching (111) sidewalls emerge naturally at **54.74°** from the surface.
- Etch depth is geometrically determined by mask opening width.
- Self-terminating — used for V-grooves, pyramids, and inkjet nozzles.

### 2.2.2 Heavy Boron-Doping Etch-Stop (BES — Boron Etch Stop)
- Silicon doped with boron at concentrations **N_A > 5 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³** becomes resistant to alkaline etching.
- Etch rate drops by **> 100×** relative to lightly-doped substrate.
- Mechanism: boron atoms introduce lattice strain and reduce the number of available Si–OH reaction sites.
- **Used for fabricating precisely-thick diaphragms, beams, and cantilever structures.**

### 2.2.3 Electrochemical Etch-Stop (ECE — Electrochemical Etch Stop)
- A **p–n junction** is reverse-biased during etching in KOH/TMAH.
- The lightly-doped p-region etches; the n-region (or heavily doped p-region) is passivated by the **depletion layer** (Welch–Chuang model).
- Enables *sub-micron* thickness control.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula arsenal** required to solve any KTU 2024 ESE question on anisotropic wet etching. **Memorize all units and assumptions.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{d}{t}$ | Vertical etch rate (depth per unit time) | $\mu\text{m/min}$ |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{R_{(100)}}{R_{(111)}}$ | Anisotropy factor (selectivity ratio) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\theta = 54.74° = \arctan(\sqrt{2})$ | Angle between (100) surface and (111) sidewall | degrees |
| 4 | $d = \dfrac{W_0 - W_b}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Etch depth from opening geometry ((100) wafer) | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 5 | $W_b = W_0 - \sqrt{2}\,d$ | Bottom opening width after etching depth $d$ | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $L = R_{\text{lat}} \cdot t$ | Lateral undercut under mask edge | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 7 | $V_{\text{etched}} = \dfrac{d^3}{\tan(54.74°)} \cdot \text{const}$ | Volume of etched pyramidal cavity | $\mu\text{m}^3$ |
| 8 | $L_{\text{comp}} = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\,d$ | Compensation square side length for convex corner | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 9 | $t_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{target}}}{R_{(100)}}$ | Required etch time for a given depth | minutes |
| 10 | $R_{\text{doped}} \approx R_{\text{undoped}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\alpha N_A}{N_0}\right)$ | Boron-doping etch rate suppression (Seidel model) | $\mu\text{m/min}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** In all KTU board answers, you **must** state the assumption that etching is in the *vertical direction only* when using these formulas. Failure to do so loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis of Common Anisotropic Etchants

| Property | KOH | TMAH | EDP | CsOH |
|----------|-----|------|-----|------|
| **Etch rate (100)** (µm/min) | **1.0 – 1.4** | 0.5 – 1.0 | 0.5 – 1.0 | 0.5 |
| **Anisotropy (R₁₀₀/R₁₁₁)** | **100 – 400** | 10 – 50 | 200 – 1000 | 100 – 200 |
| **Etches SiO₂?** | Yes (slowly) | Yes (moderately) | **No (excellent)** | Yes |
| **CMOS compatible?** | **No (K⁺ contamination)** | **Yes** | Marginal | Yes |
| **Toxicity** | Moderate | Moderate | **High (carcinogenic)** | Low |
| **Cost** | **Very low** | High | High | Moderate |
| **Selectivity to Al?** | Yes (severe) | **No** | Yes | Mild |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams:** KOH is the **default textbook etchant**. Mention TMAH only when CMOS-compatibility or aluminum metallization is required on the wafer.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

Anisotropic wet etching underpins the fabrication of:

- **Pressure sensor diaphragms** (Bosch, Honeywell, Freescale legacy processes).
- **Inkjet printer nozzle plates** (Hewlett-Packard Thermal InkJet — TIJ).
- **Microfluidic channels and chambers** (Lab-on-Chip, µTAS).
- **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) tips** and **silicon microcantilevers**.
- **Quartz and silicon microbalances** for mass sensing.
- **Through-silicon vias (TSVs)** for 3-D wafer-level packaging (modern variants using electrochemical stops).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Etch Depth vs. Mask Opening Geometry on a (100) Wafer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 3. Be prepared to reproduce it from memory.

**Problem Setup:** A square mask opening of side $W_0$ is aligned with its edges parallel to the $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions on a (100)-oriented silicon wafer. The wafer is etched in KOH. Derive the relationship between etch depth $d$ and the resulting bottom opening width $W_b$.

**Step 1 — Identify the active sidewall planes.**
When a (100) wafer is etched through a mask, the four sidewalls that develop are the four equivalent (111) planes: $(111), (\bar{1}11), (1\bar{1}1), (11\bar{1})$.

**Step 2 — Define the geometric angle.**
The angle $\theta$ between the (100) surface and any (111) sidewall satisfies:

$$
\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{n}_{(100)} \cdot \vec{n}_{(111)}}{\vert \vec{n}_{(100)} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{n}_{(111)} \vert}
$$

Evaluating for $\vec{n}_{(100)} = [001]$ and $\vec{n}_{(111)} = [111]$:

$$
\cos\theta = \dfrac{(0)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(1)}{\sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + 1^2} \cdot \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the angle explicitly.**

$$
\theta = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 54.7356° \approx 54.74°
$$

**Step 4 — Relate etch depth to lateral undercut.**
For every micrometer of vertical etch, the (111) sidewall moves inward by $\cot(54.74°) = 0.7071 = 1/\sqrt{2}$.

Therefore, after an etch depth $d$, the lateral undercut from each side is:

$$
\Delta x = d \cdot \cot(54.74°) = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Total reduction in opening width.**

Since the etch proceeds from **both** opposite sides of the opening (or all four sides for a square pit), the bottom width becomes:

$$
W_b = W_0 - 2 \cdot \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}} = W_0 - \sqrt{2}\, d
$$

**Step 6 — Solve explicitly for etch depth $d$:**

$$
d = \dfrac{W_0 - W_b}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Etch-stop condition (V-groove).**
When $W_b \to 0$, the etch is geometrically self-terminated:

$$
d_{\max} = \dfrac{W_0}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

This defines the **maximum V-groove depth** for a given mask opening — a directly examinable KTU result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Compensation Square Side Length for Convex Corner

**Problem:** A convex corner of a mask on a (100) wafer must be protected from undercutting. Determine the side length $L_c$ of a square compensation tab required for a target etch depth $d$.

**Step 1 — Identify the failure mode.**
At a convex corner, the two adjacent (111) sidewalls intersect at the surface along the $\langle 100 \rangle$ diagonal direction. There is **no (111) plane to block the etchant**, so the corner etches laterally much faster than the sidewalls. This produces the characteristic *undercut notch*.

**Step 2 — Compensation strategy.**
A small **square tab** is added at the convex corner. Its four sides are aligned along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions, identical to the main mask.

**Step 3 — Geometric analysis.**
The tab must be **fully consumed** at the exact moment the main cavity reaches depth $d$, leaving behind a *sharp, undeformed convex corner*.

**Step 4 — Determine $L_c$.**
The (111) planes defining the tab's sidewalls must recede by $\sqrt{2}\, d$ each — but the tab must be totally consumed by two converging (111) planes. After depth $d$, the tab side is reduced by $\sqrt{2}\, d$ (from both sides). Equating to zero residual side:

$$
L_c - \sqrt{2}\, d \cdot \gamma = 0
$$

where $\gamma = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ is a geometric correction for corner geometry. The accepted result in standard MEMS texts (Kovacs, Madou) is:

$$
\boxed{L_c = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\, d \approx 0.414\, d}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check.**
For an etch depth of $d = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$:

$$
L_c = (\sqrt{2} - 1) \times 50 = 0.4142 \times 50 = 20.71 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU student error:** Many students write $L_c = d/\sqrt{2}$ (incorrect). The correct factor is **$(\sqrt{2} - 1)$**, not $1/\sqrt{2}$. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Boron-Doping Etch-Rate Suppression (Seidel Model)

**Problem:** Derive the etch-rate suppression factor for heavily boron-doped silicon in KOH.

**Step 1 — Lattice strain energy.**
Boron atoms (atomic radius 0.85 Å) substituting for silicon (atomic radius 1.18 Å) introduce **compressive lattice strain** in the doped region.

**Step 2 — Surface energy argument.**
The (111) surface etch rate $R$ depends exponentially on the surface energy $\gamma_{sl}$ (solid–liquid interfacial energy). Heavily-doped regions have a higher surface energy.

**Step 3 — Arrhenius-type suppression.**

$$
R_{(111),\text{doped}} = R_{(111),\text{undoped}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a(N_A) - E_a(0)}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Empirical Seidel form.**
Seidel et al. (1990) proposed the linearized empirical expression:

$$
\dfrac{R_{\text{doped}}}{R_{\text{undoped}}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{N_A - N_0}{N_{\text{ref}} - N_0}\right)
$$

where $N_0 = 2 \times 10^{19}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ (critical doping for etch stop) and $N_{\text{ref}} = 5 \times 10^{19}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ (complete etch stop).

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation.**
At $N_A = 5 \times 10^{19}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$: $\dfrac{R_{\text{doped}}}{R_{\text{undoped}}} \to 0$ → **etch stop.**
At $N_A = 3.5 \times 10^{19}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$: ratio $\approx 0.5$ → **50 % etch rate suppression.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Question:** A (100) silicon wafer with a 600 µm × 600 µm square mask opening (edges along $\langle 110 \rangle$) is etched in **30 % KOH at 80 °C**. The (100) etch rate is **1.4 µm/min** and the (111) etch rate is **0.012 µm/min**.

**(a)** Calculate the time required to fully etch through a 400 µm-thick wafer.
**(b)** Calculate the etch-stop-limited depth if the etch is terminated by (111) planes only.

**Solution (a):**

$$
t = \dfrac{d}{R_{(100)}} = \dfrac{400 \,\mu\text{m}}{1.4 \,\mu\text{m/min}} = 285.71 \,\text{min} \approx 4 \text{ h } 46 \text{ min}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
d_{\max} = \dfrac{W_0}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{600}{\sqrt{2}} = 424.26 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

Since $d_{\max} = 424.26 \,\mu\text{m} > 400 \,\mu\text{m}$, the wafer is **fully etched before (111) self-stoppage occurs** — the etch punches through. To prevent this, the mask opening must satisfy $W_0 \leq 400 \cdot \sqrt{2} = 565.7 \,\mu\text{m}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anisotropic Etch Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: (100) Si wafer with thermal SiO2 mask] --> B[Photolithography: pattern square opening along 110 direction]
    B --> C[SiO2 etch in BHF to expose Si]
    C --> D[Immerse in KOH 30 percent at 80 deg C]
    D --> E{Etch depth exceeds threshold?}
    E -->|No| F[Continue etching; (111) sidewalls form]
    E -->|Yes| G[V-groove complete; etch stops on (111) planes]
    F --> E
    G --> H[Strip remaining SiO2 in BHF]
    H --> I[Final MEMS structure with 54.74 degree sidewalls]
```

## 4.2 Convex vs. Concave Corner Etching Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CC[Concave Corner Behavior]
        CC1[Mask with interior corner] --> CC2[(111) planes from both sides converge]
        CC2 --> CC3[ETCH STOPS at corner]
        CC3 --> CC4[Sharp preserved corner]
    end
    subgraph CV[Convex Corner Behavior]
        CV1[Mask with exterior corner] --> CV2[(111) planes diverge from corner]
        CV2 --> CV3[ETCH ATTACKS laterally]
        CV3 --> CV4[Undeformed corner NOT achieved]
        CV4 --> CV5[Add compensation square tab]
        CV5 --> CV6[Tab consumed; corner preserved]
    end
```

## 4.3 Etch-Stop Mechanism Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Single Crystal Silicon Wafer] --> Q{Select Stop Mechanism}
    Q --> R1[(111) Crystallographic Stop]
    Q --> R2[Heavy Boron Doping BES]
    Q --> R3[Electrochemical pn Junction Stop ECE]

    R1 --> S1[Requires mask aligned to 110 directions]
    S1 --> T1[Self terminating at 54.74 degree sidewalls]

    R2 --> S2[Epitaxial or diffusion doping above 5e19 per cubic cm]
    S2 --> T2[Lattice strain suppresses etch rate by factor 100]

    R3 --> S3[Reverse bias pn junction in KOH]
    S3 --> T3[Depletion layer passivates n-type region]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Anisotropic Etch Process Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Wafer Preparation]
        I1[Si Wafer (100) p-type]
        I2[Thermal Oxidation grow SiO2]
        I3[Photolithography pattern mask]
    end

    subgraph ETCH[Etching Stage]
        E1[BHF opens SiO2 window]
        E2[KOH or TMAH bath 80 deg C]
        E3[Stirring and temperature control]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Process Control Parameters]
        C1[Etch time controls depth]
        C2[Mask alignment controls geometry]
        C3[Etchant concentration controls selectivity]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[MEMS Output]
        O1[V groove or pit]
        O2[Diaphragm structure]
        O3[Microfluidic channel]
    end

    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3
    C1 -.-> E2
    C2 -.-> I3
    C3 -.-> E2
    E3 --> O1
    E3 --> O2
    E3 --> O3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define anisotropic wet etching. Why is silicon etched preferentially along (100) directions in KOH?**

**Model Answer:**
Anisotropic wet etching is the process of selective material removal from a crystalline substrate where the etch rate depends on the crystallographic orientation of the exposed surface. In single-crystal silicon etched in KOH, the (100) plane etches **100–400 times faster** than the (111) plane because the (100) plane has lower atomic packing density and exposes **two dangling bonds per surface atom**, providing abundant reactive sites for OH⁻ nucleophilic attack. In contrast, the (111) plane is the **densest-packed plane** in silicon, with only one dangling bond per atom and tightly bonded back-bonds, making it chemically resistant.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if both definition AND atomic-level justification are given.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any three etch-stop mechanisms used in anisotropic wet etching of silicon.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **(111) crystallographic etch stop** — exploits the very slow etch rate of the (111) plane in alkaline solutions; self-terminating for V-grooves on (100) wafers.
2. **Heavy boron-doping etch stop (BES)** — silicon doped with boron above **5 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³** becomes etch-resistant due to lattice strain; used for precision diaphragms.
3. **Electrochemical etch stop (ECE)** — a reverse-biased p–n junction during KOH etching passivates the n-type region via the depletion layer (Welch–Chuang mechanism).

*(Award 1 mark per correctly described mechanism.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the etch depth of a square pit etched into a (100)-oriented silicon wafer with the mask edges aligned along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions. Show that the maximum self-limiting etch depth is $W_0/\sqrt{2}$ for an opening of width $W_0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 500 µm × 500 µm square mask opening is etched in **30 % KOH at 80 °C**. The (100) etch rate is **1.4 µm/min** and the (111) etch rate is **0.01 µm/min**. Determine:
   - (i) The etch depth after **150 minutes**.
   - (ii) Whether the (111) planes self-terminate the etch before the wafer is completely etched (assume wafer thickness = 400 µm).
   - (iii) The required mask width so that V-groove termination occurs exactly at the back surface. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The (100) wafer is etched through a mask whose edges lie along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions. The four emerging sidewalls are the four equivalent (111) planes: $(111), (\bar{1}11), (1\bar{1}1), (11\bar{1})$.

The angle $\theta$ between the (100) plane and any (111) plane is:

$$
\cos\theta = \dfrac{[001] \cdot [111]}{\sqrt{1} \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

$$
\theta = 54.74°
$$

After an etch depth $d$, the lateral undercut from each side of the square opening is:

$$
\Delta x = d \cdot \cot(54.74°) = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

Since two opposing (111) planes recede toward each other from each side of the opening, the bottom width becomes:

$$
W_b = W_0 - \sqrt{2}\, d
$$

Solving for $d$:

$$
d = \dfrac{W_0 - W_b}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

When $W_b = 0$ (the two opposing (111) planes meet at the apex of the V-groove), the **maximum self-limiting etch depth** is:

$$
\boxed{d_{\max} = \dfrac{W_0}{\sqrt{2}}}
$$

At this depth, the four (111) sidewalls converge to a sharp V-shape, and the etch becomes geometrically self-terminated.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i)** Etch depth after 150 minutes:

$$
d = R_{(100)} \cdot t = 1.4 \,\mu\text{m/min} \times 150 \,\text{min} = 210 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**(ii)** Compute $d_{\max}$ for self-termination:

$$
d_{\max} = \dfrac{W_0}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{500}{\sqrt{2}} = 353.55 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

Since $d_{\max} = 353.55 \,\mu\text{m} < \text{wafer thickness } (400 \,\mu\text{m})$, the V-groove will **NOT punch through** the wafer — the (111) planes will self-terminate the etch at $d = 353.55 \,\mu\text{m}$, leaving an unfilled pyramid with apex below the back surface.

**(iii)** For V-groove termination exactly at the back surface ($d = 400 \,\mu\text{m}$):

$$
W_0 = \sqrt{2} \cdot d = \sqrt{2} \times 400 = 565.69 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_0 \approx 566 \,\mu\text{m}}
```

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **convex corner undercutting** during anisotropic wet etching of (100) silicon. With a neat diagram, describe a **compensation structure** that preserves a sharp convex corner. Derive the required compensation square side length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **KOH, TMAH, and EDP** as anisotropic etchants of silicon with respect to etch rate, anisotropy ratio, CMOS compatibility, and selectivity to SiO₂. State the chemical reaction of silicon with KOH. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Convex Corner Undercutting:**
At a convex corner of a mask on a (100) wafer, the two adjacent (111) sidewalls diverge outward, leaving **no (111) plane to shield the corner from the etchant**. The etchant therefore attacks the corner laterally at the (110) or higher-index plane rate, which is comparable to the (100) etch rate. The result is an unwanted **undercut notch** that grows during etching.

**Compensation Structure:**
A small **square tab** is added at the convex corner, aligned such that its sides lie along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions. The tab is dimensioned so that, at the moment the main cavity reaches depth $d$, the compensation tab is **just fully consumed** by the converging (111) planes, leaving behind a sharp, undeformed convex corner.

**Derivation of compensation side length $L_c$:**

Each of the four (111) planes bounding the compensation tab recedes inward by $\sqrt{2}\, d/2$ from each side. For the tab to be just consumed at depth $d$:

$$
L_c = 2 \cdot \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot (1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}) = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\, d
$$

$$
\boxed{L_c = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\, d \approx 0.414\, d}
$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

| Etchant | Etch Rate (100) µm/min | Anisotropy R₁₀₀/R₁₁₁ | CMOS Compat. | SiO₂ Etch? |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **KOH** | 1.0 – 1.4 | 100 – 400 | **No (K⁺ contamination)** | Yes (slow) |
| **TMAH** | 0.5 – 1.0 | 10 – 50 | **Yes** | Yes (moderate) |
| **EDP** | 0.5 – 1.0 | 200 – 1000 | Marginal | **No (excellent)** |

**Chemical reaction with KOH:**

$$
\text{Si} + 2\,\text{KOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SiO}_3 + 2\,\text{H}_2 \uparrow
```

KOH is preferred for its **high anisotropy and low cost**, but is **not CMOS-compatible** due to potassium ion contamination of gate oxides. TMAH is the **CMOS-compatible alternative**, while EDP provides the **best SiO₂ selectivity** but is highly toxic (carcinogenic pyrazine additives).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Anisotropic Wet Etching questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the angle 54.74°** between (100) and (111) planes explicitly — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing $L_c = d/\sqrt{2}$ with $L_c = (\sqrt{2}-1)\, d$** for compensation structures — costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not mentioning dangling bonds** as the atomic-level reason for anisotropy — costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Writing "isotropic" instead of "anisotropic"** in definitions — immediate **2-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Omitting units** in numerical answers ($d$ in µm, $t$ in min, $R$ in µm/min) — costs **0.5–1 mark** cumulatively.
> 6. **Ignoring hydrogen bubble degassing** in process explanations — costs **1 mark** for completeness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Anisotropic Wet Etching**

- ☐ **Definition:** Orientation-dependent chemical dissolution of single-crystal substrates.
- ☐ **Primary etchant:** KOH (alkaline, OH⁻ based); alternatives: TMAH, EDP, CsOH.
- ☐ **Anisotropy order:** R(110) > R(100) ≫ R(111).
- ☐ **Key angle:** (100)–(111) = **54.74°**.
- ☐ **Three etch-stop mechanisms:** (111) crystallographic, **boron doping**, **electrochemical (p–n)**.
- ☐ **Boron etch-stop threshold:** N_A > 5 × 10¹⁹ cm⁻³.
- ☐ **V-groove depth formula:** $d = (W_0 - W_b)/\sqrt{2}$.
- ☐ **Max self-limiting depth:** $d_{\max} = W_0/\sqrt{2}$.
- ☐ **Compensation tab side:** $L_c = (\sqrt{2} - 1)\, d \approx 0.414\, d$.
- ☐ **Concave corners** are **protected**; **convex corners** require compensation.
- ☐ **KOH reaction:** Si + 2 KOH + H₂O → K₂SiO₃ + 2 H₂↑.
- ☐ **TMAH** is CMOS-compatible; **EDP** has the best SiO₂ selectivity; **KOH** is cheapest.
- ☐ **Mask alignment:** edges along $\langle 110 \rangle$ directions on (100) wafers.
- ☐ **Process parameters:** temperature (60–110 °C), concentration (20–35 wt%), agitation, etch time.
- ☐ **Hydrogen bubbles** must be removed to avoid micro-masking defects.

---

**End of Module 3 — Anisotropic Wet Etching Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L2–L5).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Design and Packaging Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Key Design and Packaging Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Considerations** in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) refer to the systematic set of engineering constraints, trade-offs, and decision-making parameters that govern the architecture, material selection, geometry, fabrication sequence, and functional performance of micro-scale devices. These considerations ensure that the device simultaneously satisfies electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical, and biological requirements within the limits of microfabrication technology.

**Packaging Considerations** in MEMS refer to the multidisciplinary strategies employed to encapsulate, interconnect, protect, and isolate the micro-scale mechanical and electronic structures from operational, environmental, and handling stresses. Packaging is recognized as one of the most critical (and historically most expensive) steps in MEMS commercialization, often accounting for **60% to 80%** of the total device cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike IC packaging, MEMS packaging is *device-specific*. The package must simultaneously provide mechanical support, hermetic/selective environmental sealing, electrical interconnect, thermal management, optical access (when required), and bio-compatibility (when required), while *never* interfering with the sensing or actuation principle of the device.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have built a beautifully intricate, paper-thin mechanical wristwatch movement — full of tiny gears, springs, and a delicate balance wheel. Before you can sell or even wear this watch, you must place it inside a metal case, attach a glass face (so you can see the hands), connect the winding stem (so you can interact with it), seal it against dust and moisture, and shock-mount the entire assembly inside a watch body.

That "case, glass, stem, and seal" combination is exactly what **packaging** means for a MEMS device. And the rules you must obey while *designing* the gears, springs, and balance wheel — like "don't make a gear thinner than 10 microns," "use materials that won't corrode in humid air," "leave enough clearance so thermal expansion doesn't jam it" — are the **design considerations**.

### 1.3 Core Metrics and Standards

- **Hermeticity requirement:** Leak rate **< 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/sec of Helium** (MIL-STD-883 Method 1014).
- **Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP) cap-to-device cavity height:** Typically **1 µm to 500 µm**.
- **Operating temperature range for automotive MEMS:** **−40 °C to +125 °C**.
- **Bonding alignment tolerance:** **±1 µm** (for wafer-level bonding).
- **Typical die size for MEMS sensors:** **1 mm × 1 mm to 10 mm × 10 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics** used across the MEMS industry:
> - **Bond strength:** > 10 MPa for glass frit, > 5 MPa for eutectic Au-Sn.
> - **Outgassing rate:** < 1 × 10⁻⁶ Torr·L/sec for vacuum packages.
> - **Thermal cycling tolerance:** 1000 cycles between −55 °C and +125 °C (per MIL-STD-883).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of MEMS Design Considerations

The design of a MEMS device is governed by **seven interconnected pillars**. Each pillar imposes constraints that must be traded off against the others.

#### 2.1.1 Functional Pillar
- Defines the sensing or actuation principle (piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, thermal, optical, magnetic, or resonant).
- Sets the **input–output transfer function** and the **dynamic range**.
- Example: A capacitive accelerometer sensitivity is directly proportional to **electrode overlap area / gap²**.

#### 2.1.2 Material Pillar
- Material choice must satisfy mechanical, electrical, chemical, and fabrication constraints simultaneously.
- Common MEMS structural materials: **Single-crystal Silicon (Si)**, **Polysilicon**, **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)**, **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)**, **SU-8 polymer**, **PDMS**, **Parylene-C**, **Aluminium (Al)**, **Gold (Au)**, **Titanium (Ti)**.
- Substrate materials: **Si (100)**, **Si (111)**, **SOI (Silicon-on-Insulator)**, **Quartz**, **Glass (Pyrex 7740)**, **Sapphire (Al₂O₃)**.
- Material compatibility is verified by the **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** mismatch: $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2$.

#### 2.1.3 Fabrication Pillar
- Must be compatible with standard **CMOS or BiCMOS processes** if co-integration is required.
- Sequential photolithographic steps (each with finite alignment tolerance of approximately **±0.1 µm** to **±2 µm**).
- Release etch constraints (e.g., **HF vapor** vs. **HF liquid** vs. **XeF₂** gas-phase for sacrificial oxides).

#### 2.1.4 Mechanical Pillar
- Stress engineering: residual film stress $\sigma$ must be controlled below the **fracture strength** of the structural layer.
- Stress gradient $\frac{d\sigma}{dz}$ controls out-of-plane curvature (bowing).
- Mechanical robustness against **shock (typically 1500 g to 10 000 g)**, **vibration**, and **drop impact**.

#### 2.1.5 Electrical Pillar
- Interconnects must exhibit low **parasitic capacitance** ($C_p$), low **series resistance** ($R_s$), and controlled **characteristic impedance** ($Z_0 \approx 50\ \Omega$ for RF MEMS).
- **Pull-in voltage** for electrostatic actuators: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$.

#### 2.1.6 Thermal Pillar
- Heat dissipation: $P_{dissipated} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$, where $R_{th}$ is the thermal resistance of the package.
- Self-heating in micro-hotplates and thermal actuators.

#### 2.1.7 Reliability & Environmental Pillar
- Long-term stability under humidity, temperature cycling, and chemical exposure.
- Aging behavior: creep in metals, viscoelasticity in polymers, fatigue in silicon.

### 2.2 Hierarchical Breakdown of MEMS Packaging Considerations

MEMS packaging is classified into **three fundamental levels** of protection hierarchy:

#### 2.2.1 Level 1 — Die-Level Packaging
- Protects the bare die during dicing, handling, and wire bonding.
- Techniques: **spin-on passivation (polyimide/PI)**, **conformal coating (parylene)**, **gel encapsulation**.

#### 2.2.2 Level 2 — Device-Level Packaging
- Encloses the MEMS die with the necessary environmental control.
- **Hermetic packages:** Ceramic LCC (Leadless Chip Carrier), metal Kovar, glass-to-metal seals.
- **Non-hermetic packages:** Plastic overmold, epoxy potting.

#### 2.2.3 Level 3 — Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP)
- The MEMS die is capped with another wafer *before* dicing.
- Bonding methods: **anodic bonding**, **eutectic bonding**, **glass-frit bonding**, **fusion bonding**, **adhesive bonding (BCB, SU-8)**.
- **Cap wafer** is etched with cavities (typically **10 µm to 500 µm deep**) using KOH, TMAH, or DRIE.

### 2.3 Hermetic vs. Non-Hermetic — The Critical Trade-off

| Parameter | Hermetic Package | Non-Hermetic Package |
|---|---|---|
| Environmental Isolation | Total (gas/liquid tight) | Partial (moisture permeable) |
| Cost | High (ceramic/metal/Kovar) | Low (plastic/epoxy) |
| Weight & Size | Bulky | Compact, lightweight |
| Thermal Cycling Robustness | Excellent | Moderate |
| Typical Use | Pressure sensors, gyroscopes, IMUs | Accelerometers, microphones |
| Leak Rate Standard | < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/sec He | Not specified |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

$$
C(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x}
$$

$$
\sigma_{residual} = \sigma_0 + \frac{d\sigma}{dz} \cdot z
$$

$$
R_{thermal} = \frac{L}{k \cdot A}
$$

$$
P_{leak} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_{cavity}}{\tau_{permeation}}
$$

$$
\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{substrate} - \alpha_{package}
$$

$$
\sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
f_{resonant} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\sqrt{m_{eff} \cdot k}}{c_{damping}}
$$

| Formula Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary/Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{PI}$ | Pull-in voltage (electrostatic instability) | Volts (V) | $V_{applied} < V_{PI}$ for stable operation |
| $C(x)$ | Capacitance as a function of displacement $x$ | Farads (F) | $x < d_0/3$ (beyond this, pull-in occurs) |
| $k$ | Spring constant of suspension | N/m | $0.01$ to $10^{4}$ N/m typical |
| $d_0$ | Initial electrode gap | meters (m) | $1$ µm to $10$ µm |
| $\sigma_{thermal}$ | Thermally induced stress | Pascals (Pa) | Must be below fracture strength |
| $E$ | Young's modulus of structural layer | GPa | Si: 169 GPa, Au: 78 GPa |
| $R_{thermal}$ | Thermal resistance | K/W | Minimize for power MEMS |
| $Q$ | Quality factor (resonator) | dimensionless | Vacuum: $Q > 10^{4}$, Air: $Q < 100$ |
| $\Delta\alpha$ | CTE mismatch | ppm/K | Must be $< 1$ ppm/K for hermetic seals |
| $P_{leak}$ | Hermetic leak rate | atm·cc/sec | $< 10^{-8}$ atm·cc/sec He |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations appear every year in KTU university examinations. Master the **pull-in voltage formula** and the **thermal stress formula** — they account for roughly 70% of the numerical problems in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pull-in Voltage of an Electrostatic MEMS Actuator

We consider a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator: a movable spring-suspended plate (mass $m$, spring constant $k$) separated from a fixed plate by an initial gap $d_0$. A voltage $V$ applied across the gap creates an electrostatic attractive force that competes with the spring restoring force.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of the parallel-plate configuration.**
The capacitance between the movable plate and the fixed plate when the movable plate has deflected by an amount $x$ is:

$$
C(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x}
$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ is the permittivity of free space and $A$ is the electrode overlap area.

**Step 2 — Energy stored in the electrostatic capacitor.**
The electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor is:

$$
U_{elec}(x) = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x} \cdot V^2
$$

**Step 3 — Energy stored in the mechanical spring.**
The elastic potential energy of the deformed spring is:

$$
U_{mech}(x) = \frac{1}{2} k x^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Total potential energy of the system.**
The total potential energy is the sum of electrostatic and mechanical contributions:

$$
U_{total}(x) = \frac{1}{2} k x^{2} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{d_0 - x}
$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium condition.**
Mechanical equilibrium occurs when the net force on the movable plate is zero. Taking the derivative of the total energy with respect to displacement and equating it to zero:

$$
F_{net} = \frac{d U_{total}}{d x} = k x - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 (d_0 - x)^{2}} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the equilibrium displacement.**
Rearranging the equilibrium condition to solve for $x$:

$$
k x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 (d_0 - x)^{2}}
$$

$$
x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 k (d_0 - x)^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Condition for instability (pull-in).**
Equilibrium becomes unstable when the spring restoring force can no longer compensate for the increasing electrostatic force. Mathematically, this occurs when:

$$
\frac{d^{2} U_{total}}{d x^{2}} = 0
$$

Computing the second derivative:

$$
\frac{d^{2} U_{total}}{d x^{2}} = k - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{(d_0 - x)^{3}}
$$

Setting this equal to zero:

$$
k = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{(d_0 - x)^{3}}
$$

**Step 8 — Combine Step 6 and Step 7.**
Dividing Step 6 by Step 7:

$$
\frac{x}{1} = \frac{(d_0 - x)}{3}
$$

Solving this geometric relation:

$$
3x = d_0 - x \quad\Rightarrow\quad 4x = d_0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_{PI} = \frac{d_0}{3}
$$

**Step 9 — Substitute $x_{PI}$ back into the instability condition.**

$$
k = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{\left(d_0 - \frac{d_0}{3}\right)^{3}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{\left(\frac{2 d_0}{3}\right)^{3}}
$$

$$
k = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{\frac{8 d_0^{3}}{27}} = \frac{27\,\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{8 d_0^{3}}
$$

**Step 10 — Solve for $V_{PI}$.**

$$
V_{PI}^{2} = \frac{8 k d_0^{3}}{27\, \varepsilon_0 A}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^{3}}{27\, \varepsilon_0 A}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The pull-in displacement is *always* at exactly **one-third of the initial gap**, regardless of voltage magnitude, mass, or electrode area. This is a fundamental design rule — you must operate your capacitive MEMS *strictly below* $\frac{d_0}{3}$ to avoid instability.

### 3.2 Derivation: Thermally Induced Stress Due to CTE Mismatch

**Step 1 — Identify the source of stress.**
When two materials with different coefficients of thermal expansion ($\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$) are bonded together and subjected to a temperature change $\Delta T$, they attempt to expand or contract by different amounts. The constraint imposed by the bond generates internal stress.

**Step 2 — Define the free (unconstrained) strain mismatch.**

$$
\Delta \varepsilon = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\Delta\alpha = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2$ and $\Delta T = T_{operating} - T_{bonding}$.

**Step 3 — Convert strain to stress using Hooke's Law (uniaxial approximation).**

$$
\sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \Delta\varepsilon = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

For a thin film on a thick substrate, the biaxial stress equation is more accurate:

$$
\sigma_{thermal} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $E_f$ is the Young's modulus of the film and $\nu_f$ is the Poisson's ratio of the film.

**Step 4 — Substituting typical MEMS values for a silicon die bonded to a Kovar package.**
- $E_{Si} = 169\ \text{GPa}$, $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$
- $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6\ \text{ppm/K}$, $\alpha_{Kovar} = 5.1\ \text{ppm/K}$
- $\Delta T = 100\ \text{K}$ (e.g., from 25 °C to 125 °C)

$$
\sigma_{thermal} = \frac{169 \times 10^{9}}{1 - 0.28} \cdot (5.1 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100
$$

$$
\sigma_{thermal} = \frac{169 \times 10^{9}}{0.72} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100
$$

$$
\sigma_{thermal} \approx 234.7 \times 10^{9} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\sigma_{thermal} \approx 58.7\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to fracture strength.**
The fracture strength of silicon is approximately **7 GPa** in pristine samples but only **1–2 GPa** in micromachined devices with surface flaws. The thermal stress of ~60 MPa is well below fracture strength, but accumulated with residual stress, it can cause **die cracking**, **delamination**, or **bond failure**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Hermetic Leak Rate Permeation Time Constant

**Step 1 — Define the cavity pressure change over time.**

$$
P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

where $P_0$ is the initial cavity pressure and $\tau$ is the permeation time constant.

**Step 2 — The permeation time constant is related to leak rate $L$ and cavity volume $V$.**

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the time to reach a specified leak rate threshold.**
For a cavity volume of $V = 1\ \text{mm}^{3} = 10^{-3}\ \text{cm}^{3}$ and a leak rate $L = 10^{-8}\ \text{atm·cc/sec}$:

$$
\tau = \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-8}} = 10^{5}\ \text{sec} \approx 27.8\ \text{hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why hermeticity tests (MIL-STD-883 Method 1014) require the device to be pressurized in Helium for several hours before leak detection. The He molecules need time to permeate through any micro-leaks.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Wafer Bonding Method

| Bonding Method | Temperature (°C) | Required Surface Roughness (nm) | Hermetic? | CTE Tolerance | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fusion Bonding | 800–1100 | < 0.5 | Yes | Poor (Si–Si only) | SOI-based devices |
| Anodic Bonding | 300–450 | < 100 | Yes | Excellent (Si–Glass) | Pressure sensors |
| Eutectic (Au-Sn) | 280–320 | < 500 | Yes | Good (Au-Sn metallized) | RF MEMS, optical MEMS |
| Glass Frit | 400–450 | < 1000 | Yes (low-T) | Good | Low-cost WLP |
| Adhesive (BCB, SU-8) | 150–250 | < 500 | No | Excellent | Bio-MEMS, microfluidics |
| Thermocompression | 300–400 | < 50 | Yes | Excellent (Au-Au) | High-Q resonators |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] *Differentiate between hermetic and non-hermetic packaging of MEMS devices. List two applications where hermetic packaging is mandatory.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hermetic Packaging | Non-Hermetic Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Gas-tight enclosure with leak rate < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/sec of Helium | Partial enclosure that allows slow gas/moisture permeation |
| Materials | Ceramic, Kovar, glass-to-metal seals, wafer-bonded caps | Plastic, epoxy, silicone gel |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Reliability | Very high (multi-decade lifetimes) | Moderate (typically 5–15 years) |

**Two applications where hermetic packaging is mandatory:**
1. **Absolute pressure sensors** — require reference vacuum cavity; any gas permeation causes sensor drift.
2. **Gyroscopes** — require high-Q vacuum environment for the proof mass; even minor gas damping degrades the quality factor.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] *Define pull-in voltage in an electrostatic MEMS actuator. Why must operational displacement be limited to less than one-third of the initial gap?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The *pull-in voltage* ($V_{PI}$) is the critical applied voltage at which an electrostatic MEMS actuator undergoes a sudden, uncontrollable collapse of the movable electrode onto the fixed electrode due to a positive-feedback electrostatic instability.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^{3}}{27\, \varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

**Why limit displacement to $x < d_0/3$?**
At $x = d_0/3$, the spring restoring force can no longer compensate for the rapidly increasing electrostatic force. Beyond this point, the system enters a region of negative stiffness where any further increase in displacement causes even greater attractive force, leading to **pull-in instability** and permanent stiction failure. Therefore, designers must always operate the actuator with a **safety margin** such that the maximum operational displacement stays below $d_0/3$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

## Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - July 2024]** *With neat functional diagrams, explain the various key design considerations in MEMS. Discuss material selection criteria in detail.*

**Part (a) — 7 marks: Key Design Considerations in MEMS**

**Model Solution:**

The design of a MEMS device is governed by seven interconnected design pillars, each imposing critical constraints that must be traded off against the others.

**1. Functional Pillar (1 mark)**
This pillar defines the *purpose* of the device — the sensing or actuation principle (piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, thermal, magnetic, optical, or resonant). It establishes the device's transfer function, dynamic range, sensitivity, and bandwidth. Example: a capacitive accelerometer's sensitivity $\propto A/d_0^2$.

**2. Material Pillar (1 mark)**
Material selection must simultaneously satisfy mechanical strength, electrical conductivity/insulation, chemical inertness, thermal stability, and fabrication compatibility. Common structural materials include single-crystal silicon, polysilicon, silicon nitride, SU-8, and parylene. Substrate materials include Si (100), SOI, glass, and sapphire.

**3. Fabrication Pillar (1 mark)**
The design must be realizable using available micromachining processes — bulk micromachining (wet/dry etching), surface micromachining, LIGA, or a combination. Each process imposes geometry, minimum feature size, and aspect-ratio constraints.

**4. Mechanical Pillar (1 mark)**
Mechanical design must address stress (residual and thermal), strain, fatigue, fracture, and dynamic behavior. The natural frequency $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m_{eff}}$ must be tuned to the application. Shock survival (typically 1500 g to 10 000 g for automotive) is essential.

**5. Electrical Pillar (1 mark)**
Interconnects, bond pads, and on-chip circuitry must be designed to minimize parasitic capacitance, series resistance, and cross-talk. RF MEMS specifically require controlled $50\ \Omega$ transmission line impedance.

**6. Thermal Pillar (1 mark)**
Heat dissipation, self-heating, and thermal isolation must be managed. Thermal resistance $R_{th} = L/(kA)$ should be minimized for high-power MEMS.

**7. Reliability & Environmental Pillar (1 mark)**
Long-term stability under temperature cycling, humidity, vibration, and chemical exposure must be verified through accelerated life testing (e.g., 1000 thermal cycles per MIL-STD-883).

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Material Selection Criteria in MEMS**

**Model Solution:**

Material selection in MEMS follows a structured decision matrix based on the following criteria:

| # | Criterion | Description | Example Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mechanical Properties | Young's modulus, yield strength, fracture toughness, fatigue limit | Si: E = 169 GPa, σ_fracture ≈ 1–7 GPa |
| 2 | Thermal Properties | CTE, thermal conductivity, max operating temperature | Si: CTE = 2.6 ppm/K, k = 150 W/m·K |
| 3 | Electrical Properties | Resistivity, dielectric constant, breakdown field | Si: 10⁴ Ω·cm (intrinsic); SiO₂: ε_r = 3.9 |
| 4 | Chemical Properties | Corrosion resistance, etch selectivity, biocompatibility | Au, Pt, Ti — biocompatible; Si — native oxide forms |
| 5 | Fabrication Compatibility | CMOS compatible, etch selectivity, deposition temperature | Poly-Si: CMOS compatible; SU-8: low-temp |
| 6 | Cost & Availability | Wafer cost, processing cost, supply chain | Si: cheap; SOI: 5–10× more; Sapphire: expensive |
| 7 | Long-term Stability | Creep, aging, outgassing, stiction | Au: no oxidation; Al: corrodes; Polymers: creep |

**Discussion:**

For **structural layers**, single-crystal silicon remains the workhorse due to its excellent mechanical properties, near-zero mechanical hysteresis, and mature processing. **Piezoresistive strain gauges** are typically realized using doped polysilicon or single-crystal silicon. For **capacitive electrodes**, metals such as Al, Au, or heavily-doped poly-Si are used.

For **sacrificial layers**, silicon dioxide (SiO₂) is common because of its high etch selectivity in HF relative to silicon. For **electrical isolation**, silicon nitride (Si₃N₄) is used because of its high dielectric strength (~10 MV/cm) and low pinhole density.

For **packaging and capping**, glass (Pyrex 7740) is widely used because its CTE (3.2 ppm/K) closely matches that of silicon (2.6 ppm/K), enabling reliable anodic bonding. For **bio-MEMS**, PDMS and parylene-C are preferred because of their biocompatibility.

**Conclusion:** Material selection is not a single-criterion optimization — it is a *multi-objective design trade-off* in which mechanical, electrical, thermal, chemical, fabrication, cost, and reliability constraints must be simultaneously satisfied.

---

## Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - July 2024]** *Explain in detail the various MEMS packaging techniques. Compare wafer-level packaging and device-level packaging with their respective advantages and disadvantages.*

**Part (a) — 7 marks: MEMS Packaging Techniques**

**Model Solution:**

MEMS packaging techniques are broadly classified into **wafer-level packaging (WLP)** and **device-level packaging (DLP)**. The bonding mechanisms used in WLP are further subdivided.

**A. Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP) (4 marks):**
In WLP, the MEMS wafer is bonded to a *cap wafer* before dicing. The cap wafer contains etched cavities that provide the free-space volume required for mechanical movement.

**Bonding Methods:**

1. **Anodic Bonding** — Cap wafer (glass, Pyrex 7740) is bonded to Si wafer at **300–450 °C** with an applied voltage of **200–1000 V**. Bond forms Na⁺ ion migration creating an oxygen-rich interface. Hermetic, strong, CTE-matched for Si-glass.

2. **Fusion Bonding** — Two silicon wafers are bonded directly at **800–1100 °C** after surface activation (e.g., wet chemical or plasma). Requires very smooth surfaces (< 0.5 nm RMS roughness). Hermetic, but requires high temperature.

3. **Eutectic Bonding** — Au-Sn (80/20) alloy bonding at **280–320 °C** forms a hermetic seal with excellent fatigue resistance. Used in RF MEMS and optical MEMS.

4. **Glass-Frit Bonding** — Low-melting-point glass paste is screen-printed and reflowed at **400–450 °C** to form a hermetic seal. Tolerant of surface roughness but has a finite seal width (~100 µm).

5. **Adhesive Bonding (BCB, SU-8)** — Polymer adhesive cured at **150–250 °C**. Low temperature, high alignment tolerance, but **not hermetic**.

**B. Device-Level Packaging (DLP) (3 marks):**
In DLP, individual packaged dies are mounted into ceramic, metal, or plastic enclosures after wafer dicing.

**Techniques:**
1. **Ceramic LCC (Leadless Chip Carrier)** — Alumina or AlN substrate, Kovar lid, hermetic seal.
2. **Metal Can (TO-style)** — Kovar header with glass-to-metal feedthroughs.
3. **Plastic Overmold** — Low-cost epoxy transfer molding; non-hermetic.
4. **Conformal Coating** — Parylene-C, silicone gel; provides mechanical protection and chemical isolation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks: WLP vs. DLP Comparison**

| Aspect | Wafer-Level Packaging | Device-Level Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| Stage in Process Flow | Before dicing (wafer state) | After dicing (individual die) |
| Throughput | High (parallel processing of 1000s of dies) | Low (serial handling) |
| Cost per Device | Low (batch process) | High (individual assembly) |
| Cavity Size Control | Excellent (photolithographic precision) | Moderate (mechanical tolerances) |
| Hermeticity | Excellent (wafer bond is intrinsically hermetic) | Variable (depends on seal quality) |
| Size of Final Package | Smaller (cap is just one wafer thickness above die) | Larger (full enclosure with leads) |
| Mechanical Protection | Good (wafer cap is rigid) | Excellent (full enclosure) |
| Thermal Management | Limited (no external heat sink) | Better (can attach heat sink to package) |
| Electrical Interconnect | Through cap vias (TSV) or wire bonds | Lead frames, BGA, QFN |
| Flexibility | Low (one cap design per device) | High (can mix and match packages) |
| Typical Application | Consumer MEMS (accelerometers, gyroscopes) | Automotive, aerospace, medical |

**Conclusion:** WLP is the industry-preferred approach for high-volume, low-cost consumer MEMS (smartphones, wearables), while DLP is preferred for low-volume, high-reliability applications (aerospace, medical implants, military).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing pull-in voltage with snap-down voltage** — They are the same phenomenon. Do not introduce them as different concepts.
> 2. **Forgetting the dielectric constant** — When the gap contains a dielectric film (e.g., Si₃N₄), the pull-in formula must be modified to include $\varepsilon_r$. Many students write $V_{PI}$ without accounting for dielectric — this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical problems** — KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 marks for missing or incorrect units.
> 4. **Writing "hermetic" without defining the leak rate threshold** — Always quantify: *"hermetic means leak rate < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/sec of Helium."*
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams** — In 7-mark and 14-mark questions, a labeled diagram is *mandatory*. Without it, you lose a minimum of 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing wafer-level packaging with chip-scale packaging (CSP)** — CSP is an *IC* packaging concept. MEMS WLP includes mechanical cavity, which CSP does not.
> 7. **Not specifying bonding temperature** — Whenever you mention a bonding technique, always quote the temperature range.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pull-in voltage formula** is the single most-tested equation in Module 4 — memorize $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^{3}}{27\, \varepsilon_0 A}}$ and remember the $x = d_0/3$ result.
- **Hermeticity standard** = leak rate < **10⁻⁸ atm·cc/sec** (He), per MIL-STD-883 Method 1014.
- **Packaging cost = 60%–80%** of total MEMS device cost — packaging is *not* an afterthought.
- **Three packaging levels**: Die-level → Device-level → Wafer-level (hierarchical).
- **Anodic bonding**: Si-to-glass at 300–450 °C with high voltage; ideal for pressure sensors.
- **Eutectic bonding**: Au-Sn at 280–320 °C; ideal for RF MEMS and optical MEMS.
- **CTE mismatch** generates thermal stress $\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$; keep $\Delta\alpha < 1\ \text{ppm/K}$ for reliable hermetic seals.
- **WLP advantages**: small size, batch processing, hermeticity, low cost per device.
- **DLP advantages**: mechanical robustness, thermal management, design flexibility.
- **Wafer bonding alignment tolerance** = ±1 µm; use alignment marks.
- **Quality factor Q** dramatically increases in vacuum: $Q_{vacuum} > 10^{4}$ vs. $Q_{air} < 100$.
- **Getter materials** inside cavities absorb residual moisture and outgassed species over device lifetime.
- **Through-Silicon Vias (TSV)** are increasingly used in advanced WLP for electrical interconnect.
- **Design pillars** = Functional + Material + Fabrication + Mechanical + Electrical + Thermal + Reliability (always list all seven).
- **Always include a labeled diagram** in any 7-mark or 14-mark answer — KTU examiners explicitly reward it.
- **Never confuse "MEMS package" with "IC package"** — MEMS packages must accommodate *moving structures*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wafer Stack Thickness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wafer Stack Thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wafer Stack Thickness** is defined as the **total vertical dimensional extent of a multi-layered MEMS structure** formed by bonding two or more processed semiconductor wafers together, measured from the outermost top surface to the outermost bottom surface of the assembled stack, including all individual wafer thicknesses and intermediate bonding interface layers.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **PEICT632 – Micro Electro Mechanical Systems**, Module 4 deals with **Wafer-Level Packaging and Bonding Technologies**. Wafer Stack Thickness ($t_{stack}$) is a critical geometric parameter that determines the mechanical, thermal, and electrical behaviour of the final packaged microsystem. The mathematical representation is:

$$t_{stack} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{w_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} t_{b_j}$$

where $t_{w_i}$ is the thickness of the $i^{th}$ individual wafer, $t_{b_j}$ is the thickness of the $j^{th}$ bonding interface, and $n$ is the total number of wafers in the stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Wafer stack thickness directly influences (i) flexural rigidity $D$, (ii) resonant frequency $f_0$, (iii) out-of-plane stiffness, (iv) thermal capacitance, and (v) the overall hermeticity/encapsulation quality of MEMS devices.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine constructing a **multi-storey building** (a wafer stack) by stacking several **concrete slabs** (individual wafers) on top of each other, with a thin **layer of mortar** (bonding interface) between each slab. The total height of the building (stack thickness) equals the sum of the slab thicknesses plus the mortar layers.

Now consider three engineers inspecting this building:
- **Structural Engineer** → asks: "How thick is each floor? How much does the whole building bend under wind?" (Mechanical stiffness)
- **Acoustic Engineer** → asks: "At what frequency does the building sway dangerously?" (Resonant frequency)
- **HVAC Engineer** → asks: "How much heat does the building store?" (Thermal mass)

Just as the answers depend on the **total height and material distribution** of the building, the performance of a MEMS device depends on the **Wafer Stack Thickness** and how that thickness is distributed across bonded layers.

## 1.3 Standard Wafer Thickness Metrics (SEMI Standards)

| Wafer Diameter | Standard Thickness | Tolerance |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 100 mm (4″) | **525 µm** | ± 25 µm |
| 150 mm (6″) | **675 µm** | ± 25 µm |
| 200 mm (8″) | **725 µm** | ± 25 µm |
| 300 mm (12″) | **775 µm** | ± 25 µm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Total Thickness Variation (TTV)** — the difference between maximum and minimum thickness across a wafer — is a key quality metric. For a bonded stack, the acceptable TTV is typically **< 5 µm** for high-precision MEMS.

## 1.4 Physical Constants Relevant to Wafer Stacks

- Young's Modulus of Silicon: $E_{Si} = \mathbf{169 \; GPa}$ (⟨100⟩ direction)
- Young's Modulus of Glass (Pyrex 7740): $E_{glass} = \mathbf{63 \; GPa}$
- Density of Silicon: $\rho_{Si} = \mathbf{2330 \; kg/m^3}$
- Density of Glass: $\rho_{glass} = \mathbf{2230 \; kg/m^3}$
- Poisson's Ratio of Silicon: $\nu_{Si} = \mathbf{0.28}$
- Thermal Expansion Coefficient of Silicon: $\alpha_{Si} = \mathbf{2.6 \times 10^{-6} \; /K}$
- Thermal Expansion Coefficient of Pyrex: $\alpha_{glass} = \mathbf{3.2 \times 10^{-6} \; /K}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Breakdown of Wafer Stack Thickness

A typical MEMS wafer stack consists of the following layers (top to bottom):

1. **Cap/Handle Wafer (Top)** — Provides hermetic sealing and mechanical protection
2. **Bonding Interface 1** — Anodic / Fusion / Eutectic / Adhesive layer
3. **Device Wafer (Middle)** — Contains the active MEMS structures (beams, membranes, etc.)
4. **Bonding Interface 2** (optional) — For triple-wafer stacks
5. **Substrate/Interposer Wafer (Bottom)** — Provides electrical routing and mechanical base

### 2.1.1 Why Each Layer Matters

- **Cap wafer** → Defines the upper cavity depth and protects from contamination
- **Device wafer** → Holds the suspended structures whose thickness ($t_{device}$) sets the structural rigidity
- **Bonding interface** → Determines electrical interconnection, hermeticity, and introduces a thin compliant/diffusion zone
- **Substrate wafer** → Acts as the foundation; its thickness controls overall warpage

## 2.2 Bonding Interface Thickness by Technology

| Bonding Technology | Typical Interface Thickness | Process Temperature | Hermetic? |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| **Anodic Bonding** (Glass–Si) | **0.5 – 2 µm** (depletion + oxide) | 400 – 450 °C | Yes |
| **Fusion Bonding** (Si–Si) | **< 1 nm** (Si–O–Si bridge) | 800 – 1100 °C | Yes |
| **Eutectic Bonding** (Au–Si) | **1 – 10 µm** (Au–Si intermetallic) | 363 – 400 °C | Yes |
| **Adhesive Bonding** (SU-8/BCB) | **5 – 100 µm** | 150 – 300 °C | No |
| **Thermocompression Bonding** (Cu–Cu) | **1 – 5 µm** | 300 – 400 °C | Yes |

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" of Stack Thickness Control

- **Why control it?** → Because the device's **resonant frequency, sensitivity, bandwidth, and proof mass** are functions of the total structural thickness. A 10% error in $t_{stack}$ can shift the resonant frequency by ~15%.
- **How is it controlled?** → Via (i) precision wafer thinning (CMP, wet/dry etching, grinding), (ii) bonding layer uniformity, and (iii) post-bond annealing.
- **Measurement methods** → Contact profilometry, optical interferometry, white-light interferometry, and **SEM cross-sectioning**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All vertical-bar symbols are rendered as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--:|
| 1 | $t_{stack} = \sum t_{w_i} + \sum t_{b_j}$ | Total stack thickness (multi-wafer) | m |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{E \, t_{eff}^{3}}{12(1 - \nu^{2})}$ | Flexural rigidity per unit width of plate (composite) | N·m |
| 3 | $f_{0,plate} = \dfrac{\alpha^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho \, t_{eff}}}$ | Resonant frequency of a thin plate | Hz |
| 4 | $f_{0,cantilever} = \dfrac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{E \, I}{\rho \, A \, L^{4}}}$ | Fundamental resonant frequency of cantilever beam | Hz |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{b \, t_{eff}^{3}}{12}$ | Area moment of inertia for rectangular cross-section | m⁴ |
| 6 | $k = \dfrac{E \, b \, t_{eff}^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$ | Spring constant of end-loaded cantilever | N/m |
| 7 | $t_{eff} = \sqrt[3]{\sum_{i} \left( t_i^{3} - t_{i-1}^{3} \right)}$ | Effective thickness for composite/multi-layer stack | m |
| 8 | $\sigma_{thermal} = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu} \, \Delta\alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal mismatch stress at bonding interface | Pa |
| 9 | $\text{TTV} = t_{max} - t_{min}$ | Total Thickness Variation across wafer | m |
| 10 | $\delta_{bending} = \dfrac{q L^{4}}{D}$ | Static deflection under uniform load $q$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cube-dependence** in $t^{3}$ (rigidity) and $t^{3/2}$ (frequency) makes wafer stack thickness the **single most sensitive geometric parameter** in MEMS design. KTU examiners frequently test this dependence.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Inertial Sensors (Accelerometers/Gyroscopes):** Stack thickness sets the proof mass and suspension stiffness — directly controlling sensitivity and shock survival rating.
- **Pressure Sensors:** Membrane thickness in a stack determines the pressure range and sensitivity.
- **RF MEMS Switches:** Stack thickness sets the actuation voltage and contact gap.
- **Microfluidic Chips:** Stack thickness defines channel depth, fluidic capacitance, and dead volume.
- **Biomedical Implants (Neural Probes):** Stack thickness must be controlled to < 100 µm to match tissue compliance.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Stack Thickness for a 2-Wafer Anodic Bonded Stack

**Problem Setup:** A MEMS pressure sensor is built by anodic bonding a **Pyrex 7740 cap wafer** to a **silicon device wafer**. Given the standard wafer thicknesses and the anodic bonding interface, compute the total stack thickness.

**Given:**
- Cap wafer (Pyrex): $t_{w_1} = 675 \; \mu m$ (thinned to $500 \; \mu m$ before bonding)
- Device wafer (Si): $t_{w_2} = 675 \; \mu m$ (thinned to $400 \; \mu m$ by KOH etching)
- Anodic bonding interface: $t_b = 1.5 \; \mu m$ (measured by SEM cross-section)

**Step 1 — Identify the general formula for a 2-wafer stack:**

$$t_{stack} = t_{w_1} + t_b + t_{w_2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (convert all to SI units, metres):**

$$t_{w_1} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \; m, \quad t_{w_2} = 400 \times 10^{-6} \; m, \quad t_b = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \; m$$

**Step 3 — Perform the addition explicitly:**

$$t_{stack} = (500 + 1.5 + 400) \times 10^{-6} \; m$$

**Step 4 — Sum the numerators:**

$$500 + 1.5 = 501.5, \quad 501.5 + 400 = 901.5$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$t_{stack} = 901.5 \times 10^{-6} \; m = 901.5 \; \mu m$$

**Conclusion:** The two-wafer anodic-bonded stack has a total thickness of **901.5 µm**, dominated (≈ 99.8%) by the bulk wafer thicknesses, with the bonding interface contributing only 0.17%.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Thickness for a Composite (3-Layer) Stack

**Problem Setup:** A triple-wafer stack (used in advanced gyroscopes) has the following layered structure from top to bottom: **Si cap (50 µm) | Au-Sn bonding layer (5 µm) | Si device layer (30 µm) | Au-Sn bonding layer (5 µm) | Si handle (400 µm)**. Compute the effective mechanical thickness.

**Step 1 — For mechanical bending, the effective thickness is derived from the moment of inertia equivalence:**

$$I_{eq} = \frac{b}{12} \sum_{i} (t_i^{3} - t_{i-1}^{3})$$

where $b$ is the beam width and $t_i$ are cumulative thicknesses measured from the neutral plane.

**Step 2 — Define cumulative thicknesses (measured from bottom):**

$$t_0 = 0, \quad t_1 = 400, \quad t_2 = 400 + 5 = 405, \quad t_3 = 405 + 30 = 435, \quad t_4 = 435 + 5 = 440, \quad t_5 = 440 + 50 = 490 \; \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Apply the effective thickness formula (with $b$ cancelling):**

$$t_{eff}^{3} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \left( t_i^{3} - t_{i-1}^{3} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term (in $\mu m^{3}$):**

$$t_1^{3} - t_0^{3} = 400^{3} - 0^{3} = 64{,}000{,}000$$

$$t_2^{3} - t_1^{3} = 405^{3} - 400^{3} = 66{,}430{,}125 - 64{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}430{,}125$$

$$t_3^{3} - t_2^{3} = 435^{3} - 405^{3} = 82{,}312{,}875 - 66{,}430{,}125 = 15{,}882{,}750$$

$$t_4^{3} - t_3^{3} = 440^{3} - 435^{3} = 85{,}184{,}000 - 82{,}312{,}875 = 2{,}871{,}125$$

$$t_5^{3} - t_4^{3} = 490^{3} - 440^{3} = 117{,}649{,}000 - 85{,}184{,}000 = 32{,}465{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Sum all contributions:**

$$t_{eff}^{3} = (64{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}430{,}125 + 15{,}882{,}750 + 2{,}871{,}125 + 32{,}465{,}000) \; \mu m^{3}$$

$$t_{eff}^{3} = 117{,}649{,}000 \; \mu m^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Take the cube root:**

$$t_{eff} = \sqrt[3]{117{,}649{,}000} = 490 \; \mu m$$

**Conclusion:** The effective thickness equals the full geometric thickness, confirming that **stacking does not reduce mechanical thickness** — it preserves the full bulk rigidity. This is why thick handle wafers dominate the resonant behaviour.

## 3.3 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever in the Stack

**Problem Setup:** Using the stack from Section 3.1 ($t_{stack} = 901.5 \; \mu m$ total, but only the **Si device layer of 400 µm** is the active cantilever of length $L = 1000 \; \mu m$ and width $b = 100 \; \mu m$), compute the fundamental resonant frequency.

**Given:** $E_{Si} = 169 \times 10^{9} \; Pa$, $\rho_{Si} = 2330 \; kg/m^{3}$, $L = 1 \times 10^{-3} \; m$, $b = 100 \times 10^{-6} \; m$, $t = 400 \times 10^{-6} \; m$.

**Step 1 — Compute the area moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{b \, t^{3}}{12} = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-6}) (400 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-6})(64 \times 10^{-15})}{12} = \frac{6400 \times 10^{-21}}{12} = 533.33 \times 10^{-21} \; m^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cross-sectional area:**

$$A = b \times t = (100 \times 10^{-6})(400 \times 10^{-6}) = 40 \times 10^{-9} \; m^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the cantilever resonance formula:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E \, I}{\rho \, A \, L^{4}}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $1.875^{2} = 3.5156$ and $2\pi = 6.2832$:**

$$f_0 = \frac{3.5156}{6.2832} \sqrt{\frac{(169 \times 10^{9})(533.33 \times 10^{-21})}{(2330)(40 \times 10^{-9})(1 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator inside the square root:**

$$E \cdot I = (169 \times 10^{9})(533.33 \times 10^{-21}) = 90{,}133 \times 10^{-12} = 9.0133 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the denominator inside the square root:**

$$\rho \cdot A \cdot L^{4} = (2330)(40 \times 10^{-9})(1 \times 10^{-12}) = 93{,}200 \times 10^{-21} = 9.32 \times 10^{-17}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the ratio inside the square root:**

$$\frac{E \cdot I}{\rho \cdot A \cdot L^{4}} = \frac{9.0133 \times 10^{-8}}{9.32 \times 10^{-17}} = 0.9671 \times 10^{9} = 9.671 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 8 — Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{9.671 \times 10^{8}} = 31{,}100.5 \; rad/s$$

**Step 9 — Multiply by the prefactor:**

$$f_0 = (0.5595)(31{,}100.5) = 17{,}401 \; Hz \approx 17.4 \; kHz$$

**Conclusion:** The fundamental resonant frequency of this cantilever in the stack is **≈ 17.4 kHz**, a typical value for MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes used in consumer electronics.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensitivity of $f_0$ to Stack Thickness Variations

| $t$ (µm) | $I$ (×10⁻²¹ m⁴) | $f_0$ (kHz) | $\Delta f_0$ vs nominal |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 360 | 388.8 | 16.0 | − 8.0% |
| 380 | 457.0 | 16.7 | − 4.0% |
| **400** | **533.3** | **17.4** | **0% (nominal)** |
| 420 | 617.4 | 18.0 | + 3.4% |
| 440 | 710.0 | 18.6 | + 6.9% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $\Delta f_0 \approx 1.5 \times \Delta t$ sensitivity demonstrates the **~3/2 power law** between thickness and frequency. A 5% under-thinning shifts the resonance by ~7.5% — easily detectable in production testing.
