# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]

**"Define Wafer Stack Thickness in the context of MEMS wafer-level packaging. List the three main contributions to the total stack thickness in a 2-wafer anodic-bonded MEMS structure."**

**Model Answer:**

Wafer Stack Thickness is the total vertical dimension of a multi-wafer MEMS assembly measured from the top surface of the uppermost wafer to the bottom surface of the lowermost wafer, including all bonding interfaces.

For a 2-wafer anodic-bonded structure, the three main contributions are:
1. **Cap wafer thickness** ($t_{w_1}$): typically 300–675 µm of Pyrex or Si
2. **Anodic bonding interface thickness** ($t_b$): typically 0.5–2 µm (glass–Si depletion + oxide interlayer)
3. **Device wafer thickness** ($t_{w_2}$): typically 200–675 µm of Si, often thinned by KOH or DRIE

Mathematically: $t_{stack} = t_{w_1} + t_b + t_{w_2}$.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]

**"Why is wafer stack thickness a critical design parameter in MEMS devices? Justify with two engineering reasons."**

**Model Answer:**

Wafer stack thickness is critical because:

1. **Mechanical Rigidity Dependence (Cube Law):** The flexural rigidity $D$ and spring constant $k$ of MEMS structures scale with the **cube** of thickness ($D \propto t^{3}$, $k \propto t^{3}$). A small variation of 5% in thickness can cause a ~15% change in stiffness, directly shifting the device's mechanical response.

2. **Resonant Frequency Dependence (3/2 Power Law):** The fundamental resonant frequency $f_0$ scales as $t^{3/2}$. For accelerometers, gyroscopes, and resonators, this sets the operating bandwidth, sensitivity, and noise floor — making thickness control a yield-limiting factor in production.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: Answer any **one** full question from the Module 4 choice. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]

**(a)** A MEMS accelerometer is fabricated by anodic bonding a **Pyrex 7740 cap wafer** (initial 675 µm, thinned to 400 µm) onto a **silicon device wafer** (initial 675 µm, thinned to 300 µm). The anodic bonding interface is 2 µm. Compute the total wafer stack thickness. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the same stack, if the active silicon cantilever is 800 µm long, 80 µm wide, and 300 µm thick, compute its fundamental resonant frequency. Use $E_{Si} = 169$ GPa, $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³. Comment on the effect of a ±10% thickness variation on $f_0$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Write the formula:

$$t_{stack} = t_{Pyrex} + t_{bond} + t_{Si} = 400 + 2 + 300 = 702 \; \mu m$$

Step 2 — Express in SI units:

$$t_{stack} = 702 \times 10^{-6} \; m = 0.702 \; mm$$

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Area moment of inertia:

$$I = \frac{b \, t^{3}}{12} = \frac{(80 \times 10^{-6})(300 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12} = \frac{(80 \times 10^{-6})(27 \times 10^{-15})}{12} = 180 \times 10^{-21} \; m^{4}$$

Step 2 — Cross-sectional area:

$$A = b \times t = (80 \times 10^{-6})(300 \times 10^{-6}) = 24 \times 10^{-9} \; m^{2}$$

Step 3 — Resonant frequency:

$$f_0 = \frac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E \, I}{\rho \, A \, L^{4}}}$$

Step 4 — Numerator inside the square root:

$$E \cdot I = (169 \times 10^{9})(180 \times 10^{-21}) = 30{,}420 \times 10^{-12} = 3.042 \times 10^{-8}$$

Step 5 — Denominator inside the square root:

$$\rho \cdot A \cdot L^{4} = (2330)(24 \times 10^{-9})(800 \times 10^{-6})^{4} = (2330)(24 \times 10^{-9})(409.6 \times 10^{-18})$$

$$= 2330 \times 9.83 \times 10^{-24} = 22{,}904 \times 10^{-24} = 2.29 \times 10^{-20}$$

Step 6 — Ratio and square root:

$$\frac{E \cdot I}{\rho \, A \, L^{4}} = \frac{3.042 \times 10^{-8}}{2.29 \times 10^{-20}} = 1.328 \times 10^{12}$$

$$\sqrt{1.328 \times 10^{12}} = 1.153 \times 10^{6} \; rad/s$$

Step 7 — Final frequency:

$$f_0 = (0.5595)(1.153 \times 10^{6}) = 6.45 \times 10^{5} \; Hz \approx 645 \; kHz$$

**Comment on ±10% variation:** Since $f_0 \propto t^{3/2}$, a +10% change gives $f_0' = f_0 \times (1.10)^{1.5} = f_0 \times 1.153$, i.e., a **+15.3% increase**. A −10% change gives $f_0' = f_0 \times 0.854$, i.e., a **−14.6% decrease**. This shows the strong sensitivity of frequency to thickness control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **layered architecture** of a typical 3-wafer MEMS stack used in gyroscopes. Identify the role of each wafer and the two bonding interfaces. (7 marks)

**(b)** A triple-stack gyroscope has the following structure (top to bottom): Si cap (50 µm) – Au-Sn bond (5 µm) – Si active layer (40 µm) – Au-Sn bond (5 µm) – Si handle (400 µm). Compute the total stack thickness, the **effective mechanical thickness**, and the **dominant contribution** to the flexural rigidity. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Refer to **SECTION 4, Diagram 4.1** for the layered architecture. Explanation:

- **Top Si cap wafer** → Hermetically seals the active MEMS structure; provides environmental protection; its thickness defines the upper cavity gap.
- **First bonding interface (Au–Sn eutectic, 5 µm)** → Provides electrical interconnect and mechanical anchoring; forms an intermetallic Au–Sn alloy that is hermetic.
- **Middle Si device wafer** → Contains the suspended proof mass, comb drives, and sensing electrodes; its active layer thickness is the most critical geometric parameter.
- **Second bonding interface (Au–Sn, 5 µm)** → Connects the device wafer to the handle wafer; provides ground planes and stress-relief.
- **Bottom Si handle wafer** → Provides mechanical support, electrical routing, and thermal mass; absorbs packaging stresses.

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Total stack thickness:

$$t_{stack} = 50 + 5 + 40 + 5 + 400 = 500 \; \mu m$$

Step 2 — Cumulative thicknesses from the bottom:

$$t_0 = 0, \; t_1 = 400, \; t_2 = 405, \; t_3 = 445, \; t_4 = 450, \; t_5 = 500 \; \mu m$$

Step 3 — Effective thickness from cube contributions:

$$t_1^{3} - t_0^{3} = 64{,}000{,}000$$
$$t_2^{3} - t_1^{3} = 66{,}430{,}125 - 64{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}430{,}125$$
$$t_3^{3} - t_2^{3} = 88{,}196{,}125 - 66{,}430{,}125 = 21{,}766{,}000$$
$$t_4^{3} - t_3^{3} = 91{,}125{,}000 - 88{,}196{,}125 = 2{,}928{,}875$$
$$t_5^{3} - t_4^{3} = 125{,}000{,}000 - 91{,}125{,}000 = 33{,}875{,}000$$

Step 4 — Sum and take cube root:

$$t_{eff}^{3} = 64{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}430{,}125 + 21{,}766{,}000 + 2{,}928{,}875 + 33{,}875{,}000 = 125{,}000{,}000 \; \mu m^{3}$$

$$t_{eff} = \sqrt[3]{125{,}000{,}000} = 500 \; \mu m$$

Step 5 — Dominant contribution: The **Si handle wafer (400 µm)** contributes $64{,}000{,}000 / 125{,}000{,}000 = \mathbf{51.2\%}$ of the cube-summation. In contrast, the active Si layer (40 µm) contributes only $21{,}766{,}000 / 125{,}000{,}000 = \mathbf{17.4\%}$. **Conclusion:** The handle wafer **dominates** the flexural rigidity, which is why designers must carefully account for it in simulations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bonding interface layer:** Many students add only the two wafer thicknesses and miss the 0.5–10 µm bond layer, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing µm and mm in the same calculation leads to magnitude errors — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing unit conversions.
> 3. **Forgetting the cube law:** When asked about "effect of thickness on stiffness," students often write $k \propto t$ instead of $k \propto t^{3}$. This is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Confusing TTV with thickness:** Total Thickness Variation is a *quality metric*, not the same as the absolute stack thickness. Examiners test this distinction.
> 5. **Missing the $1-\nu^{2}$ factor:** For plate bending, the denominator is $12(1-\nu^{2})$, not just 12. Marks are lost if this is omitted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wafer Stack Thickness (Module 4, PEICT632)**

- **Definition:** Total vertical dimension of a multi-wafer MEMS assembly = $\sum t_{w_i} + \sum t_{b_j}$.
- **Standard Wafer Thicknesses (SEMI):** 4″ → 525 µm; 6″ → 675 µm; 8″ → 725 µm; 12″ → 775 µm.
- **Dominant Layer:** The handle/substrate wafer typically contributes **> 50%** of the cube-summation in flexural rigidity.
- **Bonding Interface Thickness Range:** 0.5–100 µm depending on technology (fusion < anodic < eutectic < adhesive).
- **Cube Law (RIGIDITY):** $D \propto t^{3}$ → small thickness errors cause large stiffness changes.
- **3/2 Power Law (FREQUENCY):** $f_0 \propto t^{3/2}$ → 5% thickness error → 7.5% frequency shift.
- **TTV (Total Thickness Variation):** Quality metric; must be < 5 µm for high-precision MEMS.
- **Thermal Mismatch Stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu} \Delta\alpha \Delta T$ — critical for anodic bonding of Si–glass.
- **Flexural Rigidity Formula:** $D = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ — do NOT omit $(1-\nu^{2})$.
- **Spring Constant (Cantilever):** $k = \dfrac{E b t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$.
- **Cantilever Resonant Frequency:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}}}$.
- **Effective Composite Thickness:** $t_{eff}^{3} = \sum (t_i^{3} - t_{i-1}^{3})$.
- **Measurement Methods:** Profilometry, white-light interferometry, SEM cross-section.
- **Process Sequence (Generic):** Start wafer → thin → align → bond → anneal → measure TTV → accept/reject.
- **Engineering Impact:** Drives sensitivity, bandwidth, shock survival, and thermal behaviour of accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors, and RF MEMS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wafer Dicing Concerns, Thermal Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wafer Dicing Concerns, Thermal Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wafer Dicing — The Big Picture

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Wafer dicing is the precision-controlled process of mechanically, thermally, or chemically separating an entire semiconductor wafer (containing hundreds or thousands of identical MEMS/IC dies) into individual functional chips along pre-defined "dicing streets" or "scribe lines," while preserving the structural integrity, yield, and functional performance of each die.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dicing Street (Scribe Line):** The narrow non-functional region (typically **50–100 µm wide**) reserved between adjacent die layouts to accommodate the dicing tool path. It contains test structures and alignment marks but no active circuitry.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Cutting a Multi-Block Chocolate Bar"

Imagine a giant chocolate bar where each square is a finished, fragile cookie decorated with sugar flowers (your MEMS die). Wafer dicing is the act of cleanly separating the squares **without cracking the cookie, smashing the sugar flowers, or wasting chocolate**. The "chocolate bar" is the silicon wafer, the "squares" are the dies, and the "sugar flowers" represent the delicate suspended microstructures (beams, membranes, cantilevers) that must remain pristine. Just as you would not use a sledgehammer on the cookie bar, MEMS dicing requires surgical precision because the structures can be tens of micrometres thick with sub-micron features.

---

## 1.2 Why Wafer Dicing is a Critical Concern for MEMS

Unlike standard CMOS ICs, MEMS devices contain **freestanding microstructures** — cantilevers, comb-drives, diaphragms, and suspended membranes — that are extremely vulnerable to mechanical shock, vibration, particle contamination, and moisture. Therefore, dicing concerns in MEMS are amplified compared to traditional ICs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The MEMS Dicing Paradox:** MEMS chips *must* be protected during dicing, yet many MEMS structures are designed to be exposed to the ambient environment *after* dicing. The packaging and dicing strategies must therefore allow "release and access" while preventing in-process damage.

### The Five Primary Dicing Concerns in MEMS

1. **Front-Side Chipping & Micro-cracking:** Brittle fracture propagation from the dicing blade or laser impact zone can extend microns into the active die area, weakening the die and degrading long-term reliability.

2. **Back-Side Chipping & Die-Edge Strength Degradation:** The exit point of the blade experiences tensile stress, often creating invisible sub-surface micro-cracks that reduce die fracture strength by **30–60 %** compared to a pristine wafer.

3. **Cooling Water & Debris Contamination:** Conventional blade dicing uses high-pressure DI water cooling, which can cause **stiction** (collapse adhesion of microstructures), residue deposition, and electrolyte-induced corrosion of exposed metal.

4. **Mechanical Vibration & Shock Loading:** Blade impact transmits shock waves through the wafer, which can deform or fracture suspended MEMS elements, especially large-area membranes or low-spring-constant structures.

5. **Thermal Damage (Laser Ablation):** Laser dicing generates localized temperatures exceeding **1000 °C**, which can melt metallisation, delaminate thin films, and induce thermal stress in dissimilar material stacks.

---

## 1.3 Thermal Management — The Big Picture

**Formal Definition:**
Thermal management in MEMS refers to the engineering discipline of **controlling, dissipating, and distributing heat** within and around micro-scale devices such that operating temperatures remain within specified limits, thermal gradients do not induce harmful mechanical stress, and temperature-sensitive physical phenomena (e.g., resonant frequency, fluid viscosity, gas density) remain stable and predictable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** The thermal time-constant of a MEMS device scales as **τ ≈ L²/α**, where L is the characteristic length and **α = k/(ρcₚ)** is the thermal diffusivity. Because MEMS feature lengths are small, thermal transients are fast — sometimes sub-millisecond — making heat dissipation a critical design constraint.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Cooling a Laptop CPU"

A laptop CPU is essentially a "macro-MEMS" heat problem: a tiny chip generating tens of watts in a confined space. You add a heat sink, thermal paste, fan, and sometimes heat pipes. MEMS thermal management follows the **exact same principles** at the micron scale: conduction through silicon, convection into surrounding air/fluid, sometimes radiation, often enhanced with microchannel cooling, thermoelectric coolers (TECs/Peltier modules), or on-chip heat spreaders. The key difference is that MEMS heat fluxes can be **orders of magnitude higher** per unit area due to sub-millimetre active regions.

### Why Thermal Management is Crucial in MEMS

- **Frequency Stability:** Resonant MEMS sensors (gyroscopes, resonators) shift frequency with temperature at typical rates of **−30 to −100 ppm/°C** due to Young's modulus and dimensional changes.
- **Thermal Stress:** CTE mismatch between layers (e.g., Si = **2.6 ppm/°C**, Al = **23 ppm/°C**, SiO₂ = **0.5 ppm/°C**) causes wafer bow, film cracking, and die warpage.
- **Convective Damping:** Gas-filled MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes experience damping forces that are sensitive to gas viscosity, which varies with **T⁰·⁷**.
- **Sensor Drift:** Pirani gauges, gas sensors, and thermal flow sensors depend on temperature for their operating principle — any parasitic heating cross-talk causes drift.
- **Reliability & Lifetime:** Elevated temperatures accelerate electromigration, corrosion, and fatigue — every **10 °C rise roughly halves** component lifetime (Arrhenius rule of thumb).

---

## 1.4 High-Yield Metric Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics to Memorise for KTU Exams:**
> - **Kerf width** (blade dicing): **25–50 µm**
> - **Stealth dicing kerf**: ~**0 µm** (subsurface only)
> - **Typical die fracture strength**: **200–400 MPa** (Si)
> - **Thermal conductivity of Si**: **150 W/(m·K)** (room temperature)
> - **Thermal conductivity of SiO₂**: **1.4 W/(m·K)** (≈100× lower than Si)
> - **Specific heat of Si**: **700 J/(kg·K)**
> - **Density of Si**: **2330 kg/m³**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wafer Dicing Techniques — A Taxonomy

Modern wafer dicing for MEMS can be classified into **five fundamental categories**. Each has a distinct physical mechanism, kerf profile, and suitability envelope.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Blade Dicing (Saw Dicing)

A diamond-impregnated blade rotating at **20,000–60,000 RPM** mechanically abrades the silicon along the dicing street. Cooling water (DI, ~**2–5 L/min**) carries away heat and swarf.

**Process Parameters:**
- Spindle speed: **20–60 krpm**
- Feed rate: **20–100 mm/s**
- Blade thickness: **20–50 µm**
- Exposure height: **25–50 µm**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Default:** Mechanical saw dicing remains the workhorse for >**75 %** of all silicon wafer dicing worldwide due to its throughput (multiple blades stacked in a "hub") and low cost-per-die.

### 2.1.2 Laser Dicing (Thermal Ablation)

A focused laser (typically **UV wavelength**, e.g., 355 nm Nd:YAG tripled) vaporises the silicon via local heating above the vaporisation point (~**2200 °C**). The high absorption depth in silicon requires UV wavelengths to minimise thermal diffusion zone.

**Advantages:** No mechanical force → no chipping; suitable for ultra-thin wafers (<**50 µm**) and brittle materials.

**Disadvantages:** Heat-affected zone (HAZ), debris redeposition, slower throughput.

### 2.1.3 Stealth Dicing (SD)

A **1064 nm or 1550 nm IR laser** is focused *inside* the wafer at a depth of typically **30–50 µm** below the surface. The laser creates a modified layer (dislocation network) but does NOT cut through. The wafer is then mounted on adhesive tape and mechanically separated along the modified lines. This produces **zero kerf loss** and **no front/back-side chipping**.

### 2.1.4 Plasma Dicing

The wafer is masked with photoresist leaving only the dicing streets exposed. Reactive ion etching (RIE / DRIE / ICP) then anisotropically etches through the entire wafer thickness. Plasma dicing is **mask-defined**, can handle **any shape** (curved, hexagonal), and produces **chip-strength improvements of 50–200 %** compared to blade dicing because there is no micro-crack initiation.

### 2.1.5 Scribing & Breaking (Historical)

A diamond tip scribes a shallow notch on the wafer surface, then mechanical flexure snaps the wafer. Still used for some **thick compound semiconductors** (GaAs, InP) but unsuitable for thin MEMS wafers.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Dicing Concerns in MEMS

### Concern #1 — Front-Side Chipping

Front-side chipping occurs at the **blade entry edge**. It is governed by the interaction between:
- Blade grit size (larger grit → more aggressive cutting, more chipping)
- Feed rate (higher → larger chipping)
- Blade wear state
- Wafer crystallographic orientation (Si has anisotropic fracture toughness)

**Mitigation:** Use **fine-grit blades** (mesh #2000 or finer), reduce feed rate, use **step-cut** or **dual-spindle** strategies that cut a shallow chamfer first, then complete.

### Concern #2 — Back-Side Chipping

The blade exit point (typically **20–30 µm**) is the most common failure site. Tensile stress concentration here causes **brittle fracture pop-out**, leaving a "V-shaped" notch on the back surface. This notch reduces **3-point bend die strength** dramatically and is the primary cause of low-k dielectric and MEMS membrane cracking during downstream pick-and-place.

**Mitigation:**
- "**Dice-Before-Grind (DBG)**" — thin the wafer *after* dicing streets are cut, so the exit chip happens during grinding where it does not propagate into active silicon.
- "**Spindle-tilted dicing**" — angling the blade 2–8° reduces exit chip size.
- "**UV-tape adhesive**" whose adhesion decreases upon UV exposure, used to cushion the die during and after dicing.

### Concern #3 — Stiction from Cooling Water

Many MEMS devices are released (sacrificial etch removed) *before* dicing. Cooling water creates surface tension capillary forces as it dries. If the released microstructure is compliant enough, surface tension can permanently pull it into contact with the substrate — a phenomenon known as **stiction**.

**Mitigation:**
- **Critical point drying (CPD)** post-dicing.
- **Hydrophobic self-assembled monolayer (SAM)** coating (e.g., FDTS).
- **Dimple structures** on the substrate surface to reduce contact area.
- Use **dry-dicing** techniques (plasma or stealth).

### Concern #4 — Particle & Debris Contamination

Swarf particles generated during dicing can:
- Lodge under MEMS structures and jam moving parts.
- Cause electrical shorts in on-chip circuitry.
- Contaminate optical surfaces in MOEMS devices.

**Mitigation:** High-pressure DI water rinse, **CO₂ cryogenic cleaning**, **spin-rinse-dry (SRD)** post-dicing, and cleanroom-grade adhesive tape.

### Concern #5 — Vibration-Induced Damage

The dicing blade's impact generates a shock wave propagating at the speed of sound in silicon (~**8430 m/s**). This wave reflects at die boundaries and can excite resonant modes of MEMS structures. For example, a **1 MHz gyroscope mode** can be excited if the dicing shock contains that frequency component.

**Mitigation:** Damping tape, soft-mount chuck, lower feed rate, use of **dicing frames** to mechanically isolate individual dies.

---

## 2.3 Thermal Management — The Heat Transfer Triad

Thermal management of MEMS relies on the **three classical modes of heat transfer**:

### Mode 1 — Conduction (Dominant in MEMS)

Heat flows through solid materials driven by a temperature gradient. **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** governs:

$$
\vec{q} = -k \nabla T
$$

Where:
- **q⃗** is the local heat flux vector [W/m²]
- **k** is the thermal conductivity of the material [W/(m·K)]
- **∇T** is the temperature gradient [K/m]

For a 1-D slab of cross-section A and thickness L:

$$
q = \frac{k A (T_1 - T_2)}{L}
$$

### Mode 2 — Convection

Heat transfer between a solid surface and a moving fluid (gas or liquid). Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$
q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)
$$

Where **h** is the convective heat transfer coefficient [W/(m²·K)], typically:
- Natural air convection: **h ≈ 5–25 W/(m²·K)**
- Forced air (fan): **h ≈ 25–250 W/(m²·K)**
- Water (forced): **h ≈ 500–20,000 W/(m²·K)**

### Mode 3 — Radiation

Heat transfer via electromagnetic emission (important at high T or vacuum):

$$
q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{\text{surr}}^4)
$$

Where **ε** is emissivity, **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant.

---

## 2.4 Thermal Resistance Network — The Key Engineering Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Resistance Analogy:** Thermal problems are **electrically analogous** to resistor networks. Replace temperature difference with voltage, heat flow with current, and thermal resistance with electrical resistance.

For a 1-D conduction path, the thermal resistance is:

$$
R_{th} = \frac{L}{k A}
$$

Units: **K/W** (Kelvin per Watt).

For a die-to-ambient thermal path (the classic JEDEC JESD51 stack):

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{jc} + R_{cs} + R_{sa}
$$

Where:
- **R_jc** = Junction-to-case (package internal conduction)
- **R_cs** = Case-to-heatsink (TIM interface)
- **R_sa** = Heatsink-to-ambient (convection + radiation)

The steady-state junction temperature is:

$$
T_j = T_a + P \cdot R_{\text{total}}
$$

Where **P** is the dissipated power. This equation is the **single most-tested thermal relation** in KTU board examinations.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = k A \Delta T / L$ | 1-D Fourier conduction | W |
| 2 | $R_{th} = L / (k A)$ | Thermal resistance of a slab | K/W |
| 3 | $R_{\text{total}} = \sum_i R_i$ | Series thermal resistance addition | K/W |
| 4 | $1/R_{\text{parallel}} = \sum_i 1/R_i$ | Parallel thermal resistance (multiple paths) | K/W |
| 5 | $T_j = T_a + P R_{\text{total}}$ | Junction temperature rise | K |
| 6 | $q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat flux | W |
| 7 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{\infty}^4)$ | Radiative heat flux | W |
| 8 | $\tau = L^2 / \alpha = \rho c_p L^2 / k$ | Thermal time constant | s |
| 9 | $\dot{Q} = m c_p \Delta T$ | Sensible heat stored | J |
| 10 | $f_T \propto \sqrt{k/(\rho c_p)}$ | Frequency dependence on thermal diffusivity | Hz |
| 11 | $\Delta L = \alpha_L L \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion (linear) | m |
| 12 | $\sigma_T = E \alpha_L \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (1-D constrained) | Pa |
| 13 | $P_{\text{diss}} = I^2 R = V^2 / R$ | Joule heating power | W |
| 14 | $R_{jc} = t_{\text{die}} / (k_{Si} A_{\text{die}})$ | Junction-to-case for a die | K/W |
| 15 | $\theta_{JA} = (T_j - T_A)/P$ | Standard thermal characterisation | K/W |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using **$R_{th} = L/(kA)$**, students often forget to convert units. If L is in **µm** and A in **mm²**, you MUST convert to metres and m². A typical exam mistake loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 2.6 MEMS-Specific Thermal Management Strategies

### Strategy 1 — On-Chip Heat Spreaders

Thick patterned metal (Al, Cu) or poly-Si layers are deposited over the active region to spread localised hotspots laterally. Because **k_Cu ≈ 400 W/(m·K)** and **k_Al ≈ 235 W/(m·K)**, these spreaders dramatically lower peak temperature.

### Strategy 2 — Thermal Vias

Arrays of through-silicon-vias (TSVs) filled with copper or tungsten carry heat from the active die surface to a backside heat sink. Effective thermal conductivity of a via-array is approximated by:

$$
k_{\text{eff}} = k_{\text{via}} \cdot \phi + k_{\text{Si}} \cdot (1 - \phi)
$$

Where **φ** is the via areal fill fraction.

### Strategy 3 — Microchannel Cooling

Etched microchannels (typically **50–500 µm wide, 200–500 µm deep**) integrated into the silicon die carry coolant fluid. Heat transfer coefficients can exceed **10,000 W/(m²·K)** with water at flow rates of ~**1 mL/min**. This is the **gold standard** for high-heat-flux MEMS applications (e.g., power amplifiers, laser diode arrays).

### Strategy 4 — Thermoelectric Coolers (TECs / Peltier)

By exploiting the Peltier effect, current-driven semiconductor junctions (typically Bi₂Te₃) pump heat from one side to the other. TECs are **solid-state, reversible, and precisely controllable** but have efficiencies of only **5–10 %** of Carnot.

### Strategy 5 — Phase-Change Cooling

Utilises latent heat of vaporisation (boiling) or melting to absorb large quantities of heat at near-constant temperature. Micro-scale heat pipes and thermosyphons are an active MEMS research area.

### Strategy 6 — Material Selection & Geometry

Use high-k materials (SiC: **k ≈ 490 W/(m·K)**, diamond: **k ≈ 2000 W/(m·K)**) where heat spreading is critical. Reduce hotspot area by distributing power dissipation across the chip.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Junction Temperature from a Multi-Layer Stack

> **Problem Statement:** A MEMS sensor die (Si, k = **150 W/(m·K)**) of dimensions **3 mm × 3 mm × 0.5 mm** is bonded with a **25 µm** thick die-attach (k = **1.5 W/(m·K)**) to a **1.5 mm** thick alumina package substrate (k = **24 W/(m·K)**). The substrate is mounted to a **copper heat sink** (k = **400 W/(m·K)**) of dimensions **10 mm × 10 mm × 3 mm**. Forced convection at the top surface has **h = 200 W/(m²·K)** and ambient is **T_∞ = 25 °C**. The die dissipates **P = 0.5 W**. Find the steady-state die temperature.

### Step 1 — Identify Each Thermal Resistance

We treat the heat-flow path as a series combination of three conduction resistances (die → die-attach → substrate → heat sink) plus a top-side convection resistance. However, since the heat source is at the die level and convection is at the top surface, the most accurate model is:

> Heat flows in **two directions**: (a) downward through the stack to the heat sink, and (b) upward through convection to ambient.

### Step 2 — Compute Top-Side Convection Resistance

$$
A_{\text{top}} = 3 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm} = 9 \text{ mm}^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
R_{\text{conv,top}} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A_{\text{top}}}
$$

Substituting h = 200 W/(m²·K) and A_top = 9 × 10⁻⁶ m²:

$$
R_{\text{conv,top}} = \frac{1}{200 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{conv,top}} = \frac{1}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 555.6 \text{ K/W}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Die Conduction Resistance

$$
A_{\text{die}} = 9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
L_{\text{die}} = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

$$
R_{\text{die}} = \frac{L_{\text{die}}}{k_{Si} \cdot A_{\text{die}}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{die}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{150 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{die}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.35 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.370 \text{ K/W}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Die-Attach Resistance

$$
L_{da} = 25 \text{ µm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
R_{da} = \frac{L_{da}}{k_{da} \cdot A_{\text{die}}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.35 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.852 \text{ K/W}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Substrate Resistance

$$
L_{\text{sub}} = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
R_{\text{sub}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{24 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.16 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6.944 \text{ K/W}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Heat Sink Resistance

$$
A_{\text{HS}} = 10 \times 10 = 100 \text{ mm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
L_{\text{HS}} = 3 \text{ mm} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
R_{\text{HS}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{400 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.075 \text{ K/W}
$$

### Step 7 — Identify Two Parallel Paths

Path A (downward through the stack to the heat sink base, which we assume is at ambient T_a):

$$
R_A = R_{\text{die}} + R_{da} + R_{\text{sub}} + R_{\text{HS}} = 0.370 + 1.852 + 6.944 + 0.075
$$

$$
R_A = 9.241 \text{ K/W}
$$

Path B (upward through top convection):

$$
R_B = R_{\text{conv,top}} = 555.6 \text{ K/W}
$$

Since these two paths are in parallel (both originate at the die junction and terminate at T_a):

$$
\frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{R_A} + \frac{1}{R_B}
$$

Because R_B ≫ R_A (top convection is negligible), we get:

$$
R_{\text{total}} \approx R_A \approx 9.24 \text{ K/W}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute Junction Temperature

$$
T_j = T_a + P \cdot R_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
T_j = 25 + 0.5 \times 9.24 = 25 + 4.62
$$

$$
\boxed{T_j \approx 29.6 \text{ °C}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dominant thermal bottleneck here is the **alumina substrate** (6.94 K/W), which accounts for ~75 % of the total resistance. Replacing alumina with **direct-bond-copper (DBC) substrate** or a **silicon interposer** with TSVs would dramatically reduce R_total.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 1-D Transient Conduction in a MEMS Membrane

> **Problem Statement:** A thin Si membrane (thickness L = **10 µm**, density ρ = **2330 kg/m³**, specific heat c_p = **700 J/(kg·K)**, k = **150 W/(m·K)**) is suddenly exposed to a uniform heat flux **q₀ = 10⁵ W/m²** on one face. The other face is insulated. Find the time for the membrane temperature to rise by **ΔT = 50 K**.

### Step 1 — Thermal Diffusivity

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{150}{2330 \times 700} = \frac{150}{1.631 \times 10^6} \approx 9.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Thermal Time Constant

The characteristic thermal time constant for a slab of thickness L with one-side heating is:

$$
\tau = \frac{L^2}{\alpha}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})^2}{9.2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^{-10}}{9.2 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.087 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.09 \text{ µs}
$$

### Step 3 — Energy Balance to Find ΔT

The total thermal mass of the membrane per unit area:

$$
\text{Mass/Area} = \rho L = 2330 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0233 \text{ kg/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Heat capacity/Area} = \rho c_p L = 2330 \times 700 \times 10^{-5} = 16.31 \text{ J/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Time

For a lumped-capacitance model (valid because Biot number ≪ 1 for thin membranes):

$$
q_0 \cdot t = \rho c_p L \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
t = \frac{\rho c_p L \cdot \Delta T}{q_0} = \frac{16.31 \times 50}{10^5}
$$

$$
t = \frac{815.5}{10^5} = 8.155 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 8.16 \text{ ms}}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Lumped Capacitance Assumption (Biot Number)

$$
\text{Bi} = \frac{h L}{k}
$$

If the membrane loses heat via natural convection h ≈ 10 W/(m²·K):

$$
\text{Bi} = \frac{10 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{150} \approx 6.7 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Since Bi ≪ 0.1, the lumped-capacitance model is **valid**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Microchannel Heat Sink Sizing

> **Problem Statement:** A MEMS power amplifier dissipates **P = 2 W** over an active area of **2 mm × 2 mm = 4 mm²**. Cooling water flows through an integrated microchannel heat sink of width **w = 200 µm**, depth **h_ch = 300 µm**, with **N = 25 channels** etched beneath the die. Water inlet temperature is **T_in = 25 °C** and we want ΔT_max = **20 K**. Find the required mass flow rate.

### Step 1 — Heat Capacity Rate Equation

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_{p,w} \Delta T
$$

Where c_p,w = **4186 J/(kg·K)** for water.

### Step 2 — Solve for Mass Flow Rate

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{P}{c_{p,w} \Delta T}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{2}{4186 \times 20} = \frac{2}{83720} \approx 2.39 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate

Assuming water density ρ_w = **1000 kg/m³**:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_w} = 2.39 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 1.43 \text{ mL/min}
$$

### Step 4 — Average Channel Velocity

Total cross-sectional flow area:

$$
A_{\text{flow}} = N \cdot w \cdot h_{ch} = 25 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 300 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
A_{\text{flow}} = 25 \times 6 \times 10^{-8} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\dot{V}}{A_{\text{flow}}} = \frac{2.39 \times 10^{-8}}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.0159 \text{ m/s} = 1.59 \text{ cm/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Reynolds Number (Flow Regime Check)

Hydraulic diameter:

$$
D_h = \frac{4 A_{\text{flow}}}{P_{\text{wet}}}
$$

For rectangular channels with high aspect ratio:

$$
D_h \approx \frac{2 w h_{ch}}{w + h_{ch}} = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 300}{500} = 240 \text{ µm}
$$

Reynolds number:

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho_w u_{\text{avg}} D_h}{\mu_w} = \frac{1000 \times 0.0159 \times 240 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{3.816 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}} \approx 3.82
$$

Since Re ≪ 2300, the flow is **laminar**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermal Stress in a MEMS Bimetallic Beam

> **Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of length **L = 500 µm**, width **w = 50 µm**, composed of a silicon layer (**E_Si = 170 GPa**, **α_Si = 2.6 ppm/°C**) of thickness **t₁ = 5 µm** bonded to a gold layer (**E_Au = 78 GPa**, **α_Au = 14.2 ppm/°C**) of thickness **t₂ = 1 µm**. Find the tip deflection for a temperature rise **ΔT = 100 K**.

### Step 1 — Effective Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (Bimetallic)

The Timoshenko bimetallic strip formula gives the curvature κ:

$$
\kappa = \frac{6 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \Delta T (1+m)^2}{h \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn) \left( m^2 + \frac{1}{mn} \right) \right]}
$$

Where:
- $m = t_1 / t_2 = 5$
- $n = E_1 / E_2 = 170/78 = 2.179$
- $h = t_1 + t_2 = 6 \text{ µm}$
- $\alpha_2 - \alpha_1 = (14.2 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} = 11.6 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- $\Delta T = 100 \text{ K}$

### Step 2 — Plug Numbers Into Numerator

Numerator:

$$
6 \times 11.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times (1+5)^2 = 6 \times 11.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 36
$$

$$
= 6 \times 11.6 \times 36 \times 10^{-4} = 2505.6 \times 10^{-4} = 0.25056
$$

### Step 3 — Plug Numbers Into Denominator

First term: $3(1+m)^2 = 3 \times 36 = 108$

Second term: $(1 + mn) = 1 + 5 \times 2.179 = 1 + 10.897 = 11.897$

Third term: $\left( m^2 + \frac{1}{mn} \right) = 25 + \frac{1}{10.897} \approx 25.092$

So second+third = $11.897 \times 25.092 \approx 298.55$

Total denominator (multiplied by h): $h \times (108 + 298.55) = 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 406.55 = 2.439 \times 10^{-3}$

### Step 4 — Curvature

$$
\kappa = \frac{0.25056}{2.439 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 102.7 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

### Step 5 — Tip Deflection

$$
\delta = \frac{\kappa L^2}{2}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{102.7 \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2} = \frac{102.7 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{2}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{2.568 \times 10^{-5}}{2} \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta \approx 12.8 \text{ µm}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Dicing Concern vs. Mitigation Strategy

| Dicing Concern | Root Cause | Impact Severity | Mitigation Strategy | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Front-side chipping | Blade grit impact | Moderate | Fine-grit blade, step-cut | Slower throughput |
| Back-side chipping | Blade exit tensile stress | High | Dice-Before-Grind (DBG) | Process complexity |
| Stiction | Capillary force from DI water | High | Critical point drying, plasma dice | Cost, equipment |
| Particle contamination | Swarf debris | Moderate | CO₂ cryo clean, SRD | Cycle time |
| Vibration damage | Blade shock waves | Moderate | Soft-mount chuck, dicing tape | Blade stability |
| Thermal damage (laser) | Local melting | High | Stealth dicing, UV laser | Speed |
| Kerf loss | Material removed as swarf | Low–Moderate | Plasma or stealth dicing | Mask cost |
| Die strength loss | Sub-surface micro-cracks | High | Plasma dicing (no cracks) | Etch time |
| Edge defects → reliability | Crack propagation | High | Edge rounding, anneal | Added step |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Thermal Management Technique Comparison

| Technique | Best Heat Flux Range | Typical ΔT Achieved | Cost | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Natural convection | < **0.1 W/cm²** | 20–50 K | Very low | Low-power sensors |
| Forced air | **0.1–1 W/cm²** | 15–40 K | Low | Consumer MEMS |
| Heat sink (Al/Cu) | **1–10 W/cm²** | 10–30 K | Moderate | Packaged MEMS |
| Microchannel cooling | **10–1000 W/cm²** | 5–20 K | High | Power MEMS, RF amps |
| Two-phase cooling | **100–1000 W/cm²** | 2–10 K | High | Laser diodes |
| Thermoelectric (TEC) | **0.1–10 W/cm²** | 10–60 K | Moderate | Precision sensors |
| Heat pipe / vapour chamber | **1–50 W/cm²** | 5–15 K | Moderate | High-power microsystems |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Front-Side vs. Back-Side Chipping

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
> *Differentiate between front-side and back-side chipping during wafer dicing. Why is back-side chipping considered more critical for MEMS die reliability?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Front-side chipping** is the brittle fracture that occurs at the point where the dicing blade first contacts the wafer surface. It is primarily caused by the impact load of the diamond grit and is typically shallow (**5–10 µm**). It is mainly a cosmetic and yield concern at the wafer entry edge.

- **Back-side chipping** is the brittle fracture that occurs at the point where the blade exits the wafer. It is caused by **tensile stress concentration** at the exit plane, producing larger, V-shaped pop-outs (**10–30 µm**).

- **Why more critical for MEMS:** Back-side chipping creates sub-surface micro-cracks that act as stress concentrators. These cracks propagate during downstream thermal cycling, pick-and-place handling, and packaging stress, drastically reducing **die fracture strength** (often by **30–60 %**). For thin MEMS membranes and fragile resonators, even a sub-surface crack can lead to catastrophic failure under thermal-mechanical load. (3 marks awarded for clear differentiation and reliability justification)

---

### Question 2 — Thermal Resistance Concept

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
> *Define thermal resistance. Explain the electrical analogy used in thermal analysis of MEMS packages.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition:** Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ is the ratio of steady-state temperature difference across a thermal path to the heat flow rate through it:

$$
R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} \quad [\text{K/W}]
$$

For a 1-D conduction slab, $R_{th} = L/(kA)$.

- **Electrical Analogy:** Temperature difference **ΔT** behaves like **voltage (V)**, heat flow **Q** like **current (I)**, and thermal resistance **R_th** like electrical resistance **R**. Conduction paths in series add like resistors in series; parallel paths add reciprocally. This allows the use of standard resistor-network analysis to compute junction temperature in complex 3-D MEMS packages.

- **Practical utility:** Engineers use SPICE-like thermal network models to predict junction temperature without solving full 3-D heat equations. (3 marks for definition, equation, and analogy explanation)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Wafer Dicing Concerns and Mitigation (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester]**
> *Answer the following:*
> *(a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the various wafer dicing techniques used in MEMS fabrication, comparing mechanical blade dicing, laser dicing, and plasma dicing with respect to kerf, chipping, die strength, and throughput.*
> *(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the major dicing concerns specific to MEMS devices (stiction, vibration, contamination, micro-cracks). Suggest mitigation strategies for each.*

#### Part (a) — Dicing Techniques Comparison [7 Marks]

**1. Mechanical Blade Dicing**
- A diamond-impregnated blade rotating at 20,000–60,000 rpm abrades the wafer along the dicing street.
- **Kerf width:** 25–50 µm (largest of all methods).
- **Chipping:** Significant front-side and back-side chipping occur.
- **Die strength:** Reduced by 30–60 % due to sub-surface micro-cracks.
- **Throughput:** Very high — stacked blade hubs can dice 8–12 wafers in parallel.
- **Best for:** Thick Si wafers (>200 µm) and high-volume CMOS/MEMS.

**2. Laser Dicing (UV Ablation)**
- A focused 355 nm UV laser vaporises the silicon.
- **Kerf width:** 10–25 µm (smaller than blade).
- **Chipping:** No mechanical chipping; however a **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** of 5–20 µm forms around the cut.
- **Die strength:** Better than blade since no mechanical cracks, but HAZ can weaken the crystal.
- **Throughput:** Lower than blade — single-line cutting at ~50–200 mm/s.
- **Best for:** Ultra-thin wafers (<50 µm), low-k dielectrics, brittle compound semiconductors.

**3. Plasma Dicing**
- Photoresist masks the wafer, leaving dicing streets exposed to deep reactive ion etching.
- **Kerf width:** Equal to mask dimension, can be <10 µm.
- **Chipping:** Essentially **zero** chipping.
- **Die strength:** **Best** of all methods — die strength improvements of **50–200 %** over blade dicing because the etch process produces smooth, crack-free sidewalls.
- **Throughput:** Moderate — depends on wafer thickness and etch chemistry.
- **Best for:** Ultra-thin wafers, small dies, MEMS with curved boundaries, and reliability-critical applications.

#### Part (b) — MEMS-Specific Dicing Concerns [7 Marks]

**Concern 1 — Stiction:**
- *Cause:* Capillary forces from DI water used as coolant pull released MEMS microstructures into contact with the substrate.
- *Mitigation:* Critical point drying, hydrophobic SAM coating (FDTS), dimple structures, dry-dicing methods.

**Concern 2 — Vibration Damage:**
- *Cause:* Blade impact creates shock waves that excite MEMS resonant modes.
- *Mitigation:* Damping tape, soft-mount chuck, lower feed rate, dicing frames for mechanical isolation.

**Concern 3 — Particle Contamination:**
- *Cause:* Si swarf and debris from blade wear can lodge under MEMS structures.
- *Mitigation:* CO₂ cryogenic cleaning, spin-rinse-dry, cleanroom adhesive tape.

**Concern 4 — Micro-Crack Initiation:**
- *Cause:* Tensile stress at blade exit forms sub-surface cracks that reduce fracture strength.
- *Mitigation:* Dice-Before-Grind process, plasma dicing, UV-tape adhesives.

**Concern 5 — Thermal Damage (for laser dicing):**
- *Cause:* Local temperatures exceed 1000 °C in laser ablation, damaging metallisation.
- *Mitigation:* Stealth dicing (subsurface), shorter wavelength UV lasers, controlled pulse energy.

---

### Question B — Thermal Management in MEMS (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester — Internal Choice]**
> *Answer the following:*
> *(a) [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the thermal resistance of a 1-D conduction slab and explain the thermal resistance network model used for MEMS packages. A MEMS die of area 4 mm × 4 mm and thickness 0.4 mm is bonded to a copper heat sink using 50 µm of die-attach material (k = 2 W/m·K). Compute the overall thermal resistance from die to heat sink. Given k_Si = 150 W/m·K.*
> *(b) [7 Marks] Describe microchannel cooling and thermoelectric cooling techniques for MEMS thermal management. Compare their performance in terms of heat removal capacity, control precision, and power consumption.*

#### Part (a) — Thermal Resistance Derivation and Calculation [7 Marks]

**Derivation:**

Starting from 1-D Fourier's law for a slab of cross-section A, thickness L, with surface temperatures T₁ and T₂:

$$
Q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

Integrating across the slab thickness (steady-state, no internal source):

$$
Q \int_0^L dx = -kA \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT
$$

$$
Q \cdot L = kA (T_1 - T_2)
$$

Solving for Q:

$$
Q = \frac{kA (T_1 - T_2)}{L}
$$

Defining thermal resistance as the ratio ΔT/Q:

$$
\boxed{R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad [\text{K/W}]}
$$

**Network Model:**
For a complete package, multiple thermal resistances are arranged in series (e.g., junction → die → die-attach → substrate → heat sink → ambient) and parallel (e.g., top-side convection path). The total resistance is found by series-parallel combination, and the junction temperature is $T_j = T_a + P \cdot R_{total}$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- Die: A_die = 4 × 4 = **16 mm² = 16 × 10⁻⁶ m²**, L_die = 0.4 mm = **4 × 10⁻⁴ m**
- Die-attach: L_da = 50 µm = **5 × 10⁻⁵ m**, k_da = **2 W/(m·K)**
- k_Si = **150 W/(m·K)**

Step 1 — Die conduction resistance:

$$
R_{die} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{150 \times 16 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.167 \text{ K/W}
$$

Step 2 — Die-attach resistance:

$$
R_{da} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 16 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.2 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.5625 \text{ K/W}
$$

Step 3 — Total resistance (series):

$$
R_{total} = R_{die} + R_{da} = 0.167 + 1.5625 \approx 1.73 \text{ K/W}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{total} \approx 1.73 \text{ K/W}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The die-attach layer dominates the thermal budget (~90 % of total). Reducing its thickness from 50 µm to 20 µm or using a higher-k material (e.g., silver-filled epoxy, k = 8 W/(m·K)) would dramatically reduce R_total.

#### Part (b) — Microchannel and Thermoelectric Cooling [7 Marks]

**Microchannel Cooling:**
- Microchannels (50–500 µm wide, 200–500 µm deep) are etched into the silicon die.
- Coolant (typically water or dielectric fluid) flows through these channels, removing heat directly at the die surface.
- **Heat transfer coefficient:** h = 5,000–20,000 W/(m²·K).
- **Advantages:** Highest heat flux removal (up to 1 kW/cm²), uniform temperature distribution, integrated on-chip.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires pump and external reservoir, complex packaging, risk of leakage.

**Thermoelectric (Peltier) Cooling:**
- Solid-state devices using Bi₂Te₃ semiconductor junctions exploit the Peltier effect to pump heat when DC current flows.
- **Heat removal capacity:** 1–100 W per module depending on size.
- **Advantages:** Precise temperature control (±0.01 °C), no moving parts, reversible.
- **Disadvantages:** Low efficiency (5–10 % of Carnot), generates waste heat that itself must be removed, requires DC power supply.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Parameter | Microchannel Cooling | Thermoelectric Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| Heat flux capacity | Very high (100–1000 W/cm²) | Low to moderate (0.1–10 W/cm²) |
| Temperature control precision | Moderate (±1 °C) | Very high (±0.01 °C) |
| Power consumption (parasitic) | High (pump) | Moderate (TEC current) |
| Reliability | Moderate (fluid seals) | Very high (solid state) |
| Best application | High-power RF, laser diode arrays | Precision sensors, oscillators |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Converting mm² to m² and µm to m incorrectly. Always write out the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Series vs. Parallel Thermal Paths (–2 marks):** Forgetting that top-side convection is in parallel with the bottom stack, not in series.
> 3. **Omitting Numerical Substitution (–2 marks):** Writing only the formula without showing the substituted numbers in long derivations.
> 4. **Missing the "Engineering Insight" (–1 mark):** The KTU 2024 scheme rewards interpretation of the answer — e.g., "Which layer dominates the resistance?" Always conclude with one sentence of design insight.
> 5. **Forgetting the Stefan–Boltzmann Constant (–1 mark):** In radiation-based thermal questions, write **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wafer Dicing Concerns & Thermal Management**

### Wafer Dicing Concerns

- ☐ Dicing street width is **50–100 µm** and contains only test structures.
- ☐ Five primary dicing methods: **Mechanical Blade, Laser Ablation, Stealth, Plasma, Scribe-and-Break**.
- ☐ Mechanical blade dicing is the **industry default** for >75 % of production.
- ☐ **Front-side chipping** is caused by blade grit impact (5–10 µm depth).
- ☐ **Back-side chipping** is caused by tensile stress at blade exit (10–30 µm depth) — most critical for MEMS reliability.
- ☐ Stealth dicing uses IR laser focused **inside** the wafer, leaving **zero kerf** and **zero surface damage**.
- ☐ Plasma dicing improves die fracture strength by **50–200 %** vs. blade.
- ☐ **Stiction** from DI water capillary forces can permanently damage released MEMS structures.
- ☐ **Critical point drying** is the gold-standard mitigation for stiction.
- ☐ **Dice-Before-Grind (DBG)** mitigates back-side chipping by inverting the dicing/grinding order.
- ☐ Laser dicing creates a **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** of 5–20 µm.
- ☐ Cooling water DI consumption is typically **2–5 L/min** per spindle.
- ☐ Dicing shock waves travel at speed of sound in Si (~8430 m/s).

### Thermal Management

- ☐ Three modes of heat transfer: **Conduction, Convection, Radiation**.
- ☐ **Fourier's law:** $q = -k \nabla T$ for conduction.
- ☐ **Newton's law of cooling:** $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ for convection.
- ☐ **Stefan–Boltzmann law:** $q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ for radiation.
- ☐ Stefan–Boltzmann constant: **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**.
- ☐ Thermal resistance of a slab: **R_th = L / (kA)** [K/W].
- ☐ Series thermal resistances add directly: **R_total = Σ R_i**.
- ☐ Parallel thermal resistances add reciprocally: **1/R_total = Σ 1/R_i**.
- ☐ Junction temperature: **T_j = T_a + P · R_total**.
- ☐ Thermal diffusivity: **α = k / (ρ c_p)** — for Si: **9.2 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**.
- ☐ Thermal conductivity of Si: **150 W/(m·K)**; SiO₂: **1.4 W/(m·K)**; Cu: **400 W/(m·K)**; diamond: **2000 W/(m·K)**.
- ☐ Typical h values: natural air ~10, forced air ~100, water ~10,000 W/(m²·K).
- ☐ **Microchannel cooling** can dissipate **100–1000 W/cm²**.
- ☐ **TEC efficiency** is only **5–10 %** of Carnot.
- ☐ **CTE mismatch** (Si 2.6, Al 23, Au 14.2 ppm/°C) drives thermal stress in MEMS.
- ☐ **Thermal time constant** τ ≈ L²/α — for thin MEMS membranes can be **sub-millisecond**.
- ☐ Arrhenius rule: every 10 °C rise halves component lifetime.

### High-Yield Numerical Constants (Memorise These!)

| Constant | Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| k_Si | **150 W/(m·K)** | Conduction |
| k_SiO₂ | **1.4 W/(m·K)** | Dielectric layer |
| k_Cu | **400 W/(m·K)** | Heat sink, TSV |
| k_Air | **0.026 W/(m·K)** | Insulation |
| ρ_Si | **2330 kg/m³** | Density |
| c_p,Si | **700 J/(kg·K)** | Specific heat |
| α_Si (CTE) | **2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K** | Expansion |
| σ (S-B) | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** | Radiation |
| Speed of sound in Si | **8430 m/s** | Dicing shock |

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

# Module 4 — Stress Isolation in MEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Stress Isolation** in MEMS is the deliberate design methodology in which a sensitive micro-mechanical element (sensor or actuator) is mechanically decoupled from the surrounding package, substrate, and interconnect stresses through the use of compliant suspension structures (tethers, springs, folded flexures) so that parasitic mechanical loads do not corrupt the device's transduction signal.

In other words, the sensor "island" carrying the active MEMS structure is suspended on flexible beams that act as a **mechanical low-pass filter**, transmitting only the measurand of interest (pressure, acceleration, rotation) while rejecting DC and low-frequency packaging/thermal stresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Stress isolation is *not* a single structure — it is a **co-design philosophy** spanning materials (CTE matching), geometry (folded/U-beams), packaging (die-attach, lead-frame), and electrical (anchoring ground) domains.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Explanation)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Floating Tray in a Bumpy Boat"**
> Imagine placing a cup of coffee on a tray that is suspended from the boat's ceiling by four long rubber bands.
> - The boat rocks (representing **package stress**).
> - The tray (the **MEMS sensor island**) barely tilts because the rubber bands stretch and absorb the motion.
> - The coffee inside the cup (the **proof mass / sensing element**) experiences almost no motion.
> - The rubber bands (the **compliant tethers / folded flexures**) are the *stress-isolating elements* — long, thin, and mechanically soft in unwanted directions.

> [!NOTE]
> The key engineering trade-off: the same tethers that isolate stress also lower the **mechanical bandwidth** of the sensor and can introduce **cross-axis sensitivity**. The art of stress isolation is to choose tethers that are *soft in the stress direction* but *stiff in the sensing direction*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Silicon MEMS) |
|----------|--------|------------------------------|
| Young's Modulus of Silicon | $E_{Si}$ | **130–190 GPa** (orientation dependent) |
| Density of Silicon | $\rho_{Si}$ | **2330 kg/m³** |
| CTE of Silicon | $\alpha_{Si}$ | **2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** |
| CTE of FR-4 (PCB) | $\alpha_{PCB}$ | **14–17 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** |
| CTE of Alumina package | $\alpha_{Al_2O_3}$ | **~6.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** |
| Intrinsic thin-film stress | $\sigma_0$ | **±10 to ±500 MPa** |

> [!WARNING]
> The CTE mismatch between silicon die (2.6 ppm/°C) and a standard FR-4 PCB (~15 ppm/°C) can introduce **~100 MPa** of thermal stress over a 100 °C excursion — easily larger than the signal output of a typical MEMS pressure sensor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Stress in a Packaged MEMS Device

A MEMS die experiences stress from **four primary sources**. The KTU examiner expects you to *classify and explain* each.

### (A) Intrinsic / Residual Film Stress ($\sigma_{\text{intr}}$)
- Generated during thin-film deposition (LPCVD, sputtering, electroplating).
- Caused by:
  - Lattice mismatch with substrate
  - Impurity incorporation (H, N, O)
  - Grain-boundary relaxation
  - Differential thermal contraction after deposition
- **Typical magnitude: 10 – 500 MPa**

### (B) Thermal Stress ($\sigma_{th}$)
- Arises from **CTE mismatch** between dissimilar materials.
- Bimetallic-strip equation:
$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$
where $E$ is the effective Young's modulus, $\Delta \alpha$ is the CTE difference, $\Delta T$ is the temperature excursion.

### (C) Packaging-Induced Stress
- Die-attach epoxy cure shrinkage
- Molding compound cure shrinkage (transfer molding)
- Wire-bond loop tension
- Solder reflow cooling
- Lead-frame/laminate flexure

### (D) Mechanical Handling & Environmental Stress
- Dicing, pick-and-place, board flexure
- Drop-shock, vibration
- Operating-temperature cycling

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU:** *Stress isolation targets sources (A)–(C) primarily, because they are quasi-static or low-frequency. Source (D) is mitigated by package-level design (overmold, underfill, shock mounts).*

## 2.2 The Stress Isolation Philosophy — Three Pillars

1. **Mechanical Decoupling** — Suspend the sensor island on compliant flexures.
2. **Material Decoupling** — Match CTEs; use low-stress films; anneal.
3. **Geometric Decoupling** — Use folded, serpentine, or curved beams that are stiff axially but compliant transversely.

## 2.3 The Compliant Tether as a Mechanical Low-Pass Filter

A long, slender tether behaves as a **second-order spring-mass-damper system**. The transmitted force from the substrate to the sensor island is:

$$\frac{F_{\text{sensor}}}{F_{\text{substrate}}}(j\omega) = \frac{k}{\,k - m\omega^{2} + j c \omega\,}$$

- For $\omega \ll \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (the natural frequency): force is fully transmitted (DC stress passes through).
- For $\omega > \omega_n$: force is attenuated at **–40 dB/decade**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **fundamental limitation** of passive mechanical stress isolation: *static (DC) package stress cannot be filtered out by tethers alone.* Long-term drift must be solved by **CTE matching** and **stress-compensating materials**, not just suspension.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — it covers ~70% of numerical questions in Module 4.**

| # | Quantity / Structure | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Axial stiffness of a beam | $k_a = \dfrac{E w t}{L}$ | $E$ [Pa], $w, t, L$ [m] |
| 2 | Bending stiffness of cantilever (end-load) | $k_b = \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$ | — |
| 3 | Bending stiffness of clamped-clamped beam (center-load) | $k_{cc} = \dfrac{32 E w t^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | — |
| 4 | **Folded (U-shaped) flexure** | $\dfrac{1}{k_f} = \dfrac{2L^{3}}{3EI} + \dfrac{L}{EA}$ | $I = \dfrac{wt^{3}}{12}$, $A = wt$ |
| 5 | Folded flexure, simplified ($L \gg t$) | $k_f \approx \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{8 L^{3}}$ | bending-dominated |
| 6 | **Crab-leg flexure** (symmetric folded) | $k_{cl} = \dfrac{2 E w t^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | — |
| 7 | Torsional stiffness of a beam | $k_{\theta} = \dfrac{G w t^{3}}{L} \left[\dfrac{1}{3} - \dfrac{0.21 t}{w}\left(1 - \tfrac{t^{4}}{12 w^{4}}\right)\right]$ | $G$ = shear modulus |
| 8 | Natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | [Hz] |
| 9 | Thermal stress (bimaterial) | $\sigma_{th} = E \Delta\alpha \Delta T$ | [Pa] |
| 10 | Curvature from film stress (Stoney) | $\kappa = \dfrac{6 \sigma_f t_f}{M_s t_s^{2}}$ | Stoney's equation |
| 11 | Quality factor of isolation | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$, with $\zeta$ = damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | Static transmission ratio (tether only) | $T = 1$ (DC, no attenuation by passive spring alone) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU Formula Sheet, **always state the assumptions** when using simplified forms. E.g., for the folded flexure simplified formula, write "*assuming $L \gg t$ and bending-dominated*".

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive pressure sensors (barometric, MAP):** 4-folded-beam suspension isolates the piezoresistive bridge from aluminum die-cast housing stress.
- **MEMS accelerometers (ADXL, LIS3DH):** Proof mass suspended on 4-folded beams decouples it from PCB board-flex stress.
- **MEMS gyroscopes:** Drive-mode and sense-mode resonators require extreme stress isolation to maintain <1 °/h bias stability.
- **Optical MEMS (DLP, MOEMS mirrors):** Stress isolation prevents mirror curvature (static deformation) that would distort the projected image.
- **Bio-MEMS cantilevers:** Stress isolation reduces baseline drift, enabling sub-nm deflection resolution.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Pattern:** Module 4 nearly always contains at least one **14-mark derivation** on a folded beam flexure or the Stoney equation. The derivation below is the **highest-yield analytical content** in this module.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Spring Constant of a U-Shaped Folded Beam Flexure

### 3.1.1 Geometry and Coordinate System

Consider a **U-shaped (hairpin) flexure** in the $x$–$y$ plane, with:
- Each of the two parallel beams of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$ (out-of-plane).
- The beams are rigidly connected at the two ends (the U-bend and the foot).
- The structure is fabricated from a single material of Young's modulus $E$ and shear modulus $G$.
- A **transverse force $F_y$** is applied at the free (open) end; the closed end is anchored to the substrate.

```
        ┌──────┐  ← free end (load F_y)
        │      │
   ─────┘      └─────
   Beam 1     Beam 2
   ─────┐      ┌─────
        │      │
        └──────┘  ← anchor (fixed)
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

We treat the displacement of the free end as a **superposition of three independent contributions**, all linear (small-deflection assumption).

#### Step 1 — Identify the deformation modes

For each beam (loaded at the tip, clamped at the root), the tip displacement under a transverse force $F$ has:
1. A **bending component**: $\delta_{b} = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$  (end-loaded cantilever).
2. An **axial (tensile) component** due to the moment couple at the U-bend: $\delta_{a} = \dfrac{F L}{E A}$ (this captures the elongation of the beam under axial pull generated by the moment).
3. A **shear component**: typically negligible in slender beams (Euler-Bernoulli assumption); we ignore it here.

#### Step 2 — Combine contributions from both beams

By symmetry, **both parallel beams share the load equally**: each carries $F/2$.

> Wait — let us re-examine. The classical analysis treats the displacement of the U-flexure as the displacement under a transverse force $F$ at the open end. The two beams do **not** simply share $F/2$. The total compliance of the flexure is the **series sum** of the compliances of the two beams, because the displacement at the free end is the algebraic sum of the deformation of each beam segment.

Total displacement of the free end:

$$y_{tip} = y_{\text{Beam 1 bending}} + y_{\text{Beam 2 bending}} + y_{\text{Beam 1 axial}} + y_{\text{Beam 2 axial}}$$

Because of symmetry, both bending contributions are equal, and both axial contributions are equal.

#### Step 3 — Quantify each contribution

For a single end-loaded cantilever beam under transverse tip force $F$:

$$y_{b,1} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}, \qquad y_{b,2} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$$

For axial stretching of each beam, the *effective* axial force on each beam is the moment-induced tension. For a folded flexure carrying a transverse load $F$ at the open end, the moment at the closed end is $M = F L$. This moment is balanced by an axial force pair in the two parallel beams:

$$T = \frac{M}{d} = \frac{F L}{w}$$

where $d = w$ is the spacing between the two beam centerlines. Each beam therefore elongates by:

$$\delta_a = \frac{T L}{E A} = \frac{F L^{2}}{E A w}$$

The axial elongations of the two beams contribute equally to the free-end displacement (both pull the free end in the same direction):

$$y_{a,1} = y_{a,2} = \frac{F L^{2}}{E A w}$$

#### Step 4 — Total free-end displacement

$$y_{tip} = 2 \cdot \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} + 2 \cdot \frac{F L^{2}}{E A w}$$

$$y_{tip} = F \left[\frac{2 L^{3}}{3 E I} + \frac{2 L^{2}}{E A w}\right]$$

#### Step 5 — Effective spring constant

By definition, $k = F / y_{tip}$, so:

$$\frac{1}{k_f} = \frac{2 L^{3}}{3 E I} + \frac{2 L^{2}}{E A w}$$

Substitute $I = \dfrac{w t^{3}}{12}$ and $A = w t$:

$$\frac{1}{k_f} = \frac{2 L^{3}}{3 E \, (w t^{3}/12)} + \frac{2 L^{2}}{E (w t) w} = \frac{8 L^{3}}{E w t^{3}} + \frac{2 L^{2}}{E w^{2} t}$$

#### Step 6 — Simplified form for slender beams ($L \gg w$)

For typical MEMS folded flexures, $L \gg w$ and $L \gg t$. The first term (bending) dominates the second (axial) by a factor $\sim (L/w)^2$, so:

$$\boxed{\,k_f \approx \frac{E w t^{3}}{8 L^{3}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form the KTU examiner expects. **Always state the assumption** "$L \gg t$" explicitly when you write this.

#### Step 7 — Sanity check and physical insight

- $k_f \propto t^{3}$ — thickness cubed! Doubling the beam thickness increases stiffness 8×. This is why **thin released layers** are preferred for stress isolation.
- $k_f \propto 1/L^{3}$ — tripling the beam length reduces stiffness 27×. The most cost-effective stress-isolation knob is **beam length**.
- Compared to a solid cantilever of the same footprint, the U-flexure is **2–3 orders of magnitude softer** in the transverse direction — exactly what stress isolation demands.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Stoney's Equation for Intrinsic Film Stress

Stoney's equation is the **classic KTU result for thin-film stress**, often appearing as a 7-mark sub-question.

### Problem Setup
A thin film of thickness $t_f$ is deposited on a thick substrate of thickness $t_s \gg t_f$. The film has an intrinsic biaxial stress $\sigma_f$. The substrate bends to a curvature $\kappa$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

#### Step 1 — Force and moment balance on the substrate
The film stress exerts a net force per unit width:
$$F = \sigma_f \, t_f$$
This must be balanced by a moment in the substrate (since the system is free to bend):
$$M_s = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{3}}{12(1 - \nu_s^{2})} \, \kappa$$

where $E_s$, $\nu_s$ are the substrate's Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio.

#### Step 2 — Moment equilibrium
Setting $M_s = F \cdot (t_s / 2)$ (force acts at the centroid of the film, lever arm $t_s/2$):
$$\frac{E_s \, t_s^{3}}{12(1 - \nu_s^{2})} \, \kappa = \sigma_f \, t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}$$

#### Step 3 — Solve for $\kappa$
$$\kappa = \frac{6 \, \sigma_f \, t_f \, (1 - \nu_s^{2})}{E_s \, t_s^{2}}$$

#### Step 4 — Inverting to find $\sigma_f$
$$\boxed{\,\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^{2} \, \kappa}{6 \, t_f \, (1 - \nu_s^{2})}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For silicon with $\nu_s = 0.28$, the factor $(1 - \nu_s^2) \approx 0.92$, often dropped in KTU back-of-envelope problems. **Always verify whether your examiner wants the biaxial-modulus form or the simplified engineering form.**

### Stoney's Engineering Implications for Stress Isolation
- Stoney curvature directly couples film stress to substrate bending → **feed-through** to MEMS islands.
- Stress-isolated islands can be designed with a **symmetric anchor** that cancels the Stoney curvature at the device level (use of top + bottom symmetric dielectric stacks).
- This is why LPCVD low-stress nitride is preferred over PECVD nitride for the structural layer.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stress Isolation Effectiveness vs. Tether Geometry

| Tether Geometry | $k_x$ (axial) | $k_y$ (transverse) | Cross-Axis Coupling | Typical Use |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Straight cantilever | $\dfrac{Ewt}{L}$ | $\dfrac{Ewt^{3}}{4L^{3}}$ | Low | 1-D sensors |
| **U-folded flexure** | $\dfrac{Ewt}{L}$ | $\dfrac{Ewt^{3}}{8L^{3}}$ | Low | **Most common for stress isolation** |
| Crab-leg (symmetric folded) | $\dfrac{Ewt}{2L}$ | $\dfrac{2Ewt^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | **Very low** (thermal expansion self-compensated) | High-precision gyros |
| Serpentine spring | $\dfrac{Ewt}{N L}$ | $\dfrac{Ewt^{3}}{8 N L^{3}}$ | Low | Large displacements |
| Torsion bar | 0 (free) | $\dfrac{Etw^{3}}{3L}$ | **None** (pure rotation) | Mirrors, gyros |
| Membrane (continuous) | High | High | None | Pressure sensors (not for stress isolation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **crab-leg flexure** is the *gold standard* in precision MEMS because its symmetric geometry causes thermal expansion of the two legs to *cancel* — providing intrinsic stress isolation **without** any material property matching.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A MEMS accelerometer uses a U-shaped folded flexure with $L = 400\ \mu m$, $w = 4\ \mu m$, $t = 2\ \mu m$, fabricated in polysilicon with $E = 160\ GPa$. The proof mass is $m = 1 \times 10^{-9}\ kg$. Calculate:
(a) The transverse spring constant.
(b) The natural frequency.
(c) The expected static deflection under a 1 g ($\approx 9.81\ m/s^{2}$) acceleration.

**Solution (exact):**

Step (a):
$$k_f = \frac{E w t^{3}}{8 L^{3}} = \frac{(160 \times 10^{9})(4 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{8 (400 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}$$

$$= \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{11})(4 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18})}{8 (6.4 \times 10^{-11})}$$

$$= \frac{5.12 \times 10^{-12}}{5.12 \times 10^{-10}} = 0.01\ N/m$$

Step (b):
$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{0.01}{1 \times 10^{-9}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{10^{7}} \approx 503.3\ Hz$$

Step (c):
$$F = m a = (10^{-9})(9.81) = 9.81 \times 10^{-9}\ N$$
$$y = F / k = (9.81 \times 10^{-9}) / 0.01 = 9.81 \times 10^{-7}\ m = 0.981\ \mu m$$

> [!TIP]
> A static deflection of ~1 µm is well within capacitive-sensing resolution (~0.01 µm). The flexure is soft enough for stress isolation yet stiff enough for the sensor to function.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stress Isolation

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **flow of mechanical force** from the package into the MEMS die and shows how stress-isolation structures filter it. Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol, all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and labels are clean text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Package Substrate] --> B1[Die Attach Epoxy Layer]
    B1 --> C1[MEMS Silicon Die Anchor Region]
    C1 --> D1[Compliant Tether / Folded Flexure]
    D1 --> E1[Floating Sensor Island]
    E1 --> F1[Piezoresistive or Capacitive Transducer]
    F1 --> G1[Signal Conditioning Circuit]

    H1[External Mechanical Stress Source] --> A1
    H1[External Mechanical Stress Source] --> B1
    H1[External Mechanical Stress Source] --> C1

    D1 -. attenuates stress .-> E1

    I1[Intrinsic Film Stress] --> E1
    J1[Thermal Expansion Mismatch] --> B1

    K1[Mechanical Low Pass Filter] -. represented by .-> D1
    L1[High Frequency Vibration Pass] -. allowed by .-> D1
    M1[DC Package Stress Rejected] -. partially .-> D1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows represent **mechanical/structural load paths**.
- Dotted arrows represent **stress-coupling mechanisms** (intrinsic, thermal).
- The tether `D1` is the **filter**; it passes only the desired measurand.

---

## 4.2 Stress Isolation Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Identify Stress Source] --> S2{Stress Type?}
    S2 -- DC Thermal --> S3[Use CTE Matching and Crab-Leg Flexure]
    S2 -- DC Packaging --> S4[Use Die Attach Stress Relief and Soft Tethers]
    S2 -- AC Vibration --> S5[Tune Tether Stiffness for Attenuation Above Cutoff]
    S2 -- Intrinsic Film --> S6[Anneal Films and Use Symmetric Dielectric Stack]

    S3 --> S7[Validate with FEA and Curvature Measurement]
    S4 --> S7
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7

    S7 --> S8{Bias Drift Within Spec?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Design Locked]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Iterate: Lengthen Tethers / Match Materials]
    S10 --> S1
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Tethered Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[External Pressure] --> P2[Diaphragm]
    P2 -- mechanical coupling --> P3[Stress Isolated Island]
    P3 -- via folded flexure --> P4[Proof Mass on Tethers]
    P4 -- piezoresistive bridge --> P5[Signal Output V out]
    P6[Package Stress] -. partially blocked .-> P3
    P7[Thermal Stress] -. filtered by CTE matched anchors .-> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The pressure signal `P1` reaches the diaphragm `P2` directly. The package stress `P6` is **mechanically filtered** at the folded flexure `P3`. This is the essence of stress-isolated transducer design.
