# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions expect **3-line crisp answers** with a defining equation. Writing more wastes time; writing less loses marks.

### Q1. Define stress isolation in MEMS. Why is it required? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Stress isolation is a MEMS design technique in which the active sensing element is suspended on compliant mechanical structures (tethers, folded flexures) to decouple it from package- and substrate-induced mechanical stress. It is required because:
1. **CTE mismatch** between silicon die, die-attach epoxy, and package generates **thermal stress** of the order of 10–100 MPa.
2. Such parasitic stress shifts the **zero-point output** (offset) and causes **temperature-dependent drift**, degrading sensor accuracy.
3. The signal of interest (pressure, acceleration) must be **differentially transmitted** through the compliant structure while the stress is **attenuated**.

---

### Q2. What is a folded beam flexure? Why is it preferred for stress isolation? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A folded beam flexure (or U-shaped flexure) is a structure composed of two parallel beams rigidly connected at both ends, forming a hairpin shape. It is preferred for stress isolation because:
1. Its transverse spring constant is **$k_f \approx E w t^{3} / (8 L^{3})$**, which can be made arbitrarily small by increasing the length $L$ or decreasing the thickness $t$.
2. It provides **guided motion** (low off-axis stiffness, controlled in-plane compliance), allowing the sensor island to displace without tilting.
3. Its **symmetric geometry** offers intrinsic cancellation of thermal-expansion-induced displacement when the two legs are identical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks** with **module-internal choice**. You must attempt exactly **one** of the two alternatives.

### Option A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) Derive the spring constant of a U-shaped folded beam flexure used for stress isolation. State all assumptions. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Geometry:** Two parallel beams of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$ joined at both ends. Material has Young's modulus $E$. A transverse force $F$ acts at the open end; the closed end is anchored.

**Step 2 — Identify deformation modes:** Each beam deforms by (i) bending and (ii) axial stretching. Bending deflection of a single end-loaded cantilever: $\delta_b = \dfrac{F L^3}{3 E I}$.

**Step 3 — Moment–axial coupling:** The applied moment at the closed end is $M = F L$, balanced by a tension pair $T = F L / w$ in the two beams. Each beam elongates by $\delta_a = \dfrac{T L}{E A} = \dfrac{F L^{2}}{E A w}$.

**Step 4 — Superpose both beams:** The total free-end displacement is the sum of all four contributions:

$$y_{tip} = 2 \cdot \frac{F L^3}{3 E I} + 2 \cdot \frac{F L^2}{E A w} = F \left[ \frac{8 L^3}{E w t^3} + \frac{2 L^2}{E w^2 t} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Spring constant:** $k_f = F / y_{tip}$, so:

$$\frac{1}{k_f} = \frac{8 L^3}{E w t^3} + \frac{2 L^2}{E w^2 t}$$

**Step 6 — Slender-beam simplification ($L \gg t$):** Bending dominates:

$$\boxed{\,k_f \approx \frac{E w t^3}{8 L^3}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical substitution** (use KTU-supplied or self-chosen values, e.g., $L=400\,\mu m$, $w=4\,\mu m$, $t=2\,\mu m$, $E=160$ GPa) gives $k_f \approx 0.01$ N/m.

**Assumptions stated:** small deflections, Euler-Bernoulli (no shear), symmetric loading, $L \gg t$, $t_s \gg t_f$, isotropic linear-elastic material.

---

#### Q.A(b) With suitable diagrams, explain the concept of a floating-island stress-isolated MEMS structure. Discuss the role of the tether as a mechanical low-pass filter. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):** A floating island is the central mechanical element (proof mass, diaphragm, mirror) that carries the transducer. It is connected to the surrounding fixed anchor only through **two or more compliant tethers**. The anchor transmits the package stress, but because the tethers are long and thin, the strain energy is distributed along their length and the island experiences only a fraction of the original stress. The package-stress transmission ratio $T(\omega)$ from anchor to island is given by the transfer function of a second-order system:

$$\frac{X_{island}}{X_{anchor}}(j\omega) = \frac{k}{k - m\omega^2 + j c\omega}$$

**Mechanical low-pass filter (3 marks):** This transfer function is unity at DC (static stress passes through — a key limitation), but for $\omega > \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ it rolls off at **–40 dB/decade**. Thus *high-frequency* stress transients (vibration, shock, wire-bond impact) are strongly attenuated, while the *quasi-static* measurand is sensed. Practical MEMS chips use $f_n$ in the **100 Hz – 10 kHz** range, well above signal bandwidth but below mechanical-shock spectrum.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** Draw a flow: [Anchor] → [Tether] → [Island] → [Transducer], with annotations showing $k$, $m$, $c$ and the cutoff frequency $f_n$.

---

### Option B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q.B(a) Discuss the various sources of stress in a packaged MEMS device. Explain how each source contributes to sensor error. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

| # | Source | Mechanism | Typical Magnitude | Sensor Error |
|---|--------|-----------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Intrinsic film stress** | Lattice mismatch, impurities in deposited layers | 10 – 500 MPa | Diaphragm bow, proof-mass offset |
| 2 | **Thermal stress (CTE mismatch)** | $\sigma_{th} = E \Delta\alpha \Delta T$ | 50 – 150 MPa | Temperature-dependent offset, hysteresis |
| 3 | **Die-attach cure stress** | Epoxy cure shrinkage | 10 – 50 MPa | Zero-point shift after packaging |
| 4 | **Molding compound stress** | Transfer-mold cure | 20 – 100 MPa | Long-term drift, popcorn effect |
| 5 | **Wire-bond loop tension** | Localized force at bond pad | 1 – 5 g equivalent | Localized stress concentration |
| 6 | **Board flexure / 4-point bend** | PCB bending during assembly | 100 – 1000 microstrain | In-use offset, calibration shift |

> [!NOTE]
> Each source acts as a **quasi-static bias** on the transducer. In a piezoresistive sensor, for example, a 50 MPa package stress adds directly to the bridge output, mimicking a real signal.

---

#### Q.B(b) Explain the Stoney equation and its importance in quantifying thin-film stress for MEMS stress isolation. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stoney Setup (2 marks):** A thin film of thickness $t_f$ and intrinsic stress $\sigma_f$ deposited on a thick substrate ($t_s \gg t_f$) causes the substrate to bend to a curvature $\kappa$.

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):** Equating the moment from the film stress with the bending moment of the substrate:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s t_s^{2} \kappa}{6 t_f (1 - \nu_s^{2})}$$

**Step 3 — Importance (2 marks):**
- Provides a **direct, non-destructive measurement** of intrinsic film stress using only curvature and thickness data.
- The same $\sigma_f$ feeds into the design of the stress-isolating suspension: high $\sigma_f$ requires softer, longer tethers.
- Enables **process control** — Stoney curvature measured at wafer-level allows process engineers to fine-tune deposition parameters (temperature, pressure, gas flow) to minimize $\sigma_f$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Module 4 (Stress Isolation):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in the folded-flexure derivation (–1 to –2 marks). Always write: "Euler–Bernoulli, $L \gg t$, small deflection, isotropic linear elastic."
> 2. **Mixing up $E$ and $G$** in the torsional-stiffness formula. The torsional rigidity uses $G$, not $E$.
> 3. **Conflating "stress isolation" with "stress compensation."** Compensation is a *material* strategy (matched CTE, symmetric stacks). Isolation is a *mechanical* strategy (tethers). Both are needed.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the folded-flexure derivation (two parallel beams). Examiners explicitly look for this — write "$2 \times$" before each contribution.
> 5. **In Stoney's equation, omitting the biaxial modulus** $(1-\nu_s^2)$ factor loses 1 mark. If the question explicitly says "biaxial," it must appear; if it says "engineering approximation," you may drop it — but **state** that you have dropped it.
> 6. **Confusing $k$ (stiffness) with $c$ (damping) and $m$ (mass)** in the low-pass-filter explanation. KTU examiners often ask for the natural-frequency formula — memorize $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### Key Concepts
- [ ] Stress isolation = **mechanical decoupling** of a MEMS sensor from package/substrate stress via compliant tethers.
- [ ] Tethers are **not filters for DC stress**; they attenuate only high-frequency stress above $f_n$.
- [ ] **CTE matching** is essential for eliminating DC thermal-stress feed-through.

### Essential Formulas
- [ ] $k_f \approx \dfrac{E w t^3}{8 L^3}$ (folded flexure, slender-beam limit)
- [ ] $\sigma_{th} = E \Delta \alpha \Delta T$ (bimaterial thermal stress)
- [ ] Stoney: $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s t_s^2 \kappa}{6 t_f (1 - \nu_s^2)}$
- [ ] Natural frequency: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$
- [ ] Transfer function (2nd-order LPF): $\dfrac{k}{k - m\omega^2 + j c \omega}$

### Key Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] $E_{Si} \approx 160$ GPa (poly-Si / (100) Si)
- [ ] $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³
- [ ] $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- [ ] $\alpha_{FR-4} \approx 15 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C
- [ ] Typical MEMS film stress: 10–500 MPa
- [ ] Typical $f_n$ for stress-isolated MEMS: 100 Hz – 10 kHz

### Common Design Rules of Thumb
- [ ] Use **crab-leg** or **folded** flexures for in-plane stress isolation.
- [ ] Use **membrane** or **bossed diaphragm** for pressure sensors (NOT for stress isolation).
- [ ] **Long, thin** tethers → better isolation but lower bandwidth.
- [ ] **Symmetric anchor geometry** cancels Stoney curvature.
- [ ] **Anneal** thin films to reduce intrinsic stress.
- [ ] **Symmetric dielectric stack** (e.g., oxide–nitride–oxide) balances residual stress at the structural layer.

### KTU Question Patterns to Watch For
- [ ] "Derive the spring constant of a U-shaped folded flexure" — **always a 7- or 14-mark question.**
- [ ] "Explain the floating island concept and the role of the low-pass filter" — **common 7-mark question.**
- [ ] "Discuss sources of stress in packaged MEMS" — **essay-type 7-mark question.**
- [ ] "Stoney equation derivation and significance" — **classic 7-mark question.**
- [ ] "Compare crab-leg, folded, and cantilever flexures" — **comparison question (3–7 marks).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protective Coating and Media Isolation. Wafer Level Packaging and Die Level Packaging. Hermetic Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT632 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Protective Coating and Media Isolation. Wafer Level Packaging and Die Level Packaging. Hermetic Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Protective Coating in MEMS

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
> [!NOTE]
> **Protective Coating** in MEMS is defined as the application of a thin-film material layer (organic, inorganic, or hybrid) over the functional micro-structure to provide mechanical, chemical, thermal, and biological protection against operational, environmental, and handling stresses without significantly degrading the device's sensing or actuation performance.

In KTU terminology, protective coatings form the **first line of defense** in the packaging hierarchy, applied typically at wafer-level or post-dicing using conformal deposition methods. The coating must conform to complex 3D micro-topographies while maintaining thicknesses typically in the range of **1 µm to 100 µm**.

### Key Coating Materials (KTU Board Frequently Tested)
| Material | Type | Primary Use |
|----------|------|-------------|
| Parylene C | Conformal Polymer | Bio-MEMS, neural implants |
| Parylene N | Conformal Polymer | High frequency, low dielectric loss |
| BCB (Benzocyclobutene) | Spin-on Polymer | Low-k dielectric, planarization |
| Polyimide (PI-2611) | Spin-on Polymer | Stress buffer, flexible circuits |
| SU-8 | Negative Photoresist | Thick structural layers |
| SiC, Si₃N₄, SiO₂ | Inorganic CVD | Hermetic barrier layers |
| PDMS (Polydimethylsiloxane) | Elastomer | Bio-compatible, flexible |

### Conceptual Analogy: The "MEMS Raincoat" 🧥
Imagine your MEMS device is a tiny, delicate person standing in the rain (harsh environment). Without protection, the raindrops (chemicals, moisture, biological fluids) would damage the skin (sensitive microstructure). A **protective coating acts exactly like a high-tech raincoat** — it wraps around every contour of the person, is thin enough to not restrict movement (sensing/actuation), but thick and strong enough to keep all water out. Just like a raincoat has no zippers in critical zones, a MEMS coating must be **pinhole-free and conformal** over the sharpest micro-features.

---

## 1.2 Media Isolation in MEMS

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Media Isolation** is the engineering strategy of physically and chemically separating the sensitive MEMS sensing element from the hostile external medium (liquid, gas, biological tissue, fuel, blood) using a combination of protective coatings, isolation diaphragms, gel fills, and oil-filled cavities while still allowing the desired measurand (pressure, force, chemical concentration) to reach the sensor.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Divers' Wetsuit and Glove" 🤿
When a diver works in deep water, they don't remove their hand to feel objects — they wear a **thin, flexible glove** that transmits pressure (force) but keeps corrosive saltwater away from their skin. The MEMS pressure sensor in a car engine does the same: a thin **stainless steel or silicone isolation diaphragm** flexes in response to engine oil pressure, and a **silicone gel** (the "glove") transfers this pressure to the silicon piezoresistive element while keeping the corrosive oil 100% away from the silicon. The sensor "feels" the pressure but never touches the oil.

### Media Isolation Techniques (KTU High-Yield)
1. **Gel Isolation (Silicone Gel Fill):** Inert fluorosilicone or silicone gel transfers pressure, used in automotive pressure sensors.
2. **Diaphragm Isolation:** Metallic (stainless steel 316L) or ceramic diaphragm isolates the medium.
3. **Oily Backfill:** Oil-filled cavity with isolating membrane — common in medical pressure catheters.
4. **Polymer Isolation Layers:** Parylene or polyimide for chemical sensors and biosensors.
5. **Inorganic Barrier Layers:** LPCVD Si₃N₄ or SiC for long-term hermetic isolation.

---

## 1.3 Wafer Level Packaging (WLP)

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP)** is the process of encapsulating and interconnecting MEMS/IC devices while they are still in wafer form — *before* dicing — by bonding one or more capping wafers, applying redistribution layers, or forming through-silicon vias (TSVs) at the wafer scale, thereby minimizing package footprint to near-chip-size and reducing per-die cost.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Apartment Building Built Floor by Floor" 🏢
Imagine constructing **10,000 tiny apartments at once** on a single floor of a building (the wafer), then putting a complete **roof** (cap wafer) on all of them simultaneously, and only *afterward* cutting the building into individual apartments (dicing). This is WLP — instead of building 10,000 separate houses (die-level packaging) one roof at a time, you build them all together, saving enormous time, labor, and material. Each apartment (MEMS die) gets a complete roof (cap), plumbing (TSV/electrical feedthrough), and weather sealing (hermetic seal) in a single batch process.

### Types of Wafer Bonding for WLP (KTU Most Asked)
- **Anodic Bonding** (Glass–Si, 300–450°C, 200–1000 V)
- **Eutectic Bonding** (Au–Si, 363°C; Au–Sn, 280°C)
- **Fusion Bonding** (Si–Si, 800–1000°C, no intermediate layer)
- **Glass Frit Bonding** (Low-melt glass, 400–450°C)
- **Adhesive Bonding** (BCB, SU-8, 150–300°C)
- **Thermocompression Bonding** (Au–Au, 300–400°C)

---

## 1.4 Die Level Packaging (DLP)

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Die Level Packaging (DLP)**, also called **Device Level Packaging**, refers to packaging operations performed on individual singulated dies after wafer dicing. It involves mounting the bare die into a discrete package substrate (leadframe, ceramic, or organic substrate), making wire bonds or flip-chip interconnects, and encapsulating it in a plastic, ceramic, or metal housing.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Custom Jewelry Box" 💍
After you cut the giant floor of apartments into individual units, you take each one and place it into a **premium jewelry box** with foam padding, gold wires, and an outer shell. That jewelry box is the **DLP package** — it adds size and cost but provides robust mechanical handling, standardized electrical interfaces (pins/BGAs), and proven reliability. DLP is what allows you to **hand-solder a sensor to a PCB** without breaking the fragile MEMS structure inside.

### Common DLP Package Styles
- **DIP (Dual In-line Package)**
- **SOIC, QFP, QFN** — plastic leadframe packages
- **Ceramic DIP / LCC (Leadless Chip Carrier)**
- **Metal Can / TO-style packages** — for high-reliability
- **BGA (Ball Grid Array)** and **LGA (Land Grid Array)**
- **COB (Chip-on-Board)** — direct die attach with glob-top

---

## 1.5 Hermetic Packaging

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Hermetic Packaging** is a sealing technology that produces an enclosure with a leak rate so low that no measurable amount of gas, moisture, or contaminant can permeate the cavity over the device's operational lifetime. Per **MIL-STD-883 Method 1014**, hermeticity is quantified as a maximum helium leak rate of **5 × 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s** for the internal cavity.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Underwater Watch" ⌚
A classic **Rolex Submariner** can sit 300 meters underwater for years and never let a single molecule of saltwater in. Its case is **hermetically sealed** — a metal-to-metal gasket that is mechanically and chemically perfect. MEMS hermetic packaging is the same idea: a metal/glass/ceramic cap is **sealed atom-tight** around the silicon die. Inside, an inert gas (N₂, Ar) or vacuum protects the moving microstructure from oxidation, stiction, and humidity-induced failure.

### Standard Hermeticity Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)
- **Leak Rate (L):** in **atm·cc/s** (or **He·cc/s**)
- **MIL-STD-883 Spec:** ≤ **5 × 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s** (fine leak)
- **Gross leak test:** bubble test in fluorocarbon at 125°C
- **Internal Water Vapor:** ≤ **5000 ppm** at seal (per MIL-STD-883)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Protective Coating — Operational Mechanism

### Why Protect a MEMS Device?
A bare MEMS microstructure is vulnerable to:
- **Mechanical damage:** Particles, handling, dicing debris
- **Chemical attack:** Acids, fuels, body fluids, salts
- **Electrostatic discharge (ESD):** during handling
- **Biofouling:** in biosensors (protein adsorption)
- **Stiction:** surface-to-surface adhesion at the micro-scale
- **Moisture ingress:** corrosion of metallization

### Step-by-Step Coating Logic (KTU Scoring Points)

1. **Substrate Preparation** → Dehydration bake (150–200°C, 30 min) to remove adsorbed water
2. **Adhesion Promoter Application** → Silane (e.g., A-174) vapor or liquid
3. **Coating Deposition** → Choose method based on material (see table)
4. **Curing/Cross-linking** → Thermal cure or UV exposure
5. **Quality Verification** → Film thickness (profilometer, ellipsometer), pinhole test, adhesion test (tape pull, scratch test)

### Coating Method Selection Matrix (KTU Favorite Question)
| Method | Material | Thickness | Conformality | Cost |
|--------|----------|-----------|-------------|------|
| Spin Coating | Polyimide, BCB, SU-8 | 1–100 µm | ±10% planar | Low |
| Spray Coating | Polymer dispersions | 5–50 µm | Good on 3D | Medium |
| Dip Coating | Sol-gel, PVA | 0.1–50 µm | Excellent | Low |
| CVD (LPCVD/PECVD) | Si₃N₄, SiO₂, Parylene | 0.1–10 µm | Excellent (conformal) | High |
| Vapor Deposition | Parylene C/N | 0.5–50 µm | Best conformal | Medium-High |
| Electrodeposition | Conductive polymers | 0.1–10 µm | Good on patterned | Low |

### Stress in Thin Film Coatings
Residual stress in coatings is the **single biggest cause of MEMS failure** post-coating. Total stress is:

$$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{\text{intrinsic}} + \sigma_{\text{thermal}}$$

Intrinsic stress arises from film growth mechanisms; thermal stress arises from coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch:

$$\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \cdot (\alpha_s - \alpha_f) \cdot \Delta T$$

where $E_f$ = Young's modulus, $\nu_f$ = Poisson's ratio, $\alpha_s, \alpha_f$ = CTE of substrate and film, $\Delta T$ = temperature change from deposition to room temperature.

---

## 2.2 Media Isolation — Engineering Architecture

### The Three-Layer Isolation Model
Modern media-isolated MEMS pressure sensors use a layered architecture:

**Layer 1 (Outer):** Stainless Steel Diaphragm — thickness 50–200 µm, welded or glued to package.
**Layer 2 (Middle):** Silicone Gel — Dow Corning Q3-6575, fluorosilicone for chemical resistance, Young's modulus ≈ 0.1–1 MPa.
**Layer 3 (Inner):** MEMS Piezoresistive Die — sealed from the medium.

### Pressure Transmission Through Gel
For a thin gel layer of thickness $t_g$ and shear modulus $G$, the cut-off frequency beyond which pressure transmission attenuates is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho \cdot t_g^2}}$$

This is critical for **dynamic pressure sensing** (e.g., combustion engine knock sensors).

---

## 2.3 Wafer Level Packaging — Process Architecture

### Cap Wafer Design (KTU High-Yield)
A standard WLP cap wafer has:
- **Cavity etched by KOH or DRIE** (depth 50–500 µm)
- **Bonding frame** patterned on the perimeter
- **Electrical feedthroughs** through the cap (TSV or wire-bond channels)
- **Optional getter cavity** (see Section 2.5)

### Bonding Process Comparison
| Method | Temp (°C) | Pressure | Hermeticity | Alignment | CTE Mismatch |
|--------|----------|----------|-------------|-----------|--------------|
| Anodic | 300–450 | 200–1000 V | Excellent | 1–5 µm | Manageable (Corning 7740) |
| Eutectic (Au-Sn) | 280 | 0.4–1 MPa | Excellent | 5–10 µm | Good (matched) |
| Fusion | 800–1000 | 0.1–1 MPa | Excellent | 1–2 µm | None (Si-Si) |
| Glass Frit | 400–450 | 0.5 MPa | Good | 20 µm | Good |
| Adhesive (BCB) | 250 | 0.1 MPa | Non-hermetic | 5 µm | Low |
| Thermocompression | 300–400 | 10–40 MPa | Excellent | 1–5 µm | None |

---

## 2.4 Die Level Packaging — Assembly Steps (KTU Frequently Asked)

### Standard DLP Flow
1. **Wafer Dicing** (saw or laser) → individual dies
2. **Die Attach** (epoxy, eutectic, or solder) to substrate/leadframe
3. **Wire Bonding** (Au or Al, 25–50 µm wire) or **Flip-Chip** attach
4. **Encapsulation** (transfer molding for plastic, lid sealing for ceramic)
5. **Marking, Testing, Trimming**

### Package Thermal Considerations
The **thermal resistance** $\theta_{JA}$ (junction-to-ambient) of a DLP package is critical:

$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CA}$$

For plastic QFN: $\theta_{JA}$ ≈ 30–60 °C/W. For ceramic LCC: ≈ 15–30 °C/W.

---

## 2.5 Hermetic Packaging — Theory and Equations

### Hermeticity Leak Rate Equation (Helium Leak Test)
The standard helium leak rate equation used in MIL-STD-883 is:

$$R_1 = \frac{L \cdot P_0 \cdot t}{V}$$

where:
- $R_1$ = measured leak rate (atm·cc/s)
- $L$ = true leak rate (atm·cc/s)
- $P_0$ = internal pressure (atm)
- $V$ = internal cavity volume (cc)
- $t$ = dwell time (seconds)

### Equivalent Air Leak Rate
The measured He leak rate is converted to a standard **air equivalent leak rate** $L_a$ using:

$$L_a = L_{He} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M_{He}}{M_{air}}} \cdot \frac{\eta_{air}}{\eta_{He}}$$

where $M$ = molecular mass, $\eta$ = viscosity. In practice, $L_a \approx 0.37 \cdot L_{He}$.

### Getter Material Theory
A **getter** is a reactive material placed inside the cavity to absorb outgassed species (H₂O, O₂, H₂, CO₂):

$$M_{absorbed} = \int_0^t k(T) \cdot C(t) \, dt$$

Common getters: **Barium-Aluminum-Nickel (BaAlNi)**, **Zirconium alloys (St 707)**, **Calcium oxide**.

### Hermeticity Lifetime Estimate
The time for moisture to reach a critical level $C_{crit}$ inside a cavity is:

$$t_{life} = \frac{V \cdot C_{crit}}{L \cdot P_{ext}}$$

For $V$ = 0.1 cc, $L$ = 10⁻⁹ atm·cc/s, $C_{crit}$ = 5000 ppm, $P_{ext}$ = 0.1 atm, $t_{life}$ ≈ **15.8 years**.

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}(\alpha_s - \alpha_f)\Delta T$ | Thin-film stress | Pa |
| 2 | $R_1 = \dfrac{L \cdot P_0 \cdot t}{V}$ | He leak rate | atm·cc/s |
| 3 | $L_a \approx 0.37 \cdot L_{He}$ | Air equiv. leak | atm·cc/s |
| 4 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho t_g^2}}$ | Gel cutoff freq | Hz |
| 5 | $t_{life} = \dfrac{V \cdot C_{crit}}{L \cdot P_{ext}}$ | Package lifetime | s |
| 6 | $P_{abs} = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}$ | Capacitive MEMS | Pa |
| 7 | $F_{bond} = \sigma_{bond} \cdot A_{frame}$ | Bonding strength | N |
| 8 | $\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CA}$ | Thermal resistance | °C/W |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure | Pa |
| 10 | $R_{film} = \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot t}{A}$ | Sheet resistance | Ω/sq |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** In all equations, never write $\vert x \vert$ using vertical pipes inside a table. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid markdown corruption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hermetic Package Lifetime

**Problem:** A MEMS pressure sensor is packaged in a hermetic cavity of volume $V = 0.05$ cc at internal pressure $P_0 = 1$ atm (dry N₂). The measured He leak rate is $L_{He} = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ atm·cc/s. The package is exposed to external humid air with $P_{ext} = 0.05$ atm partial pressure of H₂O. The critical moisture concentration is $C_{crit} = 5000$ ppm. Calculate the package lifetime in years.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert the helium leak rate to an effective air/moisture leak rate.

The conversion from helium to air equivalent uses the square root of molecular mass ratio. Helium mass $M_{He} = 4$ g/mol, water mass $M_{H_2O} = 18$ g/mol, and effective air mass $M_{air} = 29$ g/mol. The empirical KTU/Knudsen correlation gives:

$$L_{H_2O} = L_{He} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M_{He}}{M_{H_2O}}} \cdot 0.46$$

Evaluating the square root:

$$\sqrt{\frac{4}{18}} = \sqrt{0.2222} = 0.4714$$

Now compute $L_{H_2O}$:

$$L_{H_2O} = (5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 0.4714 \cdot 0.46 = 1.084 \times 10^{-9} \text{ atm·cc/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the lifetime equation.

$$t_{life} = \frac{V \cdot C_{crit}}{L_{H_2O} \cdot P_{ext}}$$

Convert $C_{crit}$ to atm partial pressure (since $C$ is in ppm by volume):

$$P_{crit} = 5000 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1 \text{ atm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ atm}$$

This is the internal water vapor partial pressure threshold beyond which the device fails.

**Step 3:** Substitute and solve.

$$t_{life} = \frac{0.05 \text{ cc} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ atm}}{1.084 \times 10^{-9} \text{ atm·cc/s} \times 0.05 \text{ atm}}$$

Simplify numerator:

$$0.05 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm·cc}$$

Simplify denominator:

$$1.084 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.05 = 5.42 \times 10^{-11} \text{ atm²·cc/s}$$

Compute ratio:

$$t_{life} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.42 \times 10^{-11}} = 4.613 \times 10^{6} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to years.

$$1 \text{ year} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.156 \times 10^{7} \text{ s}$$

$$t_{life} = \frac{4.613 \times 10^{6}}{3.156 \times 10^{7}} = 0.146 \text{ years} \approx 53 \text{ days}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This short lifetime is a serious red flag.** The leak rate of $5 \times 10^{-9}$ atm·cc/s is **borderline acceptable** for low-humidity environments but fails badly in humid ones. KTU examiners love this numerical conclusion: students who do not convert $C_{crit}$ to atm and who forget the mass correction lose **3–4 marks** out of 14.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Stress in a Parylene C Coating on Silicon

**Problem:** A 5 µm Parylene C film is deposited at room temperature on a silicon die. The die is later cured at 200°C and cooled to 25°C. Calculate the thermal stress at room temperature. Given: $E_f = 2.76$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.35$, $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K, $\alpha_{ParyleneC} = 6.9 \times 10^{-5}$ /K.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute $\Delta T$.

Since the film was deposited at 25°C and cooled from 200°C to 25°C, the temperature excursion for the die is:

$$\Delta T = T_{cure} - T_{room} = 200 - 25 = 175 \text{ °C} = 175 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the biaxial modulus term.

$$\frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} = \frac{2.76 \times 10^{9}}{1 - 0.35} = \frac{2.76 \times 10^{9}}{0.65} = 4.246 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the CTE difference.

$$\alpha_s - \alpha_f = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} - 6.9 \times 10^{-5} = -6.64 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /K}$$

The negative sign means the Parylene C wants to contract more than the silicon upon cooling.

**Step 4:** Compute thermal stress.

$$\sigma_{th} = 4.246 \times 10^{9} \times (-6.64 \times 10^{-5}) \times 175$$

First, compute $4.246 \times 10^{9} \times 6.64 \times 10^{-5}$:

$$4.246 \times 6.64 = 28.19 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 28.19 \times 10^{4} = 2.819 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa/K}$$

Now multiply by 175:

$$2.819 \times 10^{5} \times 175 = 4.934 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 49.34 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\alpha_s < \alpha_f$, the stress is **tensile** on cooling — a major reliability concern.

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} \approx +49.3 \text{ MPa (tensile)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 49 MPa tensile stress in a 5 µm Parylene C film can cause **cracking, delamination, and buckling**. This is why parylene C thickness in MEMS is typically limited to **<10 µm** without stress-relief patterning.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Anodic Bonding Force and Energy

**Problem:** A borosilicate glass (Corning 7740) is anodically bonded to silicon at 350°C with 800 V. The bonding frame is 200 µm wide around a 3 mm × 3 mm die. Compute the bond strength and total bonding force if the contact area is $A = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m² (frame area only). Given typical bonded interfacial strength $\sigma_{bond} = 20$ MPa for Si-glass anodic bond.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the bond force equation.

$$F_{bond} = \sigma_{bond} \cdot A_{frame}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$F_{bond} = (20 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}) \times (1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2)$$

$$F_{bond} = 24 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Verify with electrostatic pressure model.

The Maxwell stress at the glass-Si interface is:

$$P_{elec} = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r E^2$$

For borosilicate glass at 350°C, $\epsilon_r \approx 6$, the field is roughly $E = V/d_{depletion}$ with $d_{depletion} \approx 1$ µm. So:

$$E = \frac{800}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 8 \times 10^{8} \text{ V/m}$$

$$P_{elec} = 0.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 6 \times (8 \times 10^{8})^2 = 1.70 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 17 \text{ MPa}$$

This agrees with the empirically observed 20 MPa — the order-of-magnitude validation is a typical KTU follow-up question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — WLP vs DLP

| Parameter | Wafer Level Packaging (WLP) | Die Level Packaging (DLP) |
|-----------|----------------------------|---------------------------|
| Process Stage | Before dicing | After dicing |
| Throughput | Very high (batch) | Low (serial) |
| Per-Die Cost | Low | High |
| Package Size | Near chip-size | Larger (adds 30–200%) |
| Hermeticity | Excellent (cap wafer) | Requires additional seal |
| Flexibility | Low (single process) | High (per-die customization) |
| Test Access | Difficult (before dicing) | Easy (each die testable) |
| Yield Loss Source | One bad die = whole wafer | Localized |
| TSV Compatibility | Native | Difficult |
| Typical Application | Inertial sensors, microphones | Pressure sensors, RF MEMS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: MEMS Packaging Hierarchy

flowchart TB

    A --> D["Level 3: Wafer Level Packaging"]
    D --> E["Level 4: Die Level Packaging"]
    E --> F["Level 5: Hermetic Sealing"]
    B --> G["Polymer or Inorganic Film"]
    C --> H["Gel or Diaphragm"]
    D --> I["Cap Wafer and Bonding"]
    E --> J["Leadframe and Encapsulation"]
    F --> K["Getter and Inert Fill"]
    G --> L["Reliable MEMS Module"]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

    A5 --> A6["Wafer Thinning Optional"]

    A7 --> A8["WLP Complete Die"]

```

    B5 --> B6["Ceramic or Metal Lid"]
